topics.title: Standardisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Standardisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Standardisation** in ship design and shipbuilding is the systematic process of establishing, documenting, implementing, and enforcing uniform technical specifications, dimensions, components, design practices, quality criteria, and procedural rules across the entire lifecycle of a vessel — from concept design and material procurement to fabrication, assembly, outfitting, and in-service maintenance.

In the context of **KTU PBNST604 – Ship Design II (Module 1)**, standardisation refers specifically to the deliberate effort to:

- Reduce the diversity of ship components, sub-assemblies, and hull forms.
- Promote interchangeability of parts across sister vessels and even across fleets of different owners.
- Align ship designs with **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** conventions, **classification society** rules, and **ISO** standards.
- Enable **series production**, **block construction**, and **modular outfitting** in modern shipyards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBNST604 – Module 1):**
> *Standardisation* covers (i) national & international standards, (ii) classification society rules, (iii) series standardisation (standard ships, parent vessels), (iv) modular construction standards, and (v) economic benefits of standardisation in ship design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of standardisation as **"LEGO® Bricks for Shipbuilding."**

| Real-World Analogy | Shipbuilding Equivalent |
|---|---|
| LEGO bricks come in a few standard sizes (2x2, 2x4, 4x4) | Ships use standard pipe diameters, bulb angles, stiffener sections |
| Any LEGO brick snaps onto any other brick of compatible size | Standardised flanges, valves, and machinery fit on any ship of the series |
| LEGO sets have instruction manuals (standards) | Classification society rules (LR, ABS, DNV, BV, IRS) act as "manuals" |
| Buying LEGO pieces in bulk is cheaper per piece | Series production of standard ships reduces per-tonne steel cost |
| You don't reinvent LEGO for every new toy | Shipowners don't reinvent hull form for every new vessel of a series |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Standardisation means making ships "look, build, and feel" alike in critical dimensions so that we can build them cheaper, faster, safer, and maintain them anywhere in the world — just like how McDonald's makes a "Big Mac" taste the same in Tokyo, Kerala, and Toronto.

---

## 1.3 Standardisation — The Three Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> **The Standardisation Triangle (KTU Conceptual Framework):**

1. **Technical Standardisation** → Uniform dimensions, tolerances, materials, symbols, units (SI units, ISO 128 technical drawings).
2. **Production Standardisation** → Series production, block fabrication, pre-outfitting, modular zones.
3. **Operational Standardisation** → Maintenance procedures, spares commonality, crew training, classification surveys.

---

## 1.4 Key Standardising Bodies Encountered in Ship Design

| Body | Full Name | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO** | International Organization for Standardization | Global industrial standards |
| **IEC** | International Electrotechnical Commission | Electrical & electronic standards |
| **IMO** | International Maritime Organization | Maritime safety, pollution prevention conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines) |
| **IACS** | International Association of Classification Societies | Harmonisation of class rules among 12 member societies |
| **LR / ABS / DNV / BV / IRS / KR** | Lloyd's Register, American Bureau of Shipping, DNV (Det Norske Veritas), Bureau Veritas, Indian Register of Shipping, Korean Register | Classification & statutory certification |
| **ISO/TC 8** | Ships and Marine Technology | Ship-specific ISO standards |
| **IEC/TC 18** | Electrical installations in ships | Electrical ship standards |
| **BIMCO** | Baltic and International Maritime Council | Standard shipping contracts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metric for Memorisation (KTU Board):** Memorise at least **four classification societies** (LR, ABS, DNV, BV) and **three IMO conventions** (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines 1966) — these are guaranteed 2-mark short questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic of Standardisation in Ship Design

### Step 1: Why Standardise?

The shipping industry is **capital-intensive** (a VLCC costs ~$120 million, an LNG carrier ~$200 million) and **globally distributed** (a ship built in Korea may be operated from Greece, registered in Panama, and maintained in Singapore). Without standardisation:

- Spare parts cannot be sourced port-to-port.
- Crew trained on one vessel cannot operate another.
- Insurance and classification become impossible.
- Shipyard learning curves never mature.

### Step 2: Levels of Standardisation

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LEVEL 5: INTERNATIONAL  → IMO, ISO, IEC                │
│  LEVEL 4: CLASS RULES    → LR, ABS, DNV, BV, IACS       │
│  LEVEL 3: NATIONAL       → IRS (India), KR (Korea), CCS │
│  LEVEL 2: COMPANY        → Shipowner specifications     │
│  LEVEL 1: YARD INTERNAL  → Shipyard production standards│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Step 3: Dimensions of Standardisation in Ship Design

| Dimension | Example | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Hull form** | Series 60 (US), DTMB, MarAd series | US Navy / MARAD standards |
| **Structural sections** | Bulb flats, angles, T-bars | ISO 657-1, Classification rules Pt.2 |
| **Pipe sizes** | Standard pipe schedules (Sch 40, 80) | ASME B36.10 / B36.19 |
| **Electrical** | Voltage (440V/6.6kV), frequency 60Hz | IEC 60092 (ship electrical) |
| **Valves & fittings** | Flange standards, pressure ratings | ASME B16.5, ISO 7005 |
| **Hatch covers** | Steel hatch panels, cleats | ISO 5779 |
| **Mooring equipment** | Wire rope diameters, bollard ratings | ISO 3828 |
| **LSA / FFE** | Life jackets, lifeboats, fire extinguishers | SOLAS Ch. III, LSA Code, FSS Code |

### Step 4: The Economic Logic

Standardisation reduces **Cost = f(Material, Labour, Overheads, Risk)** through:

- **Material cost ↓** → bulk procurement of standard parts.
- **Labour cost ↓** → reduced rework, predictable fitting.
- **Overheads ↓** → fewer unique drawings, simpler QA/QC.
- **Risk ↓** → proven designs, known failure modes.

---

## 2.2 Series Standardisation — The "Standard Ship" Concept

### 2.2.1 Definition

A **standard ship** (also called *parent ship* or *leader vessel*) is a basic, optimised design from which a *series* of size variants are derived by simple geometric scaling or by limited parametric changes.

### 2.2.2 Famous Standard Ship Series

| Series | Originator | Year | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Liberty Ships** | US Maritime Commission | 1941 | 2,710 ships; 18 shipyards; 4.5 days/ship |
| **T2 Tankers** | US War Shipping Admin. | 1942 | Standard oil tanker series |
| **MarAd Series** | US Maritime Admin. | 1950s | C4, C5, C6 cargo series |
| **SD 14** | Austin & Pickersgill / UK | 1968 | Pioneered series production of a standard merchant ship |
| **Series 60** | US Navy/DTMB | 1950s | Systematic hull-form series for resistance optimisation |
| **Ropax series** | Various European yards | 1990s+ | Standardised ro-ro/passenger ferries |

### 2.2.3 The "Follow-on" or "Sister Ship" Principle

Ships built from the same parent design, differing only in:
- Length between perpendiculars (LBP) — scaled
- Deadweight (DWT) — scaled
- Lightweight — scaled (cubic law)
- Capacity — scaled (square law for areas, cubic for volumes)

---

## 2.3 Modular Standardisation

A **module** is a pre-assembled, pre-outfitted, three-dimensional unit that can be independently fabricated, tested, lifted, and installed on the hull block.

| Module Type | Example | Weight Range |
|---|---|---|
| Engine room module | Complete with main engine, auxiliaries | 50–500 tonnes |
| Accommodation module | Cabins, galley, HVAC pre-installed | 100–800 tonnes |
| Bow module | Bulbous bow, anchor handling | 200–800 tonnes |
| LNG cargo module | Membrane or Moss tanks | 500–2000 tonnes |
| Bridge module | Fully integrated consoles | 20–100 tonnes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Modularisation is the *highest form* of standardisation because it shifts work from the constrained quay-side to the open fabrication shop → parallel production → reduced build time.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lightweight scaling | $W_L = W_{L,0} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)^3$ | $W_L$ scales with **cubic law** (volume) | tonnes |
| 2 | Steel weight scaling | $W_{st} = W_{st,0} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)^{2.5 \text{ to } 3}$ | Empirical exponent between square & cubic law | tonnes |
| 3 | Deadweight scaling | $DWT \propto L^{2.5 \text{ to } 3}$ | Series production scaling | tonnes |
| 4 | Power scaling (effective) | $P = P_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{\Delta_0}\right)^{2/3} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{V_0}\right)^3$ | Admiralty / similar form coefficient | kW |
| 5 | Prefabrication benefit (cost) | $C_{pref} = C_0 \left(1 - k \cdot f_{pref}\right)$ | $f_{pref}$ = fraction pre-fabricated; $k$ = gain factor (≈ 0.15–0.30) | currency |
| 6 | Time reduction by series | $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-b}$ | **Learning curve** — $b \approx 0.20$ for shipbuilding | months |
| 7 | Cost per tonne reduction | $C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}$ | $n$ = hull number in series; $b$ = learning rate | currency/tonne |
| 8 | Standardisation Index | $S_I = \dfrac{N_{std}}{N_{total}}$ | $S_I \to 1$ means highly standardised | dimensionless |
| 9 | Modular outfitting fraction | $f_{mod} = \dfrac{W_{mod,erected}}{W_{steel,total}}$ | Fraction lifted as modules | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid the pipe symbol** in tables — the formulas above use parentheses, ratios, and explicit divisions only, keeping markdown tables intact.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Use-Case | Application of Standardisation |
|---|---|
| **Korean mega-yard series building** (HHI, Samsung HI) | LNG carrier series — 4–6 sister hulls in a row |
| **Indian Navy fleet standardisation** | Project 17A frigates — 7 hulls from same parent design (GRSE & MDL) |
| **Class rules harmonisation** | IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR) — one rule set for tankers & bulk carriers |
| **ISO 9001 in shipyards** | Quality management — international standard for shipyard processes |
| **SOLAS unified requirements** | One set of life-saving appliance rules accepted by all flag states |
| **Offshore wind installation vessels** | Standardised WTIV hulls from Ulstein, ESVAGT |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Do **NOT** confuse *Standardisation* with *Normalisation*. Standardisation in shipbuilding = uniform dimensions/practices. Normalisation = a heat-treatment process for steel (different topic entirely).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Lightweight Scaling (Cubic Law)

**Statement:** When a ship is geometrically scaled from a parent hull by a linear scale factor $\lambda$, the lightweight (structural + outfitting weight excluding cargo, fuel, etc.) scales with the cube of $\lambda$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &\triangleq \dfrac{L}{L_0} = \dfrac{B}{B_0} = \dfrac{D}{D_0} \\[6pt]
\text{Volume scales as } &V \propto L \cdot B \cdot D \propto \lambda^{3} \\[6pt]
\text{Since steel density } \rho &\text{ is constant, weight } W = \rho \cdot g \cdot V \\[6pt]
\therefore W_L &= W_{L,0} \cdot \lambda^{3} \\[6pt]
\boxed{W_L &= W_{L,0} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)^{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the length of a ship (λ = 2) makes it 8 times heavier in lightweight.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deadweight Scaling for Series Ships

Empirically, when scaling a parent design to a sister series, deadweight does NOT follow a strict cubic law because designers optimise scantlings (rules-based) and machinery selections.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT &= \Delta - W_L \\[6pt]
\Delta &\propto L^{3} \quad \text{(displacement)} \\[6pt]
W_L &\propto L^{3} \quad \text{(cubic)} \\[6pt]
\text{But scantlings grow only } &\propto L^{2} \text{ to } L^{2.5} \text{ due to rules-based design} \\[6pt]
\therefore DWT &\approx K \cdot L^{2.5} \quad \text{to} \quad K \cdot L^{3} \\[6pt]
\boxed{DWT &= DWT_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)^{n}, \quad 2.5 \le n \le 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** Parent ship: $L_0 = 200\,\text{m}$, $DWT_0 = 50{,}000\,\text{t}$. Find DWT for sister ship with $L = 220\,\text{m}$, assuming $n = 2.7$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT &= 50{,}000 \cdot \left(\dfrac{220}{200}\right)^{2.7} \\[6pt]
&= 50{,}000 \cdot (1.1)^{2.7} \\[6pt]
&= 50{,}000 \cdot 1.292 \\[6pt]
&= 64{,}600 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Length growth of **10%** ⇒ DWT growth of **~29%**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Learning Curve (Wright's Law)

**Statement:** When building the $n$-th hull in a series, the build time (or man-hours, or cost) reduces by a constant fraction $b$ for every doubling of cumulative output.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= T_1 \cdot n^{-b} \\[6pt]
\text{where } b &= -\dfrac{\ln(1-r)}{\ln 2} \quad \text{and } r = \text{learning rate (fraction)} \\[6pt]
\text{Typical shipbuilding learning rate: } r &= 0.20 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; b \approx 0.322 \\[6pt]
\therefore T_n &= T_1 \cdot n^{-0.322}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example

Parent hull: $T_1 = 18\,\text{months}$. Find build time for the **4th hull**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_4 &= 18 \cdot 4^{-0.322} \\[6pt]
&= 18 \cdot \dfrac{1}{4^{0.322}} \\[6pt]
&= 18 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1.602} \\[6pt]
&= 11.24\,\text{months}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Build time reduced by **~37.5%** by the 4th sister hull.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cost Reduction Through Prefabrication

**Statement:** The per-tonne steel cost decreases linearly with the pre-fabrication fraction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_n &= C_0 \cdot \left(1 - k \cdot f_{pref}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{where } &k \in [0.15, 0.30] \text{ (yard-specific gain factor)} \\
&f_{pref} = \text{fraction of steel erected as pre-fabricated blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example

Baseline hand erection cost $C_0 = \text{USD } 2500/\text{t}$. Yard adopts pre-fabrication $f_{pref} = 0.7$. Gain factor $k = 0.25$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_n &= 2500 \cdot \left(1 - 0.25 \cdot 0.7\right) \\[6pt]
&= 2500 \cdot \left(1 - 0.175\right) \\[6pt]
&= 2500 \cdot 0.825 \\[6pt]
&= \text{USD } 2062.50/\text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Cost reduced by **USD 437.50/tonne** (~17.5% saving).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Standardisation Strategies

| Strategy | Cost Impact | Time Impact | Risk Impact | Flexibility | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full series (SD14 style)** | ⬇⬇⬇ (high) | ⬇⬇⬇ | ⬇⬇ | ⬇ (low) | Cargo, tankers, bulkers — 5+ hulls |
| **Class standardisation** | ⬇ | ⬇ | ⬇⬇ | ⬆ (high) | One-off complex vessels (Navy) |
| **Modular outfitting** | ⬇⬇ | ⬇⬇⬇ | ⬇ | ⬆ | Offshore, cruise, naval auxiliaries |
| **Component standardisation** | ⬇ | Neutral | ⬇ | ⬆⬆ | Mixed fleet operations |
| **Sister ship (2–3 hulls)** | ⬇ | ⬇ | ⬇ | ⬆ | Bulk carriers, product tankers |
| **No standardisation** | Baseline | Baseline | Baseline | Maximum | Truly novel / prototype ships |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Standardisation Index

A ship contains **820 unique parts**. After standardisation, only **165 unique parts** are retained (rest are common across the series). Compute the Standardisation Index.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_I &= \dfrac{N_{std}}{N_{total}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{820 - 165}{820} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{655}{820} \\[6pt]
&= 0.799 \\[6pt]
&\approx 79.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** ~80% of parts are common across the series → strong standardisation, eligibility for series-production cost benefits.

---

## 3.7 The "Parent → Sister" Geometric Scaling Table

For a parent vessel with $L_0 = 150\,\text{m}$, $B_0 = 22\,\text{m}$, $D_0 = 12.5\,\text{m}$, $\Delta_0 = 18{,}000\,\text{t}$, compute sister vessel with $L = 165\,\text{m}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{165}{150} = 1.10 \\[6pt]
B &= 22 \cdot 1.10 = 24.2\,\text{m} \\[6pt]
D &= 12.5 \cdot 1.10 = 13.75\,\text{m} \\[6pt]
\Delta &= 18{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^3 = 18{,}000 \cdot 1.331 = 23{,}958\,\text{t} \\[6pt]
\text{Cubic check: } \Delta &= \rho_{sw} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_B \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.025 \cdot 165 \cdot 24.2 \cdot 13.75 \cdot C_B \text{ t} \\[6pt]
\text{implying } C_B &\approx 0.343 \text{ (reasonable for a mid-sized bulker)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Every linear dimension grows by **10%**, every area by **21%**, every volume / weight by **33.1%**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
