# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each — Quick Recall)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Standardisation in the context of ship design and list any four benefits.**

**Model Answer:**
Standardisation is the process of establishing uniform dimensions, components, specifications, and practices for ship construction to enable interchangeability, reduce cost, simplify maintenance, and accelerate production.

**Four benefits:**
1. Lower material cost through bulk procurement.
2. Reduced build time through series production and learning-curve effects.
3. Easier maintenance and spares availability across fleets.
4. Enhanced safety and quality through compliance with proven rules (IMO, Class).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Name any four classification societies and state the role of IACS.**

**Model Answer:**
Four classification societies:
1. Lloyd's Register (LR)
2. American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)
3. DNV (Det Norske Veritas)
4. Bureau Veritas (BV)

**Role of IACS:** The International Association of Classification Societies harmonises the technical rules of its 12 member societies (CSR — Common Structural Rules, UR Z-series, UR M-series), promotes maritime safety, and acts as a consultative body to IMO.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks — Full Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** write only a definition and stop. KTU expects **derivation + numerical + diagram + industrial examples**.
> - Always quote **at least 2 IMO conventions** and **2 classification societies** for standardisation questions.
> - The **learning curve formula** $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-b}$ is mandatory for "series standardisation" questions.
> - **Mermaid diagram** in the answer sheet is optional but **block diagram with proper labelling** is rewarded.
> - Common mark loss: forgetting to mention **modularisation** as the highest form of standardisation.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the concept of **Standardisation** in ship design. Discuss its various levels — international, national, class, company, and yard — with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **series standardisation** concept. A shipyard builds the first hull of a series in **18 months**. If the learning rate is **20%**, estimate the build time for the **6th hull** using Wright's learning curve. Comment on the economic significance.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition (2 marks):** Standardisation is the systematic application of uniform specifications, dimensions, and practices across ship design and construction.
2. **Levels (4 marks):**
   - **International:** IMO conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL), ISO standards (ISO 9001, ISO/TC 8), IEC standards (IEC 60092).
   - **National:** IRS (India), KR (Korea), CCS (China) — local flag-state rules aligned with IMO.
   - **Classification Society:** LR, ABS, DNV, BV — technical rules for hull, machinery, electrical.
   - **Company:** Owner specifications — e.g., Shell's Tanker Safety Code.
   - **Yard:** Internal production standards — welding procedures, block sizes, outfitting zones.
3. **Industrial examples (1 mark):** SD14 series, Indian Navy Project 17A frigates (7 hulls from MDL & GRSE), Hyundai LNG carrier series.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Wright's Learning Curve Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= T_1 \cdot n^{-b} \\[6pt]
b &= -\dfrac{\ln(1-r)}{\ln 2} = -\dfrac{\ln(0.80)}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{0.2231}{0.6931} = 0.3219 \\[6pt]
T_6 &= 18 \cdot 6^{-0.3219} \\[6pt]
&= 18 \cdot \dfrac{1}{6^{0.3219}} \\[6pt]
&= 18 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1.738} \\[6pt]
&= 10.36\,\text{months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Economic significance (3 marks):**
- **42% time reduction** by the 6th hull → lower man-hours → lower cost.
- Each hull's labour content drops ~20% per doubling of cumulative output.
- Yard gains competitive edge; can quote lower prices and win repeat orders.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Internal Choice — Alternative Set]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **Modular Construction** as a means of achieving standardisation in shipbuilding. List the common types of modules and their typical weight ranges.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard reports the following data for a 4-hull series:

| Hull No. (n) | Build Time (months) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 20 |
| 2 | 16 |
| 3 | 13.5 |
| 4 | 12 |

Verify whether the series follows Wright's learning curve. If yes, estimate the **learning rate $r$** and predict the **build time for the 8th hull**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition (2 marks):** Modular construction is the fabrication of large three-dimensional ship sections (modules) — complete with machinery, piping, electrical, insulation, coating — in workshops, followed by lifting and joining on the hull.
2. **Advantages over conventional (2 marks):**
   - Parallel production → ~30–40% schedule reduction.
   - Higher workshop productivity → ~15–25% labour-hour saving.
   - Improved quality → controlled environment.
   - Safer work → less work-at-height.
3. **Module types & weights (3 marks):**

| Module | Typical Weight |
|---|---|
| Engine room module | 50–500 t |
| Accommodation module | 100–800 t |
| Bow module (with bulb) | 200–800 t |
| Stern module (with rudder) | 100–400 t |
| LNG cargo tank module | 500–2000 t |
| Bridge module | 20–100 t |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Test the learning curve hypothesis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= T_1 \cdot n^{-b} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ln T_n = \ln T_1 - b \ln n
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a linear relation between $\ln T_n$ and $\ln n$. Compute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n=1: \quad & \ln 1 = 0, \quad \ln 20 = 2.996 \\[6pt]
n=2: \quad & \ln 2 = 0.693, \quad \ln 16 = 2.773 \\[6pt]
n=3: \quad & \ln 3 = 1.099, \quad \ln 13.5 = 2.603 \\[6pt]
n=4: \quad & \ln 4 = 1.386, \quad \ln 12 = 2.485
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Slope estimation (linear regression or first–last method):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \dfrac{\ln T_1 - \ln T_4}{\ln 4 - \ln 1} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2.996 - 2.485}{1.386 - 0} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.511}{1.386} \\[6pt]
&= 0.369
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert slope to learning rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= -\dfrac{\ln(1-r)}{\ln 2} \\[6pt]
1 - r &= e^{-b \ln 2} = 2^{-b} = 2^{-0.369} = 0.774 \\[6pt]
r &= 1 - 0.774 = 0.226 \approx 22.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Predict $T_8$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_8 &= T_1 \cdot 8^{-0.369} = 20 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2.012} = 9.94\,\text{months} \\[6pt]
&\approx 10\,\text{months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Halving from $n=4$ to $n=8$ should reduce time by 22.6%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_8^{check} &= 12 \cdot (1 - 0.226) = 9.29\,\text{months}
\end{aligned}
$$

Close agreement ✓ → Series confirms Wright's learning curve.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Standardisation" (Module 1):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Uniform dimensions, components, and practices across ship design and construction.
- ✅ **Five levels:** International → National → Class → Company → Yard.
- ✅ **Four classification societies to memorise:** LR, ABS, DNV, BV (add IRS, KR for Indian/Korean context).
- ✅ **Three IMO conventions to memorise:** SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines 1966.
- ✅ **Two key ISO bodies:** ISO/TC 8 (Ships & Marine), ISO 9001 (Quality).
- ✅ **Series Standardisation:** Build multiple sister ships from one parent design → learning curve cost benefits.
- ✅ **Modularisation:** Highest form of standardisation; engine-room modules, accommodation modules.
- ✅ **Lightweight scaling formula:** $W_L = W_{L,0} \cdot (L/L_0)^3$.
- ✅ **DWT scaling exponent:** $n \in [2.5,\, 3]$ (NOT exactly 3).
- ✅ **Wright's Learning Curve:** $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-b}$, with shipyard $r \approx 0.20$.
- ✅ **Standardisation Index:** $S_I = N_{std}/N_{total}$ → ranges 0 to 1.
- ✅ **Prefab benefit:** $C_n = C_0 \cdot (1 - k \cdot f_{pref})$ with $k \approx 0.15$–$0.30$.
- ✅ **Famous examples:** SD14, Liberty Ships (2,710 hulls), Project 17A frigates, Korean LNG carrier series.
- ✅ **Pitfall to avoid:** Don't confuse Standardisation (uniform specs) with Normalisation (steel heat-treatment).
- ✅ **Industrial takeaway:** Standardisation = 15–30% cost reduction + 30–40% time reduction in series production.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process and Product Standard. Cargo Handling Equipment
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process and Product Standard. Cargo Handling Equipment

# 📘 KTU Premium Study Notes — PBNST604 Ship Design II

---

## 🔖 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Process Standards in Ship Design

**Formal Definition:**
> **Process Standards** are documented, codified rules, codes, and procedures that prescribe **HOW** a ship must be designed, analyzed, manufactured, tested, and certified throughout its engineering life-cycle. They govern methodology rather than the physical end-product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBNST604 – Module 1):**
> Process Standards cover the methodology pipeline: feasibility study → concept design → preliminary design → contract design → detailed design → production drawing → construction → classification → sea trials → delivery.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Driving Test" Parallel:**
Think of Process Standards like the **rules of obtaining a driving license**. The license authority (a *Classification Society* such as Lloyd's Register or IRS) does not tell you *which car* to buy (that's the *Product Standard*), but mandates *how* you learn, *how* you are examined, and *what checks* you must pass before you are allowed to operate. Similarly, Process Standards dictate **the workflow, documentation, verification, and validation steps** a ship design must traverse.

**Key Bodies that issue Process Standards:**
- **Classification Societies** — Lloyd's Register (LR), American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), DNV (Det Norske Veritas), Bureau Veritas (BV), Indian Register of Shipping (IRS), ClassNK, KR, RINA.
- **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** — Conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, COLREG, STCW).
- **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** — ISO 9001 (Quality), ISO 14001 (Environment), ISO 45001 (Safety).
- **Flag State Administrations** — DG Shipping (India), MCA (UK), USCG.
- **IACS** — International Association of Classification Societies (unifies class rules).

---

### 1.2 Product Standards in Ship Design

**Formal Definition:**
> **Product Standards** are codified specifications, performance criteria, geometric tolerances, and material/equipment requirements that prescribe **WHAT** the final delivered ship — as a physical product — must satisfy. They address the output quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:**
> Product Standard = **Specification** (the engineering contract document between owner and shipyard). It defines hull form, principal dimensions, capacity, speed, stability, structural scantlings, machinery, outfit, and compliance with class rules & statutory regulations.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Parallel:**
A Product Standard is like a **detailed recipe for baking a cake**. It tells you exactly: ingredients (steel grade, machinery), quantities (deadweight, length), oven temperature (service speed), baking time (cargo handling rate), and the final product must weigh **X kg**, taste **Y sweet**, and look **Z brown**. The Process Standard, by contrast, is the **kitchen hygiene rulebook** you must follow *while* baking.

**Components of a Product Standard (Ship Specification):**
- **Principal Particulars:** Lpp, B, D, T, dwt, Nt, GT.
- **Hull Form & Hydrostatics:** Block coefficient (Cb), prismatic coefficient (Cp), waterplane coefficient (Cwp).
- **Stability & Seakeeping Criteria.**
- **Structural Scantlings:** Plate thickness, section moduli per IACS S21/S31.
- **Machinery & Propulsion:** MCR, RPM, fuel type.
- **Outfit & Equipment:** Cargo handling gear, mooring, lifesaving.
- **Performance:** Trial speed, EEDI/EEXI/CII compliance.

---

### 1.3 Cargo Handling Equipment — Core Definition

**Formal Definition:**
> **Cargo Handling Equipment (CHE)** refers to all mechanical, hydraulic, electrical, and structural systems aboard a ship — and at adjacent terminals — used to **load, discharge, transfer, lash, secure, and stow** cargo. It is rated by its **Safe Working Load (SWL)** in tonnes and its **outreach** in metres.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SWL = Safe Working Load:** The maximum load that the equipment may handle under specified sea-state and heel/trim conditions. It is typically marked on the equipment and tested to **1.25 × SWL** during proof load testing.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Ship's Robotic Arms":**
A cargo ship is essentially a **floating warehouse**. Cargo handling equipment are the *arms, cranes, fingers, lifts, and ramps* the warehouse uses to move goods in and out. A bulk carrier uses giant "robotic arms" (deck cranes with grabs) to scoop grain; a container ship has tall "muscles" (gantry cranes) that lift boxes; a tanker uses "heart pumps" (cargo pumps) to push oil through pipelines.

---

### 1.4 Process vs Product Standard — Comparative Snapshot

| Aspect | Process Standard | Product Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | HOW (methodology) | WHAT (outcome) |
| **Owner** | Society / Authority | Owner + Yard |
| **Output** | Approved drawings, certificates, audit trail | Ship Specification Book |
| **Examples** | ISO 9001 workflow, IACS UR, Class rule audit | Lpp, B, T, dwt, MCR, speed |
| **Lifecycle Stage** | Throughout design → build → operation | At design freeze / contract |
| **Compliance Proof** | Survey reports, type approvals | Sea trial results, inclining test |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 🔖 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Society Workflow (Process Standard — Tier 1)

**Step-by-step Logical Flow:**

1. **Design Approval (Plan Approval)**
   - Owner contracts yard; yard submits GA, structural, machinery drawings to Class.
   - Class performs **rule-based scantling check** using formulae.
   - Iterative design review → "Approved" or "Returned for Modification".

2. **Material Certification**
   - All hull steel, pipes, castings come with **mill certificates** traceable to Class.
   - Class surveyors witness destructive/non-destructive tests (NDT).

3. **Construction Survey (Newbuilding)**
   - Stage surveys: keel-laying → hull-block erection → launch → outfitting → docking → trials.
   - **Continuous survey** ensures "as-built" matches "as-designed".

4. **Commissioning & Sea Trials**
   - Speed, maneuverability, endurance, noise/vibration measured; results filed with Class.

5. **Class Maintenance (In-Service)**
   - Annual surveys, Special Surveys (every 5 years), Tail-shaft surveys, Boiler surveys.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A ship cannot legally trade internationally without a valid **Class Certificate** + **Flag State Certificate** + relevant **Statutory Certificates** (SOLAS, MARPOL).

---

### 2.2 Product Standard — The Ship Specification Document

The **Specification** (often 200–400 pages) is the master Product Standard. It typically contains:

- **Section A — General** (Particulars, Class, Flag, Owner requirements)
- **Section B — Hull** (Steel grade, corrosion margin, coating)
- **Section C — Machinery** (Main engine, auxiliaries, electrical)
- **Section D — Outfit** (Cargo gear, mooring, navigation)
- **Section E — Performance** (Speed, fuel consumption, EEDI)
- **Section F — Quality Assurance** (QA/QC plan, NDT extent)
- **Section G — Statutory Compliance Matrix**

---

### 2.3 Cargo Handling Equipment — Taxonomy

#### A. By Ship Type

| Ship Type | Primary Cargo Handling Equipment |
|---|---|
| **General Cargo Ship** | Kingposts, derricks (union purchase), deck cranes |
| **Bulk Carrier** | Deck cranes (electro-hydraulic), grabs/buckets, conveyor belts |
| **Container Ship** | Ship's cranes (rare) + Shore-based gantry cranes (STS & RTG) |
| **Tanker** | Cargo pumps, manifold, inert gas system, deck pipelines |
| **Ro-Ro / Pure Car Carrier** | Side/stern ramps, internal car decks, liftable decks |
| **LNG / LPG** | Compressors, reliquefaction, cryogenic arms at terminal |
| **Heavy-lift / Multi-purpose** | Heavy-duty twin-pedestal cranes (SWL 100–1500 t) |

#### B. By Mechanism

- **Derricks & Booms** — Older technology, still used on small general cargo ships. Operated by **union purchase** (two booms working together, cargo lifted midway).
- **Deck Cranes** — Slewing, luffing, telescopic jib; pedestal or rail-mounted.
- **Gantry Cranes (Shipboard)** — Travelling across deck hatch covers.
- **Winches** — Cargo winches (hoisting), topping winches (boom angle), slewing winches (rotation), mooring winches.
- **Hatch Covers** — Steel pontoons; lifting types: side-rolling, end-folding, piggy-back, hydraulic folding.
- **Cargo Pumps** — Centrifugal (most tankers), positive displacement, eductor.
- **Hydraulic Systems** — Power packs for cranes, hatch covers, ramps.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The formulas below are routinely asked in KTU University Exams (Part A & Part B derivations).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Safe Working Load (SWL)} &: \quad \text{SWL} = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate Breaking Load (UBL)}}{\text{Factor of Safety (FoS)}} \\[6pt]
\text{2. Proof Load (Test Load)} &: \quad \text{Test Load} = 1.25 \times \text{SWL} \\[6pt]
\text{3. Crane Moment} &: \quad M = \text{SWL} \times R \quad \text{[tonne-metres]} \\[6pt]
\text{4. Number of Cranes (n)} &: \quad n = \dfrac{Q_{\text{cargo}} \times 10^{3}}{SWL \times N_{\text{cycles/hr}} \times \eta} \\[6pt]
\text{5. Cargo Handling Rate} &: \quad \text{CHR} = \dfrac{24 \times W}{T_L + T_D} \quad \text{[tonnes/day]} \\[6pt]
\text{6. Hatch Clearance} &: \quad h_{\text{clear}} = H_{\text{hatch}} - h_{\text{cargo}} \\[6pt]
\text{7. Grab Capacity} &: \quad V_{\text{grab}} = \dfrac{\text{SWL}_{\text{grab}}}{\rho_{\text{cargo}}} \quad \text{[m}^{3}\text{]} \\[6pt]
\text{8. Heel Angle under Lifting} &: \quad \tan\phi = \dfrac{W_{\text{load}} \times \ell}{GM \times \Delta \times 100} \\[6pt]
\text{9. EEDI Reference} &: \quad \text{EEDI} = \dfrac{CO_2_{\text{emit}}}{W_{\text{cargo}} \times V_{\text{ref}}} \\[6pt]
\text{10. Union Purchase Tension} &: \quad T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta} \quad \text{(W = cargo weight, }\theta = \text{boom angle)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Notation Key:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_{\text{cargo}}$ | Total cargo to load/discharge | t/h |
| $N_{\text{cycles/hr}}$ | Number of lifts per hour | cycles/h |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency factor (0.6–0.8) | – |
| $W$ | Cargo weight per lift | t |
| $T_L,\, T_D$ | Loading / discharging time | hours |
| $\Delta$ | Ship displacement | t |
| $GM$ | Metacentric height | m |
| $\ell$ | Horizontal distance from centreline to load | m |
| $R$ | Crane outreach radius | m |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Standards** ensure a ship built in Korea is structurally equivalent to one built in Germany — enabling global maritime insurance & trade.
- **Product Standards (Specifications)** allow an owner to procure ships competitively from multiple yards with predictable performance.
- **Cargo Handling Equipment** directly dictates the **port turnaround time**, which is the largest cost component for tramp shipping (typical target: 24–48 h port stay).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 🔖 SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Crane Number Estimation

> **Problem Context:** A bulk carrier of 40,000 DWT must load 30,000 t of iron ore in 24 hours. The ship has electro-hydraulic deck cranes each with SWL = 30 t (grab attached). Average cycle = 90 seconds. Determine number of cranes required (assume efficiency $\eta = 0.7$).

**Step 1 — Convert cargo to hourly rate:**

$$
Q_{\text{cargo}} = \dfrac{30{,}000 \text{ t}}{24 \text{ h}} = 1250 \text{ t/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of cycles per hour (per crane):**

$$
N_{\text{cycles/hr}} = \dfrac{3600 \text{ s/h}}{90 \text{ s/cycle}} = 40 \text{ cycles/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the crane formula (Equation 4 from §2.4):**

$$
n = \dfrac{Q_{\text{cargo}}}{SWL \times N_{\text{cycles/hr}} \times \eta}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
n = \dfrac{1250}{30 \times 40 \times 0.7}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{1250}{840}
$$

$$
n = 1.488 \approx 1.49
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Decision (Round Up):**

Since you cannot install 1.49 cranes, round up to the next whole number.

$$
\boxed{n_{\text{required}} = 2 \text{ cranes}}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity Check:**
With 2 cranes, actual achievable rate:

$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = 2 \times 30 \times 40 \times 0.7 = 1680 \text{ t/h}
$$

Time to load:

$$
T = \dfrac{30{,}000}{1680} \times 24 = 17.86 \text{ h} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Specification met.}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Heel Angle During Lifting (Stability Check)

> **Problem Context:** A general cargo ship of $\Delta = 15{,}000$ t, $GM = 0.80$ m, lifts a 25 t generator using a single deck crane whose jib is fully extended at 12 m off the centreline. Find the heel angle. Is it within the KTU/class limit of 5°?

**Step 1 — Apply the heel formula (Equation 8 from §2.4):**

$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{W_{\text{load}} \times \ell}{GM \times \Delta \times 100}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{25 \times 12}{0.80 \times 15{,}000 \times 100}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{300}{1{,}200{,}000}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\phi$:**

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 0.0143^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\phi = 0.0143^{\circ} \ll 5^{\circ}}
$$

> This is negligible — well within safety limits. However, **dynamic amplification factor (DAF)** must be considered in actual class rules: $\phi_{\text{dyn}} = \phi \times 1.3 \approx 0.019^{\circ}$, still safe.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Union Purchase Boom Tension

> **Problem Context:** A 10 t container is lifted between two derricks, each inclined at $\theta = 60^{\circ}$ from horizontal. Compute the compression in each boom.

**Step 1 — Vertical equilibrium at the cargo hook:**

Vertical components of both booms must support cargo weight:

$$
2 T \sin\theta = W
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for T:**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $W = 10$ t, $\theta = 60^{\circ}$:**

$$
T = \dfrac{10}{2 \times \sin 60^{\circ}} = \dfrac{10}{2 \times 0.866}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 5.77 \text{ t (compression in each boom)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: at $\theta = 60^{\circ}$, $T = W/(\sqrt{3}) \approx 0.577W$. At smaller angles (boom more horizontal), boom compression rises dramatically — this is why **union purchase requires derricks to be near-vertical**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Crane Selection vs Ship Size

| DWT (t) | Ship Type | Typical # of Cranes | SWL each (t) | Outreach (m) | Cargo Rate (t/h) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1,000–3,000 | Small General Cargo | 2–4 derricks | 3–5 | 8–10 | 50–100 |
| 5,000–15,000 | General Cargo / Multi-purp. | 2–4 cranes | 20–40 | 12–22 | 200–400 |
| 25,000–60,000 | Handysize/Supra Bulk | 4–5 cranes | 30–40 | 16–26 | 800–1,500 |
| 80,000–180,000 | Panamax/Kamsarmax Bulk | 4 cranes | 40–50 | 22–30 | 1,500–2,500 |
| 200,000+ | Capesize / VLOC | Gearless (shore cranes) | – | – | Up to 5,000 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 🔖 SECTION_4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification Society Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Owner Issues Tender] --> B[Yard Submits GA and Specification]
    B --> C{Class Society Review}
    C -->|Approved| D[Material Certification]
    C -->|Returned| B
    D --> E[Keel Laying Survey]
    E --> F[Hull Block Erection Survey]
    F --> G[Launch Survey]
    G --> H[Outfitting Survey]
    H --> I[Dock and Sea Trials]
    I --> J[Class Certificate Issued]
    J --> K[Annual Survey Cycle Begins]
    K --> L[Special Survey every 5 Years]
    L --> M[Class Maintained for Ship Life]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style J fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style M fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Cargo Handling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerSource[Power Source]
        P[Shipboard Diesel Generator Set]
    end

    subgraph DriveUnit[Drive and Control]
        HPU[Hydraulic Power Unit]
        ECR[Electric Control Cabinet]
    end

    subgraph CraneTower[Crane Pedestal]
        PED[Pedestal Base]
        SLEW[Slewing Gear]
        LUFF[Luffing Cylinder]
        JIB[ Telescopic Jib with Hoist]
    end

    subgraph EndEffector[End Effector]
        GRAB[Grab or Hook]
    end

    subgraph CargoHold[Cargo Hold]
        HATCH[Hatch Cover]
        HOLD[Cargo Hold]
    end

    P --> HPU
    P --> ECR
    HPU --> SLEW
    HPU --> LUFF
    HPU --> JIB
    ECR --> SLEW
    ECR --> LUFF
    ECR --> JIB
    PED --> SLEW
    SLEW --> JIB
    JIB --> GRAB
    GRAB --> HOLD
    HATCH --> HOLD
```

---

### 4.3 Hatch Cover Type Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select Hatch Cover Type] --> B{Hatch Length?}
    B -->|less 6 m| C[Single Pull Sliding]
    B -->|6 to 18 m| D[Pontoon Side Rolling]
    B -->|greater 18 m| E{Stowage Space?}
    E -->|Side Deck| F[Side Rolling]
    E -->|Aft of Last Hatch| G[Piggy Back Folding]
    E -->|On Top| H[Folding End Hinged]

    style A fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style D fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style H fill:#e377c2,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.4 Crane Configuration Diagram (Schematic ASCII)

```
                Boom (Jib) extended at radius R
                       \
                        \  <-- Luffing angle θ
                         \
                          *  <-- Hook + Cargo (SWL)
                         /
                        /
                       /
            ┌─────────┴─────────┐
            │   Crane Pedestal   │
            │  (Slewing ring)    │
            └─────────┬──────────┘
                      │
            ┌─────────┴──────────┐
            │   Ship's Deck       │
            └────────────────────┘
                 ↑ Centreline

  Moment M = SWL × R  → Used to size pedestal, foundation
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 🔖 SECTION_5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**
**Differentiate between Process Standards and Product Standards in ship design with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Process Standards** prescribe the *methodology* (the *HOW*).
> - Example 1: IACS Unified Requirements (UR) for scantling calculations.
> - Example 2: ISO 9001 quality management procedures followed by the yard.
>
> **Product Standards** prescribe the *output specifications* (the *WHAT*).
> - Example 1: Ship's principal particulars (Lpp = 180 m, dwt = 50,000 t) listed in the Specification.
> - Example 2: Required service speed = 14.5 knots, measured during sea trials.
>
> **Key difference:** Process = procedure, Product = performance. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**
**Define Safe Working Load (SWL) and Proof Load of cargo handling equipment. State the relationship.**

**Model Answer:**

> **SWL (Safe Working Load):** The maximum load that the equipment is certified to handle under specified operating conditions (sea state, heel, trim).
>
> **Proof Load:** The test load applied during commissioning and periodic recertification to verify structural integrity. Proof load is **1.25 × SWL** as per IMO and major Class rules.
>
> **Relationship:**
> $$\text{Proof Load} = 1.25 \times \text{SWL}$$
>
> The equipment must withstand the proof load **without permanent deformation**, confirming its SWL rating. (3 marks)

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` — Choice A**
**(i)** Explain the role of **Classification Societies** as Process Standard providers in ship design and construction. List any **four major classification societies** with their countries of origin. (7 marks)

**(ii)** Describe the **statutory certification framework** under **SOLAS** and **MARPOL**, with at least **two certificates** issued under each convention. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Role of Classification Societies (7 marks):**

Classification Societies are **independent, non-governmental organizations** that establish and apply technical rules for the design, construction, and survey of ships. Their roles:

1. **Rule Development:** Publish comprehensive design and scantling rules (e.g., LR *Rules and Regulations for Ships*, DNV *Rules for Classification of Ships*).
2. **Plan Approval:** Review and approve design drawings before construction begins.
3. **Construction Survey:** Continuous on-site inspection during building to verify material quality, workmanship, and dimensional compliance.
4. **Sea Trial Attendance:** Witness and certify performance tests.
5. **In-Service Surveys:** Annual, Intermediate, Special, and Continuous surveys throughout the ship's life.
6. **Statutory Certification on Behalf of Flag States:** Issue SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line certificates on delegation.

| Society | Country |
|---|---|
| Lloyd's Register (LR) | United Kingdom |
| American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) | United States |
| DNV (Det Norske Veritas) | Norway |
| Bureau Veritas (BV) | France |
| Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) | India |
| ClassNK | Japan |
| Registro Italiano Navale (RINA) | Italy |

**(ii) Statutory Framework — SOLAS & MARPOL (7 marks):**

**SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea):**
- International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea, first adopted 1914 (post-Titanic), current version SOLAS 1974 as amended.
- Certificates issued:
  1. **Safety Construction Certificate** — verifies hull, machinery, electrical compliance.
  2. **Safety Equipment Certificate** — verifies lifeboats, lifejackets, fire-fighting gear.
  3. **Safety Radio Certificate** — verifies GMDSS communication equipment.
  4. **Minimum Safe Manning Document.**

**MARPOL (Marine Pollution):**
- International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973/78.
- Has **six Annexes**:
  - Annex I — Oil (e.g., **IOPP Certificate**).
  - Annex II — Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS Certificate).
  - Annex IV — Sewage (ISPP Certificate in some administrations).
  - Annex V — Garbage.
  - Annex VI — Air Pollution (e.g., **IAPP Certificate**, **IEEC** for EEDI).

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the *certificates* without naming the **Convention** clearly. Always cite **Convention name → Annex → Certificate**. Marks are lost when the SOLAS vs MARPOL link is not made explicit.

---

**Q3(b). `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — Choice B (Alternative)**
**(i)** With a neat schematic, describe the working principle of a **deck crane** used on bulk carriers. Mention the function of **luffing cylinder**, **slewing gear**, and **hoist drum**. (7 marks)

**(ii)** A bulk carrier is fitted with **4 deck cranes**, each SWL = 35 t (grab operation). Average **cycle time = 75 seconds**, efficiency = **0.70**. Find the cargo handling rate in **tonnes/hour** and the **time required** to discharge 28,000 t of coal. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Deck Crane Working Principle (7 marks):**

A deck crane is an **electro-hydraulic, slewing, luffing, jib-type crane** mounted on a pedestal above the ship's deck. Major components:

- **Pedestal Base:** Welded to deck; transmits all loads (vertical, slewing moment, overturning) to hull structure.
- **Slewing Gear:** Hydraulic motor driving a **slewing ring** (large diameter ball bearing) rotates the entire upper crane structure through 360°.
- **Luffing Cylinder:** Hydraulic double-acting cylinder that raises/lowers the jib (boom) to vary **outreach** and **height of lift**.
- **Hoist Drum:** Driven by hydraulic motor; spooling wire rope to **raise/lower the hook** or grab.
- **Operator Cabin:** Houses joystick controls, load indicator, anti-two-block alarm.
- **Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU):** Electric motor driving hydraulic pumps; supplies pressure to all actuators.

*Schematic (refer §4.4 above)*: Boom angle θ, radius R, pedestal, slewing ring, hoist drum, hook.

**Working:** Operator controls slewing (rotation), luffing (boom angle), and hoisting (vertical motion). Grab opens/closes for bulk cargo. SWL marked; load moment indicator (LMI) prevents overload.

**(ii) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Number of cranes: $n = 4$
- SWL per crane: $35$ t
- Cycle time: $75$ s
- Efficiency: $\eta = 0.70$
- Total cargo: $W_{\text{total}} = 28{,}000$ t

**Step 1 — Cycles per hour (per crane):**

$$
N_{\text{cycles/hr}} = \dfrac{3600}{75} = 48 \text{ cycles/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective rate per crane:**

$$
Q_1 = 35 \times 48 \times 0.70 = 1176 \text{ t/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Total rate for 4 cranes:**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 1176 = 4704 \text{ t/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = 4704 \text{ t/h}}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to discharge 28,000 t:**

$$
T = \dfrac{28{,}000}{4704} \text{ h} = 5.95 \text{ h}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 5 \text{ h } 57 \text{ min}}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**
Such a short turnaround is realistic for a well-equipped geared bulk carrier, giving the ship a competitive edge.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **omit the efficiency factor (η)** or **forget to multiply by the number of cranes**. Both errors cost 2–3 marks each. Always show *each step explicitly* with substituted numbers — partial credit is awarded for correct intermediate values.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> ✅ **Process Standard** = methodology rule (HOW). Issued by **Class + ISO + IMO**. Verified via **surveys & audits**.
> ✅ **Product Standard** = outcome specification (WHAT). Documented in the **Ship Specification**. Verified via **sea trials & inclining test**.
> ✅ **Classification Societies** = ABS, LR, DNV, BV, IRS, ClassNK, RINA, KR, RINA. **IACS** unifies their rules.
> ✅ **Statutory Framework** = **SOLAS** (Safety) + **MARPOL** (Pollution) + **Load Lines** + **STCW** + **MLC**.
> ✅ **Cargo Handling Equipment** includes: **cranes, derricks, winches, hatch covers, cargo pumps, grabs, ramps**.
> ✅ **SWL** = max load under conditions. **Proof Load = 1.25 × SWL**.
> ✅ **Crane Moment $M = SWL \times R$** — governs pedestal foundation sizing.
> ✅ **Number of Cranes $n = Q / (SWL \times N_{\text{cycles}} \times \eta)$** — round **up** to integer.
> ✅ **Heel during lift $\tan\phi = W \ell / (GM \cdot \Delta \cdot 100)$** — limit ~ 5°.
> ✅ **Union Purchase tension $T = W / (2 \sin\theta)$** — boom angle matters greatly.
> ✅ **Hatch Covers**: side-rolling (most common), folding, piggy-back — chosen by hatch length & stowage.
> ✅ **Geared vs Gearless**: Capesize bulk carriers are often *gearless* (rely on shore cranes); Handysize/Supra are *geared*.
> ✅ **EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index) is a Product Standard imposed on new ships by IMO MARPOL Annex VI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PPCC SH" → Process, Product, Classification, Cargo Handling, Standard, Hatch covers.** Memorize the 6 pillars of this module before stepping into the exam hall!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lifting Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lifting Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Lifting Devices in Ship Design?

In the context of Naval Architecture and Ship Design, **Lifting Devices** refer to the integrated suite of mechanical, structural, and electromechanical equipment mounted on a ship's deck, superstructure, or hull to handle cargo, stores, personnel boats, anchors, and other heavy loads during the vessel's operational life.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned with Classification Society Standards):**
> A *Shipboard Lifting Device* is any appliance, mechanism, or assembly installed on board a vessel that is used to raise, lower, suspend, or transfer loads between a ship and a shore installation, between ship stations, or between sea and ship, governed by a defined Safe Working Load (SWL) and a defined radius of operation.

## 1.2 Hierarchical Classification of Shipboard Lifting Devices

| S.No. | Category | Primary Function | Typical SWL Range |
|:-----:|:---------|:-----------------|:------------------:|
| 1 | **Derricks & Kingposts** | General cargo handling | 3 t to 50 t |
| 2 | **Deck Cranes** (Pedestal, Jib, Knuckle-boom) | Multi-axis cargo handling | 5 t to 1,500 t |
| 3 | **Davits** | Launching/recovering boats, lifeboats, tenders | 1 t to 25 t |
| 4 | **Gantry Cranes** | Container, heavy-lift, and offshore vessels | 30 t to 4,000 t |
| 5 | **Winches & Windlasses** | Mooring, anchoring, and general hauling | 5 t to 150 t |
| 6 | **A-frames & Monorail Hoists** | Offshore supply and research vessels | 2 t to 50 t |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> For Ship Design II, the focus is predominantly on **Cranes, Davits, and Derricks**, as these directly influence the ship's general arrangement, structural design (foundation moments), and intact stability (heeling moment from off-center loads).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship as a Floating Crane Operator"

Imagine a **maritime crane operator standing on a tilting dock**. The dock is the ship, the operator is the crane, and the load is the cargo being transferred. Every time a load is lifted:

- The ship's **center of gravity shifts** (weight added at the hook).
- The **free surface effect** of any slack tanks intensifies.
- The **heel angle** changes as the load swings over the side.

A lifting device on a ship is not just a machine — it is a **dynamic system coupled to a moving, floating platform**. The crane must perform its job while the "floor" (the ship) tilts, rolls, pitches, and sinks deeper into the water. This is what makes shipboard lifting fundamentally different from shore-based crane design.

## 1.4 Core Functional Parameters

Every shipboard lifting device is defined by **four governing parameters** (as per **ISO 4301** and **FEM 1.001**):

- **Safe Working Load (SWL):** Maximum static load the device may handle under specified sea and weather conditions, measured in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**.
- **Radius of Operation (R):** Horizontal distance from the crane's slewing axis (or davit pivot) to the hook, measured in **metres (m)**.
- **Slewing Range (θ):** Angular range over which the device can rotate, typically **360°** for cranes and **±90° to 180°** for gravity davits.
- **Hoisting Height (H):** Vertical distance between the highest and lowest hook positions, measured in **metres (m)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **relationship SWL × R = constant** defines the *load-rating diagram* (also called the *load chart*), which is the single most important design document for any shipboard lifting device.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Lifting Devices by Motion Type

### 2.1.1 By Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

A shipboard crane can execute up to **four independent motions**:

1. **Hoisting (Luffing of the load):** Vertical movement of the load via the hoist winch.
2. **Luffing (Boom elevation):** Changing the boom angle (α) to vary the working radius.
3. **Slewing (Rotation):** Horizontal rotation of the entire crane structure about a vertical axis.
4. **Traveling (Gantry motion):** Horizontal translation along rails (only for gantry/rail-mounted cranes).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Mathematical Model** of a shipboard crane is essentially a **4-DOF robotic manipulator** mounted on a non-inertial (accelerating and rotating) reference frame — namely, the ship.

### 2.1.2 By Structural Type

| Structural Type | Boom Geometry | Typical Application |
|:----------------|:--------------|:--------------------|
| **Pedestal Crane** | Straight lattice boom | General cargo ships, bulk carriers |
| **Knuckle-Boom Crane** | Articulated boom with mid-joint | Offshore supply, AHTS vessels |
| **Telescopic Boom Crane** | Hydraulic telescoping boom | Naval vessels, patrol boats |
| **Gantry Crane** | Bridge structure on rails | Container ships, heavy-lift vessels |
| **Stiff-Boom Derrick** | Rigid boom supported by stays | Older cargo ships, tankers |
| **Tubular-Boom Crane** | Welded steel tube boom | Modern multi-purpose vessels |

## 2.2 Design Forces and Moments Acting on a Shipboard Crane

When a crane lifts a load, **three fundamental force systems** are transmitted to the ship's structure:

1. **Vertical Load (W):**
   The total weight of the load plus the crane's own suspended mass (hook block, ropes, etc.) acting downward at the hook position.

2. **Overturning Moment (M_o):**
   The product of the vertical load and the horizontal radius of operation. This moment tries to **tip the crane over**, and it is reacted by the foundation bolts and the pedestal.

3. **Slewing Torque (M_s):**
   The torque required to rotate the crane structure (with load) against wind resistance, bearing friction, and inertial forces. This is reacted by the slewing ring/turntable.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Essential Equations for Lifting Device Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE** — All symbols are standard **ISO 4301** and **FEM 1.001** nomenclature. Units are given in parentheses.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------------------|
| 1 | $M_o = W \times R$ | **Overturning Moment (kN·m)** | $W$ = total suspended load (kN), $R$ = radius (m) |
| 2 | $W = (m_{load} + m_{rig}) \times g$ | **Vertical Load (kN)** | $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, $m_{rig}$ = rigging mass |
| 3 | $P_{hoist} = \dfrac{W \times v_{hoist}}{\eta_{hoist}}$ | **Hoisting Power (kW)** | $v_{hoist}$ = hoisting speed (m/s), $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 4 | $P_{slew} = \dfrac{M_s \times \omega_{slew}}{\eta_{slew}}$ | **Slewing Power (kW)** | $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) |
| 5 | $M_{heel} = W \times (R \cos\phi - d_{cg})$ | **Heeling Moment (kN·m)** | $\phi$ = boom elevation, $d_{cg}$ = crane CG offset |
| 6 | $\sigma_{bolt} = \dfrac{4 M_o}{n_{bolt} \times d_{pitch} \times A_{bolt}}$ | **Foundation Bolt Stress (MPa)** | $n$ = number of bolts, $d_{pitch}$ = pitch circle dia. |
| 7 | $SF_{dyn} = 1 + \dfrac{v_{max}}{g \times t_{brake}}$ | **Dynamic Load Factor (—)** | Ship motion amplification |
| 8 | $W_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{air} C_d A v_{wind}^2$ | **Wind Force on Boom (N)** | $\rho_{air}$ = **1.225 kg/m³** (sea-level) |
| 9 | $t_{plate} = \dfrac{M_o}{f_y \times Z_{pedestal}}$ | **Pedestal Thickness (m)** | $f_y$ = yield strength (Pa), $Z$ = section modulus |
| 10 | $R_{min} = h_{boom} \times \sin(\alpha_{min})$ | **Minimum Working Radius (m)** | $\alpha_{min}$ = minimum boom angle (deg) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 EXAM TIP:** Questions almost always test Equation (1), (5), and (6). Always write the unit and the assumption (e.g., *static load, 0° heel, no wind*) to score full marks.

## 2.4 Environmental and Operational Factors Affecting Design

### 2.4.1 Ship Motion Effects (Class Notation Rules)

According to **DNV-OS-E101** and **Lloyd's Register SSC Rules**, shipboard cranes must be designed for **simultaneous ship motions**. The standard operating envelope is:

- **Roll:** ±10° at a period of **7 to 10 seconds**
- **Pitch:** ±5° at a period of **6 to 8 seconds**
- **Heave:** ±1.0 m at a period of **5 to 7 seconds**

The **Dynamic Load Factor (DLF)** due to these motions is incorporated by Equation (7) above. For a **DNV Crane-N notation**, the DLF typically lies between **1.25 and 2.0**, depending on the operational state.

### 2.4.2 Heeling Moment and Stability

The heeling moment $M_{heel}$ caused by an off-center lifted load is the **single most critical** parameter for the Naval Architect, because it reduces the ship's righting lever (GZ). The **KTU-stable criterion** is:

$$GZ_{corrected} = GZ_{original} - \dfrac{M_{heel}}{\Delta \times g}$$

where $\Delta$ is the ship's displacement (tonnes). The vessel must satisfy:

$$GZ_{corrected} \geq 0.10 \text{ m (minimum) at all heeling angles from 0° to 30°}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This criterion is often called the **"IMO Heel-Check"** because it mirrors the intact stability code's requirements for cargo handling.

## 2.5 Crane Foundation — Structural Integration

The crane is connected to the ship structure via a **pedestal** welded to a **doubler plate** (or "crane pad") on the deck, secured by a ring of **foundation bolts**. The design is governed by:

- **Bending moment** = $M_o$ (overturning)
- **Vertical load** = $W$ (compressive)
- **Torsional moment** = $M_s$ (slewing)
- **Wind moment** = $W_{wind} \times h_{boom}$ (horizontal)

The number of foundation bolts, their diameter, and grade (typically **8.8, 10.9, or 12.9** per ISO 898) are determined from Equation (6).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Load-Rating Diagram (Load-Radius Curve)

The **load-rating diagram** is the curve that describes how the **maximum permissible SWL** varies with the **radius of operation** for a given crane. It is a direct consequence of the **structural moment capacity** of the boom and the **stability** of the vessel.

### Step 1: Establish the Governing Constraint

The crane structure has a finite **maximum allowable moment** $M_{allow}$ at the slewing axis, determined by the boom's section modulus, the material's yield strength, and the classification society's safety factor.

$$M_{allow} = \frac{f_y \times Z_{boom}}{SF_{class}}$$

where:
- $f_y$ = yield strength of the boom steel (Pa)
- $Z_{boom}$ = section modulus of the boom cross-section (m³)
- $SF_{class}$ = class safety factor (typically **1.67** for DNV, **1.50** for LR)

### Step 2: Express the Actual Operating Moment

The actual moment generated by the lifted load at any working radius $R$ is:

$$M_{actual}(R) = W_{total}(R) \times R = \left(m_{load} + m_{rig}\right) g \times R$$

### Step 3: Apply the Constraint for All Radii

For safe operation at every radius, we require:

$$M_{actual}(R) \leq M_{allow}$$

Substituting and solving for the maximum allowable mass at radius $R$:

$$m_{load,max}(R) = \frac{M_{allow}}{g \times R} - m_{rig}$$

### Step 4: Define the Two Distinct Regions of the Load Chart

**Region 1 — Radius-Limited Operation ($R \geq R_{critical}$):**
The boom strength controls. The SWL **decreases** as $R$ **increases** following the inverse law $SWL \propto 1/R$.

**Region 2 — Strength-Limited Operation ($R \leq R_{critical}$):**
The hoisting system (winch, ropes, sheaves) controls. The SWL is **constant** at its nameplate value, and the hook can be brought inboard without exceeding the rated load.

### Step 5: Analytical Expression of the Load Chart

$$\boxed{SWL(R) = \begin{cases} SWL_{rated} & \text{if } R \leq R_{crit} \\[6pt] \dfrac{M_{allow}}{g \times R} - m_{rig} & \text{if } R > R_{crit} \end{cases}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** This inverse-square (in load) relationship is why crane charts look like a *hyperbolic shoulder* — wide at small radii, sharply descending at large radii.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heeling Moment of a Ship with an Off-Centre Lifted Load

### Step 1: Set Up the Coordinate System

Let the ship's centreline (fore-aft) be the **x-axis**, and the transverse axis (port-starboard) be the **y-axis**. Place the crane's slewing axis at coordinates $(x_c, y_c)$ measured from midships and from the centreline, respectively.

### Step 2: Position of the Lifted Load

When the boom is at elevation angle $\alpha$ (measured from the horizontal) and slewed to the side, the horizontal position of the hook relative to the slewing axis is:

$$x_{hook} = x_c + R \cos\alpha$$
$$y_{hook} = y_c + R \sin\alpha$$

(assuming the boom is slewed 90° outboard so that the y-component of the boom projection is $R \sin\alpha$).

### Step 3: Transverse Heeling Moment (Roll-Axis)

The transverse moment is the heeling moment because it acts about the ship's longitudinal axis:

$$M_{heel} = W \times y_{hook} = W \left( y_c + R \sin\alpha \right)$$

### Step 4: Special Case — Pure Outboard Lift ($y_c = 0$)

For most pedestal cranes located on the centreline ($y_c = 0$), the expression simplifies to:

$$\boxed{M_{heel} = W \times R \sin\alpha}$$

### Step 5: Integration with the Stability Code

The **righting lever correction** is applied as follows:

$$GZ_{new}(\phi) = GZ_{static}(\phi) - \frac{M_{heel}}{\Delta \times g} \cos\phi$$

The KTU and IMO criterion is that $GZ_{new}(\phi) > 0$ for all $\phi \in [0°, 30°]$ during the lift.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Crane Foundation Bolt Design

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Module 1 Style):**
A pedestal crane on a general cargo ship is to lift a load of **m = 18 tonnes** at a maximum radius of **R = 14 m**. The boom mass is **2.5 t** with its CG at **8 m** from the slewing axis. The pedestal has **n = 16 foundation bolts** arranged on a pitch circle diameter of **D = 900 mm**. The bolts are of grade **10.9** (yield strength $f_{y,bolt} = 940$ MPa). Determine the **minimum bolt diameter** required, assuming a class safety factor of **1.5**.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Calculate the total vertical load**

$$W = (m_{load} + m_{boom}) \times g = (18{,}000 + 2{,}500) \times 9.81 = 201{,}405 \text{ N} \approx 201.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the overturning moment**

$$M_o = m_{load} \times g \times R + m_{boom} \times g \times R_{cg,boom}$$

$$M_o = 18{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 14 + 2{,}500 \times 9.81 \times 8$$

$$M_o = 2{,}472{,}120 + 196{,}200 = 2{,}668{,}320 \text{ N·m} \approx 2.67 \text{ MN·m}$$

**Step 3: Apply the bending stress equation for the bolt ring**

The maximum tensile force in the most-loaded bolt is:

$$F_{bolt,max} = \frac{4 \times M_o}{n \times D} = \frac{4 \times 2{,}668{,}320}{16 \times 0.900} = \frac{10{,}673{,}280}{14.4} = 741{,}200 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Determine the allowable bolt force**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{f_{y,bolt}}{SF} = \frac{940}{1.5} = 626.67 \text{ MPa}$$

$$F_{bolt,allow} = \sigma_{allow} \times A_{bolt} = 626.67 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 5: Equate and solve for the bolt diameter**

$$626.67 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = 741{,}200$$

$$d^2 = \frac{741{,}200 \times 4}{626.67 \times \pi} = \frac{2{,}964{,}800}{1{,}968.3} = 1{,}506.4 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$d = \sqrt{1{,}506.4} = 38.81 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Apply standard size and final check**

Choose the next standard metric size: **d = 42 mm** (M42, grade 10.9).

Verification:

$$A_{bolt} = \frac{\pi \times 0.042^2}{4} = 1.385 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\sigma_{actual} = \frac{741{,}200}{1.385 \times 10^{-3}} = 535.2 \text{ MPa} < 626.67 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Answer:** The minimum standard foundation bolt size is **M42 in grade 10.9 steel**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Judgment:** A prudent Naval Architect would also add a **10% margin** for fatigue, since shipboard cranes experience **~10⁵ to 10⁶ load cycles per year**, and foundation bolts are notoriously prone to fatigue failure at the thread root.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Crane Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT SIGNALS AND LOADS]
        L1[Load Mass m_load]
        L2[Rigging Mass m_rig]
        L3[Wind Force W_wind]
        L4[Ship Roll Angle phi]
        L5[Ship Pitch Angle theta]
    end

    subgraph DYNAMICS[CRANE DYNAMICS]
        nodeA[Boom Structure]
        nodeB[Hoist Winch]
        nodeC[Slewing Ring]
        nodeD[Pedestal and Foundation]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT FORCES TO SHIP STRUCTURE]
        O1[Vertical Force W at Hook]
        O2[Overturning Moment M_o at Pedestal]
        O3[Slewing Torque M_s at Turntable]
        O4[Heeling Moment M_heel on Ship Hull]
    end

    subgraph RESPONSE[SHIP RESPONSE]
        R1[Heel Angle Change]
        R2[Deck Plate Stress]
        R3[Foundation Bolt Tension]
        R4[Global Hull Deflection]
    end

    L1 --> nodeA
    L2 --> nodeA
    L3 --> nodeA
    L4 --> nodeA
    L5 --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> O1
    nodeC --> O2
    nodeC --> O3
    nodeD --> O4
    O1 --> R1
    O2 --> R2
    O3 --> R3
    O4 --> R4
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Crane Selection and Design Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Define Cargo Profile] --> B[Determine Required SWL]
    B --> C[Determine Required Radius]
    C --> D[Calculate Maximum Overturning Moment]
    D --> E[Select Crane Type]
    E --> F[Establish Load Chart]
    F --> G[Compute Heeling Moment]
    G --> H[Verify Intact Stability]
    H --> I[Design Foundation]
    I --> J[Class Approval and Certification]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of a Shipboard Pedestal Crane (Side Elevation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SIDEVIEW[SIDE ELEVATION OF SHIPBOARD PEDESTAL CRANE]
        A1[Deck Level y equals 0] 
        A2[Pedestal Height h_p equals 2.5 m]
        A3[Boom Pivot Point]
        A4[Boom Length L equals 18 m]
        A5[Boom Angle alpha equals 35 deg]
        A6[Load Hook Position]
        A7[Operating Radius R equals 14 m]
        A8[Heel Reference Line]
    end
    
    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A3 --- A4
    A4 --- A5
    A5 --- A6
    A6 --- A7
    A7 --- A8
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Crane Type Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Operational Requirement] --> Q1{SWL less than 30 tonnes}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Heavy lift needed}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Offshore operations}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Stiff Boom Derrick]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5[Knuckle Boom Crane]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q6[A Frame or Monorail]
    Q3 -->|No| Q7[Pedestal Crane]
    Q4 --> R[Selected Crane Type]
    Q5 --> R
    Q6 --> R
    Q7 --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above represent *functional and logical relationships* rather than literal physical geometry. The side-elevation view is symbolic — actual shipboard cranes are designed in 3D CAD (e.g., AVEVA Marine, NAPA, or RhinoShip) and verified through FEA packages like ANSYS or Femap.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **Safe Working Load (SWL)** of a shipboard crane. Explain why the SWL of a crane is specified as a function of the working radius rather than a single number.

**Model Answer:**
The Safe Working Load (SWL) of a shipboard crane is the maximum load (including the weight of the lifting gear such as hook block, slings, and spreaders) that the crane is permitted to lift at a given working radius under defined environmental conditions (no wind, level ship, calm sea).

The SWL is radius-dependent because the **overturning moment** $M_o = W \times R$ must remain within the **structural moment capacity** of the boom. As the radius increases, the allowable load must decrease so that the product $W \times R$ stays constant. Hence, the load-rating chart shows a hyperbolic relationship: at small radii, the winch and ropes govern (constant SWL); at large radii, the boom strength governs (SWL ∝ 1/R). **(3 Marks — Complete)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between a **gravity davit** and a **luffing davit** used for lifeboat launching. State one advantage of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Gravity Davit | Luffing Davit |
|:--------|:--------------|:--------------|
| Launch Mechanism | Load weight rotates the davit arm; arc of fall is gravity-driven | Hydraulic or wire-rope actuator rotates the arm |
| Crew Effort | Low (gravity-assisted) | Moderate (actuator-driven) |
| Advantage | Simplicity, fail-safe in power loss | Controlled descent, lower heeling moment during launch |

A gravity davit's primary advantage is **mechanical simplicity and fail-safe operation** under gravity alone. A luffing davit's primary advantage is **precise control of the lifeboat's lowering rate and reduced impact loads on the ship's structure**. **(3 Marks — Complete)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Mandatory Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a pedestal-type shipboard crane**. List the major loads acting on it during operation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **pedestal crane** consists of a vertical cylindrical column (the pedestal) welded to a reinforced deck plate (the crane pad). The pedestal supports a **slewing ring** on which the operator's cabin, the machinery housing, and the lattice boom are mounted. The boom is hinged at its base to the slewing platform and is raised or lowered by a luffing cylinder or wire-rope pendant.

**Major Loads Acting on a Pedestal Crane During Operation:**

1. **Vertical Load (W):** Self-weight of crane + lifted load + rigging, acting downward.
2. **Overturning Moment (M_o):** $W \times R$, trying to tip the crane outboard.
3. **Slewing Torque (M_s):** Resisted by the slewing ring bearings, generated by wind on boom and inertia.
4. **Wind Load (W_wind):** Lateral pressure on the boom and load, given by $0.5 \rho C_d A v^2$.
5. **Dynamic Load Factor (DLF):** Amplification due to ship motions (roll, pitch, heave).

**Sketch (Schematic — refer to Section 4.3 above for block representation).**

**(7 Marks — Full credit for parts (i) sketch with labels, (ii) five named loads, (iii) correct units.)**

---

> **(b)** A shipboard crane of SWL **25 tonnes** at **12 m radius** is to be mounted on the foredeck. Calculate the **overturning moment** at the slewing axis. If the ship heels by **5°** to the side of the lift, compute the **corrected righting lever** at 10° heel, given the static GZ = **0.32 m** and displacement **Δ = 12,000 tonnes**. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Total Vertical Load**

$$W = m_{total} \times g = 25{,}000 \times 9.81 = 245{,}250 \text{ N} = 245.25 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Overturning Moment**

$$M_o = W \times R = 245.25 \times 12 = 2{,}943 \text{ kN·m} \approx 2.943 \text{ MN·m}$$

**Step 3: Heeling Moment During 5° Heel**

For a centreline crane with boom elevation $\alpha$, the transverse arm of the lifted load increases:

$$M_{heel} = W \times R \cos(\alpha) \times \tan(\phi_{ship})$$

Assuming boom nearly horizontal (worst case, $\alpha \approx 0$, $\cos\alpha \approx 1$):

$$M_{heel} = 245.25 \times 12 \times \tan(5°) = 245.25 \times 12 \times 0.0875 = 257.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Righting Lever Correction**

$$GZ_{corrected} = GZ_{static} - \frac{M_{heel}}{\Delta \times g}$$

$$GZ_{corrected} = 0.32 - \frac{257.5 \times 10^3}{12{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81}$$

$$GZ_{corrected} = 0.32 - \frac{257{,}500}{117{,}720{,}000} = 0.32 - 0.00219 = 0.318 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Stability Check**

$$GZ_{corrected} = 0.318 \text{ m} > 0.10 \text{ m (IMO minimum)} \quad \checkmark$$

**Answer:** The overturning moment is **2.943 MN·m**, and the corrected GZ is **0.318 m**. The vessel **satisfies the IMO stability criterion** for the lift at 5° heel. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Explain the **design procedure for foundation bolts of a shipboard crane**. Derive the equation used to compute the **maximum tensile force in the most-loaded bolt**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The foundation of a shipboard crane is a circular array of high-tensile bolts (typically grade **8.8, 10.9, or 12.9**) connecting the crane's pedestal to a reinforced **doubler plate** welded to the ship's deck. The design ensures that the overturning moment and vertical load are transferred safely to the hull.

**Design Procedure:**

1. **Determine the maximum overturning moment** $M_o$ from the worst-case SWL and maximum radius.
2. **Determine the vertical compressive load** $W$ (self-weight + lifted load).
3. **Assume a number of bolts** $n$ (typically 8, 12, 16, 20, 24) and a pitch circle diameter $D$.
4. **Compute the maximum bolt tension** from the bending of the bolt ring:

$$F_{bolt,max} = \frac{4 M_o}{n \times D}$$

*(The derivation of this formula is as follows: The total moment $M_o$ is resisted by a couple formed by the bolt tension $T$ acting at a lever arm $D$. For a pair of diametrically opposite bolts, the resisting moment is $T \times D$. With $n/2$ such pairs, the total is $(n/2) \times T \times D$. Equating: $M_o = (n/2) T D$, hence $T = 2 M_o / (n D)$. However, the maximum bolt experiences **twice** the average because the neutral axis is at the centre: this gives the factor of 4 in the numerator — the standard ring-bolt formula.)*

5. **Select bolt diameter** so that:

$$\sigma_{bolt} = \frac{F_{bolt,max}}{A_{bolt}} \leq \frac{f_{y,bolt}}{SF_{class}}$$

6. **Verify the compressive stress** in the pedestal and doubler plate against buckling.
7. **Check fatigue** using the DNV or LR S-N curves for the appropriate number of cycles.

**(7 Marks — Full credit for (i) procedural steps 1–7, (ii) derivation of bolt force formula with neutral-axis explanation, (iii) mention of fatigue.)**

---

> **(b)** A ship's crane has a **boom of length 20 m**, mass **3 tonnes** with CG at **9 m from the pivot**, and lifts a **load of 30 tonnes** at a radius of **16 m**. The foundation has **12 bolts on a 1200 mm pitch circle diameter**. Determine: (i) the **maximum overturning moment**, (ii) the **maximum bolt tension**, and (iii) the **required bolt diameter** for grade 10.9 steel (f_y = 940 MPa) with SF = 1.5. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Total Loads**

- $m_{load} = 30{,}000$ kg, $W_{load} = 294{,}300$ N
- $m_{boom} = 3{,}000$ kg, $W_{boom} = 29{,}430$ N

**Step 2: Overturning Moment**

$$M_o = (30{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 16) + (3{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 9)$$

$$M_o = 4{,}708{,}800 + 264{,}870 = 4{,}973{,}670 \text{ N·m} \approx 4.974 \text{ MN·m}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Bolt Tension**

$$F_{bolt,max} = \frac{4 \times M_o}{n \times D} = \frac{4 \times 4{,}973{,}670}{12 \times 1.200} = \frac{19{,}894{,}680}{14.4} = 1{,}381{,}575 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Allowable Bolt Stress**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{940}{1.5} = 626.67 \text{ MPa} = 626.67 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Required Bolt Area**

$$A_{req} = \frac{F_{bolt,max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{1{,}381{,}575}{626.67 \times 10^6} = 2.205 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 2205 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6: Required Bolt Diameter**

$$d_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{req}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2205}{\pi}} = \sqrt{2807.4} = 53.0 \text{ mm}$$

Choose the next standard size: **M56** (nominal dia. 56 mm, stress area 2030 mm² — verify with one size up to **M64** for adequate margin: stress area = 2670 mm², which gives actual stress = 1,381,575/2670 = 517 MPa, well within 626.67 MPa).

**Answer:**
(i) Overturning moment = **4.974 MN·m**
(ii) Maximum bolt tension = **1.382 MN**
(iii) Required bolt diameter = **M64 (grade 10.9)** for a 10% engineering margin. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON PITFALLS — Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Lifting Device Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the rigging mass.** Many students calculate the overturning moment using *only* the load mass, omitting the hook block, slings, and spreader beam. Always add $m_{rig}$ — it is typically **5–10% of the SWL**.
> 2. **Confusing radius with boom length.** The **radius** $R$ is the horizontal projection of the boom (boom length × cos α), **not** the boom length itself.
> 3. **Using static GZ without ship motion correction.** In a real KTU numerical, you must subtract the heeling moment using the formula in Section 2.4.2.
> 4. **Incorrect units.** The overturning moment must be in **kN·m** (or MN·m), and bolt stress in **MPa**. Wrong units = 1 mark deducted per occurrence.
> 5. **Omitting the safety factor.** Classification societies **always** apply an SF; do not forget to divide the yield strength by it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — LIFTING DEVICES (MODULE 1)**
>
> - **Lifting Devices** are ship-mounted equipment for cargo transfer, boat handling, and mooring; classified into cranes, davits, derricks, winches, and gantries.
> - **Key Parameters:** SWL (tonnes), Radius R (metres), Slewing Range θ (degrees), Hoisting Height H (metres).
> - **Core Equations to Memorise:**
>   - Overturning Moment: $M_o = W \times R$
>   - Heeling Moment (centreline crane): $M_{heel} = W \times R \sin\alpha$
>   - Foundation Bolt Force: $F_{bolt} = 4M_o / (nD)$
>   - Hoisting Power: $P = W v / \eta$
>   - Dynamic Load Factor: $DLF = 1 + v_{max}/(g t_{brake})$
> - **Load-Rating Diagram:** Hyperbolic relationship; $SWL \times R = \text{constant}$ for $R > R_{crit}$.
> - **Class Rules:** DNV-OS-E101, LR SSC Rules, ABS Crane Rules; ship motion envelope is ±10° roll, ±5° pitch, ±1 m heave.
> - **Stability Criterion:** $GZ_{corrected} \geq 0.10$ m for heeling angles 0° to 30°.
> - **Foundation Bolts:** Grade 8.8 / 10.9 / 12.9 per ISO 898; design for bending + tension + fatigue.
> - **Wind Force Formula:** $W_{wind} = 0.5 \rho_{air} C_d A v^2$ with $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³.
> - **Davits vs. Cranes:** Davits are for boats (low SWL, restricted arc); Cranes are for cargo (high SWL, full 360° slewing).
> - **Four Degrees of Crane Motion:** Hoisting, Luffing, Slewing, Traveling.
> - **Always include a 10% engineering margin** in foundation bolt selection for fatigue.
> - **For 14-mark KTU questions**, structure the answer as: *Given → Diagram → Formula → Substitution → Result → Comment/Check.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargo Hatches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cargo Hatches # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Cargo Hatch**: A rectangular or square opening cut into the weather deck or a cargo hold deck of a merchant ship, fitted with a **removable or sliding cover**, surrounded by a raised vertical wall known as the **hatch coaming**, designed to provide watertight integrity, structural continuity, and safe access for cargo handling operations. A cargo hatch is not merely a hole in the deck; it is an engineered **structural discontinuity** that must simultaneously satisfy three conflicting demands: 1. **Large clear opening** to allow rapid loading/discharging of cargo (containers, bulk, general cargo, etc.). 2. **Watertight integrity** under green sea loading, storm conditions, and accidental flooding. 3. **Structural strength** equivalent to or greater than the parent deck plating it replaces. > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL 1966/1988), Regulation 16**, every cargo hatch on the freeboard and superstructure decks must be fitted with an efficient means of closure and a coaming of minimum prescribed height. Per **SOLAS Chapter II-1**, hatch covers must withstand specified design pressures (e.g., 3.5 kN/m² on weather deck) while maintaining weathertightness. ### 1.2 Associated Terminology (KTU Board Key) - **Hatch Coaming** – The vertical raised lip surrounding the hatch opening. Made of welded steel plates stiffened by brackets and webs. - **Hatch Cover** – The closure mechanism (steel plate assembly) sealing the opening. - **Clear Opening** – The unobstructed horizontal dimensions available for cargo passage (Length × Breadth). - **Hatchway** – The collective term for opening + coaming + cover. - **Gutter Bar / Drain Channel** – Welded at the lower edge of the coaming to direct water to the scuppers. - **Dogging Arrangement** – Mechanical wedge/clamps to compress the cover sealing gasket. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!TIP] > **Think of a cargo hatch like the roof of a delivery truck — except the truck is floating, fighting 15-metre waves, and the "roof" weighs several tonnes.** Imagine a giant steel bathtub (the ship) carrying valuable goods. The deck of this bathtub is pierced by large holes (hatches) so a crane can drop cargo inside. Now, to prevent ocean water from pouring in, you weld a small **raised wall (coaming)** around the hole — like the rim of a glass — and place a **steel lid (hatch cover)** on top. The lid is held down by clamps (dogs) and sealed with rubber gaskets. The bigger the lid, the more it bends under its own weight and the sea's pressure; so it needs internal ribs (stiffeners) for strength — much like a small wooden bridge needs supporting beams. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Minimum coaming height (Weather deck)** = **600 mm** (ICLL Reg. 16). - **Minimum coaming height (Superstructure deck)** = **300 mm**. - **Standard design pressure (weather deck hatch cover)** = **3.5 kN/m²** (IACS UR S21). - **Standard design pressure (deckhouse top)** = **2.0 kN/m²**. - **Safety factor on yield** (hatch cover steel) = **1.67** for steel grades with σ_y = 235 N/mm². - **Standard steel grade for hatch plating** = **AH32 / DH32 / EH32** (high-tensile shipbuilding steel). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Requirements of a Cargo Hatch System A properly designed cargo hatch must satisfy the following KTU/Class enumerated requirements: - **Weathertightness**: Prevent ingress of green seas, spray, and rainwater. - **Strength**: Withstand the design head of water + self-weight + cargo impact. - **Operability**: Permit opening/closing by 2–3 crew within prescribed time (e.g., 5 minutes for most modern designs). - **Stowability**: Folded or stacked covers must stow compactly to free deck space. - **Maintainability**: Replaceable gaskets, accessible hinge points, grease-able bearings. - **Safety**: Anti-slip surface, footman loops, securing dogs. ## 2.2 Classification of Cargo Hatch Covers (KTU Board Favourite) ### A. By Operating Mechanism 1. **Pontoon Hatch Covers** – Single-piece, sliding steel boxes; pushed by hydraulic rams. 2. **Side-Rolling Hatch Covers** – Roll on tracks fitted on the coaming sides. 3. **End-Folding (Folding Patio) Covers** – Multiple panels that fold telescopically. 4. **Stacking Covers** – Individual panels that stack vertically (rare, for special vessels). 5. **Lift-Away / Quick-Acting Covers** – Removed by crane. 6. **Piggy-Back Covers** – Smaller cover sits atop main cover for additional load cases. ### B. By Stiffener Orientation - **Transverse-stiffened covers** (stiffeners run athwartships). - **Longitudinal-stiffened covers** (stiffeners run fore-aft). - **Orthogonally stiffened** (both directions). ## 2.3 Design Loads on Hatch Covers (IACS Unified Requirement S21) The hatch cover plate and stiffeners must be designed for the **greater** of: 1. **Sea pressure head** at the design draft: $$ p_{sea} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{eff} = 10.05 \, h_{eff} \, \text{kN/m}^{2} $$ where $\rho$ = 1.025 t/m³, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², and $h_{eff}$ is the effective head in metres. 2. **Minimum design pressure** mandated by class: $$ p_{design} = \max(p_{sea}, \, 3.5 \, \text{kN/m}^{2}) $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Notes | |---|-----------|-----------------|-------|-------| | 1 | Min. coaming height (weather deck) | $h_{c} \geq 600$ | mm | ICLL Reg. 16 | | 2 | Min. coaming height (superstructure) | $h_{c} \geq 300$ | mm | ICLL Reg. 16 | | 3 | Sea pressure head | $p = 10.05 \cdot h_{eff}$ | kN/m² | $\rho g$ factor | | 4 | Min. design pressure (weather) | $p_{d} = 3.5$ | kN/m² | IACS UR S21 | | 5 | Plate thickness (IACS formula) | $t = s \cdot f \cdot \sqrt{p / \sigma_{allow}}$ | mm | Hatch plating | | 6 | Corrosion addition | $+ 2.0$ to $+ 2.5$ | mm | Add to $t$ | | 7 | Gutter bar size (typical) | $80 \times 10$ | mm | Flat bar welded | | 8 | Compressible gasket thickness | $15 - 25$ | mm | Neoprene/EPDM | | 9 | Dog spacing (typical) | $400 - 600$ | mm c/c | Manual wedges | | 10 | Skid bar height | $25 - 40$ | mm | Protects gasket | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical LaTeX Note**: The table above uses $\vert$ only inside math expressions; pipe characters have been avoided in raw cells to preserve markdown table integrity. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bulk carriers** (Capesize, Panamax) use single-piece pontoon covers; **container ships** favour folding covers because of high opening frequency (every port call). - **Tankers** typically have no cargo hatches (deck piping only). - **Reefer ships** use insulated hatch covers with PU foam core. - **Ro-Ro vessels** may have full-width folding covers that integrate with the ship's side door. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Hatch Cover Plate Thickness (IACS UR S21) The plate of a hatch cover is treated as an **orthotropically supported plate panel** under uniform pressure. For an unstiffened plating field: ### Step 1: Ideal plate thickness under uniform lateral load For a simply supported rectangular plate, the maximum bending moment per unit width is: $$ M = \alpha \cdot p \cdot \ell^{2} $$ where $\alpha$ is a boundary coefficient (typically $\alpha \approx 0.05$ for square panels, reducing for rectangular geometries). ### Step 2: Section modulus of plate per unit width $$ Z = \frac{t^{2}}{6} \quad \text{(per mm width)} $$ ### Step 3: Equating bending stress to allowable The bending stress in the plate: $$ \sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{\alpha \cdot p \cdot \ell^{2}}{t^{2}/6} = \frac{6 \, \alpha \, p \, \ell^{2}}{t^{2}} $$ Setting $\sigma = \sigma_{allow} / \gamma$ (where $\gamma$ is safety factor, typically 1.67 for plate): $$ \frac{6 \, \alpha \, p \, \ell^{2}}{t^{2}} = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{1.67} $$ ### Step 4: Solve for plate thickness $t$ Rearranging the equation for $t$: $$ t^{2} = \frac{6 \, \alpha \, p \, \ell^{2} \cdot 1.67}{\sigma_{allow}} $$ $$ t = \ell \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10.0 \, \alpha \, p}{\sigma_{allow}}} $$ For a typical hatch cover field with $\alpha \approx 0.05$: $$ t = \ell \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \, p}{\sigma_{allow}}} $$ Where: - $t$ = required plate thickness in **mm** - $\ell$ = shorter stiffener spacing in **mm** - $p$ = design pressure in **N/mm²** (divide kN/m² by 1000) - $\sigma_{allow}$ = yield strength in **N/mm²** (235 for AH32) ### Step 5: Add corrosion and fabrication allowance $$ t_{actual} = t_{calculated} + t_{corrosion} + t_{fabrication} $$ Standard allowance: $t_{corrosion} = 2.0$ mm for hatch covers; $t_{fabrication} = 0.5$ mm. ### Worked Numerical Example **Given**: - Hatch cover plate stiffener spacing $\ell = 600$ mm - Design pressure $p = 3.5$ kN/m² $= 0.0035$ N/mm² - Material: AH32 steel, $\sigma_y = 315$ N/mm² - Allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 315 / 1.67 = 188.6$ N/mm² **Solution**: $$ t = 600 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 0.0035}{188.6}} $$ $$ t = 600 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.00175}{188.6}} $$ $$ t = 600 \cdot \sqrt{9.28 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ t = 600 \times 3.046 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ t = 1.83 \, \text{mm} $$ Apply minimum practical thickness (IACS usually enforces $t_{min} = 5$ mm for hatch plating): $$ t_{min,actual} = 1.83 + 2.0 + 0.5 = 4.33 \, \text{mm} \rightarrow \text{adopt } t = 6 \, \text{mm} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Coaming Height from ICLL Regulation 16 Per ICLL Reg. 16, the coaming height above the deck is given by: $$ h_{coaming} \geq \max \left( h_{min,class}, \, h_{camber} \right) $$ ### Step 1: Class minimum - Weather deck: $h_{min,class} = 600$ mm. - Superstructure deck: $h_{min,class} = 300$ mm. ### Step 2: Sheeting (camber) correction For a deck with sheer and camber, the coaming height is measured vertically above the **sheer line**. The camber correction is added to ensure the **freeboard** is preserved: $$ h_{effective} = h_{min,class} + c_{deck} \cdot \frac{B}{2} $$ where $c_{deck}$ = standard camber, typically 1/50 of the breadth $B$ (per ISO/IMO sheer plan). ### Step 3: Bulwark rule (hatches behind bulwarks) If the hatch is fitted within a bulwark of height $h_b$, the coaming may be reduced to: $$ h_{coaming,reduced} = h_{min,class} - 0.5 \, h_b \quad \text{but} \geq 300 \, \text{mm} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Hatch Cover Types | Parameter | Pontoon | Side-Rolling | End-Folding | Lift-Away | |-----------|---------|--------------|-------------|-----------| | Opening time | 3–5 min | 4–6 min | 2–4 min | 30–60 min (crane) | | Weight (per m²) | 110–140 kg | 100–130 kg | 90–120 kg | 80–100 kg | | Stowage area | 0.5–1 × hatch | 0.3–0.5 × hatch | 0.2–0.3 × hatch | 1 × hatch (on quay) | | Weather resistance | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent | | Capital cost | High | High | Medium-High | Low | | Maintenance | Medium | Medium | High | Low | | Typical vessel | Bulk carriers | Mixed cargo | Container ships | General cargo | ## 3.4 Detailed Calculation: Section Modulus of Hatch Coaming Plate The coaming is analysed as a **vertical cantilever plate** fixed at the deck line, with the top edge free. Under hydrostatic pressure from green seas: ### Step 1: Maximum bending moment at the fixed (lower) edge For a cantilever of height $h_c$ under triangular pressure distribution (max at top = $\rho g h$): $$ M_{max} = \frac{p_{top} \cdot h_c^{2}}{6} $$ ### Step 2: Section modulus of coaming plate (per unit width) For a coaming plate of thickness $t_c$ supported by brackets at spacing $b$: $$ Z = \frac{t_c^{2}}{6} \, \text{(per mm width)} $$ ### Step 3: Required thickness $$ t_c = \sqrt{\frac{6 \cdot M_{max}}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}} = h_c \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p_{top}}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}} $$ ### Step 4: Add corrosion allowance $$ t_{c,actual} = t_c + 2.0 \, \text{mm (coaming is in highly corrosive splash zone)} $$ > [!TIP] > In KTU exam questions, you are typically asked to verify the adequacy of a given coaming thickness. Always start by computing the **effective head** at the hatch coaming's mid-height, not the deck edge. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cargo Hatch System ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Ship Weather Deck"] --> nodeB["Hatch Coaming

On Coaming / Above"] classDef structural fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sealing fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef operating fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeE,nodeF,nodeH,nodeL structural class nodeG,nodeI,nodeJ,nodeK sealing class nodeD,nodeM,nodeN operating ``` ## 4.2 Component Cross-Sectional View (Schematic Block) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Hatch Cover Plate
t = 6 to 10 mm"] --> nodeB["Stiffener Web
