# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 4. Worked Example — Designing a Tanker vs. a Container Ship (Comparative)

This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark problem pattern:** *"Given a payload and service speed, determine the principal dimensions and form coefficients for TWO different ship types and explain the design trade-offs."*

### 4.1 Problem Statement

> **Design a vessel to carry 50,000 tonnes of cargo at a service speed of 15 knots. First as a Tanker, then as a Container Ship. Compare the two designs.**

### 4.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### **STEP 1 — Establish the Design Triangle**

For any ship, three parameters are **assumed known**:
- Deadweight $DWT = 50{,}000 \text{ t}$
- Service speed $V_s = 15 \text{ knots} = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$
- Cargo type (tanker vs. container)

We use the **Admiralty Coefficient method** to estimate displacement $\Delta$.

A typical value for merchant ships:
$$C_A \approx 450 \text{ to } 600 \text{ (empirical)}$$

Choose $C_A = 500$ for initial estimate.

#### **STEP 2 — Estimate Displacement**

For the **Tanker** (high $C_B$):

$$\Delta = DWT + LWT$$

Using the empirical LWT ratio for tankers:
$$\frac{LWT}{DWT} \approx 0.30 \text{ to } 0.40$$

Take $LWT = 0.35 \times DWT = 0.35 \times 50{,}000 = 17{,}500 \text{ t}$

$$\Delta_{tanker} = 50{,}000 + 17{,}500 = 67{,}500 \text{ t}$$

For the **Container Ship** (lower $C_B$, more steel for cell guides):

$$\frac{LWT}{DWT} \approx 0.45 \text{ to } 0.55$$

Take $LWT = 0.50 \times 50{,}000 = 25{,}000 \text{ t}$

$$\Delta_{container} = 50{,}000 + 25{,}000 = 75{,}000 \text{ t}$$

#### **STEP 3 — Estimate Required Power (Admiralty Coefficient)**

Rearrange the Admiralty formula:

$$P = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{C_A}$$

Convert $V_s = 15 \text{ knots}$ to SI:
$$V_s = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$$

For the **Tanker**:

$$P_{tanker} = \frac{(67{,}500)^{2/3} \cdot (7.716)^3}{500}$$

Compute term by term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta^{2/3} &= (67{,}500)^{2/3} \\
&= \left(6.75 \times 10^4\right)^{2/3} \\
&= \left(6.75\right)^{2/3} \times \left(10^4\right)^{2/3} \\
&\approx 3.57 \times 10^{2.667} \\
&\approx 357.2 \\
\end{aligned}$$

(Note: $6.75^{2/3} = (6.75^{1/3})^2 \approx 1.889^2 \approx 3.57$)

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s^3 &= (7.716)^3 \\
&\approx 7.716 \times 7.716 \times 7.716 \\
&\approx 59.54 \times 7.716 \\
&\approx 459.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^3 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{tanker} &= \frac{357.2 \times 459.4}{500} \\
&= \frac{164{,}108}{500} \\
&\approx 328.2 \text{ kW (effective)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to Brake Power assuming propulsive efficiency $\eta_D = 0.65$ and shaft efficiency $\eta_S = 0.98$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_B &= \frac{P_E}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S} \\
&= \frac{328.2}{0.65 \times 0.98} \\
&= \frac{328.2}{0.637} \\
&\approx 515 \text{ kW} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**This is unrealistically low — the Admiralty coefficient chosen was too high.** This signals we should refine the design using the **Series 60** or **Holtropt-Mennen** method, but for KTU board purposes the procedure is what matters.

For the **Container Ship** at 15 knots (unusually low for a container — usually 20–25 knots):

$$P_{container} = \frac{(75{,}000)^{2/3} \cdot (7.716)^3}{500}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta^{2/3} &= (75{,}000)^{2/3} \\
&\approx (7.5)^{2/3} \times 10^{2.667} \\
&\approx 3.84 \times 464.16 \\
&\approx 1782.4 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — recalculation. The exponent $10^{2.667} \approx 464$. Let's redo:

$$\begin{aligned}
(7.5 \times 10^4)^{2/3} &= 7.5^{2/3} \times 10^{8/3} \\
&= 7.5^{2/3} \times 10^{2.6667} \\
&\approx 3.84 \times 464 \\
&\approx 1782 \\
\end{aligned}$$

That seems too large. Let me re-verify with logarithms:

$$\log_{10}(75{,}000) = 4.875$$
$$\frac{2}{3} \times 4.875 = 3.250$$
$$10^{3.250} \approx 1778$$

So $\Delta^{2/3} \approx 1778$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{container} &= \frac{1778 \times 459.4}{500} \\
&= \frac{816{,}813}{500} \\
&\approx 1633.6 \text{ kW (effective)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_B &= \frac{1633.6}{0.637} \\
&\approx 2564 \text{ kW} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Key takeaway:** The container ship needs **~5× more power** than the tanker for the same DWT at the same speed because of its **lower $C_B$** (finer, more resistant hull).

#### **STEP 4 — Estimate Principal Dimensions**

Using empirical length formulas:

**For Tanker** ($C_B = 0.82$, $L/B = 5.5$, $B/T = 2.4$):

Use the **displacement formula** with assumed $C_B$:

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$$

With $\nabla = \Delta / \rho = 67{,}500 / 1.025 = 65{,}854 \text{ m}^3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
65{,}854 &= 0.82 \times L_{pp} \times (L_{pp}/5.5) \times (L_{pp}/(5.5 \times 2.4)) \\
&= 0.82 \times \frac{L_{pp}^3}{72.6} \\
&\approx 0.01130 \cdot L_{pp}^3 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Solving:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{pp}^3 &= \frac{65{,}854}{0.01130} \\
&\approx 5{,}828{,}673 \\
L_{pp} &\approx 180.0 \text{ m} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
- $B = L_{pp}/5.5 = 180.0/5.5 \approx 32.7 \text{ m}$
- $T = B/2.4 = 32.7/2.4 \approx 13.6 \text{ m}$

**For Container Ship** ($C_B = 0.60$, $L/B = 7.0$, $B/T = 2.6$):

With $\nabla = 75{,}000 / 1.025 = 73{,}171 \text{ m}^3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
73{,}171 &= 0.60 \times L_{pp} \times (L_{pp}/7.0) \times (L_{pp}/(7.0 \times 2.6)) \\
&= 0.60 \times \frac{L_{pp}^3}{127.4} \\
&\approx 0.004710 \cdot L_{pp}^3 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{pp}^3 &= \frac{73{,}171}{0.004710} \\
&\approx 15{,}535{,}031 \\
L_{pp} &\approx 249.4 \text{ m} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
- $B = L_{pp}/7.0 = 249.4/7.0 \approx 35.6 \text{ m}$
- $T = B/2.6 = 35.6/2.6 \approx 13.7 \text{ m}$

#### **STEP 5 — Verify Speed-Length Ratio**

$$\begin{aligned}
SLR_{tanker} &= \frac{7.716}{\sqrt{180.0}} = \frac{7.716}{13.42} \approx 0.575 \quad \text{(sub-critical, low wave resistance ✓)} \\
SLR_{container} &= \frac{7.716}{\sqrt{249.4}} = \frac{7.716}{15.79} \approx 0.489 \quad \text{(sub-critical ✓)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

#### **STEP 6 — Comparative Analytical Matrix**

| Parameter | Tanker | Container Ship | Ratio (C/T) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deadweight (t) | 50,000 | 50,000 | 1.00 |
| Lightweight (t) | 17,500 | 25,000 | 1.43 |
| Displacement (t) | 67,500 | 75,000 | 1.11 |
| $C_B$ | 0.82 | 0.60 | 0.73 |
| $L_{pp}$ (m) | 180.0 | 249.4 | 1.39 |
| $B$ (m) | 32.7 | 35.6 | 1.09 |
| $T$ (m) | 13.6 | 13.7 | 1.01 |
| $L/B$ | 5.5 | 7.0 | 1.27 |
| Effective Power (kW) | 328 | 1,634 | 4.98 |
| Brake Power (kW) | 515 | 2,564 | 4.98 |
| Speed-Length Ratio | 0.575 | 0.489 | 0.85 |

### 4.3 Engineering Interpretation (For Full KTU Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the container ship is ~40% longer for the same cargo?**
>
> Because a **TEU is a fixed 20-ft × 8-ft × 8-ft box** (≈ 33 m³). To carry the equivalent volumetric cargo (≈ 50,000 t of general goods at 1.5 t/m³ → 33,300 m³ ≈ **1,000 TEU**), the ship needs enormous **deck length and breadth** — hence the high $L/B$ ratio of **7.0**. The tanker, by contrast, uses its **3D hull volume** for cargo, so it can be much shorter and chunkier.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Eye:** In your 14-mark answer, always close with a **paragraph interpretation**, not just numbers. State *"The container ship requires 5× more propulsive power and is 40% longer because its mission profile demands both high service speed AND deck area for cellular cargo handling — a fundamentally different design optimization than the volumetric tanker."*

# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 7. KTU-Style Practice Questions

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Classify merchant ships by service type and explain why the block coefficient of a tanker is significantly higher than that of a container ship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Merchant ships are classified by service type into **tankers** (liquid bulk), **dry bulk carriers** (ore, grain, coal), **container ships** (unitised TEU boxes), **general cargo ships**, **Ro-Ro vessels**, **passenger ships**, and **reefers**.

The **block coefficient** $C_B$ measures hull fullness, where $C_B = \nabla / (L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T)$.

A **tanker** has $C_B \approx 0.80\text{–}0.85$ because its mission is to carry **maximum liquid cargo volume** at **low-to-moderate speed** (~14–16 knots). The mission optimisation maximises internal volume, so the hull is made "tub-shaped" with minimal fine ends.

A **container ship** has $C_B \approx 0.55\text{–}0.66$ because its mission requires **high service speed** (20–25 knots) AND **vast deck/bay area** for stacking TEUs. A fine hull reduces wave-making resistance (which scales with $V^4$), enabling the required speed at acceptable propulsive power. Therefore the container ship's hull is **long, slender, and underbody-fine**, while the tanker's hull is **short, broad, and full-bodied**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Define the Speed-Length Ratio (SLR) and state its significance in selecting the hull form of a ship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Speed-Length Ratio** is defined as:

$$SLR = \frac{V}{\sqrt{L_{pp}}}$$

where $V$ is the service speed in m/s and $L_{pp}$ is the length between perpendiculars in metres.

**Significance:**
1. It determines the **wave-making resistance regime** of the ship — sub-critical ($SLR < 0.80$), trans-critical ($0.80\text{–}1.20$), or super-critical ($SLR > 1.20$).
2. It directly governs the choice of **hull form coefficient** $C_B$ — slower ships can have fuller hulls ($C_B \approx 0.82$ for tankers, $SLR \approx 0.55$); faster ships must have finer hulls ($C_B \approx 0.60$ for container ships, $SLR \approx 0.49$).
3. It is the **key scaling parameter** used in model-to-full-scale extrapolation during towing-tank tests (Froude similarity). **(3 marks)**

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

## ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted Module 1]`

**(a)** Classify the various **types of merchant ships** by their functional service role. For each major category, state the typical range of **(i)** deadweight, **(ii)** service speed, and **(iii)** block coefficient $C_B$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipowner requires a vessel to carry **35,000 tonnes of iron ore** at **14 knots** between two coastal ports. Using the Admiralty coefficient method, **estimate the principal dimensions** ($L_{pp}$, $B$, $T$) and the **effective propulsive power** for this vessel, assuming it is designed as a **Capesize bulk carrier**. State and justify all assumed empirical ratios. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Ship Type | Deadweight Range (t) | Service Speed (knots) | $C_B$ Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crude Oil Tanker (VLCC) | 200,000 – 320,000 | 15 – 16 | 0.80 – 0.85 |
| Product Tanker | 10,000 – 80,000 | 14 – 15 | 0.78 – 0.82 |
| Capesize Bulk Carrier | 150,000 – 400,000 | 13 – 15 | 0.78 – 0.83 |
| Panamax Bulk Carrier | 60,000 – 100,000 | 14 – 15 | 0.75 – 0.80 |
| Handysize Bulk Carrier | 15,000 – 60,000 | 13 – 14 | 0.72 – 0.78 |
| Container Ship (Feeder) | <3,000 TEU | 16 – 18 | 0.55 – 0.65 |
| Container Ship (ULCV) | >14,000 TEU | 20 – 25 | 0.58 – 0.66 |
| General Cargo | 5,000 – 25,000 | 14 – 18 | 0.60 – 0.70 |
| Pure Car Carrier | 4,000 – 8,000 cars | 18 – 22 | 0.55 – 0.65 |
| LNG Carrier | 70,000 – 270,000 m³ | 19 – 21 | 0.65 – 0.75 |
| Cruise Liner | 50,000 – 230,000 GT | 18 – 24 | 0.55 – 0.65 |

*(Marks awarded for: correct classification, plausible DWT/speed/$C_B$ ranges, brief justification linking cargo and form.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $DWT = 35{,}000 \text{ t}$, $V_s = 14 \text{ knots}$

**Assumed empirical ratios for Capesize bulk carrier:**
- $\dfrac{LWT}{DWT} \approx 0.30$ → $LWT = 10{,}500 \text{ t}$
- $\Delta = DWT + LWT = 45{,}500 \text{ t}$
- $C_B = 0.80$, $L/B = 6.0$, $B/T = 2.5$
- Admiralty Coefficient $C_A = 450$

**Step 1 — Volumetric displacement:**
$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{45{,}500}{1.025} \approx 44{,}390 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Convert speed:**
$$V_s = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.202 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Effective power:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta^{2/3} &= (45{,}500)^{2/3} \\
\log_{10}(45{,}500) &= 4.658 \\
\tfrac{2}{3} \times 4.658 &= 3.105 \\
10^{3.105} &\approx 1274 \\
V_s^3 &= (7.202)^3 \approx 373.5 \\
P_E &= \frac{1274 \times 373.5}{450} \approx \frac{475{,}800}{450} \approx 1057 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $L_{pp}$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot \frac{L_{pp}}{6.0} \cdot \frac{L_{pp}}{6.0 \times 2.5} \\
&= \frac{0.80 \cdot L_{pp}^3}{90} \\
&\approx 0.00889 \cdot L_{pp}^3 \\
L_{pp}^3 &= \frac{44{,}390}{0.00889} \approx 4{,}993{,}250 \\
L_{pp} &\approx 171.0 \text{ m} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Breadth and Draft:**
$$B = \frac{L_{pp}}{6.0} = \frac{171.0}{6.0} \approx 28.5 \text{ m}$$
$$T = \frac{B}{2.5} = \frac{28.5}{2.5} \approx 11.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Final Answers:**
- $L_{pp} \approx 171 \text{ m}$
- $B \approx 28.5 \text{ m}$
- $T \approx 11.4 \text{ m}$
- $P_E \approx 1057 \text{ kW}$
- Brake Power $P_B = \dfrac{1057}{0.637} \approx 1659 \text{ kW} \approx 1.66 \text{ MW}$

*(Note: At 35,000 DWT the vessel is technically Handymax rather than Capesize — but the question stipulates Capesize assumptions, so we honour them. In real design, a 35k DWT bulker would be a Handymax/Supramax.)*

---

## ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the aid of a **block-level functional diagram**, explain how the **mission profile** of a ship determines its **hull form**, **resistance regime**, and **propulsion requirement**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **container ship** is to operate at a service speed of **22 knots** carrying **8,000 TEU**. Estimate the **displacement**, **principal dimensions**, and **effective power** using the following assumptions: $C_B = 0.62$, $L/B = 7.2$, $B/T = 2.6$, $\dfrac{LWT}{DWT} = 0.50$, Admiralty Coefficient = 550, and average TEU payload mass = 10 tonnes. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

The mission profile is the **root decision** in ship design. The diagram below (reproduced from Section 5) captures the decision flow:

> **Mission Profile → Cargo Form → Hull Family → $C_B$ → Resistance → Power**

- A **tanker** mission (volume maximisation, slow speed) → high $C_B$ (~0.82) → sub-critical SLR → low wave resistance → modest power.
- A **container ship** mission (speed + deck area) → low $C_B$ (~0.60) → trans-critical SLR → high wave resistance → high power (~50–80 MW for ULCVs).
- A **naval frigate** mission (speed + seakeeping + weapons) → very low $C_B$ (~0.45) → super-critical SLR → very high resistance → gas-turbine power (~30–50 MW).

The **block diagram** in Section 5 of these notes maps this exact hierarchy and is the recommended visual for the answer. *(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 4 for explanation linking each step.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** 8,000 TEU × 10 t/TEU = 80,000 t cargo
Service speed: 22 knots

**Step 1 — DWT:**
Assume cargo DWT = 80,000 t, fuel + stores + crew ≈ 5,000 t:
$$DWT \approx 85{,}000 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2 — Displacement:**
$$LWT = 0.50 \times DWT = 42{,}500 \text{ t}$$
$$\Delta = 85{,}000 + 42{,}500 = 127{,}500 \text{ t}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric displacement:**
$$\nabla = \frac{127{,}500}{1.025} \approx 124{,}390 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Speed conversion:**
$$V_s = 22 \times 0.5144 = 11.317 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_s^3 = (11.317)^3 \approx 1449.5$$

**Step 5 — Effective power:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta^{2/3} &= (127{,}500)^{2/3} \\
\log_{10}(127{,}500) &= 5.106 \\
\tfrac{2}{3} \times 5.106 &= 3.404 \\
10^{3.404} &\approx 2535 \\
P_E &= \frac{2535 \times 1449.5}{550} \approx \frac{3{,}674{,}500}{550} \approx 6681 \text{ kW} \approx 6.68 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Principal dimensions:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= 0.62 \times L_{pp} \times \frac{L_{pp}}{7.2} \times \frac{L_{pp}}{7.2 \times 2.6} \\
&= \frac{0.62 \cdot L_{pp}^3}{134.78} \\
&\approx 0.004600 \cdot L_{pp}^3 \\
L_{pp}^3 &= \frac{124{,}390}{0.004600} \approx 27{,}041{,}304 \\
L_{pp} &\approx 300.6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$B = \frac{300.6}{7.2} \approx 41.8 \text{ m}$$
$$T = \frac{41.8}{2.6} \approx 16.1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — SLR check:**
$$SLR = \frac{11.317}{\sqrt{300.6}} = \frac{11.317}{17.34} \approx 0.653$$

This is in the **sub-critical-to-trans-critical** regime, consistent with a modern container ship.

**Final Answers:**
- $L_{pp} \approx 301 \text{ m}$, $B \approx 41.8 \text{ m}$, $T \approx 16.1 \text{ m}$
- $P_E \approx 6.68 \text{ MW}$
- Brake Power $P_B \approx \dfrac{6.68}{0.637} \approx 10.5 \text{ MW}$

*(7 marks — 2 for displacement/LWT, 2 for dimensions, 2 for power, 1 for SLR justification.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on "Types" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ship-size nomenclature with ship TYPE.** A "VLCC" is a SIZE-class within the tanker TYPE. Mixing up "VLCC," "Suezmax," "Aframax" with ship categories loses 1–2 marks. **Remember:** VLCC/Suezmax/Aframax are TANKER size classes; Capesize/Panamax/Handymax are BULKER size classes.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s** when using the Admiralty Coefficient in SI units. If you plug knots directly into the formula $P = \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3 / C_A$, your answer will be **off by a factor of $\approx 4.7$**. Always write $V (\text{m/s}) = V (\text{kn}) \times 0.5144$.
>
> 3. **Quoting $C_B$ values without context.** A bare number like "$C_B = 0.75$" with no link to ship type earns 0 marks. Always write *"For a Capesize bulk carrier, $C_B = 0.80$ because…"*
>
> 4. **Skipping the Speed-Length Ratio check.** At the end of any sizing problem, ALWAYS compute $V / \sqrt{L_{pp}}$ and confirm it matches the expected regime for the chosen ship type. Examiners specifically look for this sanity check.
>
> 5. **Mixing up $L/B$ and $B/T$.** $L/B$ is **slenderness** (resistance-dominated). $B/T$ is **stability-dominated**. Confusing them costs 1 mark minimum.
>
> 6. **Using LWT and DWT interchangeably.** $LWT$ is the EMPTY ship. $DWT$ is the LOAD (cargo + fuel + stores). $\Delta = LWT + DWT$. This is a classic 1-mark trap.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — "Types of Ships"**

- ✅ Ship **type** is the **first decision** in the design spiral — it cascades into every subsequent calculation.
- ✅ **Primary classification** is by **service / functional role**: Tanker, Bulker, Container, General Cargo, Ro-Ro, Passenger, Reefer.
- ✅ **Block coefficient $C_B$** is the single most important form parameter; tankers ≈ 0.82, bulkers ≈ 0.78, containers ≈ 0.60, frigates ≈ 0.45.
- ✅ **Speed-Length Ratio** $SLR = V / \sqrt{L_{pp}}$ determines the **wave-resistance regime**.
- ✅ **Form follows function**: cargo form (liquid / dry bulk / unitised / wheeled / passenger) directly dictates hull form.
- ✅ **Admiralty Coefficient** $C_A = \Delta^{2/3} V_s^3 / P$ is the canonical power-sizing tool — **always use SI units**.
- ✅ **MARPOL double-hull** requirement for tankers (post-2010) is a regulatory consequence of ship type.
- ✅ **Size-class nomenclature** (VLCC, Capesize, ULCV) is **distinct from type nomenclature** (tanker, bulker, container).
- ✅ **Modern emerging types** include wind-assisted ships, dual-fuel LNG carriers, autonomous container vessels — be ready to discuss them in 2024-scheme exam.
- ✅ **Always end** sizing problems with: (a) a numerical summary table, (b) an SLR check, (c) a 1-paragraph engineering interpretation.

> **Best of luck with your KTU Board Examination! 🚢📚**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Ship Design II** under the KTU 2024 scheme, **"Design Aspects"** refers to the comprehensive set of *interlocking engineering considerations, physical constraints, geometric parameters, hydrodynamic coefficients, and economic trade-offs* that govern the translation of an owner's operational requirement (mission profile, route, cargo, speed) into a feasible, safe, and commercially viable ship form. It encompasses the **principal dimension selection**, **form coefficient estimation**, **capacity-speed-power integration**, **regulatory compliance** (Classification societies, IMO, SOLAS, MARPOL), and **iterative design-loop logic** (design spiral).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus (Module 1):** This module anchors the *front-end engineering* of any ship project — i.e., everything that happens **before** structural scantlings are drawn. The student is expected to internalize that **ship design is a multi-variable constrained optimization problem**, not a sequential calculation exercise.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are an architect tasked with designing a *delivery truck* for a logistics company. The boss says: *"Carry 20 tonnes of cargo, drive at 80 km/h, fuel-efficient, low cost."* You cannot just pick any size — you are constrained by:
- **Bridge heights** (limits vertical dimension),
- **Road widths and turning radii** (limits horizontal dimensions),
- **Engine bay size** (limits mechanical fit),
- **Weight laws on bridges** (limits total mass),
- **Customer budget** (limits material choice).

You iterate — guess size → check if it carries the load → check if it goes the speed → adjust. **Ship design is identical**, except:
- The "roads" are oceans with waves (introducing Froude scaling),
- The "bridges" are canals (Panama, Suez — limiting beam and draft),
- The "weight laws" are strength and stability criteria (GM, freeboard, intact & damage stability),
- The "budget" is the **Newbuilding Price (NBP)** vs **Cargo Ton-Mile Cost (CTMC)**.

So *Design Aspects* is essentially the **rulebook and the scoring system** used by the naval architect to play this multi-objective optimization game.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No single "best ship" exists. Every design is a *compromise* — faster ships are longer (more steel → costlier), wider ships carry more cargo but need more power, deeper drafts carry more cargo but are restricted in shallow harbours.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Standard Definition | Real-World Meaning |
|------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Displacement (Δ)** | Total mass of water displaced by the ship (tonnes) | "How heavy the ship is, fully loaded" |
| **Deadweight (DWT)** | Total variable load the ship can carry (tonnes) | "How much cargo + fuel + crew + stores" |
| **Lightweight (LW)** | Weight of the empty ship (steel, machinery, outfit) | "The bare ship's weight" |
| **Block Coefficient (C_B)** | Ratio of displaced volume to the enclosing block L×B×T | "How 'full' the hull is" — fuller = more cargo, slower |
| **Service Speed (V_S)** | Contract speed at design draft in calm sea (knots) | "The speed the owner pays for" |
| **EHP / SHP** | Effective / Shaft Horse Power (kW or HP) | "How much push the engine delivers" |
| **Froude Number (F_n)** | V / √(gL) — ratio of inertial to gravitational forces | "How wave-friendly the hull is" |
| **Newbuilding Price (NBP)** | Total cost to construct the vessel (USD millions) | "Price tag of the ship" |

---

## 1.4 Why "Design Aspects" is the Foundation Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical note:** Module 1 of PBNST604 is *deliberately* placed first because every downstream subject — Resistance & Propulsion (Module 2), Structural Design (Module 3), Stability (Module 4), and Economics (Module 5) — depends on the **principal dimensions and form coefficients** chosen here. A wrong C_B choice cascades into wrong powering, wrong steel weight, wrong stability — and an unbuildable design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ship Design Process — An Iterative Reality

The classic **Design Spiral** (Evans, 1959; Watson, 1998) visualizes design not as a linear sequence but as an **iterative converging spiral**:

**Mission Requirement → Weight Estimate → Principal Dimensions → Hull Form → Stability → Powering → Economics → (back to Weight, with corrections).**

Each revolution of the spiral brings the design closer to convergence. Modern KTU pedagogy uses the term **"Design Spiral Revisited"** to include regulatory loops (Class approval, IMO compliance) and production loops (weldability, build strategy).

---

## 2.2 Hierarchical Design Constraints

Design constraints fall into **three tiers**:

1. **Hard Constraints (Non-negotiable):** Length overall (port/canal limits), beam (Panamax ≤ 32.31 m, Suezmax ≤ 50.0 m, Malaccamax ≤ 32.31 m), draft (harbour depth), regulatory tonnage (IMO, SOLAS damage stability index *R* ≥ 0.7 for passenger ships).
2. **Soft Constraints (Optimizable):** Service speed, deadweight, range, fuel consumption, manning.
3. **Economic Constraints (Objective-driven):** Minimum freight rate, maximum cargo intake, return on investment, payback period.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Exam questions frequently ask to "List design constraints" — always segregate into *Owner-driven*, *Statutory*, and *Site-driven* constraints.

---

## 2.3 Principal Dimensions and Their First-Order Relationships

Let:
- **L** = Length between perpendiculars (m)
- **B** = Moulded breadth (m)
- **T** = Moulded draft (m)
- **∇** = Displaced volume (m³)
- **Δ** = Displacement mass = ρ × ∇ (tonnes, with ρ ≈ 1.025 t/m³ for seawater)

The **enclosing block volume** is L × B × T, so:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \times B \times T}$$

This **single coefficient** is the single most important form descriptor in preliminary design — it tells you whether the hull is "block-like" (full-form, like a tanker, C_B ≈ 0.80–0.85) or "needle-like" (fine-form, like a frigate, C_B ≈ 0.40–0.55).

---

## 2.4 The Family of Form Coefficients

| Coefficient | Definition | Physical Meaning | Typical Range (Modern Ships) |
|-------------|-----------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **C_B** (Block) | ∇ / (LBT) | Overall fullness of hull | 0.50 (warship) → 0.85 (tanker) |
| **C_P** (Prismatic) | ∇ / (A_M × L) | Distribution of volume along length | 0.55 → 0.85 |
| **C_M** (Midship) | A_M / (B × T) | Fullness of the midship section | 0.90 → 0.99 |
| **C_W** (Waterplane) | A_W / (L × B) | Fullness of waterplane area | 0.65 → 0.90 |
| **C_V** (Vertical prismatic) | ∇ / (A_W × T) | Vertical distribution of volume | 0.55 → 0.85 |

**Crucial linkage** that examiners love:

$$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$

And:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Confusing *prismatic coefficient* (longitudinal) with *vertical prismatic coefficient*. Remember: C_P relates volume to *midship area × length*, while C_V relates it to *waterplane area × draft*.

---

## 2.5 Capacity–Speed–Power Relationship

This is **the** central equation of ship design economics. Given a required deadweight (DWT) and service speed (V_S), the required propulsive power scales with:

- **Power** ∝ (Displacement)^(2/3) × (Speed)³ — derived from Froude scaling and Admiralty tradition.

The **Admiralty Coefficient** (C_A) is a powerful heuristic that bundles everything:

$$C_A = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \times V_S^{3}}{P_S}$$

where P_S is the installed Shaft Power (kW or HP depending on convention). For a given ship type and era, C_A is approximately constant (e.g., a modern container ship has C_A ≈ 500–700 in metric units). This lets the designer estimate power *before* any resistance calculation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** If the displacement increases by 8%, the required power (at constant speed) increases by approximately 5% (because of the 2/3 exponent). If the speed increases by 10%, power increases by 33%. **Speed is expensive; deadweight is cheap.**

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Module 1 (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Δ = ρ × ∇ | Displacement from displaced volume | tonnes |
| 2 | ∇ = L × B × T × C_B | Volume from principal dimensions | m³ |
| 3 | Δ = 1.025 × L × B × T × C_B | Mass displacement in seawater | tonnes |
| 4 | DWT = Δ − LW | Deadweight from displacement & lightweight | tonnes |
| 5 | DWT/Δ | Deadweight ratio (efficiency of carrying) | dimensionless |
| 6 | C_B = ∇ / (LBT) | Block coefficient | dimensionless |
| 7 | C_P = C_B / C_M | Prismatic coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | C_W = A_W / (LB) | Waterplane coefficient | dimensionless |
| 9 | F_n = V / √(gL) | Froude number | dimensionless |
| 10 | C_A = Δ^(2/3) × V_S³ / P_S | Admiralty coefficient | varies |
| 11 | EHP = R_T × V_S / 1000 | Effective horsepower (kW) | kW |
| 12 | η = EHP / SHP | Propulsive efficiency | dimensionless |
| 13 | L/B, L/T, B/T | Length-to-beam, length-to-draft, beam-to-draft ratios | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote ρ = **1.025 t/m³** for *seawater* and **1.000 t/m³** for *fresh water*. A ship moving from salt to fresh water will *sink deeper* by approximately 2.5% of draft — a favourite short-answer question.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These relations are not academic abstractions — they drive every commercial ship design:
- A **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** uses C_B ≈ 0.83 and DWT/Δ ≈ 0.86 (very efficient).
- A **container ship** sacrifices C_B (~0.65) for speed and uses C_A to right-size the engine.
- A **naval frigate** uses C_B ≈ 0.50 and prioritizes endurance (range × payload) over cargo.

The capacity-speed-power trio is also the foundation of **ship optimization software** (e.g., Paramarine, MAXSURF, NAPA) used by shipyards globally.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Displacement from Principal Dimensions

**Objective:** Express ship mass (Δ) directly in terms of L, B, T and the block coefficient.

**Step 1 — Start with the Archimedes principle:**

$$\Delta = \rho \times \nabla$$

where ρ = density of water (t/m³), ∇ = displaced volume (m³).

**Step 2 — Replace ∇ with the enclosing block × fullness factor:**

The displaced volume equals the volume of the *enclosing rectangular box* (L × B × T) multiplied by the *block coefficient* (C_B), which captures how much of that box is actually occupied by the hull:

$$\nabla = L \times B \times T \times C_B$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into Archimedes relation:**

$$\Delta = \rho \times L \times B \times T \times C_B$$

**Step 4 — Specialize for seawater (ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³):**

$$\boxed{\Delta_{sw} = 1.025 \times L \times B \times T \times C_B \quad \text{[tonnes]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **first equation a naval architect writes** in preliminary design. It links four geometric quantities (L, B, T, C_B) to a single physical quantity (mass). Changing any one of them propagates through resistance, stability, strength, and cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deadweight to Displacement Ratio

**Objective:** Express the *carrying efficiency* of a ship.

**Step 1 — Define Deadweight:**

DWT is the variable mass the ship carries: cargo + fuel (Bunker) + fresh water + lubricating oil + crew + stores + provisions.

$$DWT = W_{cargo} + W_{fuel} + W_{stores} + W_{crew} + W_{misc}$$

**Step 2 — Define Displacement (loaded condition):**

$$\Delta = DWT + LW$$

where LW = lightweight = steel weight + machinery weight + outfit weight.

**Step 3 — Solve for the ratio:**

$$\frac{DWT}{\Delta} = 1 - \frac{LW}{\Delta}$$

**Step 4 — Practical interpretation:**

A higher DWT/Δ means the ship is more "efficient" at carrying variable load. For a tanker, this ratio reaches ~0.86; for a container ship, ~0.60; for a warship, ~0.25 (because the lightweight of weapons systems dominates).

**Example numerical evaluation:**
If Δ = 100,000 t and LW = 14,000 t, then:

$$\frac{DWT}{\Delta} = 1 - \frac{14000}{100000} = 1 - 0.14 = 0.86$$

So DWT = 86,000 t.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU high-yield fact:** The DWT/Δ ratio is *itself* a function of ship size — larger ships generally achieve higher ratios because of square-cube scaling (volume grows as L³ while surface grows as L², reducing relative lightweight per tonne).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Admiralty Coefficient (Capacity-Speed-Power Linkage)

**Objective:** Derive the empirical formula relating displacement, speed, and power.

**Step 1 — Start from dimensional reasoning (Froude's scaling):**

From ship model testing and Froude's hypothesis, the wave-making resistance scales approximately as:

$$R_W \propto \Delta^{2/3} \times V^{2}$$

(For low Froude numbers, calm water, similar form.)

**Step 2 — Effective Horse Power equals resistance times speed:**

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \times V_S}{1000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

where R_T is the *total resistance* (kN) and V_S is the speed in m/s.

**Step 3 — Combine:** Substituting resistance:

$$EHP \propto \Delta^{2/3} \times V_S^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Account for propulsive efficiency:**

The Shaft Horse Power (SHP) must overcome EHP plus mechanical losses:

$$SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D \times \eta_T} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \eta_O = \frac{EHP}{SHP}$$

where η_O is the *overall propulsive efficiency*.

**Step 5 — Rearrange to define the Admiralty Coefficient:**

$$C_A = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \times V_S^{3}}{SHP}$$

This is a *constant* for a given ship type and technological era. By rearranging:

$$SHP = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \times V_S^{3}}{C_A}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example:**

Consider a bulk carrier with Δ = 50,000 t, V_S = 14 knots, C_A = 600 (typical metric).

$$\Delta^{2/3} = 50000^{0.6667} \approx 13572$$

$$V_S^{3} = 14^{3} = 2744$$

$$SHP = \frac{13572 \times 2744}{600} \approx 62{,}069 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** The Admiralty coefficient is **NOT a fundamental law** — it is empirical. If you are asked to "derive" it, you must explicitly state that the exponent 2/3 on displacement comes from *Froude's law of comparison* (R ∝ W^(2/3) for similar forms), while the exponent 3 on speed comes from EHP = R × V.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Speed-Length Ratio vs. Froude Number

**Objective:** Show why Froude number is the fundamental similarity parameter for ships.

**Step 1 — Dimensional homogeneity requirement:**

A ship model's resistance behaviour must be transferable to the full-scale ship. The governing physical phenomena are gravity (g) and inertia (ρ, V). Forming a dimensionless group:

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \times L}}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalently in the older naval-architecture notation:**

The **Speed-Length Ratio (SLR)** is:

$$SLR = \frac{V_S}{\sqrt{L}} \quad [\text{knots}/\sqrt{\text{ft}}]$$

**Step 3 — Conversion identity:**

$$F_n = \frac{V_{m/s}}{\sqrt{g \times L_{m}}} \approx 0.298 \times \frac{V_{kts}}{\sqrt{L_{ft}}}$$

**Step 4 — Critical Froude numbers:**

- F_n ≈ 0.20 → ship in *sub-critical* regime (resistance grows gently).
- F_n ≈ 0.40 → hull speed for displacement craft (transverse wave interference peaks).
- F_n > 0.45 → *planing / semi-displacement* regime (special hull forms).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always quote F_n as a decimal, never in knots/√ft. Modern ITTC (International Towing Tank Conference) standards use SI Froude number.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Capacity-Speed Check

**Problem (typical KTU Part B):**
A Panamax bulk carrier has the following preliminary dimensions:
- L_pp = 200 m, B = 32.2 m, T = 12.5 m, C_B = 0.78, LW = 9,000 t, V_S = 14.5 knots.
- Estimate: (a) Full-load displacement, (b) Deadweight, (c) Required SHP using C_A = 650.

**Solution:**

(a) Displacement in seawater:

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 200 \times 32.2 \times 12.5 \times 0.78$$

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 200 \times 32.2 \times 12.5 \times 0.78$$

Computing stepwise:
- 200 × 32.2 = 6,440
- 6,440 × 12.5 = 80,500
- 80,500 × 0.78 = 62,790
- 62,790 × 1.025 = 64,360 t

So **Δ ≈ 64,360 t**.

(b) Deadweight:

$$DWT = \Delta - LW = 64360 - 9000 = 55{,}360 \text{ t}$$

(c) Required SHP:

$$\Delta^{2/3} = 64360^{2/3}$$

Using logarithms: ln(64360) ≈ 11.072; × 2/3 ≈ 7.381; exp(7.381) ≈ 16,007.

$$V_S^{3} = 14.5^{3} = 3048.6$$

$$SHP = \frac{16007 \times 3048.6}{650} \approx \frac{48{,}815{,}000}{650} \approx 75{,}100 \text{ kW}$$

So **SHP ≈ 75,100 kW ≈ 100,700 HP**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Ship Design Spiral (Modernized)

This block-level architecture flow shows the *iterative convergence* of the ship design process as taught in KTU Module 1. Each pass tightens the design until all constraints are simultaneously satisfied.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["MISSION REQUIREMENT"] --> nodeB["OWNER BRIEF CARGO TYPE SPEED RANGE"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["WEIGHT ESTIMATE DEADWEIGHT LIGHTWEIGHT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PRINCIPAL DIMENSIONS L B T"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["HULL FORM CHOICE C_B C_P C_M"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["STABILITY CHECK GM FREEBOARD"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["RESISTANCE AND POWERING EHP SHP"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["ECONOMIC EVALUATION CTMC NBP ROI"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"ALL CONSTRAINTS SATISFIED"}
    nodeI -->|NO REFINEMENT NEEDED| nodeJ["CONVERGENCE FREEZE DESIGN"]
    nodeI -->|YES ADJUST WEIGHTS OR FORM| nodeC
    nodeF -->|REGULATORY LOOP| nodeK["CLASS APPROVAL SOLAS MARPOL"]
    nodeK --> nodeF
    nodeG -->|PRODUCTION LOOP| nodeL["BUILDABILITY CHECK WELDING STEEL GRADE"]
    nodeL --> nodeG
```

**Reading guide for KTU students:**
- The central horizontal spine (A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H → I) is the **primary design spiral**.
- The two **feedback loops** (F ↔ K and G ↔ L) are the modern additions: regulatory and buildability checks that did not exist in the original 1959 spiral.
- The decision diamond (I) is the **convergence gate** — in real shipyards this iteration may repeat 30–50 times before the design is "frozen" for contract.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Hierarchy of Design Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot["DESIGN CONSTRAINTS"] --> nodeH["HARD CONSTRAINTS NON NEGOTIABLE"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeS["SOFT CONSTRAINTS OPTIMIZABLE"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeE["ECONOMIC CONSTRAINTS OBJECTIVE DRIVEN"]
    nodeH --> nodeH1["CANAL LIMITS PANAMAX SUEZMAX"]
    nodeH --> nodeH2["HARBOUR DRAFT RESTRICTIONS"]
    nodeH --> nodeH3["IMO REGULATIONS SOLAS MARPOL"]
    nodeH --> nodeH4["CLASS RULES BV DNV LR"]
    nodeS --> nodeS1["SERVICE SPEED V_S"]
    nodeS --> nodeS2["DEADWEIGHT DWT"]
    nodeS --> nodeS3["RANGE AND ENDURANCE"]
    nodeS --> nodeS4["MANNING LEVELS"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["MINIMUM FREIGHT RATE"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["NEWBUILDING PRICE NBP"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["RETURN ON INVESTMENT ROI"]
    nodeE --> nodeE4["PAYBACK PERIOD"]
```

**Reading guide:**
- **Hard constraints** are set by external infrastructure (canals, harbours) and statutory bodies (IMO, Class). The designer has *zero degrees of freedom* here.
- **Soft constraints** are negotiated with the owner; the designer's job is to *optimize* these within the hard constraints.
- **Economic constraints** form the **objective function** — the designer's "scoreboard".

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Form Coefficient Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeV["DISPLACED VOLUME NABLA"] --> nodeCB["C_B BLOCK NABLA LBT"]
    nodeV --> nodeCP["C_P PRISMATIC NABLA A_M L"]
    nodeV --> nodeCV["C_V VERTICAL NABLA A_W T"]
    nodeAM["MIDSECTION A_M"] --> nodeCM["C_M MIDSHIP A_M BT"]
    nodeAW["WATERPLANE A_W"] --> nodeCW["C_W WATERPLANE A_W LB"]
    nodeCB --> nodeR1["C_B C_P TIMES C_M"]
    nodeCP --> nodeR1
    nodeCM --> nodeR1
```

**Reading guide:** The *relationships* are:
- C_B = C_P × C_M
- C_V relates to the *vertical* form and is rarely used in KTU exams.
- All coefficients are dimensionless numbers between 0 and 1.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Capacity-Speed-Power Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["START OWNER GIVES DEADWEIGHT AND SPEED"] --> node2["ASSUME INITIAL C_B FROM SHIP TYPE DATABASE"]
    node2 --> node3["ASSUME INITIAL LW BY EMPIRICAL FORMULA"]
    node3 --> node4["COMPUTE DELTA DWT PLUS LW"]
    node4 --> node5["COMPUTE L B T RATIOS"]
    node5 --> node6["CHECK HARD CONSTRAINTS CANAL HARBOUR"]
    node6 -->|VIOLATED| node7["REVISE L B OR T"]
    node7 --> node5
    node6 -->|PASSED| node8["COMPUTE SHP FROM ADMIRALTY C_A"]
    node8 --> node9["CHECK SOFT CONSTRAINTS SPEED FUEL"]
    node9 -->|VIOLATED| node10["ADJUST C_B OR V_S"]
    node10 --> node2
    node9 -->|PASSED| node11["FREEZE PRELIMINARY DESIGN"]
```

**Reading guide:** This is the *algorithmic flowchart* a naval architect follows in Excel-based preliminary design tools. KTU Part B questions on "design procedure" expect exactly this kind of structured flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define Block Coefficient and Prismatic Coefficient. State one design implication of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Block Coefficient (C_B)** is the ratio of the displaced volume of the ship (∇) to the volume of its enclosing rectangular block of length L, breadth B, and draft T:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \times B \times T}$$

It is a measure of the **overall fullness** of the hull. A high C_B (≈ 0.80) indicates a full-form ship such as a tanker — efficient for cargo but slow. A low C_B (≈ 0.50) indicates a fine-form ship such as a frigate — fast but carries less.

The **Prismatic Coefficient (C_P)** is the ratio of displaced volume to the product of midship area and length:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \times L}$$

It measures the **longitudinal distribution** of volume. A high C_P (≈ 0.85) means the volume is concentrated around midships (typical of full ships); a low C_P (≈ 0.60) means volume is distributed toward the ends (typical of fine ships with good wave entry).

**Design implication:** C_B directly influences resistance and cargo capacity; C_P directly influences the wave-making resistance and the position of the longitudinal centre of buoyancy (LCB). [3 marks]

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the Admiralty Coefficient? Why is it useful in preliminary ship design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Admiralty Coefficient (C_A)** is an empirical coefficient that combines displacement, speed, and installed shaft power into a single dimensionless indicator of propulsive efficiency:

$$C_A = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \times V_S^{3}}{P_S}$$

where Δ is displacement (tonnes), V_S is service speed (knots), and P_S is shaft power (kW).

**Usefulness in preliminary design:**
1. It allows the designer to **estimate the required shaft power** before performing detailed resistance calculations — only three inputs are needed.
2. It serves as a **benchmark** — if the calculated C_A deviates significantly from typical values for that ship type and era, the design is likely flawed.
3. It enables **rapid trade-off studies** — comparing alternative designs at the sketch stage.

[3 marks]

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions of PBNST604 carry 14 marks with internal choice. The pattern below is **two sub-parts (a) and (b), each 7 marks**, mirroring actual KTU End Semester Examination papers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: (a) Discuss, with suitable sketches, the concept of the Design Spiral as applied to ship design. Explain how modern regulatory and buildability loops have been added to the classical spiral. [7 Marks]**

**(b) A bulk carrier has the following preliminary dimensions: L_pp = 180 m, B = 30 m, T = 11 m, C_B = 0.78, LW = 7,500 t, V_S = 14 knots. Determine:**
**(i) Full-load displacement, (ii) Deadweight, (iii) DWT/Δ ratio, (iv) Required shaft power using Admiralty coefficient C_A = 650. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Design Spiral [7 Marks]**

The Design Spiral, originally proposed by **D. H. Evans (1959)** and later refined by **D. G. M. Watson (1998)**, is a conceptual model that represents the iterative nature of ship design. Unlike a linear sequence, the design process is a **cyclical convergence** where each iteration refines the previous estimate.

**Stages of the classical spiral:**
1. **Mission Requirement:** Owner specifies cargo type, route, speed, and deadweight.
2. **Weight Estimate:** Lightweight and deadweight are estimated based on similar ships.
3. **Principal Dimensions:** Length, beam, and draft are chosen using empirical databases (e.g., C_B from ship-type charts).
4. **Hull Form:** Form coefficients (C_B, C_P, C_M) and lines plan are developed.
5. **Stability:** GM, freeboard, and damage stability (for passenger ships) are checked.
6. **Resistance & Powering:** EHP and SHP are estimated; engine selected.
7. **Economics:** Cargo ton-mile cost and newbuilding price are evaluated.
8. **Convergence:** If all constraints are satisfied, design is frozen; otherwise, return to stage 2 or 3.

**Modern additions:**
- **Regulatory loop:** After stability checks, the design is verified against **Class rules (DNV, BV, LR)** and **IMO regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL)**. Non-compliance forces a return to stage 5.
- **Buildability loop:** After powering, the design is checked for **weldability, steel grade availability, and block construction strategy**. Issues force a return to stage 6.

In modern shipyards, the design spiral may rotate **30–50 times** before convergence. [7 marks]

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:** L_pp = 180 m, B = 30 m, T = 11 m, C_B = 0.78, LW = 7,500 t, V_S = 14 knots, C_A = 650.

**(i) Full-load displacement:**

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \times L \times B \times T \times C_B$$

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 180 \times 30 \times 11 \times 0.78$$

Stepwise computation:
- 180 × 30 = 5,400
- 5,400 × 11 = 59,400
- 59,400 × 0.78 = 46,332
- 46,332 × 1.025 = **47,490 tonnes**

**(ii) Deadweight:**

$$DWT = \Delta - LW = 47490 - 7500 = \mathbf{39{,}990 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**(iii) DWT/Δ ratio:**

$$\frac{DWT}{\Delta} = \frac{39990}{47490} = 0.8422 \approx \mathbf{0.84}$$

**(iv) Required shaft power:**

$$SHP = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \times V_S^{3}}{C_A}$$

Computing Δ^(2/3):
- ln(47490) ≈ 10.770
- × 2/3 ≈ 7.180
- exp(7.180) ≈ 12,978

Computing V_S³:
- 14³ = 2,744

$$SHP = \frac{12978 \times 2744}{650} = \frac{35{,}623{,}632}{650} \approx \mathbf{54{,}805 \text{ kW}}$$

**Answer Summary:**
- (i) Δ ≈ 47,490 t
- (ii) DWT ≈ 39,990 t
- (iii) DWT/Δ ≈ 0.84
- (iv) SHP ≈ 54,805 kW

[7 marks total]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) List and explain the various design constraints that a naval architect must consider during the preliminary design of a merchant ship. [7 Marks]**

**(b) Explain the geometric meaning of the following form coefficients, with neat sketches: (i) Block coefficient, (ii) Prismatic coefficient, (iii) Midship coefficient, (iv) Waterplane coefficient. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Design Constraints [7 Marks]**

A naval architect must satisfy **three categories** of design constraints simultaneously:

**1. Hard Constraints (Statutory & Site-Driven) — Non-negotiable:**
- **Canal limitations:** Panamax beam ≤ 32.31 m, Suezmax beam ≤ 50.0 m, length limits.
- **Harbour draft:** Maximum permissible draft at loading/discharge ports.
- **Class rules:** Structural strength, stability, fire protection (e.g., BV, DNV, LR, ABS).
- **IMO regulations:** SOLAS (life-saving, fire safety), MARPOL (pollution prevention), Load Lines (freeboard), Tonnage Measurement.
- **Stability criteria:** Intact GM, damage stability (R-index for passenger ships).

**2. Soft Constraints (Owner-Driven, Negotiable):**
- **Service speed:** Often a trade-off with fuel consumption.
- **Deadweight:** Subject to canal/harbour constraints.
- **Range:** Determined by fuel capacity and bunker consumption.
- **Manning:** Ergonomic and operational considerations.
- **Cargo gear:** Crane capacity for general cargo ships.

**3. Economic Constraints (Objective Function):**
- **Minimum freight rate (MFR):** The lowest rate at which the ship breaks even.
- **Newbuilding price (NBP):** Total construction cost.
- **Return on investment (ROI):** Typically 10–15% over 20 years.
- **Cargo ton-mile cost (CTMC):** Total operating cost per tonne-mile.

The designer's role is to **optimize the soft and economic constraints within the hard constraints**. [7 marks]

**(b) Form Coefficients — Geometric Meaning [7 Marks]**

| Coefficient | Geometric Interpretation | Sketch Description |
|-------------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| **(i) Block Coefficient (C_B)** | Ratio of displaced volume ∇ to enclosing rectangular box L × B × T. Visualizes the hull as filled into a rectangular box. | A rectangular box L×B×T with the hull inscribed; the ratio of hull volume to box volume. |
| **(ii) Prismatic Coefficient (C_P)** | Ratio of displaced volume ∇ to midship area × length. Visualizes the hull as a prism stretched from the midship section. | A horizontal prism of constant cross-section A_M, length L; the ratio of actual hull volume to this prism. |
| **(iii) Midship Coefficient (C_M)** | Ratio of actual midship area A_M to its enclosing rectangle B × T. Visualizes the fullness of the cross-section at amidships. | The midship section B×T rectangle with the actual girth shape inside; the ratio of their areas. |
| **(iv) Waterplane Coefficient (C_W)** | Ratio of actual waterplane area A_W to enclosing rectangle L × B. Visualizes the fullness of the waterplane. | The waterplane rectangle L×B with the actual waterline outline inside; the ratio of their areas. |

**Critical relationship:** C_B = C_P × C_M — the block coefficient is the product of longitudinal (C_P) and transverse (C_M) fullness.

**Typical values:**
- C_B: 0.50 (warship) to 0.85 (tanker)
- C_P: 0.55 (fine) to 0.85 (full)
- C_M: 0.90 (sharp) to 0.99 (full)
- C_W: 0.65 (fine) to 0.90 (full)

[7 marks total]

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on Module 1 — Design Aspects:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³** — using 1.000 gives the *fresh water* displacement, not seawater. Examiner deducts **½ to 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing C_P with C_V** — Vertical prismatic coefficient is rarely asked; if the question says "prismatic", it almost always means *longitudinal* C_P = ∇/(A_M × L).
> 3. **Mixing up units in the Admiralty Coefficient** — If V_S is in knots and Δ in tonnes, the constant C_A is in metric units. Mixing imperial and metric causes **unit errors that propagate through the entire calculation** — typical 2–3 mark deduction.
> 4. **Drawing the design spiral as a straight line** — It is a *spiral*, not a sequence. The iteration must be emphasized; a linear flow gets only partial credit.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions for C_B** — C_B is not a constant; it depends on ship type, speed, and form. Always say "Assume C_B = 0.78 for a Panamax bulk carrier at 14 knots" before using it.
> 6. **Skipping the convergence loop in the sketch** — The spiral must show a *return arrow* from economics/regulatory stages back to weight/dimensions. Linear-only sketches get 60–70% of design-procedure marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Δ = ρ × ∇ = 1.025 × L × B × T × C_B** is the foundational equation — memorize it.
- **DWT = Δ − LW**; **DWT/Δ** is the carrying efficiency ratio.
- **C_B = C_P × C_M** is the most-tested coefficient relationship.
- **C_A = Δ^(2/3) × V_S³ / SHP** — use for preliminary power estimation.
- **Froude Number F_n = V / √(gL)** governs wave-making resistance similarity.
- **Hard constraints** (canals, IMO, class) are non-negotiable; **soft** (speed, DWT) are optimizable; **economic** (CTMC, ROI) are the objective function.
- **Design Spiral** = iterative convergence, not linear sequence; include regulatory and buildability feedback loops.
- **Density of seawater = 1.025 t/m³**; freshwater = 1.000 t/m³; ship sinks ~2.5% deeper in freshwater.
- **DWT/Δ** is highest for tankers (~0.86), lowest for warships (~0.25).
- **Speed is expensive** — power scales as V³ (10% speed increase → 33% power increase).
- **L/B, L/T, B/T ratios** are preliminary design checks (e.g., L/B ≈ 6 for tankers, ≈ 7–8 for container ships).
- **Typical C_B values:** tanker ≈ 0.83, bulk carrier ≈ 0.78, container ship ≈ 0.65, warship ≈ 0.50.
- **The Admiralty coefficient is empirical, not fundamental** — its exponents come from Froude scaling.
- **Always show units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- **Draw the design spiral with a return arrow** to score full marks on design-procedure questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Rudder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of Rudder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **rudder** is a primary directional control surface fitted at the **stern (aft)** of a ship, mounted to the **rudder stock**, which pivots about a vertical axis (the stock centerline) and produces a **side force (lateral hydrodynamic force)** when rotated by an angle from the ship's centerline plane. This side force, when resolved along the ship's transverse axis, generates a **yawing moment** about the ship's center of gravity, thereby changing the vessel's heading.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBNST604 - Ship Design II), the **Design of Rudder** module covers the determination of:
- **Rudder area** (planform area required for adequate maneuverability per IMO Resolution A.601(15) and maneuvering standards).
- **Rudder force and torque** acting on the rudder stock (the heart of structural design).
- **Rudiments of Rudder Stock Design** — stock diameter using combined bending + torsion theory.
- **Rudder plating thickness**, **pintle and gudgeon** sizing, and **rudder horn** structural strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to master the **empirical rudder area formulas**, the **derivation of stock diameter from combined stress**, and the **calculations of rudder torque using Rudiman (or Motora) coefficients**. Numerical design problems of 14 marks are routinely asked from this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Rudder as a "Submerged Wing in a River"

Imagine you are wading through a fast-flowing river with a small paddle. If you twist the paddle at an angle to the flow, the water pushes against the paddle's flat surface. Because the paddle is offset behind your body, the push is *not* in line with your center of mass — it twists you sideways, and you turn. 

A ship's **rudder works on exactly the same principle**, but with three crucial differences:
1. The "river flow" is created by the ship's own forward motion (the water rushes past the hull at the ship's service speed $V$).
2. The "paddle" (rudder) sits in the **propeller slipstream**, which dramatically amplifies the side force because of higher local water velocity.
3. The twist (rudder angle $\alpha$) is applied by a **tiller or helm gear**, while the structural backbone is the **rudder stock** — a vertical steel shaft that must resist the enormous bending and twisting loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Sea water density: $\rho = 1.025 \; t/m^3 = 1025 \; kg/m^3$
> - Yield strength of mild rudder stock steel: $\sigma_y = 240 \; MPa$ (typical for grade 410 / 460)
> - Safety factor (KtN / DNV / IRS): $S.F. = 1.5$ to $2.0$ for ultimate rudder force
> - Block coefficient range for merchant ships: $C_B = 0.55$ to $0.85$

## 1.3 Classification of Rudders (KTU Board-Relevant)

For exam purposes, the student must know the **six principal rudder types**:

| Rudder Type | Description | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Unbalanced (Single Plate) Rudder** | Hinge axis at leading edge; entire area is aft of stock. Maximum torque. | Older single-screw vessels |
| **Balanced Rudder** | Hinge axis located at ≈ 0.20 to 0.30 of chord from leading edge. Reduces torque by ≈ 30–40%. | Most modern merchant ships |
| **Semi-Balanced Rudder** | Hinge axis at leading edge, but upper portion is cut away (above the propeller). | Tugs, trawlers |
| **Spade Rudder** | No hinges; entirely supported by the rudder stock. Requires strong horn. | Single-screw merchant ships, naval vessels |
| **Twin Rudder (Bi-Rudder)** | Two spade rudders, one on each side of the centerline. | Twin-screw ships, azimuth control |
| **Flap Rudder (Kort Nozzle with Flap)** | Main rudder plus a trailing flap that deflects extra (up to ±70°). | Highly maneuverable ships, tugs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Procedure — The Six Logical Steps

The **design of a rudder** in KTU-style numerical problems follows a fixed, repeatable sequence. Mastering this sequence is the difference between a 7-mark and a 14-mark answer.

**Step 1 — Decide the rudder type and hull form factor**
Choose from spade, balanced, semi-balanced based on whether the ship is single-screw/twin-screw and the desired maneuverability.

**Step 2 — Estimate the required rudder area ($A_R$)**
The KTU board typically tests **two empirical area formulas**:
- **Harvald's formula (older but still tested):** $A_R = k \cdot L \cdot T$, where $k$ is an empirical coefficient.
- **Manoeuvring standard formula (IMO-based, modern):** $A_R = \dfrac{L \cdot d}{100} \cdot \left(1.5 + 1.3 \, \dfrac{C_B}{0.7}\right) \cdot f_{fwd} \cdot f_{bkw} \cdot f_{lwl}$
where $L$ is the length between perpendiculars, $d$ is the design draft, and $f$ are correction factors (forward / backward / LWL ratios).

**Step 3 — Determine the local water velocity at the rudder ($v_R$)**
The rudder sits in the **propeller slipstream**, so its local velocity is much higher than the ship speed. The standard KTU formula is:
$$v_R = V \cdot (1 + w) \cdot \sqrt{1 + C_T}$$
where $w$ is the **Taylor wake fraction** (typically 0.20 to 0.40 for single-screw ships) and $C_T$ is the **thrust loading coefficient** of the propeller. For a simpler exam, the KTU often gives $v_R$ directly or asks the student to use $v_R = 1.6 V$ as a rule of thumb.

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum rudder side force ($F_R$)**
Using the lift analogy of a thin aerofoil in a uniform flow:
$$F_R = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v_R^{2} \cdot A_R \cdot C_L$$
where $C_L$ is the **lift coefficient** at the design rudder angle. The **maximum** $C_L$ occurs at $\alpha \approx 30°$ to $35°$ and is about $C_{L,max} = 1.2$ to $1.4$. The KTU board frequently gives $C_{L,max} = 1.3$.

**Step 5 — Compute the rudder torque ($Q$)**
Torque is the **moment of the side force about the rudder stock axis**. For a balanced rudder:
$$Q = C_Q \cdot F_R \cdot b$$
where $b$ is the **mean chord length of the rudder** and $C_Q$ is the **torque coefficient** (0.20 to 0.30 typically, but the KTU often gives a value directly). For an unbalanced rudder, $C_Q \approx 0.5$.

**Step 6 — Size the rudder stock diameter ($d$)**
The stock must resist **combined bending and torsion**. The standard approach (covered in detail in Section 3) is the **maximum shear stress theory** for circular shafts:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^{3}} \cdot \sqrt{M^{2} + Q^{2}} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{2 \cdot S.F.}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **most-tested formula set** in KTU PBNST604 Module 1 examinations. Memorize every line, including units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical KTU Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rudder Area (simple) | $A_R = k \cdot L \cdot T$ | $m^2$ | $k = 1/30$ to $1/50$ |
| 2 | Rudder Area (IMO) | $A_R = \dfrac{L \cdot d}{100} \cdot K$ | $m^2$ | $K \approx 1.5$ to $3.0$ |
| 3 | Local velocity (KTU short form) | $v_R = k_v \cdot V$ | $m/s$ | $k_v = 1.4$ to $1.6$ |
| 4 | Local velocity (full form) | $v_R = V \cdot (1+w) \cdot \sqrt{1+C_T}$ | $m/s$ | $w = 0.20$ to $0.40$ |
| 5 | Rudder Side Force | $F_R = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_R^{2} A_R C_L$ | $N$ | $C_L = 1.2$ to $1.4$ |
| 6 | Rudder Torque | $Q = C_Q \cdot F_R \cdot b$ | $N \cdot m$ | $C_Q = 0.20$ to $0.30$ |
| 7 | Stock Bending Moment | $M = F_R \cdot \ell$ | $N \cdot m$ | $\ell$ = stock length to bearing |
| 8 | Stock Diameter (combined) | $d^{3} = \dfrac{16 \cdot S.F.}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y} \cdot \sqrt{M^{2} + Q^{2}}$ | $m$ | $S.F. = 1.5$ to $2.0$ |
| 9 | Pintle Shear Stress | $\tau_p = \dfrac{4 F_R}{\pi d_p^{2}}$ | $Pa$ | $\tau_p \le 80 \; MPa$ |
| 10 | Rudder Plate Thickness | $t = 0.04 \sqrt{s \cdot k}$ | $mm$ | $s$ = stiffener spacing, $k = 240$ |

> **Notation Guard (Anti-Markdown-Break):** Note that all absolute-value-like bars in the table (e.g., $\sqrt{M^{2}+Q^{2}}$) use parentheses or are written with $\sqrt{ \; }$, **never** the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol, to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **rudder is the only control surface that can be hand-tuned for a specific ship class**. Naval architects use this design to:
- Ensure compliance with **IMO Resolution MSC.137(76)** standards for turning circle diameter ($< 5 L$ at 35° rudder angle).
- Determine the **rudder torque for steering gear sizing** (which then feeds into the electrical motor rating).
- Calculate the **rudder stock scantlings** for class society approval (DNV, LR, BV, IRS).
- Provide input for the **steering gear power calculation** (Module 5 of the broader syllabus).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rudder Stock Diameter from Combined Bending & Torsion

This is the **most important derivation in Module 1** and is worth up to 7 marks on its own. The KTU board expects every line of the algebra to be shown.

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem

A vertical rudder stock of circular cross-section (diameter $d$) is subjected simultaneously to:
- A **bending moment** $M$ (from the side force $F_R$ acting at a distance $\ell$ from the lower bearing).
- A **torque** $Q$ (from the moment of the side force about the stock axis).

We must find the **minimum stock diameter $d$** that keeps the **maximum equivalent stress** within the allowable yield limit, given a safety factor $S.F.$.

### 3.1.2 Stress Components in a Circular Shaft

For a solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$:

**Bending stress (normal stress at the outer fiber):**
$$\sigma = \frac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d^{3}}$$

**Torsional shear stress (at the outer surface):**
$$\tau = \frac{16 \, Q}{\pi \, d^{3}}$$

### 3.1.3 Failure Criterion — Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Guest's Theory)

For a combined loading of bending and torsion, the **principal stresses** at the critical (outermost) fiber are:
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$$

The **maximum shear stress** in the element is:
$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$$

Substituting $\sigma$ and $\tau$ from above:
$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{16 \, M}{2 \pi \, d^{3}}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{16 \, Q}{\pi \, d^{3}}\right)^{2}}$$

Factoring out the common term $\dfrac{16}{\pi d^{3}}$:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi \, d^{3}} \cdot \sqrt{M^{2} + Q^{2}}$$

### 3.1.4 Applying the Safety Factor

The allowable shear stress for a ductile rudder stock material is taken as **half the yield stress**, divided by a safety factor $S.F.$:
$$\tau_{max} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{2 \cdot S.F.}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{16}{\pi \, d^{3}} \cdot \sqrt{M^{2} + Q^{2}} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{2 \cdot S.F.}$$

Rearranging for $d^{3}$:
$$d^{3} \ge \frac{32 \cdot S.F. \cdot \sqrt{M^{2} + Q^{2}}}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y}$$

This is the **final KTU-board formula** for the rudder stock diameter. A small numerical example is given below.

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, 7-Mark Style)

> **Given:** A single-screw cargo ship has rudder side force $F_R = 80 \; kN$ acting at a distance $\ell = 4.5 \; m$ above the lower rudder bearing. The rudder torque is $Q = 18 \; kN \cdot m$. Yield strength of stock steel $\sigma_y = 240 \; MPa$. Safety factor $S.F. = 1.6$. Find the stock diameter.

**Solution Step 1 — Bending moment:**
$$M = F_R \cdot \ell = (80 \times 10^{3}) \times 4.5 = 3.6 \times 10^{5} \; N \cdot m$$

**Solution Step 2 — Compute $M^2 + Q^2$:**
$$M^{2} = (3.6 \times 10^{5})^{2} = 1.296 \times 10^{11} \; (N \cdot m)^{2}$$
$$Q^{2} = (18 \times 10^{3})^{2} = 3.24 \times 10^{8} \; (N \cdot m)^{2}$$
$$M^{2} + Q^{2} \approx 1.299 \times 10^{11} \; (N \cdot m)^{2}$$
$$\sqrt{M^{2} + Q^{2}} \approx 3.604 \times 10^{5} \; N \cdot m$$

**Solution Step 3 — Substitute into the stock-diameter formula:**
$$d^{3} \ge \frac{32 \times 1.6 \times 3.604 \times 10^{5}}{\pi \times 240 \times 10^{6}}$$

Numerator $= 32 \times 1.6 \times 3.604 \times 10^{5} = 1.8449 \times 10^{7}$

Denominator $= \pi \times 240 \times 10^{6} = 7.5398 \times 10^{8}$

$$d^{3} \ge 2.4468 \times 10^{-2} \; m^{3}$$

$$d \ge \sqrt[3]{2.4468 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 0.291 \; m$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 290 \; mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU rounding:** Always round $d$ **up to the next standard 10-mm increment**. So a $d = 290 \; mm$ stock would be ordered as **$d = 300 \; mm$**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Force, Moment and Stress for Each Rudder Type

| Rudder Type | Side Force $F_R$ | Bending Arm $\ell$ | Bending Moment $M$ | Torque Coeff. $C_Q$ | Stock Stress State |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unbalanced | Max ($F_R$) | Largest | Largest | ≈ 0.50 | Severe combined loading |
| Balanced (20% balance) | Same $F_R$ | Same $\ell$ | Same $M$ | ≈ 0.25 | Moderate torsion |
| Semi-Balanced | Slightly less | Same | Same $M$ | ≈ 0.30 | Moderate |
| Spade (single screw) | Same $F_R$ | Reduced (compact) | Reduced $M$ | ≈ 0.25 | Combined but balanced |
| Twin Rudder (per stock) | $F_R / 2$ | Reduced | $M/2$ per stock | $C_Q \cdot F_R \cdot b / 2$ | Lowest individual load |

> **Inference:** This matrix shows that a **balanced spade rudder** is the *most* structurally efficient choice because it minimizes both $M$ and $Q$ without reducing the side force. This is why it is the **default in 90% of modern merchant ships**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rudder Force Flow & Stock Loading

The figure below maps the **complete load path** from hydrodynamic flow to structural response of the rudder stock. Each alphanumeric node represents a stage in the design chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Ship Service Speed V] --> B1[Wake Fraction w]
    A1 --> B2[Thrust Coeff C_T]
    B1 --> C1[Local Velocity v_R at Rudder]
    B2 --> C1
    A2[Rudder Area A_R] --> D1[Side Force F_R]
    C1 --> D1
    A3[Lift Coeff C_L at alpha_max] --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Bending Moment M on Stock]
    A4[Stock Length ell] --> E1
    D1 --> F1[Hydraulic Torque Q on Stock]
    A5[Rudder Chord b] --> F1
    A6[Torque Coeff C_Q] --> F1
    E1 --> G1[Combined Stress in Stock]
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Stock Diameter d]
    A7[Yield sigma_y] --> H1
    A8[Safety Factor SF] --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Selected d from Standard Tables]
    D1 --> J1[Pintle Shear Force]
    H1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Pintle Diameter d_p]
    D1 --> L1[Rudder Plate Bending]
    A9[Stiffener Spacing s] --> L1
    L1 --> M1[Rudder Plate Thickness t]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rudder Stock Assembly

This diagram shows the **physical assembly** of a typical spade/balanced rudder with its bearings, pintles and the load path from the hull to the rudder horn.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HullStern
        H1[Rudder Horn at Hull Stern]
        H2[Sole Piece at Keel]
    end
    subgraph RudderStockAssembly
        S1[Upper Stock Bearing]
        S2[Lower Stock Bearing in Rudder Trunk]
        S3[Rudder Stock Vertical Shaft]
        S4[Pintle at Bottom of Rudder]
    end
    subgraph RudderBladeStructure
        R1[Main Plate of Rudder]
        R2[Horizontal Stiffeners]
        R3[Vertical Web Frames]
    end
    subgraph SteeringGear
        G1[Tiller Arm]
        G2[Hydraulic Ram or Rotary Actuator]
    end
    H1 --> S3
    H2 --> S4
    S3 --> S1
    S3 --> S2
    S3 --> R1
    S4 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R1 --> R3
    S3 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Vector Diagram of Forces on a Spade Rudder

The following schematic is a **Mermaid-rendered vector flow** showing how the side force $F_R$ is decomposed into the bending moment $M$ and the torque $Q$ that load the stock. This is the single most diagrammed concept in KTU past papers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Propeller Slipstream] -->|Hits Rudder| F1[Side Force F_R]
    F1 -->|Perpendicular distance b| Q1[Torque Q about Stock Axis]
    F1 -->|Vertical arm ell| M1[Bending Moment M at Lower Bearing]
    Q1 --> S1[Critical Section of Stock at Rudder Trunk]
    M1 --> S1
    S1 -->|Combined Stress| D1[Required Stock Diameter d]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** Note that $F_R$ is the *single* side force that simultaneously produces both $M$ (because the force is offset vertically from the lower bearing) and $Q$ (because the force is offset horizontally from the stock axis by the chord arm $b$). This **dual loading** is what makes rudder stock sizing non-trivial.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a balanced rudder. How does it differ from a semi-balanced rudder in terms of torque?**

**Model Answer:**
A **balanced rudder** is one in which the **hinge axis is shifted forward from the leading edge by about 20%–30% of the chord length**, so that a portion of the rudder area lies ahead of the axis. This forward area produces a torque that **opposes** the torque from the main aft portion, thereby **reducing the net torque** required from the steering gear by 30%–40%.

A **semi-balanced rudder**, in contrast, is essentially an unbalanced rudder (hinge at leading edge) but with the **upper portion cut away above the propeller slipstream**. Only the part of the rudder that is immersed in the propeller race contributes effectively to the side force, and the cut-away portion is shaped so as to provide partial balance. The torque reduction is **less** than that of a true balanced rudder.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the formula for maximum rudder side force. Explain the significance of the local velocity $v_R$ as compared to ship speed $V$.**

**Model Answer:**
$$F_R = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, v_R^{2} \, A_R \, C_L$$

**Significance of $v_R$:** The rudder is not operating in still water; it sits in the **propeller slipstream**, which accelerates the water locally to a velocity significantly higher than the ship's service speed $V$. Because the side force is **proportional to the square of the local velocity**, an under-estimate of $v_R$ leads to a dangerously under-sized stock. Empirical KTU rule: $v_R = (1.4 \text{ to } 1.6) \cdot V$ for single-screw ships, and $v_R \approx V$ for twin-screw ships (no slipstream acceleration).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]

**A bulk carrier has the following particulars:**
- Length between perpendiculars: $L = 150 \; m$
- Design draft: $T = 8.5 \; m$
- Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.72$
- Service speed: $V = 14 \; knots = 7.20 \; m/s$
- Wake fraction: $w = 0.30$
- Thrust loading coefficient: $C_T = 0.45$
- Lift coefficient at design angle: $C_L = 1.3$
- Torque coefficient: $C_Q = 0.25$
- Rudder chord (mean): $b = 2.4 \; m$
- Distance from lower bearing to force center: $\ell = 4.0 \; m$
- Yield strength of stock: $\sigma_y = 240 \; MPa$
- Safety factor: $S.F. = 1.5$

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate (i) the rudder area using $A_R = \dfrac{L \cdot T}{50}$, and (ii) the maximum side force $F_R$ on the rudder.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate (i) the rudder torque $Q$, (ii) the bending moment $M$ on the stock, and (iii) the required stock diameter $d$ using the combined stress theory.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Side Force Calculation

**Step (i) — Rudder area:**
$$A_R = \frac{L \cdot T}{50} = \frac{150 \times 8.5}{50} = 25.5 \; m^{2}$$

**Step (ii) — Local velocity at the rudder:**
$$v_R = V \cdot (1 + w) \cdot \sqrt{1 + C_T}$$
$$v_R = 7.20 \times (1 + 0.30) \times \sqrt{1 + 0.45}$$
$$v_R = 7.20 \times 1.30 \times \sqrt{1.45}$$
$$v_R = 7.20 \times 1.30 \times 1.2042 \approx 11.27 \; m/s$$

**Maximum side force:**
$$F_R = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v_R^{2} \cdot A_R \cdot C_L$$
$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (11.27)^{2} \times 25.5 \times 1.3$$
$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 127.01 \times 25.5 \times 1.3$$
$$F_R \approx 2.158 \times 10^{6} \; N \approx 2158 \; kN$$

#### Part (b) — Stock Sizing

**Step (i) — Rudder torque:**
$$Q = C_Q \cdot F_R \cdot b = 0.25 \times 2.158 \times 10^{6} \times 2.4$$
$$Q \approx 1.295 \times 10^{6} \; N \cdot m = 1295 \; kN \cdot m$$

**Step (ii) — Bending moment:**
$$M = F_R \cdot \ell = 2.158 \times 10^{6} \times 4.0 = 8.632 \times 10^{6} \; N \cdot m = 8632 \; kN \cdot m$$

**Step (iii) — Stock diameter (combined stress):**
$$d^{3} \ge \frac{32 \cdot S.F.}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y} \cdot \sqrt{M^{2} + Q^{2}}$$

Compute $M^2 + Q^2$:
$$M^{2} = (8.632 \times 10^{6})^{2} = 7.451 \times 10^{13}$$
$$Q^{2} = (1.295 \times 10^{6})^{2} = 1.677 \times 10^{12}$$
$$M^{2} + Q^{2} \approx 7.619 \times 10^{13}$$
$$\sqrt{M^{2} + Q^{2}} \approx 8.728 \times 10^{6} \; N \cdot m$$

Substituting:
$$d^{3} \ge \frac{32 \times 1.5}{\pi \times 240 \times 10^{6}} \times 8.728 \times 10^{6}$$
$$d^{3} \ge \frac{48 \times 8.728 \times 10^{6}}{7.5398 \times 10^{8}}$$
$$d^{3} \ge \frac{4.189 \times 10^{8}}{7.5398 \times 10^{8}} \approx 0.5556 \; m^{3}$$

$$d \ge \sqrt[3]{0.5556} \approx 0.821 \; m$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 820 \; mm}$$

Adopting the next standard 50-mm increment:
$$\boxed{d_{adopted} = 850 \; mm}$$

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks — Internal Choice]

**For a single-screw cargo ship of length $L = 120 \; m$ and draft $T = 7.0 \; m$, the rudder area by Harvald's formula is $A_R = 0.018 L T$. The propeller slipstream velocity $v_R = 1.5 V$, where $V = 7.5 \; m/s$. The design lift coefficient is $C_L = 1.3$ and the rudder chord is $b = 1.8 \; m$. The torque coefficient is $C_Q = 0.22$. The stock length to the lower bearing is $\ell = 3.5 \; m$. Adopt $\sigma_y = 235 \; MPa$ and $S.F. = 1.6$.**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate (i) the rudder area, and (ii) the maximum side force on the rudder.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate (i) the rudder torque, (ii) the bending moment on the stock, and (iii) the required stock diameter.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a)

**Step (i):**
$$A_R = 0.018 \times L \times T = 0.018 \times 120 \times 7.0 = 15.12 \; m^{2}$$

**Step (ii) — Local velocity:**
$$v_R = 1.5 \times 7.5 = 11.25 \; m/s$$

**Side force:**
$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (11.25)^{2} \times 15.12 \times 1.3$$
$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 126.56 \times 15.12 \times 1.3$$
$$F_R \approx 1.273 \times 10^{6} \; N \approx 1273 \; kN$$

#### Part (b)

**Step (i) — Torque:**
$$Q = 0.22 \times 1.273 \times 10^{6} \times 1.8 = 5.041 \times 10^{5} \; N \cdot m \approx 504 \; kN \cdot m$$

**Step (ii) — Bending moment:**
$$M = 1.273 \times 10^{6} \times 3.5 = 4.456 \times 10^{6} \; N \cdot m \approx 4456 \; kN \cdot m$$

**Step (iii) — Stock diameter:**
$$\sqrt{M^{2}+Q^{2}} = \sqrt{(4.456 \times 10^{6})^{2} + (5.041 \times 10^{5})^{2}} \approx 4.484 \times 10^{6} \; N \cdot m$$

$$d^{3} \ge \frac{32 \times 1.6 \times 4.484 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 235 \times 10^{6}}$$
$$d^{3} \ge \frac{51.2 \times 4.484 \times 10^{6}}{7.381 \times 10^{8}}$$
$$d^{3} \ge \frac{2.296 \times 10^{8}}{7.381 \times 10^{8}} \approx 0.311 \; m^{3}$$
$$d \ge 0.678 \; m \approx 680 \; mm$$

$$\boxed{d_{adopted} = 700 \; mm \; (next \; 50 \; mm \; increment)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most often lose marks in this module:**
> 1. **Using ship speed $V$ instead of local velocity $v_R$.** The side force uses $v_R$ (slipstream velocity), not $V$. A direct substitution of $V$ in the $F_R$ formula underestimates the force by up to 50% — and the stock diameter scales with the cube root of force, so this error propagates dangerously.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor.** The KTU board explicitly asks for the safety factor to be applied. Skipping $S.F.$ costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up bending moment arm $\ell$ with the rudder chord $b$.** These are two different distances: $\ell$ is **vertical** (used for $M$), and $b$ is **horizontal** (used for $Q$). Examiners flag this swap immediately.
> 4. **Not rounding up to the next standard size.** Real engineering always rounds *up*; rounding down is a design failure and is penalized.
> 5. **Using the wrong failure theory.** Guest's theory (max shear stress) is the KTU default for ductile rudder stock. Using the **maximum normal stress theory** or the **distortion energy theory** without the examiner's allowance leads to mark deduction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Rudder area** is the *first* design quantity. Use empirical formulas: $A_R = k L T$ (with $k = 1/30$ to $1/50$) or the IMO-based formula. The 14-mark questions always begin here.
- **Local velocity $v_R$** is **NOT** the ship speed. For single-screw ships use $v_R = V(1+w)\sqrt{1+C_T}$ or the rule of thumb $v_R \approx 1.5 V$.
- **Side force** is the lift on a thin aerofoil: $F_R = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_R^{2} A_R C_L$, with $C_{L,max} \approx 1.3$ at $\alpha \approx 30°$–$35°$.
- **Torque** uses the rudder chord $b$: $Q = C_Q F_R b$, with $C_Q \approx 0.20$–$0.30$ for balanced rudders.
- **Bending moment** uses the vertical stock length $\ell$: $M = F_R \ell$.
- **Stock diameter** comes from **Guest's combined stress theory**: $d^{3} \ge \dfrac{32 \cdot S.F. \cdot \sqrt{M^{2}+Q^{2}}}{\pi \sigma_y}$.
- **Pintle sizing** uses pure shear: $\tau_p = \dfrac{4 F_R}{\pi d_p^{2}}$.
- **Rudder plate thickness** follows: $t = 0.04 \sqrt{s \cdot k}$, where $s$ is stiffener spacing in mm and $k$ is the panel aspect-ratio factor.
- **Balanced rudders** reduce torque by 30%–40% by shifting the hinge axis 20%–30% forward of the leading edge.
- **Standard KTU rounding rule**: always round $d$ **up** to the next 10 mm or 50 mm increment.
- **Failure theory** is **Maximum Shear Stress (Guest)** for ductile stock material — do not use max normal stress without permission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final word of advice for KTU 2024 PBNST604 students:** Practice at least **three** full 14-mark problems from this module before the exam.
