The numerical pattern is highly repetitive; once you master the six-step procedure in Section 2, every variant of the question reduces to plugging numbers into the same five formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anchoring and Mooring Arrangement. Types of Anchors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Anchoring and Mooring Arrangement. Types of Anchors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Anchoring System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anchoring System (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An *anchoring system* is a critical deck machinery arrangement that provides a ship with the means to hold its position in a defined geographical location against the action of environmental forces (wind, current, wave drift) by employing a heavy metallic implement (anchor) connected to the hull through a chain cable system.

The anchoring arrangement forms part of the **Hull Outfit and Deck Machinery** of a ship and is governed by classification society rules (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS) and the **SOLAS Convention** (Safety of Life at Sea) requirements.

## 1.2 Mooring System — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Mooring System (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *mooring system* is the assembly of ropes/wires, winches, capstans, bollards, and fairleads used to secure a ship alongside a berth, quay, dolphin, buoy, or jetty such that the vessel remains station-keeping under varying environmental and operational load conditions.

## 1.3 Key Components — Terminology Overview

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Anchor** | The metallic device that grips the seabed |
| **Chain Cable (Studded Link Chain)** | Transmits the holding force from anchor to ship |
| **Hawse Pipe** | Tubular guide through the hull side for chain passage |
| **Windlass** | Mechanical machine for raising/lowering the anchor |
| **Chain Stopper** | Secondary securing device to relieve windlass load |
| **Bitter End** | Inboard end of chain cable, shackled to a pad eye on deck |
| **Chain Locker** | Compartment below deck for stowing chain |
| **Spurling Pipe** | Watertight pipe from deck to chain locker |
| **Hawser / Mooring Line** | Rope or wire used for berthing |
| **Capstan / Mooring Winch** | Applies tension to mooring lines |
| **Bollard / Bitt / Fairlead** | Point of attachment / guidance for mooring lines |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Intuitive Understanding):**
> Think of a ship the way you would think of a **camel tied to a peg in the desert**. The *anchor* is the metal hook dug into the sand; the *chain* is the rope that lets the camel graze within a circle but not run away; the *windlass* is the pulley system that lets you lift the hook back up; the *hawse pipe* is the hole in the tent wall through which the rope passes. **Mooring lines** are like the *multiple ropes you tie when parking a car* in a tight garage — one rope fore, one aft, one on each side (called *spring lines* and *breast lines*) so the vehicle cannot move in any direction.

**Why both Anchoring AND Mooring?**
- **Anchoring** is used in open water, harbours, or sheltered anchorages where there is no quay.
- **Mooring** is used when the ship is berthed alongside a jetty/buoy.
- Together they ensure the ship can hold station in **any operational scenario**.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics in Anchoring

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Number of Anchors (per SOLAS & IRS Rules):**
> - Every ocean-going ship must carry **3 anchors** (2 **bower anchors** + 1 **stream anchor**).
> - Additional **spare anchor** carried in some vessels.
> - The **emergency steering gear anchor** is an additional small anchor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Windlass Capacity Rule of Thumb:**
> The windlass must be capable of lifting the anchor plus **75 m of chain cable** at a heaving rate of **0.015 m/s** under **half-load** test condition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy of the Anchoring System

The anchoring system must perform three fundamental functions:

1. **Station Keeping** — Hold the ship in position against **windage, current, and wave drift forces**.
2. **Emergency Holding** — Provide holding force during **engine/steering failure (dragging situation)**.
3. **Recovery** — Allow the anchor to be **hauled inboard** without damage to the hull or the equipment.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Force Transmission

$$\text{Wind/Current Force} \rightarrow \text{Hull (via chain)} \rightarrow \text{Chain cable} \rightarrow \text{Anchor shank} \rightarrow \text{Anchor flukes (grip seabed)}$$

The **holding power** of an anchor is fundamentally a function of:
- **Self-weight** of the anchor (W)
- **Soil/Seabed properties** (soft mud vs. hard sand vs. rock)
- **Fluke geometry and penetration angle**
- **Buried depth** of the fluke (deeper = higher holding power)

## 2.2 Design Considerations — KTU High-Yield Points

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Design Criteria:**
> 1. Number of anchors per SOLAS = **3 minimum**.
> 2. Mass of each bower anchor = **0.45 % to 1.0 %** of the ship displacement (typical range for general cargo vessels).
> 3. Length of chain cable = **approximately 12 to 15 shackle lengths** for the bower system (1 shackle = 27.5 m).
> 4. Chain cable diameter (d) is determined by the **grade** of the chain (Q1/Q2/Q3/U1/U2/U3 according to ISO and IRS rules).
> 5. The **holding power to anchor weight ratio (K_H)** ranges from **3 to 4** for ordinary anchors, **4 to 6** for HHP, and **6 to 8** for SHHP anchors.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Holding Power | $F_H = W_a \cdot K_H$ | kN | $W_a$ = anchor weight, $K_H$ = holding factor |
| 2 | Mass of Anchor (approx) | $W_a = (0.0045 \text{ to } 0.01) \cdot \Delta$ | tonnes | $\Delta$ = ship displacement |
| 3 | Number of Shackles (Bower) | $n = 12 \text{ to } 15$ | shackles | Each shackle = 27.5 m |
| 4 | Total Chain Length | $L = 27.5 \cdot n$ | metres | Sum of shackle lengths |
| 5 | Chain Breaking Load (Q2 grade) | $F_{BL} = 0.0254 \cdot d^2 \cdot (44 - 0.08 d)$ | kN | d in mm; IRS formula |
| 6 | Chain Proof Load (Q2 grade) | $F_{PL} = 0.625 \cdot F_{BL}$ | kN | Standard 62.5% of BL |
| 7 | Chain Mass per metre | $m_c = 0.0219 \cdot d^2$ | kg/m | Studded chain approximation |
| 8 | Wind Force on Hull (anchored) | $F_{wind} = 0.5 \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_x \cdot V_w^2$ | kN | $A_x$ = projected lateral area |
| 9 | Current Force on Hull | $F_{cur} = 0.5 \cdot \rho_w \cdot C_c \cdot A_l \cdot V_c^2$ | kN | $A_l$ = underwater lateral area |
| 10 | Total Environmental Force | $F_E = F_{wind} + F_{cur} + F_{wave}$ | kN | Vector sum (worst case) |
| 11 | Safety Factor (chain) | $SF = F_{BL} / F_E$ | dimensionless | $\geq 1.5$ typically required |
| 12 | Catenary Sag (h) | $h = \sqrt{(F \cdot L) / w}$ | m | F = horizontal force, w = weight/m of chain |
| 13 | Holding Power Test (HHP) | $F_{test} = 1.5 \cdot W_a$ | kN | For HHP classification |
| 14 | Holding Power Test (SHHP) | $F_{test} = 2.0 \cdot W_a$ | kN | For Super HHP |
| 15 | Minimum Windlass Pull | $P_{min} = 1.25 \cdot W_a + 0.75 \cdot W_c$ | kN | $W_c$ = chain weight per shackle |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Pipe Symbol in Tables:**
> In the equations above, all absolute value/division notations have been replaced with `·` or `\frac` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do **not** write `|d|` in your answer scripts; use proper LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The anchoring/mooring system is **mission-critical** in the following industries:

- **Offshore Oil & Gas** — Mooring of FPSOs, semi-submersibles, and drilling rigs (chain-wire-chain systems, polyester ropes).
- **Naval Defence** — Tactical anchoring for submarines, frigates, and aircraft carriers.
- **Merchant Shipping** — Berthing/unberthing at ports globally.
- **Fishing Vessels** — Operational holding on fishing grounds.
- **Research Vessels** — Station-keeping during oceanographic operations.
- **Recreational Boating** — Yacht and pleasure craft anchoring at bays and islands.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Holding Power of an Anchor

### Given Data
Let us derive the **holding power** of an anchor embedded in a soft clay seabed.

**Assumptions:**
- Anchor fluke acts as an inclined plate (angle $\alpha$ with seabed).
- Soil passive resistance $R_p$ acts normal to the fluke.
- Self-weight component contributes to penetration.

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram of Anchor Fluke

The fluke experiences:
- **Self-weight** $W_a$ acting vertically downward.
- **Pull-out force** $F$ acting along the chain (tangent to the fluke).
- **Passive soil reaction** $R_p$ acting normal to the fluke surface.

### Step 2: Resolve Forces

The holding force is determined when the system is in **limiting equilibrium**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_H &= R_p \cdot \cos\alpha + W_a \cdot \sin\alpha - C \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $R_p$ = passive earth pressure resistance of the soil
- $\alpha$ = angle of fluke with vertical
- $C$ = cohesion of the soil
- $A$ = surface area of the fluke

### Step 3: Substitute Soil Mechanics Expression

For a *cohesionless* seabed (sand), using Rankine's passive earth pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot z^2 \cdot K_p \cdot B
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil (typically **18 kN/m³** for sand)
- $z$ = depth of embedment (typically 2-3 times the fluke length)
- $K_p$ = passive earth pressure coefficient
- $B$ = fluke breadth

### Step 4: Compute Holding Power Coefficient $K_H$

The *holding power coefficient* is defined experimentally as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_H &= \frac{F_H}{W_a}
\end{aligned}
$$

This coefficient is the most important figure-of-merit for comparing anchor performance:

| Anchor Type | Typical $K_H$ |
|---|---|
| Admiralty (Stocked) | 2 - 3 |
| Admiralty (Stockless) | 2.5 - 3.5 |
| Bruce | 4 - 5 |
| Danforth | 5 - 6 |
| Hall (HHP) | 5 - 7 |
| AC-14 (SHHP) | 7 - 9 |
| Stevpris (SHHP) | 8 - 10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Society Definition of HHP/SHHP:**
> A **High Holding Power (HHP)** anchor is one whose $K_H$ in soft seabed (e.g., soft mud) is **at least 2 times** that of an ordinary stockless anchor of equal weight. A **Super High Holding Power (SHHP)** anchor must achieve **at least 4 times** the holding power of an ordinary stockless anchor.

## 3.2 Derivation: Chain Cable Breaking Load (IRS Rule for Q2 Grade)

### Step 1: Empirical Formula (IRS Rules)

The breaking load of Q2 grade studded chain cable is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{BL} &= 0.0254 \cdot d^2 \cdot (44 - 0.08 d)
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $F_{BL}$ = breaking load in **kN**
- $d$ = chain diameter in **mm**

### Step 2: Proof Load Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{PL} &= 0.625 \cdot F_{BL}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Working Load Calculation (with safety factor 1.5)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{W} &= \frac{F_{BL}}{SF} = \frac{F_{BL}}{1.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A bulk carrier of displacement $\Delta = 50,000$ tonnes is to be fitted with bower anchors. Compute (a) the mass of each anchor, (b) the chain cable diameter assuming Q2 grade, and (c) the breaking load.

**Given:**
- $\Delta = 50,000$ tonnes
- Assume anchor weight fraction = 0.0075 (mid-range for cargo ships)
- Chain grade: Q2

**Solution (a): Anchor Mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= 0.0075 \cdot \Delta = 0.0075 \cdot 50,000 = 375 \text{ tonnes}  \quad \text{(WRONG - this is over)} \\
W_a &= 0.0075 \cdot 50,000 \cdot 10^3 = 3.75 \times 10^5 \text{ kg} = 375 \text{ kg} \quad \text{(CORRECT)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap:** Always express displacement in kg when applying the fraction. A common student mistake is to write "tonnes" and then forget to convert, leading to absurdly oversized anchors.

**Solution (b): Chain Diameter (Iterative Approach)**

For Q2 grade, IRS prescribes a relationship between anchor mass and chain diameter. For a 375 kg anchor, the standard **$d \approx 50 \text{ mm}$** is selected from the classification tables.

**Solution (c): Breaking Load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{BL} &= 0.0254 \cdot (50)^2 \cdot (44 - 0.08 \cdot 50) \\
F_{BL} &= 0.0254 \cdot 2500 \cdot 40 \\
F_{BL} &= 2540 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of Safety Factor:**

Let environmental force $F_E = 1200 \text{ kN}$ (typical for a 50,000 t vessel in a moderate storm).

$$
\begin{aligned}
SF &= \frac{F_{BL}}{F_E} = \frac{2540}{1200} = 2.12 \quad \text{✓ Acceptable} 
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Anchor Types

| Property | Admiralty Stockless | Bruce | Danforth | Hall | AC-14 (SHHP) | Mushroom |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_H$ (soft mud) | 2.5 | 4.5 | 5.5 | 6.0 | 8.0 | 1.5 |
| Fluke Angle (°) | 50 | 45 | 30 | 30 | 32 | Flat |
| Weight (typical) | High | Medium | Low | Low | Low | Very High |
| Seabed Suitability | Most | All | Soft | Soft | Most | Soft mud only |
| Stowing Ease | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Good | Good | Poor |
| Holding per Weight | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Best | Worst (mooring) |
| Main Use | General | Multi-purpose | Yachts | Yachts | Offshore | Perm. moorings |

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Anchoring & Mooring System Architecture

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### **Q1.** Differentiate clearly between an **anchoring system** and a **mooring system** on a ship. State the SOLAS-prescribed minimum number of anchors. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Anchoring System** is used to hold the ship in **open water or anchorage grounds** (where there is no quay) by means of an anchor buried in the seabed, connected to the ship by a chain cable. It is primarily used in **rivers, harbours, sheltered anchorages**, or **open sea station-keeping**.

> **Mooring System** is used to secure the ship **alongside a berth, jetty, buoy, or dolphin** using synthetic ropes, wire ropes, or high-modulus lines connected to bollards and winches on the ship and to fixed points on the shore.

> **SOLAS Minimum** = **3 anchors** (2 bower + 1 stream).

---

### **Q2.** What is a **High Holding Power (HHP) anchor**? How is it classified by classification societies? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> An **HHP anchor** is one whose holding capacity in soft seabed conditions is **at least 2 times** that of an ordinary stockless anchor of the **same weight**. A **Super HHP (SHHP)** anchor achieves at least **4 times** the ordinary anchor's holding power. These classifications are awarded by **classification societies** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS) based on **standard seabed tests**. Examples: **Danforth, Hall** (HHP); **AC-14, Stevpris** (SHHP).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the complete **anchoring arrangement** of a modern ocean-going ship. Label all major components and explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The complete anchoring arrangement consists of the following components:

**1. Anchor (Bower Type):** The metallic digging device, stowed in a **hawse pipe** on the forecastle deck. Made of forged or cast steel, weight typically **0.45 – 1.0 %** of ship displacement.

**2. Chain Cable:** Studded link chain, length typically **12 to 15 shackles** (= **330 to 412.5 m**); each shackle = 27.5 m; diameter determined by IRS rules for the chain grade.

**3. Hawse Pipe:** A heavy fabricated steel tube fitted on the **shell plating** at the forecastle; allows chain to pass from outside to inside the ship.

**4. Windlass:** Mechanical machine on the forecastle deck consisting of a **wildcat** (sprocket wheel) and **brake band**; used to raise/lower the anchor and chain.

**5. Chain Stopper:** A **secondary securing device** to relieve the windlass of static load when the ship is at anchor.

**6. Bitter End:** The inboard end of the chain, secured by a **shackle** to a **pad eye** on the hull's forward collision bulkhead.

**7. Chain Locker:** A **watertight compartment** below the forecastle for stowing the chain; the chain is paid out and hauled in from here.

**8. Spurling Pipe:** A **watertight vertical pipe** connecting the deck level to the chain locker; prevents water ingress.

**9. Devil's Claw / Lever:** A manual device used to secure the chain directly to the deck.

#### **(b)** A bulk carrier has a displacement of **80,000 tonnes**. Estimate the (i) anchor mass, (ii) chain cable diameter, and (iii) breaking load for a Q2 grade chain. Use a chain length of 14 shackles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\Delta = 80,000 \text{ t} = 80 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}$

**(i) Anchor Mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= 0.0075 \cdot \Delta = 0.0075 \cdot 80,000 \cdot 1000 \\
W_a &= 600,000 \text{ kg} = 600 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Chain Cable Diameter:**

For a 600 kg bower anchor, from **IRS tables (Q2 grade)**, the standard chain diameter is **$d = 56 \text{ mm}$**.

**(iii) Breaking Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{BL} &= 0.0254 \cdot d^2 \cdot (44 - 0.08 d) \\
F_{BL} &= 0.0254 \cdot (56)^2 \cdot (44 - 0.08 \cdot 56) \\
F_{BL} &= 0.0254 \cdot 3136 \cdot 39.52 \\
F_{BL} &= 3148.5 \text{ kN} \approx 3.15 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Chain Length Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L = 14 \cdot 27.5 = 385 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a)** With neat diagrams, classify the **different types of ship anchors** based on construction and application. Discuss the merits and demerits of **stockless** vs. **stocked** anchors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Anchors:**

| Basis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **By Construction** | Stocked and Stockless |
| **By Holding Power** | Ordinary, HHP, SHHP |
| **By Application** | Bower, Stream, Kedge, Spare |

**Stockless Anchor (Admiralty Pattern):**
- ✅ **Advantages:** Self-stowing in the hawse pipe; no external stock to handle; rapid deployment.
- ❌ **Disadvantages:** Lower $K_H$ (≈ 2.5–3.5); needs chain to hold the shank flat on the seabed; cannot hold well in rocky seabeds.

**Stocked Anchor:**
- ✅ **Advantages:** Higher $K_H$ (≈ 3.5–4.5) when freshly cast; works in rocky/rough seabeds; reliable in all soils.
- ❌ **Disadvantages:** Manual handling of stock; slower deployment; vulnerable stock damage; large deck space required.

> Hence, **stockless anchors** dominate modern merchant ship design due to **rapid handling and stowage efficiency**, while **stocked anchors** are now limited to small craft and naval ships.

#### **(b)** Two anchors of mass **500 kg each** are deployed. If the wind force on the hull is estimated at **900 kN** at the design wind speed, calculate the **minimum holding power required per anchor** and comment on whether an HHP anchor would suffice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total Environmental Force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{total} = 900 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Force per Anchor (assuming equal sharing)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{per\,anchor} = \frac{900}{2} = 450 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Required Holding Power per Anchor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{H,\,req} = 1.5 \cdot F_{per\,anchor} = 1.5 \cdot 450 = 675 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

(where 1.5 is the standard safety factor on the environmental load)

**Step 4: Compare with Anchor Performance**

- **Ordinary Stockless Anchor:** $F_H = W_a \cdot K_H = 500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3.0 = 14,715 \text{ N} = 14.7 \text{ kN}$ (in kN, $W_a$ in kN).

  *Re-casting in consistent units:* $W_a = 500 \text{ kg} = 4.905 \text{ kN}$

  Ordinary anchor: $F_H = 4.905 \cdot 3.0 = 14.7 \text{ kN}$ — **Insufficient**.

- **HHP Anchor:** $K_H = 6.0 \Rightarrow F_H = 4.905 \cdot 6.0 = 29.4 \text{ kN}$ — **Still Insufficient** for a single 500 kg anchor against 675 kN demand.

> **Conclusion:** The 500 kg anchor is **massively undersized** for the 900 kN environmental force. The required anchor mass for an HHP anchor is:
>
> $$W_a = \frac{F_{H,\,req}}{K_H} = \frac{675}{6.0} = 112.5 \text{ kN} \equiv 11,470 \text{ kg} \approx 11.5 \text{ tonnes per anchor}$$
>
> This indicates that for a vessel experiencing 900 kN wind, the ship is likely a very large vessel (e.g., VLCC, ULCC), and the anchor sizes in the example are illustrative only. **HHP anchors are necessary for adequate safety**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert displacement from tonnes to kg. The fraction (0.0075) is applied to **displacement in kg**, not tonnes.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using the wrong chain grade formula. Q2 grade is the most common; Q1 and Q3 have **different constants** in the breaking load formula.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **HHP** and **SHHP** holding factors (2× and 4× ordinary, respectively).
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to apply the **safety factor (1.5)** on environmental loads before computing required anchor holding power.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Mixing up **shackle** (27.5 m) with **fathom** (1.83 m) — examiners specifically test unit awareness.
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** Writing `|d|` (pipe character) in markdown/LaTeX tables. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in formal KTU scripts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Remember Bullets):**
> - **SOLAS requires 3 anchors** minimum (2 bower + 1 stream).
> - **Anchor weight** = 0.45 % – 1.0 % of ship displacement.
> - **Chain cable length** = 12 to 15 shackles (1 shackle = 27.5 m).
> - **Q2 grade chain breaking load** = $0.0254 \cdot d^2 \cdot (44 - 0.08 d)$ kN, with **d in mm**.
> - **Proof load = 0.625 × Breaking load** for Q2 grade.
> - **HHP** = 2× ordinary anchor holding power in soft mud.
> - **SHHP** = 4× ordinary anchor holding power in soft mud.
> - **Windlass** must lift **anchor + 75 m of chain** at **0.015 m/s** under half-load.
> - **Hawse pipe** is the shell-side guide; **Spurling pipe** is the watertight deck-to-locker pipe; **Chain locker** is the stowage compartment.
> - **Bitter end** is the inboard end of the chain, secured to the hull.
> - **Most common stockless anchor in merchant ships** = Admiralty pattern.
> - **Best HHP for soft mud** = Danforth, Hall.
> - **Best SHHP for general use** = AC-14, Stevpris.
> - **Mushroom anchor** is for **permanent moorings**, not for active ship anchoring.
> - **Kedge anchor** is for **warping and shifting berths** (lighter and easily deployable).
> - **Catena formula for catenary sag** = $h = \sqrt{(F \cdot L) / w}$ where F is horizontal force and w is weight/m.
> - **Environmental force on anchored ship** = $F_E = F_{wind} + F_{current} + F_{wave}$ (vectorially combined).
> - **Standard safety factor** on chain breaking load against environmental load = **≥ 1.5**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anchor Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Anchor Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **anchor chain** (also called **anchor cable**, **chain cable**, or **chain rode**) is the heavy-duty, flexible steel linkage assembly that physically and mechanically connects the **ship's anchoring system** (windlass, chain stopper, bitter end) to the **anchor** resting on the seabed. It transmits the **holding force** generated by the embedded anchor back to the vessel, providing the **mooring restraint** necessary to hold a ship against environmental loads (wind, current, waves) when at single-point moor or in restricted waters.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **ISO 1704** and **Classification Society Rules (IACS UR W26/W27)**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anchor Chain**: A standardized, studded or studless, calibrated steel chain of graded tensile strength (Grade U1, U2, U3 or U1S, U2S, U3S), manufactured to a specific nominal bar diameter, link pitch, and link geometry, used to transmit the breaking-holding capacity of the anchor to the vessel's hull-mounted securing arrangements.

### Key Engineering Constants & Standards

- **Steel density for chain**: **$\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- **Standard shackle length**: **$L_{shackle} = 12.5 \text{ fathoms} = 22.86 \text{ m}$**
- **Standard pitch of link**: **$P = 6 \times d_{bar}$** (where $d_{bar}$ is the nominal bar diameter)
- **Standard outside link length**: **$L_{link} = 3.4 \times d_{bar}$** (approx.)
- **Modulus of Elasticity of chain steel**: **$E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **camping on a windy hilltop** with a heavy-duty SUV. To keep the SUV from rolling away in a storm, you throw a **buried pickaxe into the soil** (this is your **anchor** — it grips the ground). But the pickaxe on its own cannot stop the vehicle; you need a **sturdy, heavy chain** linking the pickaxe to the SUV's tow hook.

Now translate this to a ship:

| Camping Analogy | Shipboard Equivalent |
|-----------------|----------------------|
| Buried pickaxe | **Anchor** fluked into seabed |
| Tow chain | **Anchor chain cable** |
| Tow hook on SUV | **Anchor shackle** at deck connection |
| SUV pulling against wind | **Ship** dragged by wind/current loads |
| Storage compartment for chain | **Chain locker** below deck |

The chain must be **heavy** (so its catenary weight helps hold the ship, not just the anchor's holding power), **flexible** (to wrap around a wildcat drum and store in coils), **strong** (to transmit peak storm loads), and **fatigue-resistant** (to survive thousands of anchor drops).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a chain and not a wire rope?** Chain has three unique advantages: (1) **self-weight catenary** provides holding even if anchor drags slightly, (2) **no stretch** under sudden loads (modulus is high, creep minimal), and (3) **high abrasion resistance** when dragged across rocky seabeds.

## 1.3 Function and Role in the Mooring System

The anchor chain performs **five critical engineering functions**:

1. **Load Transmission** — Carries the tensile load from the ship to the anchor.
2. **Catenary Buffer** — Its submerged self-weight creates a vertical reaction component, reducing the horizontal pull on the anchor (improving holding by ~30–50%).
3. **Shock Absorption** — The chain's elastic elongation and catenary geometry absorb dynamic snap loads from wave action.
4. **Stability** — A heavy chain resists horizontal drift better than a taut light line.
5. **Length Adjustment** — Provides variable scope (ratio of paid-out chain to water depth) to suit seabed and weather conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of Anchor Chain

A chain is **not one continuous piece** — it is an assembly of discrete components:

### 2.1.1 The Common Link (Standard Link)

The most basic element. Two interlocking half-rings are forge-welded (open die welding) into a closed oval. Each common link has:
- **Bar diameter** $d$ (the nominal diameter, e.g., 50 mm, 76 mm)
- **Pitch** $P = 6d$ (distance from center to center of adjacent links)
- **Outside length** $L_o = 3.4d$ (approx.)
- **Inside width** $w_i = 2.6d$ (approx.)

### 2.1.2 The Stud

A **crossbar** welded across the inside of the link. The stud:
- Prevents the link from kinking or collapsing under load.
- Increases the chain's effective cross-section.
- Reduces wear between interlocked links.
- Adds **~10–15%** to the weight per meter compared to studless chain.

### 2.1.3 End Links, Joining Shackles & Common Shackles

- **End Link**: A slightly enlarged link at the termination of each chain length.
- **Joining Shackle (D-Shackle)**: A forged bow-and-pin connector joining two lengths ("shots") of chain. Typically every **12.5 fathoms (22.86 m)** — i.e., **one shackle length**.
- **Common Shackle**: A general-purpose shackle within a length.
- **Anchor Shackle**: Special enlarged shackle at the anchor end.
- **Detachable Link**: Used as an alternative to a shackle for joining.

> [!NOTE]
> A **"shot of chain"** is the length between two joining shackles = **one shackle length = 22.86 m**. Modern builders often use detachable links, but shackles are still the standard reference for counting chain length.

### 2.1.4 Swivel

Inserted in the chain near the anchor end to prevent twisting from being transmitted up the cable (e.g., when the ship swings around the anchor). Typically located **1–2 shackles** from the anchor.

## 2.2 Types of Anchor Chain

| Type | Description | Strength Grade | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------------|----------|
| **Studded Chain** | Has welded cross-bar studs inside each link | U1, U2, U3 | Most common for permanent ship fittings |
| **Studless Chain** | No studs; higher-grade steel | U1S, U2S, U3S | Special applications, higher BL per kg |

**Grade nomenclature (IACS / ISO 1704):**

| Grade | Tensile Class | Material Yield Strength | Typical Use |
|-------|---------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **U1 / U1S** | Ordinary Strength | ~400 MPa | Small craft, light duty |
| **U2 / U2S** | Higher Tensile | ~490 MPa | Standard merchant ships |
| **U3 / U3S** | Extra High Tensile | ~690 MPa | Large tankers, VLCCs, naval |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following **standard ship-design empirical formulas** are universally used in ship design offices and are tested in KTU exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below assume the **nominal bar diameter $d$ is in millimeters (mm)** and outputs are in **SI units** (kN, kg, m).

### 2.3.1 Weight Formulas

| Quantity | Studded Chain | Studless Chain |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| Weight per meter, $w$ | $w = 0.0218 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg/m}$ | $w = 0.0193 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg/m}$ |
| Weight per shackle, $W_{sh}$ | $W_{sh} = 0.4985 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg}$ | $W_{sh} = 0.4413 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg}$ |
| Weight per fathom, $w_f$ | $w_f = 0.0398 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg/fm}$ | $w_f = 0.0353 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg/fm}$ |

(For a shackle of 22.86 m, $W_{sh} = w \times 22.86 = 0.0218 d^2 \times 22.86 \approx 0.4985 d^2$ kg)

### 2.3.2 Strength Formulas

**Breaking Load** $BL$ in kN:

| Chain Grade | Studded Factor $K$ | Studless Factor $K$ |
|-------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| U1 / U1S | $K = 0.0180$ | $K = 0.0150$ |
| U2 / U2S | $K = 0.0240$ | $K = 0.0220$ |
| U3 / U3S | $K = 0.0290$ | $K = 0.0270$ |

$$BL = K \cdot d^2 \cdot (44 - 0.08 \, d) \;\; \text{kN}$$

**Proof Load** $PL$ (load applied during factory testing):

$$PL = 0.70 \times BL$$

**Working Load** $WL$ (in service, with safety factor ~1.5):

$$WL = \frac{BL}{SF} = \frac{BL}{1.5}$$

### 2.3.3 Length Formulas

**Number of shackles per anchor** (typical):

| Ship Type | Number of Shackles |
|-----------|--------------------|
| Small craft (LOA < 100 m) | $N = 6$ |
| Medium merchant (LOA 100–180 m) | $N = 8$ |
| Large merchant / tanker (LOA > 180 m) | $N = 10$ to $14$ |

**Total chain length:**

$$L_{total} = N \times 22.86 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Total chain weight (one anchor):**

$$W_{chain} = N \times W_{sh} = N \times 0.4985 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg (studded)}$$

### 2.3.4 Catenary & Holding Force

The **catenary sag** of the chain reduces the horizontal pull on the anchor. If $T_0$ is the horizontal tension and $w_c$ is the chain's submerged weight per unit length:

$$T_{max} = T_0 + w_c \cdot s$$

where $s$ is the length of chain hanging below the horizontal at the hawse pipe.

The **vertical component** $V$ of tension at the anchor:

$$V = w_c \cdot L_{catenary}$$

**Resultant tension** at the anchor (combined horizontal + vertical):

$$R = \sqrt{T_0^2 + V^2}$$

This resultant is what the anchor must resist — a portion is borne by the **chain's own catenary weight**, reducing the required anchor holding force.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

These formulas are **directly used by Classification Society surveyors** during new-build survey, during dry-dock chain range inspection, and during the **annual chain cable testing** where each shot is subjected to the proof load. They also underpin the **mooring analysis software** (e.g., OPTIMOOR, MIMOSA, AquaPLUS) used to compute static and dynamic mooring loads for floating installations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Weight Per Meter of Studded Chain

We begin from the **fundamental principle**: chain is a series of identical links of bar diameter $d$ and mean circumference $C_{mean}$.

### Step 1 — Bar Cross-Sectional Area

The chain link is forged from a circular bar of diameter $d$ (mm). The cross-sectional area of the bar is:

$$A_{bar} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

### Step 2 — Mass Per Unit Length of One Link

For a link of mean diameter $D_m$ (typically $D_m \approx 4d$ to $5d$), the mean circumference of the bar forming one link is:

$$C_{mean} = \pi \cdot D_m = \pi \cdot (4.5 \, d) = 14.137 \, d \;\; \text{mm}$$

(using mean ratio $D_m/d \approx 4.5$ for standard studded links).

The volume of one link (excluding the stud, which adds ~10%):

$$V_{link} = A_{bar} \times C_{mean} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \times 14.137 \, d = 11.10 \, d^3 \;\; \text{mm}^3$$

### Step 3 — Mass Per Unit Pitch

The chain advances by one pitch $P = 6d$ per link. The mass per unit length is the mass per pitch divided by the pitch:

$$m' = \frac{\rho \cdot V_{link}}{P} = \frac{7850 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/mm}^3 \times 11.10 \, d^3}{6d} = 0.01452 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg/m}$$

### Step 4 — Add Stud Contribution (≈ 10–15%)

The welded stud adds approximately **50% more material per pitch** to a single link:

$$m'_{total} = m'_{link} \times (1 + 0.50) = 0.01452 \times 1.50 \, d^2 = 0.0218 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg/m}$$

This **matches the empirical formula** used in ship design:

$$\boxed{w_{studded} = 0.0218 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg/m, with } d \text{ in mm}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Breaking Load of Studded Chain (Grade U2)

The empirical formula $BL = K \cdot d^2 \cdot (44 - 0.08d)$ is calibrated against destructive tensile tests on full-scale chain links. It can be derived from first principles with empirical constants:

### Step 1 — Material Ultimate Tensile Stress

For Grade U2 chain steel, the ultimate tensile strength is:

$$\sigma_u \approx 490 \text{ MPa} = 490 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

### Step 2 — Effective Stress-Carrying Area

A studded chain link carries load through **three bar cross-sections in parallel** (two side bars + one stud acting as a transverse stiffener). The effective area is:

$$A_{eff} = 2.4 \times A_{bar} = 2.4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = 1.885 \, d^2 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Theoretical Breaking Force

$$F_{th} = \sigma_u \times A_{eff} = 490 \times 1.885 \, d^2 = 924 \, d^2 \;\; \text{N} = 0.924 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kN}$$

### Step 4 — Empirical Correction Factor (Stress Concentration, Link Curvature)

The curved geometry of the link introduces **stress concentration** that reduces the effective load capacity. The reduction factor is empirically:

$$\eta = (44 - 0.08 \, d) / 44$$

The factor $(44 - 0.08d)$ decreases linearly from $44$ at $d=0$ mm to roughly $35$ at $d=110$ mm, reflecting the loss of strength with increasing curvature (bigger links bend more sharply).

### Step 5 — Final Empirical Formula

$$BL = K \cdot d^2 \cdot (44 - 0.08 \, d)$$

where for Grade U2 studded chain, $K = 0.024$ kN/mm² gives:

$$BL_{U2,studded} = 0.024 \, d^2 (44 - 0.08 \, d) \;\; \text{kN, with } d \text{ in mm}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem**: A bulk carrier is to be fitted with **U2 grade studded anchor chain** of nominal bar diameter **$d = 76 \text{ mm}$**. Calculate:
> (a) Weight per meter
> (b) Weight per shackle (22.86 m)
> (c) Breaking Load
> (d) Proof Load
> (e) Working Load (SF = 1.5)
> (f) Total chain weight for **10 shackles per anchor**

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**(a) Weight per meter:**

$$w = 0.0218 \, d^2 = 0.0218 \times 76^2 = 0.0218 \times 5776 = 125.92 \;\; \text{kg/m}$$

**(b) Weight per shackle:**

$$W_{sh} = 0.4985 \, d^2 = 0.4985 \times 5776 = 2879.3 \;\; \text{kg} \approx 2879 \text{ kg} \approx 2.88 \text{ t}$$

**(c) Breaking Load:**

$$BL = 0.024 \times d^2 \times (44 - 0.08 \, d)$$

$$BL = 0.024 \times 5776 \times (44 - 0.08 \times 76)$$

$$BL = 0.024 \times 5776 \times (44 - 6.08)$$

$$BL = 0.024 \times 5776 \times 37.92$$

$$BL = 138.624 \times 37.92 = 5256.7 \;\; \text{kN}$$

**(d) Proof Load (70% of BL):**

$$PL = 0.70 \times BL = 0.70 \times 5256.7 = 3679.7 \;\; \text{kN}$$

**(e) Working Load (SF = 1.5):**

$$WL = \frac{BL}{1.5} = \frac{5256.7}{1.5} = 3504.5 \;\; \text{kN}$$

**(f) Total weight for 10 shackles per anchor:**

$$W_{total} = N \times W_{sh} = 10 \times 2879.3 = 28793 \;\; \text{kg} \approx 28.79 \text{ t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check**: For comparison, a Panamax bulk carrier typically carries ~**8–10 shackles of 76 mm chain** per anchor, weighing **23–29 tonnes per anchor**. The total chain carried (3 anchors with 10/10/8 shackles) is roughly **80–85 tonnes** — a substantial structural load on the bow!

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Chain Selection Logic

A ship designer chooses chain diameter using **Classification Society Rules** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS). The empirical approach ties chain diameter to **Equipment Number (EN)**:

$$EN = \Delta^{2/3} + 2 \cdot h \cdot B + 0.1 \cdot A$$

where $\Delta$ = displacement (tonnes), $h$ = effective hull height, $B$ = breadth, $A$ = projected windage area.

The **chain diameter** is then read from a table (Lloyd's) correlating EN to $d$. Typical results:

| Ship Type | Displacement (t) | Equipment Number | Chain $d$ (mm) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| General cargo, 5000 DWT | ~6500 | ~370 | 50 |
| Bulk carrier, 35 000 DWT | ~45 000 | ~1300 | 62–68 |
| VLCC, 300 000 DWT | ~350 000 | ~4500 | 84–96 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **proof load test** is mandatory at the manufacturer's works and at every 5-year survey. The chain is loaded to $PL$ and inspected for cracks or permanent elongation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Anchor Chain System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the anchor chain system on a typical merchant vessel, from the seabed anchor up through the deck machinery and into the storage locker.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    seabedA[ANCHOR ON SEABED]
    anchorShackleA[ANCHOR SHACKLE]
    swivelA[SWIVEL]
    endShotA[END SHOT 1 SHACKLE]
    mainShotA[MAIN CHAIN CABLE N SHACKLES]
    bitterEndA[BITTER END SEIZED TO HULL]
    lockerA[CHAIN LOCKER STORAGE BELOW DECK]
    hawseA[HAWSE PIPE THROUGH HULL]
    stopperA[CHAIN STOPPER AND COMPRESSOR]
    wildcatA[WINDLASS WILDCAT DRUM]
    brakeA[CHAIN CABLE LIFTER AND BRAKE]
    deckA[SHIP FOREDECK ABOVE]

    seabedA --> anchorShackleA
    anchorShackleA --> swivelA
    swivelA --> endShotA
    endShotA --> mainShotA
    mainShotA --> hawseA
    hawseA --> stopperA
    stopperA --> wildcatA
    wildcatA --> brakeA
    brakeA --> deckA

    mainShotA -.stows into.-> lockerA
    lockerA -. bitter end secured.-> bitterEndA
```

## 4.2 Component-by-Component Functional Description

| Node | Component | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **ANCHOR ON SEABED** | Stockless or stocked anchor | Generates holding force in seabed soil |
| **ANCHOR SHACKLE** | Oversized bow-and-pin shackle | Connects anchor shank to chain end link |
| **SWIVEL** | Rotating joint | Prevents torque transmission during ship swing |
| **END SHOT** | First 22.86 m of chain | Provides clearance and shock absorption near anchor |
| **MAIN CHAIN CABLE** | $N$ shackles of $U$-grade chain | Carries load from anchor to vessel |
| **HAWSE PIPE** | Steel pipe through bow plating | Guides chain from external to internal |
| **CHAIN STOPPER** | Pelican hook / compressor | Holds chain when windlass is disengaged |
| **WINDLASS WILDCAT** | Sprocketed drum | Heaves chain and anchor |
| **CHAIN CABLE LIFTER** | Gypsy wheel | Applies braking tension during paying out |
| **CHAIN LOCKER** | Sub-deck storage compartment | Stows the chain in self-coiled piles |
| **BITTER END** | Last link, secured inside hull | Emergency release point (slip shackle) |

## 4.3 Link Geometry Schematic (ASCII Block Diagram)

```
         STUDDED CHAIN LINK
         ==================

         +-------o   <- end link pin hole
         |       |
         |       |
         |   +---+---+   <- bar diameter d
         |   |       |
         |   | STUD  |   <- cross-bar stud
         |   |       |
         |   +---+---+
         |       |
         |       |
         +-------o

   Pitch P = 6d
   Bar diameter d
   Inside width ~ 2.6d
   Outside length ~ 3.4d
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **stud** is what differentiates studded from studless chain. Studless chain is used when extremely high breaking load per unit weight is required (e.g., FPSO mooring chains at very deep water), but it is more prone to kinking during handling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model]`
**Q: Define "shackle of chain cable" and state its length. Why is the chain cable measured in shackles rather than metres?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **shackle of chain cable** is the standard unit of length used in shipbuilding and seamanship to measure anchor chain. By convention (based on the traditional British fathom system still adopted by IACS):

$$\text{1 shackle} = 12.5 \text{ fathoms} = 12.5 \times 1.8288 \text{ m} = 22.86 \text{ m} \approx 90 \text{ ft}$$

It is **measured in shackles rather than metres** because the anchor chain is manufactured, supplied, joined and certified in standard 22.86 m lengths — each terminating in a **joining shackle**. This unit-of-account standardizes procurement, replacement surveys and chain certificate records across all classification societies (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV, IRS).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model]`
**Q: Differentiate between studded and studless anchor chain. State one typical application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Studded Chain | Studless Chain |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| **Stud** | Cross-bar welded inside every link | No stud |
| **Weight per meter** | Higher ($\approx 0.0218 \, d^2$) | Lower ($\approx 0.0193 \, d^2$) |
| **Strength/weight ratio** | Lower | Higher |
| **Kink resistance** | Excellent (stud prevents deformation) | Lower |
| **Application** | Standard ship fittings, bow chain | FPSO mooring in deep water, high-strength needs |

**Typical applications:**
- **Studded chain**: Permanent anchor chain for merchant ships, naval vessels, and offshore support vessels.
- **Studless chain**: Permanent mooring systems of offshore platforms (FPSOs, semi-submersibles) in deep water, where weight-saving per unit strength is critical.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model]` (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** A bulk carrier is to be fitted with **U2 grade studded anchor chain** of nominal bar diameter **$d = 62 \text{ mm}$**. Calculate:
  (i) Weight per meter
  (ii) Weight per shackle
  (iii) Breaking Load (BL)
  (iv) Proof Load (PL)
  (v) Working Load (WL) using safety factor **SF = 1.5**

**(b)** If the ship carries **8 shackles of chain per anchor** and **3 anchors** (two bower anchors + one spare/kedge at bow, or one stream anchor), estimate the **total chain weight** carried on board.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):** Given $d = 62 \text{ mm}$, Grade U2 studded chain.

**(i) Weight per meter:**

$$w = 0.0218 \, d^2 = 0.0218 \times (62)^2 = 0.0218 \times 3844 = 83.80 \;\; \text{kg/m}$$

**(ii) Weight per shackle:**

$$W_{sh} = 0.4985 \, d^2 = 0.4985 \times 3844 = 1916.23 \;\; \text{kg} \approx 1.92 \text{ t}$$

**(iii) Breaking Load:**

$$BL = 0.024 \times d^2 \times (44 - 0.08 \, d)$$

$$BL = 0.024 \times 3844 \times (44 - 0.08 \times 62)$$

$$BL = 0.024 \times 3844 \times (44 - 4.96)$$

$$BL = 0.024 \times 3844 \times 39.04$$

$$BL = 92.256 \times 39.04 = 3602.3 \;\; \text{kN}$$

**(iv) Proof Load (70% of BL):**

$$PL = 0.70 \times 3602.3 = 2521.6 \;\; \text{kN}$$

**(v) Working Load (SF = 1.5):**

$$WL = \frac{BL}{1.5} = \frac{3602.3}{1.5} = 2401.5 \;\; \text{kN}$$

**Part (b):** For 8 shackles per anchor and 3 anchors:

$$W_{anchor} = 8 \times W_{sh} = 8 \times 1916.23 = 15329.8 \;\; \text{kg} \approx 15.33 \text{ t}$$

$$W_{total} = 3 \times W_{anchor} = 3 \times 15329.8 = 45989.4 \;\; \text{kg} \approx 46.0 \text{ t}$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Weight per meter | **83.80 kg/m** |
| Weight per shackle | **1916.23 kg (1.92 t)** |
| Breaking Load | **3602.3 kN** |
| Proof Load | **2521.6 kN** |
| Working Load | **2401.5 kN** |
| Total chain on board | **~46.0 t** |

---

### Question B (Alternative to Question A) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model]` (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Explain the **five major functional components** of an anchor chain assembly between the windlass and the anchor. Sketch a labelled block diagram showing their arrangement.

**(b)** A VLCC is to be fitted with **U3 grade studless anchor chain** of nominal diameter **$d = 84 \text{ mm}$**. Calculate:
  (i) Weight per meter
  (ii) Breaking Load
  (iii) The minimum number of shackles required if the chain must weigh **at least 25 tonnes per anchor**.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Five Major Functional Components** (7 marks)

1. **Anchor Shackle** (oversized bow shackle): Connects the chain end link to the **anchor shank** ring. Must be stronger than the chain itself.

2. **Swivel**: A rotating joint installed typically one or two shackles from the anchor. Allows the chain to twist freely without transmitting torque to the windlass, especially when the ship swings about the anchor.

3. **End Shot (First Shackle)**: The first 22.86 m of chain adjacent to the anchor. Provides shock absorption during anchor impacts and clearance from the hull.

4. **Joining Shackles / Detachable Links**: Connect the discrete **shots** (22.86 m sections) of chain. Each joining point allows individual shot replacement during survey.

5. **Chain Cable (Main Length)**: The bulk of $N$ shackles forming the working length of the cable, stowed in the chain locker and paid out over the wildcat.

**Labelled Block Diagram** (already covered in SECTION 4.1 above).

**Part (b):** Given $d = 84 \text{ mm}$, U3 studless chain.

**(i) Weight per meter (studless):**

$$w = 0.0193 \, d^2 = 0.0193 \times (84)^2 = 0.0193 \times 7056 = 136.18 \;\; \text{kg/m}$$

**Weight per shackle:**

$$W_{sh} = 0.4413 \, d^2 = 0.4413 \times 7056 = 3113.81 \;\; \text{kg} \approx 3.11 \text{ t}$$

**(ii) Breaking Load (U3 studless, $K = 0.027$):**

$$BL = 0.027 \times d^2 \times (44 - 0.08 \, d)$$

$$BL = 0.027 \times 7056 \times (44 - 0.08 \times 84)$$

$$BL = 0.027 \times 7056 \times (44 - 6.72)$$

$$BL = 0.027 \times 7056 \times 37.28$$

$$BL = 190.512 \times 37.28 = 7102.3 \;\; \text{kN}$$

**(iii) Minimum number of shackles for 25 t per anchor:**

$$N_{min} = \frac{W_{required}}{W_{sh}} = \frac{25000 \text{ kg}}{3113.81 \text{ kg/shackle}} = 8.03$$

Since we need **at least 25 tonnes**, we round **up** to the next integer:

$$\boxed{N_{min} = 9 \text{ shackles per anchor}}$$

This gives actual weight:
$$W = 9 \times 3113.81 = 28024.3 \text{ kg} \approx 28.0 \text{ t} \;\; (\text{satisfies } \geq 25 \text{ t})$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in formulas**: The formula $w = 0.0218 \, d^2$ requires **$d$ in mm**. Substituting $d$ in metres or cm gives an answer off by factors of 1000 or 10,000 — an **automatic 3–5 mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
>
> 2. **Mixing studded and studless constants**: Do **not** use $w = 0.0218 \, d^2$ for studless chain (correct is $0.0193 \, d^2$). Similarly, do not mix grade factors (U1 = 0.018, U2 = 0.024, U3 = 0.029 for studded).
>
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the $(44 - 0.08d)$ bracket**: A common error is to write $BL = 0.024 \times d^2$ and skip the second factor, which inflates the result by ~10–15%.
>
> 4. **Confusing Proof Load with Working Load**: $PL$ is the factory test load (70% of BL). $WL$ uses a **safety factor of 1.5** on the BL. They are **different** — do not equate them.
>
> 5. **Not showing the chain is in kN, not tonnes**: Always label the BL/PL units clearly as **kN** (or convert explicitly to tonnes if required: $1 \text{ t} \approx 9.81 \text{ kN}$).
>
> 6. **Rounding errors in the bracket term**: Always evaluate $(44 - 0.08d)$ carefully; one decimal place error propagates into the final answer.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the bitter end**: When asked for "components", missing the bitter end and chain locker costs 1–2 marks — these are essential KTU syllabus elements.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Anchor Chain**

- ✅ **Anchor chain** transmits the holding force from the embedded anchor to the ship.
- ✅ **Studded chain** has cross-bar studs inside each link; **studless** does not.
- ✅ **Grade U2** (Higher Tensile) is the standard merchant-ship grade; **U3** is for VLCCs and naval.
- ✅ **Standard shackle length** = 12.5 fathoms = **22.86 m** (use this constant religiously).
- ✅ **Weight formula** (studded): $w = 0.0218 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg/m}$, with $d$ in **mm**.
- ✅ **Weight formula** (studless): $w = 0.0193 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg/m}$.
- ✅ **Breaking Load** (studded U2): $BL = 0.024 \, d^2 (44 - 0.08d) \;\; \text{kN}$.
- ✅ **Proof Load** $PL = 0.70 \times BL$.
- ✅ **Working Load** $WL = BL / 1.5$.
- ✅ **Pitch of link** $P = 6d$; outside length $\approx 3.4d$; inside width $\approx 2.6d$.
- ✅ **Chain size** is selected via Classification Society's **Equipment Number** $EN = \Delta^{2/3} + 2hB + 0.1A$.
- ✅ **Chain components**: anchor shackle → swivel → end shot → joining shackles → main chain → bitter end.
- ✅ **Chain stopper / compressor** holds the chain when the windlass is disengaged.
- ✅ **Bitter end** is the inboard end, secured to the hull inside the chain locker (often with a **slip shackle** for emergency release).
- ✅ **Swivel** is placed ~1–2 shackles from the anchor to prevent torque transmission.
- ✅ **Chain locker** is the sub-deck stowage compartment; chain piles itself by self-coiling.
- ✅ **Total chain weight** for a ship with 3 anchors at $N$ shackles each: $W_{total} = 3N \times 0.4985 \, d^2 \;\; \text{kg (studded)}$.
- ✅ **Standard sizes**: 22, 25, 28, 31, 34, 37, 40, 43, 46, 49, 52, 56, 60, 64, 68, 73, 78, 84, 90, 96, 102, 108, 114, 120, 126 mm.
- ✅ **Catenary effect**: Submerged chain weight creates vertical reaction → reduces horizontal pull on the anchor.
- ✅ **Dynamic shock load** during heavy weather can reach **1.5× to 2× the static load** — this is why the 1.5 SF is used.
- ✅ **Inspection interval**: Chain is **range-inspected** every 5 years and **proof-load tested** during special surveys (Class rules).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anchor Windlass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Anchor Windlass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anchor Windlass** is a self-contained deck machinery unit, usually electrically or hydraulically driven, installed on the **forecastle deck** of a ship, used for **hoisting (weighing) and letting-go (dropping) the bower anchors together with their associated anchor chain cables**, and additionally for **warping, mooring and hose-handling operations** at the forward station.

According to **SOLAS Reg. II-1/3-4** and **Classification Society Rules (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS)**, every ship engaged in international voyages must be fitted with power-operated anchor windlasses capable of performing the anchor-handling function under all foreseeable service conditions, including the **"one anchor up and one anchor down"** (also called *abnormal operation*) condition.

The windlass integrates three functional machines into one compact assembly:

1. **Cable Lifter / Wildcat (Gypsy)** – A sprocket wheel with pockets that mesh with the chain links and lift the chain vertically.
2. **Warping Drum (or Whelps Drum)** – A smooth or whelped drum used for handling mooring ropes, hawsers and wire ropes during berthing/underthing.
3. **Chain Stopper & Riding Pawl** – A safety device that takes over the chain load once the anchor is hove up to the hawse pipe, preventing the anchor from running back into the sea.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a giant mechanical "fishing reel" bolted to the ship's bow.** Imagine trying to lift a small car (the anchor, weighing **5–15 tonnes**) from the seabed using a toothed wheel that grips chain links one-by-one like a bicycle sprocket gripping a chain — that's the **wildcat**. Now add a second smooth wheel next to it for pulling ropes during docking — that's the **warping drum**. Finally, imagine a "claw" that locks the chain in place once the anchor is stowed, so the motor doesn't have to constantly hold the load — that's the **chain stopper + riding pawl**.

In simple words:
- **Wildcat** = the toothed wheel that grips chain.
- **Warping Drum** = smooth drum for ropes.
- **Brake** = keeps the load from running back if power fails.
- **Motor/Prime Mover** = the engine that turns everything.

Without the windlass, the crew would have to manually pull up the anchor using capstans and sheer legs — physically impossible for a modern vessel of even modest size.

## 1.3 Governing Standards & Design Reference

| Parameter | Standard / Reference |
|---|---|
| Equipment Number (EN) | **ISO 3828 / IACS S21** |
| Anchor Mass Schedule | **IACS UR 21 / SOLAS Reg. II-1/3** |
| Brake Holding Load (BHL) | **IRS Pt.3 Ch.4 / LR Pt.4 Ch.3** |
| Trial Pull & Overspeed Test | **IACS UR 21, Section 4** |
| Minimum Hoisting Speed | **9 m/min at the rated load (CL. 9 m/min)** |
| Normal Working Load (NWL) | **~46 % of Breaking Load of chain** |
| Proof Load (PL) | **~70 % of Breaking Load of chain** |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Key Design Numbers (KTU Board-Favourite)

- **Density of steel chain in air:** $\rho_{chain} \approx 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- **Specific weight of seawater:** $\gamma_{sw} = 10.05 \text{ kN/m}^{3}$
- **Submerged factor for chain (typical empirical value):** $k_{sub} = 0.87$ (i.e., chain weighs **87 %** of its in-air weight when fully submerged).
- **Minimum hoisting speed (regulatory):** **9 m/min** at normal working load.
- **Trial pull test speed:** **3 m/min** (overload condition with proof load).
- **Brake holding:** typically **40 % of breaking load** or **60 % of proof load**, whichever is greater.
- **Mechanical efficiency** of standard electric windlass gear train: $\eta = 0.65$ to $0.75$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Anatomy of an Anchor Windlass

The windlass performs three distinct tasks:

**(a) Letting Go (Dropping the Anchor):**
- Brake released, anchor + chain free-fall under gravity.
- Brake controls the runaway speed (pay-out rate ≈ 60–90 m/min).
- Wildcat rotates freely, chain feeds out to seabed.

**(b) Weighing Anchor (Hoisting):**
- Motor drives the wildcat, which engages chain links and hauls the chain vertically up the hawse pipe.
- Chain links lock into the *wildcat pockets* (commutator-like pockets machined to chain link dimensions).
- Speed ≈ **9 m/min** at normal working load.

**(c) Holding / Securing the Anchor:**
- Once anchor is **a-turn and a-half** above the sea bed and firmly seated in the hawse pipe, **chain stopper** takes the load.
- **Riding pawl** drops into the chain link to prevent backward motion.
- Brake applied — motor de-energised.

## 2.2 Types of Anchor Windlasses

| Type | Drive Axis | Typical Use | Merits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vertical Shaft Windlass** | Vertical (motor on deck above wildcat) | Merchant ships, tankers, bulk carriers | Compact footprint; wildcat at deck level |
| **Horizontal Shaft Windlass** | Horizontal (motor inboard) | Naval combatants, smaller vessels | Easy access for maintenance; multiple cable lifters |
| **Warping Endlass (combined)** | Either | Multipurpose forecastle | Wildcat + drum on same shaft |
| **Hydraulic Windlass** | Hydraulic motor drive | Modern offshore vessels | Stepless speed control; high torque density |

## 2.3 Design Loads — The "Why" Behind the Numbers

The total load that the windlass must lift consists of three components:

$$
W_{total} = W_{anchor} + W_{chain,\,sub} + W_{friction}
$$

Where each term is non-trivial:

1. **$W_{anchor}$** — Mass of one bower anchor at full seabed embedment. Determined from **IACS S21 Equipment Number** schedule.
2. **$W_{chain,\,sub}$** — Weight of the entire length of chain hanging overboard, **subtracting buoyancy**:
   > The chain is in seawater — Archimedes' principle reduces effective weight by approximately **13 %** (specific gravity of steel ≈ 7.85 vs. seawater ≈ 1.025).
3. **$W_{friction}$** — Friction in hawse pipe, chain pipe, wildcat pocket engagement, and pipe bend. Empirical: ~**10–15 %** of static load.

## 2.4 Brake Holding Capacity — Regulatory Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 40 %?** The brake must hold the load if power fails *mid-hoist*. Class rules dictate the brake shall restrain **40 % of the chain breaking load** as the absolute minimum. This is because the shock load during riding at anchor can transiently spike to roughly 60–70 % of breaking load, and the brake must never *slip* during such events but only during *intentional* letting-go.

Brake holding capacity:
$$
F_{brake} = 0.40 \times L_{BL}
$$

where $L_{BL}$ is the manufacturer's **breaking load** of the chain cable (kN).

For **proof load** testing:
$$
F_{brake} \geq 0.60 \times L_{PL} \quad \text{where } L_{PL} = 0.70 \times L_{BL}
$$

## 2.5 Speed, Power & Motor Sizing Logic

Power required at the wildcat (output shaft):
$$
P_{out} = \frac{W_{total} \times v}{60} \quad [\text{Watts, if } W \text{ in N, } v \text{ in m/min}]
$$

Motor input power (allowing for mechanical efficiency):
$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{mech}}
$$

For **overnight test / trial pull** (proof load at slower speed):
$$
P_{trial} = \frac{1.5 \times W_{NWL} \times 3}{60 \times \eta} \quad (\text{speed reduced to 3 m/min})
$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** that the KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examiner can credibly ask for in Module-2 Anchor Windlass problems. Memorise these — they are the **only** equations needed for a 14-mark derivation question.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equipment Number | $EN = \Delta^{2/3} + 2Bh + 0.1A$ | dimensionless | Used to look up anchor mass |
| 2 | Submerged chain weight | $W_{ch} = 0.87 \times w_c \times L$ | kgf | $w_c$ = chain mass per metre in air |
| 3 | Total hoisting load | $W = W_a + 0.87 w_c L$ | kgf | Ignoring friction (simplified) |
| 4 | Brake Holding Load | $F_B = 0.40 \times BL$ | kN | Class regulation minimum |
| 5 | Hoisting Speed (rated) | $v = 9 \text{ m/min}$ | m/min | At Normal Working Load |
| 6 | Trial-pull Speed | $v_t = 3 \text{ m/min}$ | m/min | At 1.5 × NWL |
| 7 | Output Power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{60}$ | W (or HP if $/75$) | $W$ in N, $v$ in m/min |
| 8 | Motor Power | $P_m = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{60 \cdot \eta}$ | W | $\eta \approx 0.65 - 0.75$ |
| 9 | Wildcat torque | $T = \dfrac{W \cdot D_{wc}}{2 \cdot i \cdot \eta}$ | N·m | $i$ = gear ratio |
| 10 | Chain link pitch | $p = 6 \times d_{chain}$ | mm | Empirical (stud link) |
| 11 | Chain mass per metre | $w_c = 0.0218 \, d^2$ | kg/m | Empirical (stud link chain) |
| 12 | Proof Load | $PL = 0.70 \times BL$ | kN | Class standard |

> **Note on Table Convention:** The "$\vert$" symbol is intentionally avoided in table cells above to comply with the KTU-Premier markdown rendering safeguard — use plain expressions instead.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Maritime Safety:** Without a properly designed windlass, an emergency *let-go* of the anchor during typhoon/cyclone could fail → ship drifts aground → pollution, loss of life.
- **Statutory Compliance:** Failure to meet brake-holding / 9 m/min speed requirements leads to **detention by Port State Control**.
- **Commercial Throughput:** Faster anchor handling reduces port-turnaround time (an anchor operation can take 30–45 minutes).
- **Real-world Failures:** Famous cases like *MV Rena* (2011), *MV Wakashio* (2020) highlight what happens when anchoring systems fail.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is the **analytical backbone** of the 14-mark question. Three full derivations are shown — each at the level expected of a KTU ESE answer script.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Total Hoisting Load on the Wildcat (Standard Board Problem)

**Given:**
- Anchor mass: $W_a = 9000 \text{ kgf}$
- Chain diameter: $d = 70 \text{ mm}$ (U2 stud-link)
- Length of chain outboard: $L = 200 \text{ m}$
- Specific gravity of steel: $7.85$

**Step 1 — Chain mass per metre (empirical, KTU reference):**
$$
w_c = 0.0218 \times d^2
$$

Substituting $d = 70$ mm:
$$
w_c = 0.0218 \times (70)^2 = 0.0218 \times 4900 = 106.82 \text{ kg/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Weight of full chain in air:**
$$
W_{ch,air} = w_c \times L = 106.82 \times 200 = 21\,364 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Step 3 — Submerged weight of chain** (using empirical factor $k_{sub} = 0.87$):
$$
W_{ch,sub} = 0.87 \times 21\,364 = 18\,587 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Step 4 — Total hoisting load (ignoring friction for design simplicity):**
$$
W_{total} = W_a + W_{ch,sub} = 9000 + 18\,587 = 27\,587 \text{ kgf}
$$

> **Valuation Tip:** Many students forget to apply the **0.87 submerged factor**. If you use $W_{ch,air}$ directly, your answer is **15 % too high** — examiners specifically test this in Module 2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Motor Power Calculation (Board-Favourite)

**Given (continuing from §3.1):**
- $W_{total} = 27\,587 \text{ kgf}$
- Hoisting speed: $v = 9 \text{ m/min}$ (regulatory minimum)
- Mechanical efficiency: $\eta = 0.70$

**Step 1 — Convert load to Newtons** (SI mandate for power in Watts):
$$
W_{total} = 27\,587 \times 9.81 = 270\,629 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Output (shaft) power:**
$$
P_{out} = \frac{W_{total} \times v}{60} = \frac{270\,629 \times 9}{60}
$$

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\,435\,661}{60} = 40\,594 \text{ W} \approx 40.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Motor input power** (accounting for gear train efficiency):
$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{40\,594}{0.70} = 57\,991 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 58.0 \text{ kW} \quad (\approx 77.7 \text{ HP})}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical selection:**
A standard **60 kW (≈ 80 HP)** electric motor with a 20 % service margin is selected, rated for **30-minute** intermittent duty (S3 or S4 per IEC 60034).

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Brake Holding Capacity Verification (14-Mark Question Anchor)

**Given:**
- Chain breaking load (U2, 70 mm): $BL = 4200 \text{ kN}$ (Class reference table)
- Required brake factor: $0.40 \times BL$

**Step 1 — Regulatory minimum brake holding:**
$$
F_B = 0.40 \times 4200 = 1680 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Check against proof load requirement:**
$$
PL = 0.70 \times BL = 0.70 \times 4200 = 2940 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
0.60 \times PL = 0.60 \times 2940 = 1764 \text{ kN}
$$

Since $0.60 \times PL = 1764 \text{ kN} > 0.40 \times BL = 1680 \text{ kN}$, the **governing value is 1764 kN**.

$$
\boxed{F_{B,\,design} = \max(0.40\,BL,\; 0.60\,PL) = 1764 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 3 — Torque on the brake drum:**

Assume band brake on a drum of diameter $D_b = 0.6 \text{ m}$:
$$
T_{brake} = F_B \times \frac{D_b}{2} = 1764 \times \frac{0.6}{2} = 1764 \times 0.3 = 529.2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students apply the brake force tangentially without converting through the drum radius. Examiners specifically look for the **half-diameter** factor.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — "How Each Parameter Interacts"

| Design Parameter | Increases When... | Effect on Motor Power | Effect on Brake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anchor mass $\uparrow$ | Ship size / EN $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ linearly | $\uparrow$ linearly |
| Chain length outboard $\uparrow$ | Deeper anchorage | $\uparrow$ linearly | Marginal $\uparrow$ |
| Chain diameter $d \uparrow$ | Larger ship | $\uparrow$ as $d^2$ | $\uparrow$ as $d^2$ |
| Hoisting speed $v \uparrow$ | Operational choice | $\uparrow$ linearly | No direct effect |
| Mechanical efficiency $\eta \downarrow$ | Older / poorly maintained | $\uparrow$ motor rating | No direct effect |
| Submerged factor (0.87) misapplied | Student error | Inflates load by 15 % | Inflates brake by 15 % |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Block Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of an anchor windlass system — the physical interconnections between prime mover, gear train, brakes, and deck machinery. This is the standard "block-diagram fallback" approach recommended by KTU-Premier for engineering schematics.

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Anchor Windlass Drive Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source: Electric Motor / Hydraulic Motor / Steam Engine] --> B[Coupling and Shaft Brake]
    B --> C[Primary Gear Reduction Unit]
    C --> D[Secondary Gear Reduction and Clutch]
    D --> E[Wildcat Shaft Assembly]
    E --> F1[Wildcat / Gypsy Cable Lifter]
    E --> F2[Warping Drum Whelps]
    E --> F3[Band Brake Drum]
    E --> F4[Stern Roller or Cable Lifter]
    F1 --> G1[Anchor Chain Engagement via Pocket Teeth]
    F1 --> G2[Hawse Pipe Vertical Lift Path]
    G1 --> H[Anchor Chain Cable to Seabed]
    G2 --> I[Bower Anchor at Hawse Pipe]
    H --> J[Sea Bed and Embedded Anchor]
    K[Chain Stopper and Riding Pawl Assembly] --> G1
    L[Control Console on Bridge or Forward Station] --> A
    M[Local Hand Control on Forecastle] --> A
    B --> N[Emergency Manual Release Lever]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **prime mover (A)** can be electric, hydraulic, or (historically) steam. The **brake (B)** sits immediately after the motor — it is the *first line of defence* in a power failure.
- The gear train **(C, D)** reduces motor RPM (~ 1500 rpm) to wildcat RPM (~ 5–10 rpm).
- The wildcat shaft **(E)** carries **four** discrete functions simultaneously: cable lifter, warping drum, brake drum, and (sometimes) stern roller. This is why a windlass is called a *combined* machine.
- The chain stopper **(K)** is mechanically **independent** of the motor — it can hold the load even if the entire drive system fails.

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Power Flow & Load Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Electrical Supply 440V 3-phase] --> P2[Electric Motor 60 kW]
    P2 --> P3[Reduction Gearbox ratio 100:1]
    P3 --> P4[Wildcat Shaft]
    P4 --> P5[Wildcat engages Chain Links]
    P5 --> P6[Chain lifts Anchor upward]
    P6 --> P7[Anchor stowed in Hawse Pipe]
    P7 --> P8[Chain Stopper engaged Riding Pawl drops]
    P8 --> P9[Brake applied Motor de-energised]
```

**Valuation Hint:** When drawing this in the exam, examiners look for the *correct sequencing* — **brake application must come AFTER the chain stopper takes load**, otherwise the brake alone holds the full seabed load and may slip.

## 4.3 Component Cross-Section (Schematic Text Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WildcatAssembly["Wildcat Assembly - Vertical Shaft Type"]
        direction TB
        W1[Top: Motor Mounting Flange] --> W2[Upper Bearing Housing]
        W2 --> W3[Drive Pinion]
        W3 --> W4[Bull Gear]
        W4 --> W5[Wildcat Shaft]
        W5 --> W6[Wildcat with Chain Pockets]
        W6 --> W7[Lower Bearing Housing]
        W7 --> W8[Deck Plate Foundation]
        W6 --> W9[Warping Drum on Same Shaft]
        W5 --> W10[Band Brake Drum]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical shaft windlasses** are the most common arrangement on merchant ships. Note that the **wildcat, warping drum, and brake drum are all on a SINGLE vertical shaft**, which simplifies alignment and maintenance but requires robust bearing design to handle both axial (chain weight) and radial (brake torque reaction) loads.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`
**Q: Define "Anchor Windlass" and list its three primary functions on board a ship.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> An **anchor windlass** is a deck-mounted machinery unit used for hoisting and letting-go of the ship's bower anchors along with their chain cables, and for warping/mooring operations. (1 mark)
>
> The three primary functions are:
> 1. **Weighing (hoisting) the anchor** from the seabed using the wildcat. (1 mark)
> 2. **Letting go (dropping) the anchor** under controlled brake pay-out. (0.5 marks)
> 3. **Warping / mooring operations** using the warping drum for hawsers and ropes. (0.5 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Why is the brake holding capacity of an anchor windlass specified as 40 % of the chain breaking load?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The brake must hold the load when power fails mid-hoist without slipping during normal riding-at-anchor conditions. (1 mark)
>
> Shock loads during riding can transiently reach **60–70 %** of breaking load; the brake must restrain these without slip. (1 mark)
>
> Class rules (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS) stipulate a minimum of **40 % of breaking load** OR **60 % of proof load**, whichever is greater, to provide a safety margin. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the constructional features of a **vertical shaft anchor windlass** and the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship is fitted with a **9000 kgf** bower anchor and **70 mm U2 stud-link chain cable** of length **200 m**. Calculate:
- (i) Total hoisting load on the wildcat at the moment the anchor breaks out of the seabed. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) Required motor power assuming hoisting speed = 9 m/min and mechanical efficiency = 0.70. **(3 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> Constructional features of vertical shaft anchor windlass (each component 0.5–1 mark):
> 1. **Electric motor / prime mover** — mounted on a platform above deck.
> 2. **Shaft brake (band or disc type)** — applied immediately when power fails.
> 3. **Reduction gearbox** — reduces motor rpm to wildcat rpm.
> 4. **Wildcat (cable lifter)** — toothed sprocket wheel with pockets matching chain link geometry.
> 5. **Warping drum** — smooth drum on same shaft for handling mooring ropes.
> 6. **Brake drum** — for the band brake.
> 7. **Chain stopper** — mechanical pawl that takes over once anchor is stowed.
> 8. **Riding pawl** — falls into chain link to prevent backward motion.
> 9. **Bearing housings** (upper and lower) — support the vertical shaft.
> 10. **Local control stand** — push-button controls on forecastle.
>
> (Use the Mermaid block diagram from §4.1 for the sketch.)

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **(i) Total hoisting load:** (4 marks)
> - Chain mass per metre: $w_c = 0.0218 \times 70^2 = 106.82 \text{ kg/m}$ (1 mark)
> - Chain weight in air: $W_{ch,air} = 106.82 \times 200 = 21\,364 \text{ kgf}$ (1 mark)
> - Submerged chain weight: $W_{ch,sub} = 0.87 \times 21\,364 = 18\,587 \text{ kgf}$ (1 mark)
> - Total hoisting load: $W_{total} = 9000 + 18\,587 = 27\,587 \text{ kgf} \approx 270.6 \text{ kN}$ (1 mark)
>
> **(ii) Motor power:** (3 marks)
> - Output power: $P_{out} = (270\,629 \times 9) / 60 = 40\,594 \text{ W} \approx 40.6 \text{ kW}$ (1.5 marks)
> - Motor input: $P_m = 40.6 / 0.70 = 57.99 \text{ kW} \approx 58.0 \text{ kW}$ (1.5 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Discuss the **regulatory requirements** (SOLAS and Class Society rules) for the brake holding capacity and trial-pull testing of an anchor windlass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a windlass fitted with **U2 stud-link chain of 78 mm diameter**, calculate:
- (i) The **minimum brake holding capacity** as per classification rules. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) The **torque** required at the brake drum of diameter 0.65 m. **(3 marks)**

(Given for U2 chain, 78 mm: Breaking Load = 5390 kN, Proof Load = 3773 kN)

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> 1. **SOLAS Reg. II-1/3-4.1.1** — every ship must be fitted with anchor windlasses, capstans and chain stoppers of adequate strength. (1 mark)
> 2. **SOLAS Reg. II-1/3-4.2** — design load conditions (abnormal: one anchor up, one anchor down). (1 mark)
> 3. **Brake holding capacity** must be at least **40 % of breaking load** or **60 % of proof load** of the chain cable. (1.5 marks)
> 4. **Trial pull test** — every windlass must be tested at **1.5 × normal working load** at a reduced speed of **3 m/min** for 30 minutes without damage. (1.5 marks)
> 5. **Overspeed test** — letting-go test to verify brake control during pay-out. (1 mark)
> 6. **Operational test** — full anchor drop and recovery in service conditions. (1 mark)

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **(i) Brake holding capacity:** (4 marks)
> - $F_B = 0.40 \times BL = 0.40 \times 5390 = 2156 \text{ kN}$ (1.5 marks)
> - $0.60 \times PL = 0.60 \times 3773 = 2263.8 \text{ kN}$ (1.5 marks)
> - Governing value: $F_B = \max(2156,\; 2263.8) = 2263.8 \text{ kN}$ (1 mark)
>
> **(ii) Brake torque:** (3 marks)
> - $T_{brake} = F_B \times D_b / 2 = 2263.8 \times 0.325 = 735.7 \text{ kN·m}$ (3 marks)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes that COST MARKS in Module 2 Anchor Windlass questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.87 submerged factor** — Examiners *deliberately* set numbers that make this mistake yield a "round" but **wrong** answer. You lose 2–3 marks silently.
> 2. **Using breaking load instead of working load for motor sizing** — motor is sized for **Normal Working Load (~ 0.46 × BL)** at **9 m/min**, not for the breaking load.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing kgf and kN without conversion. Always convert to SI (Newtons) before computing power in Watts.
> 4. **Forgetting the half-diameter in torque calculation** — Torque = Force × Radius, **NOT** Force × Diameter. This single error costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the regulatory source** — every brake and speed number must cite **SOLAS / IACS / Class rule**. A correct number without a citation loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Drawing a horizontal-shaft windlass when asked for vertical** — examiners check diagram orientation. Use the schematic from §4.1.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ **Anchor Windlass** = deck machinery on forecastle for handling anchors + chains + warping.
- ☐ **Three-in-one machine**: Wildcat + Warping Drum + Brake Drum on a single shaft.
- ☐ **Two safety devices**: Chain Stopper (manual) + Riding Pawl (automatic gravity drop).
- ☐ **Vertical-shaft type** is industry standard for merchant ships.
- ☐ **Total hoisting load** = Anchor mass + (0.87 × Chain mass in air). Never forget the **0.87 submerged factor**.
- ☐ **Minimum hoisting speed** = **9 m/min** at Normal Working Load (regulatory).
- ☐ **Trial-pull speed** = **3 m/min** at **1.5 × NWL**.
- ☐ **Brake holding** = $\max(0.40 \times BL,\; 0.60 \times PL)$.
- ☐ **Motor power formula**: $P_m = (W \times v) / (60 \times \eta)$.
- ☐ **Chain mass per metre (empirical)**: $w_c = 0.0218 \times d^2$ (kg/m).
- ☐ **Proof Load** = $0.70 \times$ Breaking Load.
- ☐ **Mechanical efficiency** of standard windlass gear train = **0.65 to 0.75**.
- ☐ **Torque at brake drum** = Force × (Diameter / 2).
- ☐ **Governing standards**: SOLAS Ch. II-1, IACS UR 21, ISO 3828 (Equipment Number).
- ☐ **"Abnormal condition"** = one anchor up, one anchor down — windlass must handle this simultaneously.
- ☐ **Common examination traps**: missing 0.87 factor; wrong torque radius; mixing BL/PL/NWL; unit errors.

> **Final 30-second mental hook:** *"9 metres per minute, 87 percent submerged, 40 percent of breaking — these three numbers alone answer 80 % of Module 2 anchor windlass problems."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capstan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Capstan ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Capstan** is a vertical, rotating, spindle-shaped mechanical device mounted on the weather deck of a ship, primarily used for applying heavy pull to hawsers, mooring lines, towing lines, and messenger wires during mooring, unmooring, warping, and anchoring operations. It is essentially the **vertical axis counterpart of a windlass**, with the drum axis oriented perpendicular to the deck. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with classification society rules (e.g., **IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, Class NK**), a capstan is classified as a piece of **deck machinery** under **Mooring and Anchoring Systems**, governed by the **Safe Working Load (SWL)** and **Design Load** (SWL × Factor of Safety) criteria. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBNST604 Module 2):** > The capstan is studied under **"Design of Deck Machinery – Mooring Equipment."** Focus areas include the capstan equation, design load estimation, drum/barrel geometry, power calculations, and selection of drive systems (electric/hydraulic/steam). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are trying to pull a heavy boat toward a dock using a rope. If you simply pull with your hands, you cannot generate enough force, and the rope would just slip. Now imagine wrapping the rope around a fixed, rotating post (like a bollard) several times. Suddenly, you can hold enormous loads with just a small force at the free end. The more wraps you take, the more your effective pulling capacity increases.
