- Chain locker: watertight, coaming ≥ 300 mm, ventilated, drained overboard via non-return valve

### B. Equipment Number
- Unified IACS dimensionless index for sizing mooring/anchoring equipment
- Formula: **EN = (Δ/100)^(2/3) + 2h + 10 + A/10**
- Δ = moulded displacement in tonnes (NOT DWT)
- h = effective height in metres (waterline to uppermost continuous deck)
- A = projected lateral windage area in m²
- Higher EN → heavier anchors, larger chain, higher BHL winches
- Container ships and VLCCs have high EN due to large windage area

### C. Mooring Systems
- Minimum **6 lines** per OCIMF (more for large vessels: 8, 12, 16)
- Line types: wire rope, HMPE (Dyneema), polyester, nylon
- Line configuration: **head, stern, breast, spring** (typically 2 each)
- MBL ≥ **1.25 × T_design**
- FoS = MBL/T_op ≥ **1.5 to 3.0**
- Winch BHL ≈ **0.6 × MBL**
- Bollard SWL ≈ **0.75 × MBL**
- N_min = F_env / (0.6 × MBL)

### D. Key Constants to Memorize
- Air density: ρ_a = **1.225 kg/m³**
- Wind drag coefficient (ship): C_d ≈ **1.0 – 1.4**
- Design utilization factor (OCIMF): f_d = **0.6**
- Wind force: F = C_d × ½ρ_a V² × A
- Minimum mooring lines: **6** (OCIMF)
- Bitter end strength factor: **1.25 × MBL**

### E. Mnemonics for KTU Exam
- **"DHA"** — **D**isplacement, **H**eight, **A**rea → Equipment Number
- **"HSBS"** — **H**ead, **S**tern, **B**reast, **S**pring → Mooring Line Types
- **"B-B-C-F-S"** — **B**ollard, **B**itts, **C**hain Stopper, **F**airlead, **S**hackle → Mooring Fittings

---

**END OF NOTES — Module 2: Bitter End Connection, Equipment Number Calculation, Mooring Systems**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deck Fittings and Structural Arrangement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Deck Fittings and Structural Arrangement # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Deck Fittings **Deck fittings** are the auxiliary structural and mechanical components installed on the exposed and protected decks of a ship to facilitate **mooring, anchoring, cargo handling, safety, ventilation, and closure of openings**. These fittings are designed to transmit high localized loads arising from ropes, chains, anchors, and cargo gear into the primary hull structure without compromising watertight integrity or structural strength. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBNST604 - Module 2):** > *Deck fittings encompass all equipment attached to the deck for operational functions — mooring fittings (bollards, bitts, fairleads, rollers), anchoring fittings (hawse pipes, cable holders, windlass), cargo fittings (masts, derricks, hatches), safety fittings (railings, bulwarks, freeing ports, davits), and closing appliances (doors, hatches, scuttles).* ## 1.2 Definition of Structural Arrangement **Structural arrangement** refers to the systematic planning, positioning, and integration of superstructures, deckhouses, casings, and platform decks on a ship's hull to provide **operational spaces, weather protection, navigation, accommodation, and access between compartments**, while ensuring compliance with structural strength, stability, and freeboard regulations. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Ship as a Building on a Floating Platform"** > > Imagine a multi-storied office building sitting on a giant floating raft. **Deck fittings** are like the *door handles, handrails, anchor points for cranes, and window latches* — small but critical components that make the building functional. The **structural arrangement** is the *floor plan* — where the reception (bridge), offices (accommodation), elevator shafts (engine casing), and staircases (companion ways) are placed so that people can move around safely and efficiently. > > Just as you wouldn't place the elevator in the basement without a shaft, you wouldn't place the ship's bridge without a proper deckhouse or the windlass without reinforced foundations. ## 1.4 Classification of Deck Fittings (Standard KTU Taxonomy) | Category | Examples | Primary Function | |---|---|---| | **Mooring Fittings** | Bollards, Bitts, Fairleads, Rollers, Panama Chocks, Mooring Winches | Securing the ship to a berth | | **Anchoring Fittings** | Hawse Pipes, Cable Holders, Bitter End, Windlass, Chain Stopper | Stowing and deploying the anchor chain | | **Cargo Fittings** | Masts, Derricks, Cranes, Cargo Hatches, Hatch Covers, Coamings | Loading and securing cargo | | **Safety Fittings** | Lifeboat Davits, Bulwarks, Guard Rails, Freeing Ports, Life Raft Cradles | Personnel safety and emergency response | | **Closing Appliances** | Watertight Doors, Hatches, Scuttles, Manholes | Maintaining watertight/subdivision integrity | | **Ventilation Fittings** | Cowls, Mushroom Vents, Goosenecks, Supply/Exhaust Fans | Air circulation to compartments | ## 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics - **SWL (Safe Working Load):** The maximum load a fitting is rated to handle under normal service, expressed in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**. - **Breaking Load (BL):** The load at which the fitting is expected to fail; typically **BL = 1.5 × SWL** to **2.0 × SWL** depending on classification society rules. - **Factor of Safety (FoS):** Ratio of ultimate/breaking load to design load. - **Standard Mooring Line Size:** **22 mm to 76 mm** diameter depending on ship size. - **Standard Mooring Line Material:** Polypropylene, polyester, or steel wire ropes. - **Standard Freeboard (Bulwark Height):** Typically **1.0 m to 1.4 m** above the working deck. - **Standard Hawse Pipe Diameter:** Equal to or slightly larger than the chain diameter. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mooring Fittings — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Bollards A **bollard** is a short, stout vertical post (cast steel or fabricated) used for securing mooring lines on the open deck. The base is bolted through doubler plates into the deck plating and primary stiffeners. - **Types:** Cross bollard, double cross bollard, T-head bollard, pillar bollard, kidney bollard, quick-release hook bollard. - **Load Path:** Rope → Bollard head → Bollard shank → Base plate → Doubler → Deck plating → Hull girder. - **Standard SWL:** Ranges from **5 tonnes** (small craft) to **150 tonnes** (VLCCs and container ships). ### 2.1.2 Bitts (Mooring Bitts) **Bitts** are paired vertical posts used for taking turns of mooring rope. They have a higher rope capacity than bollards because of multiple turns. **Double bitts** are used for heavy-duty mooring (e.g., tug assistance). ### 2.1.3 Fairleads and Rollers **Fairleads** are devices that change the direction of a mooring line without causing excessive chafe. The line passes over a sheave (roller) supported by a robust pedestal. - **Types:** Pedestal fairlead, Panama fairlead, roller fairlead, closed fairlead, universal fairlead. - **Function:** Reduce rope wear, guide rope to winch drum, allow rope to lead from any direction within an arc. ### 2.1.4 Mooring Winches and Capstans - **Mooring winches:** Provide the mechanical tension to haul in mooring lines. Self-tensioning winches maintain constant line tension automatically. - **Capstans:** Vertical drum used for line handling (hauling direction only, not tension holding). - **Drum Capacity:** Typically **3 wraps** of the mooring line plus 2 turns on the bitts. - **Drum Diameter:** Must be at least **15× the line diameter** (DNV/ABS rule of thumb). ### 2.1.5 Mooring Line Tensions and Loads The tension in a mooring line depends on: - **Wind force** on the superstructure and hull above waterline. - **Current force** on the underwater hull. - **Wave-induced surge motion** at the berth. ## 2.2 Anchoring Fittings — Detailed Theory ### 2.2.1 Windlass The **windlass** is the prime mover for anchor handling. It performs three functions: 1. Hoisting (lifting) the anchor. 2. Lowering the anchor. 3. Holding the chain cable against the anchor force. - **Components:** Wildcat (chain wheel), brake band, clutch, warping drum, stripper. - **Power Source:** Electric motor (most modern) or hydraulic motor (LNG carriers). - **Design Load:** **1.25 × the weight of the anchor** for static hoisting. ### 2.2.2 Hawse Pipes **Hawse pipes** are heavy steel castings or fabrications built into the hull at the bow, through which the anchor chain passes from the chain locker to the outside. They are reinforced with **spool pieces** to prevent hull deformation. - **Angle:** Usually inclined **15° to 30°** from horizontal. - **Closure:** Fitted with **shell-opening covers** (hinged or sliding) and **guillotine valves** to prevent water ingress if the anchor is lost. ### 2.2.3 Chain Cable and Stopper - **Chain cable grade:** Studded link chain, Grade U1, U2, U3 (higher grade = higher strength). - **Chain diameter** determined by **equipment number (EN)** per IACS UR A1. - **Chain Stopper:** A heavy-duty fitting that takes the load of the chain when the windlass brake is opened for maintenance. Located between the hawse pipe and the windlass. ### 2.2.4 Bitter End and Chain Locker - **Bitter end:** The inboard end of the chain cable, secured to a **bitter end pin** in the chain locker. - **Chain locker:** Watertight compartment below the forecastle deck, sized to stow the entire anchor chain (typically **12–15 shackles**, each shackle = 27.5 m). ## 2.3 Structural Arrangement — Components and Logic ### 2.3.1 Forecastle Deck The **forecastle** is a raised deck at the bow, extending aft from the stem for some length. It provides: - Protection against head seas (green seas). - Working space for mooring and anchor handling. - Houses the windlass, chain locker, and forward mooring equipment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** > *The forecastle height is typically **1.5 m to 2.5 m** above the main deck for cargo ships. Modern bulk carriers and tankers often have NO forecastle (low freeboard) but compensate with a forward ballast tank.* ### 2.3.2 Poop Deck The **poop deck** is the raised deck at the stern, providing protection to the accommodation and providing working space for aft mooring. ### 2.3.3 Bridge Deck and Bridge Superstructure The **bridge superstructure** contains the navigation bridge, chart room, master's cabin, and radio room. It is typically divided into multiple decks: - **Compass platform (top deck):** Houses the navigation bridge, radars, antennas. - **Bridge deck:** Chart room, officer cabins. - **Boat deck:** Lifeboats, liferafts (on passenger ships). ### 2.3.4 Engine Casing (Casing Top) The **engine casing** is a light structure built over the main engine and shaft tunnel on a single-island vessel. It: - Provides ventilation uptakes for the engine room. - Supports funnel (upright exhaust). - Allows access to the engine room from the main deck. ### 2.3.5 Bulwarks and Guard Rails - **Bulwarks:** Solid plating on the edge of the exposed deck, height **1.0–1.4 m**, with **freeing ports** cut in the lower edge to allow water to escape. - **Guard rails:** Open railings (wire + stanchions) used where bulwarks are not required. Typically **three courses** at heights 0.5 m, 0.85 m, 1.0 m. ### 2.3.6 Deckhouses **Deckhouses** are multi-deck superstructures providing accommodation, navigation, and service spaces. They are designed per SOLAS and Load Line Convention requirements. ### 2.3.7 Companion Ways and Skylights - **Companion way:** A raised hatch with sliding or hinged covers providing access between decks in the deckhouse or superstructure. - **Skylight:** A glazed or steel-framed structure providing light to spaces below. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield and frequently appear in University Exams.** | # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Equipment Number (EN) | $EN = \Delta^{2/3} + 2 \cdot h \cdot B + 0.1 \cdot A$ | (EN unitless) | For chain/anchor/windlass sizing | | 2 | Wind Pressure | $P_w = 0.613 \cdot C_h \cdot C_s \cdot V^2$ | N/m² | $V$ in m/s, $C_h$ height coefficient | | 3 | Wind Force on Superstructure | $F_w = P_w \cdot A_{projected}$ | N | $A$ = projected area in m² | | 4 | Mooring Line Breaking Load (CB) | $CB = 44 \cdot d^2 \cdot R_r$ | kN | $d$ = line diameter (mm), $R_r$ = grade factor | | 5 | Bollard SWL Design Load | $L_{design} = 1.5 \cdot SWL$ | kN | Per IACS S21 | | 6 | Factor of Safety (Bollard) | $FoS = BL / SWL$ | — | Typically **1.5 to 2.0** | | 7 | Chain Cable Weight | $W_c = 0.0218 \cdot d^2$ | kg/m | Studded chain approximation | | 8 | Windlass Hoisting Load | $F_h = 1.25 \cdot W_{anchor}$ | kN | Static design condition | | 9 | Brake Holding Load (Windlass) | $F_b = 1.5 \cdot W_{anchor} + W_{chain\ supported}$ | kN | Total supported weight | | 10 | Freeing Port Area | $A_{fp} \geq 0.07 \cdot A_{bulwark}$ | m² | Per Load Line Convention | | 11 | Bulwark Height | $h_b \leq 1.5$ m | m | Beyond this, rail must be fitted | | 12 | Guard Rail Spacing | $\leq 2.2$ m between stanchions | m | SOLAS Reg. II-1/3 | | 13 | Hawse Pipe Reinforcement Area | $A_{rein} \geq 2 \times$ Hull Plate | — | Empirical rule | | 14 | Deck Doubler Thickness | $t_{doubler} \geq 1.5 \cdot t_{deck\ plate}$ | mm | To prevent local buckling | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Shipyards** use these formulas in the **early design phase** to specify equipment and steel. - **Classification societies** (DNV, ABS, LR, BV) use EN and FoS formulas to certify fittings. - **Port authorities** use mooring line calculations to design bollards on the quay. - **Naval architects** use freeing port and bulwark formulas to ensure stability in heavy weather. - **Structural engineers** use deck doubler and reinforcement rules to prevent fatigue cracking at deck fittings. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Equipment Number (EN) and Its Use in Sizing Chain and Windlass The **Equipment Number (EN)** is a dimensionless parameter used to standardize anchor and chain sizing. It is derived as the sum of three components: displacement, freeboard, and windage area. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define the ship parameters: $$\Delta = \text{displacement in tonnes}$$ $$B = \text{moulded breadth in metres}$$ $$h = \text{effective height of the ship's hull above the waterline}$$ $$A = \text{projected lateral windage area above waterline}$$ **Step 2:** Define the Equipment Number as the sum of contributions: $$EN = EN_{\Delta} + EN_{h} + EN_{A}$$ where each contribution is computed from a specific formula. **Step 3:** Substitute the standard IACS UR A1 expressions: $$EN = \Delta^{2/3} + 2 \cdot h \cdot B + 0.1 \cdot A$$ **Step 4:** Once EN is calculated, use IACS tables to determine: - Anchor mass ($W_{anchor}$ in kg). - Chain diameter ($d$ in mm). - Total chain length ($L_c$ in metres, typically **12–15 shackles**). **Step 5:** Cross-check the windlass hoisting capacity: $$F_{windlass} \geq 1.25 \cdot W_{anchor}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example: Equipment Number for a Panamax Bulk Carrier **Given:** - Displacement, $\Delta = 75{,}000$ tonnes - Breadth, $B = 32.2$ m - Effective hull height, $h = 12.5$ m - Projected windage area, $A = 1200$ m² **Solution:** Step 1: Compute displacement contribution: $$EN_{\Delta} = \Delta^{2/3} = 75{,}000^{2/3}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} 75{,}000^{2/3} &= \left(75{,}000^{1/3}\right)^2 \\ &= \left(\sqrt[3]{75{,}000}\right)^2 \\ &\approx (42.17)^2 \\ &\approx 1778.3 \end{aligned} $$ Step 2: Compute hull height contribution: $$EN_h = 2 \cdot h \cdot B = 2 \cdot 12.5 \cdot 32.2 = 805.0$$ Step 3: Compute windage area contribution: $$EN_A = 0.1 \cdot A = 0.1 \cdot 1200 = 120$$ Step 4: Sum the contributions: $$ \begin{aligned} EN &= EN_{\Delta} + EN_h + EN_A \\ &= 1778.3 + 805.0 + 120 \\ &= 2703.3 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $EN \approx 2703$. From IACS tables, this corresponds to **3 anchors (1 spare)**, each of mass approximately **10,500 kg**, with studded chain of diameter **102 mm**. ## 3.3 Derivation: Mooring Line Breaking Load **Step 1:** Define the rope diameter and grade factor: $$d = \text{rope diameter in mm}$$ $$R_r = \text{rope material grade factor}$$ Standard grade factors: $R_r = 1.0$ (polypropylene), $R_r = 1.4$ (polyester), $R_r = 3.0$ (steel wire). **Step 2:** Apply the empirical breaking-load formula (DNV-OS-E301): $$CB = 44 \cdot d^2 \cdot R_r \quad [\text{kN}]$$ **Step 3:** Derive the Safe Working Load (SWL) by applying Factor of Safety $FoS$: $$SWL = \frac{CB}{FoS}$$ For mooring lines, $FoS = 3$ (per OCIMF MEG4 standard for fibre ropes). ### Worked Example: Polyester Mooring Line **Given:** $d = 52$ mm, polyester ($R_r = 1.4$), $FoS = 3$. $$ \begin{aligned} CB &= 44 \cdot (52)^2 \cdot 1.4 \\ &= 44 \cdot 2704 \cdot 1.4 \\ &= 166{,}566 \text{ N} \\ &= 166.6 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ $$ SWL = \frac{CB}{FoS} = \frac{166.6}{3} \approx 55.5 \text{ kN} $$ **Result:** The mooring line has an SWL of **~55.5 kN**. ## 3.4 Derivation: Freeing Port Area (Per Load Line Convention) **Step 1:** Identify the bulwark area in the appropriate quarter: $$A_{bulwark} = L_b \cdot h_b \quad [\text{m}^2]$$ where $L_b$ is the bulwark length, $h_b$ is the bulwark height. **Step 2:** Apply the minimum freeing port area: $$A_{fp,min} = 0.07 \cdot A_{bulwark}$$ **Step 3:** Distribute the area: - If bulwark length > 0.04L (where L is ship length), divide freeing port area into 4 quarters with appropriate distribution. ### Worked Example: Bulwark Freeing Port Sizing **Given:** Bulwark length = 60 m, Bulwark height = 1.2 m. $$ \begin{aligned} A_{bulwark} &= 60 \cdot 1.2 = 72 \text{ m}^2 \\ A_{fp,min} &= 0.07 \cdot 72 = 5.04 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Minimum freeing port area is **5.04 m²**. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Material, Strength, and Application | Fitting | Material | Typical SWL (tonnes) | BL/SWL Ratio | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | Cross Bollard | Cast Steel (ASTM A27) | 5–150 | 1.5 | Berthing, breast lines | | Double Bitt | Cast Steel / Fab Steel | 10–200 | 1.5–2.0 | Heavy mooring, tug lines | | Panama Fairlead | Cast Steel | 5–100 | 1.5 | Multi-direction fairleading | | Windlass Brake | Steel (Brake Lined) | 100–500 | 2.0 | Anchor chain holding | | Hawse Pipe | Cast Steel | N/A (structural) | 2.0 | Chain passage at bow | | Guard Rail Stanchion | Galv. Steel | N/A (safety) | 2.0 | Edge protection | | Davit (Lifeboat) | Structural Steel | 5–25 (per boat) | 4.0 | Lifeboat launching | ## 3.6 Detailed Procedural Logic — Selecting a Bollard for a Ship The selection process follows a logical sequence: 1. **Compute the expected mooring load** from wind + current + wave surge. 2. **Determine the largest single mooring line load** at the bollard (typically the breast line). 3. **Add a dynamic amplification factor** of 1.5 for snatching loads. 4. **Select a bollard** with SWL $\geq$ computed dynamic load. 5. **Verify base plate thickness** using FE analysis (or empirical rule of thumb: base plate area $\geq$ $5 \times$ shank cross-section). 6. **Check deck doubler reinforcement** for stress concentration at the cutout. 7. **Confirm classification society approval** (DNV/ABS/LR certificate). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Ship Layout — Top View Showing Deck Fittings and Structural Arrangement The diagram below illustrates a typical general cargo ship layout showing the placement of mooring, anchoring, and structural arrangement components. ```mermaid flowchart TB Bow["BOW Section"] FCDeck["Forecastle Deck"] Windlass["Windlass with Wildcat"] HawsePipe["Hawse Pipe Pair"] BollardB["Forward Bollards"] FairleadB["Panama Fairleads"] MidShip["MID-SHIP Region"] MainDeck["Main Working Deck"] Hatch1["Cargo Hatch 1 with Coaming"] Hatch2["Cargo Hatch 2 with Coaming"] Mast["Cargo Mast and Derrick"] VentCowl["Ventilation Cowl"] BridgeStr["Bridge Superstructure Multi-Deck"] BridgeWin["Bridge Wings with Mooring Winches"] FWinchFwd["Forward Mooring Winch"] FWinchAft["Aft Mooring Winch"] Stern["STERN Section"] PoopDeck["Poop Deck"] Ladder["Accommodation Ladder"] Lifeboat["Lifeboat on Davits"] Funnel["Funnel with Uptake"] EngineCase["Engine Casing"] Bulwark["Bulwark with Freeing Ports"] Bow --> HawsePipe Bow --> BollardB Bow --> FCDeck FCDeck --> Windlass FCDeck --> FairleadB FCDeck --> MidShip MidShip --> Hatch1 MidShip --> Hatch2 MidShip --> Mast MidShip --> VentCowl MidShip --> BridgeStr BridgeStr --> BridgeWin BridgeStr --> Funnel BridgeStr --> EngineCase BridgeStr --> Lifeboat BridgeStr --> FWinchFwd BridgeStr --> FWinchAft FWinchFwd --> Stern FWinchAft --> Stern Stern --> PoopDeck Stern --> Ladder MidShip --> Bulwark ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Windlass and Anchor System ```mermaid flowchart LR ChainL["Chain Locker
Stores 12 to 15 Shackles"] BitterEnd["Bitter End Pin
Secures Chain Inboard"] Stopper["Chain Stopper
Load Transfer Device"] Wildcat["Wildcat Chain Wheel
Hoisting and Lowering"] Brake["Band Brake
Holds Chain Load"] WarpDrum["Warping Drum
Mooring Line Handling"] Motor["Electric Motor
Prime Mover"] Clutch["Clutch Assembly
Engage Disengage"] HawseOut["Hawse Pipe
Chain Passage to Sea"] Anchor["Stockless Anchor
Weight W anchor"] ChainL --> BitterEnd ChainL --> Stopper Stopper --> Wildcat Wildcat --> Brake Wildcat --> WarpDrum WarpDrum --> Motor Motor --> Clutch Clutch --> Wildcat Wildcat --> HawseOut HawseOut --> Anchor ``` ## 4.3 Bollard Load Path Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD Rope["Mooring Rope
Tension T"] BollardHead["Bollard Head
Receives Rope Turn"] BollardShank["Bollard Shank
Vertical Post"] BasePlate["Base Plate
Distributes Load"] Doubler["Doubler Plate
Local Reinforcement"] DeckPlate["Deck Plating
Hull Girder Top"] HullGirder["Hull Girder
Primary Strength Member"] Rope --> BollardHead BollardHead --> BollardShank BollardShank --> BasePlate BasePlate --> Doubler Doubler --> DeckPlate DeckPlate --> HullGirder ``` ## 4.4 Superstructure Tier Arrangement ```mermaid flowchart TB Top["Compass Platform
Wheelhouse Radars Antennas"] L2["Bridge Deck
Chart Room Officer Cabins"] L3["Boat Deck
Lifeboats Liferafts"] L4["Accommodation Deck
Crew Cabins Galley"] L5["Main Deck
Cargo Holds Engine Casing"] L6["Tween Deck
Cargo or Tanks"] Top --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]** Define a **bollard** and state the standard factor of safety used for its design. **Model Answer:** A **bollard** is a short vertical post (cast steel or fabricated) installed on the open deck to which mooring lines are made fast. It consists of a **head**, **shank**, and **base plate** bolted through a deck doubler into the primary hull structure. The standard **factor of safety (FoS)** for bollards is between **1.5 and 2.0** (typically **2.0**) — meaning the breaking load is **2 × the Safe Working Load (SWL)**. This ensures that under normal berthing/snatching loads, the bollard does not yield or fracture. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]** What is the function of **freeing ports** in a ship's bulwark? **Model Answer:** **Freeing ports** are openings cut in the lower edge of a bulwark on the exposed decks of a ship. Their function is to allow **accumulated seawater** (from heavy weather, green seas, or rainfall) to **drain rapidly back into the sea**, thereby: 1. Preventing the dangerous accumulation of water on deck (which could cause **loss of stability** or **free surface effect**). 2. Reducing the **deck load** on the hull girder. Per the **Load Line Convention**, the total area of freeing ports in any one quarter must be at least: $$A_{fp} \geq 0.07 \cdot A_{bulwark}$$ where $A_{bulwark}$ is the projected bulwark area in that quarter. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks) ### Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and function of the following mooring fittings: - (i) Cross Bollard - (ii) Double Bitt - (iii) Panama Fairlead **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A ship has displacement **$\Delta = 50{,}000$ tonnes**, breadth $B = 28$ m, effective height $h = 10$ m, and windage area $A = 950$ m². Compute the **Equipment Number (EN)** and from the result, determine the **anchor mass** and **chain diameter** using IACS tables. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a)** Detailed Description: **(i) Cross Bollard:** A cross bollard has **two horizontal cross arms** forming a "+" shape on top of a vertical shank. It can accept mooring ropes from any horizontal direction and provides **multiple rope turns** for heavy moorings. The base plate is reinforced by a **doubler plate** of thickness 1.5 times the deck plate. **(ii) Double Bitt:** A double bitt consists of **two parallel vertical posts** connected by a crosshead. It allows multiple turns of the mooring rope to be taken, distributing the load over both posts. It is used for **heavy mooring operations** where high rope tension is expected (e.g., tug assist berthing). **(iii) Panama Fairlead:** A Panama fairlead is a **closed-type fairlead** with rollers mounted in a robust pedestal casting. It guides the mooring line from the bollard/bitts to the winch drum, allowing the rope to lead from any direction within an arc of up to **180°** without chafe. It is particularly useful where mooring lines have to change direction sharply. **(b)** Numerical Solution: Step 1: Compute displacement contribution: $$ EN_{\Delta} = \Delta^{2/3} = 50{,}000^{2/3} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 50{,}000^{1/3} &\approx 36.84 \\ 50{,}000^{2/3} &\approx (36.84)^2 \approx 1357.2 \end{aligned} $$ Step 2: Compute hull height contribution: $$EN_h = 2 \cdot h \cdot B = 2 \cdot 10 \cdot 28 = 560$$ Step 3: Compute windage contribution: $$EN_A = 0.1 \cdot A = 0.1 \cdot 950 = 95$$ Step 4: Sum: $$ EN = 1357.2 + 560 + 95 = \boxed{2012.2} $$ Step 5: From IACS UR A1 tables: - For EN = 2012 → **Number of anchors = 3** (2 in service + 1 spare) - **Anchor mass per head ≈ 8,400 kg** - **Chain diameter ≈ 90 mm (U2 grade)** - **Total chain length = 13 shackles × 27.5 m = 357.5 m** --- ### Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** With the aid of a labelled diagram, describe the **structural arrangement** of a typical **general cargo ship** showing forecastle, bridge superstructure, poop deck, and engine casing. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the function and design requirements of **bulwarks, freeing ports, and guard rails** as per SOLAS and the Load Line Convention. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a)** Structural Arrangement of a General Cargo Ship: A general cargo ship typically has the following structural arrangement from bow to stern: 1. **Forecastle Deck** — Raised deck at the bow (1.5–2.5 m above main deck). Houses the windlass, chain locker, forward mooring equipment, and forward bosun's store. Protects forward cargo hatches from head seas. 2. **Main Deck (Working Deck)** — The principal working deck running amidships. Contains cargo hatches with coamings, mast/derrick pedestals, ventilation cowls, and midships mooring fittings. 3. **Bridge Superstructure** — Multi-deck structure amidships (or aft). Contains the navigation bridge, accommodation, galley, mess rooms, and officer cabins. Topped by the compass platform with radars and antennas. 4. **Engine Casing** — Light steel structure over the main engine space. Provides ventilation uptakes and supports the funnel. Accommodates exhaust piping. 5. **Poop Deck** — Raised deck at the stern providing weather protection to the accommodation and containing aft mooring equipment. 6. **Bulwarks with Freeing Ports** — Solid plating along the deck edge with cut-outs for water drainage. **(b)** Bulwarks, Freeing Ports, and Guard Rails: **Bulwarks:** - Solid plating at the deck edge of exposed decks, height typically **1.0–1.4 m**. - Designed to prevent personnel from falling overboard and to deflect spray. - Must be supported by **bulwark stays** (triangular brackets) at intervals of about **1.5–2.0 m**. **Freeing Ports:** - Cut-outs in the lower edge of the bulwark. - Per Load Line Convention: $A_{fp} \geq 0.07 \cdot A_{bulwark}$ per quarter. - Fitted with **bars, grids, or shutters** (depending on location) to prevent accidental passage of crew. **Guard Rails:** - Used where bulwarks are not practical (e.g., on small vessels, work boats). - Three courses at heights **0.5 m, 0.85 m, 1.0 m**. - Stanchion spacing **≤ 2.2 m**. - Top wire (or chain) rated to take a **75 kg horizontal load**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common Mistakes that cost marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Anchor mass is in kg, displacement in tonnes. Mixing units causes full-mark loss. > 2. **Not stating design standards** — Always mention **IACS UR A1**, **SOLAS Ch. II-1**, or **Load Line Convention** as appropriate. > 3. **Skipping the load path** in mooring fittings — Examiners award marks for explaining **how the load reaches the hull girder**. > 4. **Confusing freeing port ratio** — Students often write $0.7 \cdot A_{bulwark}$ instead of $0.07 \cdot A_{bulwark}$ — this is a 10× error. > 5. **Omitting Factor of Safety** in bollard design questions. > 6. **Not labelling diagrams** — A sketch without labels earns 0 marks in KTU valuation. > 7. **Confusing cross bollard with double cross bollard** — Double cross has 4 arms (one extra at right angles to the other). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deck Fittings and Structural Arrangement** - **Bollard SWL** is the **maximum allowable rope tension** under normal use; **Breaking Load (BL) = 1.5–2.0 × SWL**. - **Double bitts** handle heavier loads than single bollards due to multiple rope turns. - **Panama fairleads** redirect rope direction and prevent chafe; used at deck edges and bulwark penetrations. - **Windlass** performs three functions: **hoisting, lowering, and braking** the chain cable. - **Hawse pipes** are inclined **15°–30°** and reinforced with spool pieces to prevent hull deformation. - **Chain stopper** is the safety device that holds chain load when windlass brake is released. - **Bitter end** is the inboard end of the chain secured to a pin inside the **chain locker**. - **Freeing port area** $A_{fp} \geq 0.07 \cdot A_{bulwark}$ per quarter (Load Line Convention). - **Bulwark height** limited to **1.5 m**; above this, guard rails must be added. - **Guard rail heights**: **0.5 m, 0.85 m, 1.0 m**; stanchion spacing ≤ **2.2 m**. - **Equipment Number (EN)** formula: $EN = \Delta^{2/3} + 2 h B + 0.1 A$ — used to size anchors, chain, and windlass. - **Windlass hoisting load**: $F_h \geq 1.25 \cdot W_{anchor}$ (static design condition). - **Forecastle, bridge, and poop decks** are the three principal raised structures on a cargo ship. - **Engine casing** provides ventilation uptakes and supports the funnel. - **Mooring line breaking load (CB)** for fibre ropes: $CB = 44 \cdot d^2 \cdot R_r$ [kN], $d$ in mm. - **Factor of safety** for mooring lines (fibre) = **3.0**; for wire rope = **2.5**. - **Chain cable weight** (studded) ≈ $0.0218 \cdot d^2$ kg/m. - **Bollard base plate doubler** thickness ≥ **1.5 × deck plate** to prevent local buckling. - **Skylight and companion way** provide light and access between decks. - **SOLAS Reg. II-1/3** governs guard rail and bulwark requirements. - **Load Line Convention** governs freeing port sizing — critical exam point.

topics.title: Design of General Arrangement (GA) of a Ship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of General Arrangement (GA) of a Ship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Arrangement (GA) of a Ship** is the master engineering document that defines the **spatial distribution, geometric form, and functional zoning** of every compartment, structure, system, and access route within the hull envelope of a vessel. It is the single integrated drawing (typically presented as a **Sheer Plan**, **Body Plan**, and a series of **Deck/Platform Plans**) on which the Naval Architect consolidates hull form, subdivision, machinery placement, cargo/ballast tankage, accommodation, and safety-of-life provisions into one coherent, regulation-compliant layout.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the GA is the *output artefact* of the **Ship Spatial Design Process**, and it acts as the contractual and regulatory baseline for:
- Hull structural scantlings determination
- Outfitting and piping routing
- Electrical load take-off
- Statutory surveys (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, Flag State)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ship as a Floating High-Rise Apartment"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine designing a **40-storey residential + commercial complex** that must (a) float, (b) move forward on its own power, (c) survive a *flood* in any one section without sinking, (d) house 30+ people for months, and (e) carry refrigerated and dry cargo. The GA is the **architectural master plan** of this floating building — it dictates *who sleeps where, where the engines sit, where the fuel is stored, where lifeboats hang, and how water-tight bulkheads divide the structure so that a single breach does not doom the whole vessel.*

The vertical dimension is the ship's **depth (D)**, the horizontal length is **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)**, and the breadth is **Beam (B)**. Just like a building has floors (decks), the ship has **decks, platforms, and flats** that subdivide the internal volume into usable zones.

---

## 1.3 KTU Board-Relevant Terminology Glossary

| Term | Board-Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Sheer Plan** | Longitudinal vertical projection (side view) showing hull profile, deck edges, bulkheads | Primary GA view |
| **Body Plan** | Transverse sections showing hull cross-sections at frame stations | Hull-form integration with subdivision |
| **Deck Plan** | Horizontal cut through each deck showing compartment boundaries | Compartment-by-compartment design |
| **Watertight Bulkhead (WT BH)** | A division meeting SOLAS Ch. II-1 watertight integrity | Damage-stability core |
| **Subdivision Load Line (SL Line)** | Reference line used for damage-stability calculations | SOLAS regulation 4 |
| **Permeability (μ)** | Fraction of a compartment's volume that can be flooded | Damage stability input |
| **Floodable Length** | Maximum permissible length of a flooded compartment | SOLAS Regulation 5 |
| **Margin Line** | Line 76 mm below the bulkhead deck edge | Statutory reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Constant to Memorise:** The SOLAS **margin line** is fixed at **76 mm (3 inches)** below the bulkhead deck at the side. Any progressive flooding must not submerge this line — this is a recurring 3-mark question trigger.

---

## 1.4 Why GA Design is the Heart of Ship Design

The GA is **not** a standalone drawing — it is the *integrator* that consumes inputs from every other design discipline:

```
Hull Form ──┐
            ├──►  GENERAL ARRANGEMENT  ──►  Statutory Compliance + Buildability
Propulsion ─┤
Electrical ─┤
Safety ─────┘
```

Every change to displacement, block coefficient, or machinery selection ripples back into the GA, making it the **iterative convergence point** of the entire design spiral.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of GA Design Logic

The design of a ship's GA is performed in **five sequential layers**. Each layer is a *pre-condition* for the next.

### Layer 1 — Mission & Operational Requirements
- **Voyage profile:** coastal, ocean-going, restricted service
- **Cargo mix:** bulk, container, tanker, Ro-Ro, passenger
- **Crew complement:** statutory minima + owner's policy
- **Speed / range / endurance**
- *Output:* Spatial envelope, deck count, tank volume budget

### Layer 2 — Regulatory Framework (Mandatory Inputs)
- **SOLAS 1974** — Chapters II-1 (Subdivision & Stability), II-2 (Fire Protection), III (Life-saving), IV (Radio)
- **MARPOL Annex I/IV/V** — Oil pollution, sewage, garbage
- **Load Line Convention** — Freeboard and subdivision
- **Flag State & Class Rules** — DNV, LR, ABS, IRS
- *Output:* Watertight bulkhead positions, fire-class boundaries, escape route topology

### Layer 3 — Hull Form Constraints
- **Length (LBP / LWL)**, **Beam (B)**, **Depth (D)**, **Draft (T)**, **Block Coefficient (C_B)**
- **Bilinear / V-shaped hull form** influencing machinery space size
- **Bow flare & stern overhang** affecting fore/aft GA closure
- *Output:* Available internal volume = $LBP \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_{block}$

### Layer 4 — Functional Zoning (The Core Design Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "3-Zone Rule" of KTU GA Design:**
> 1. **Operating Zone** — Bridge, machinery spaces, cargo spaces, working flats
> 2. **Habitation Zone** — Cabins, mess rooms, recreation, hospital, galley, pantries
> 3. **Service & Safety Zone** — Pump rooms, CO₂ rooms, emergency generator, lifeboat embarkation

Each zone has its **own deck allocation, ventilation class, and fire integrity rating**.

### Layer 5 — Compartmentation & Subdivision
- Watertight bulkheads placed at SOLAS-derived **floodable length** intervals
- Fire-rated divisions (A-60, A-30, A-0, B-15, C) per SOLAS Ch. II-2
- A-class divisions: **A-60** (highest), **A-30**, **A-15**, **A-0**
- B-class: **B-15** (1 hr)
- C-class: continuous to deck but not gas-tight

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — GA Design Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Note:** The following formulae appear in **over 70% of KTU ESE Part-B (14-mark) GA questions.** Memorise with units.

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block Coefficient | Hull-volume availability |
| 2 | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot A_M}$ | Prismatic Coefficient | Hull form & GA clearance |
| 3 | $\mu_i = \dfrac{V_{flood,i}}{V_{gross,i}}$ | Permeability of compartment *i* | Damage-stability input |
| 4 | $f = \dfrac{L_{max}}{L_{BP}}$ | Permissible floodable length factor | SOLAS Reg. 5–8 |
| 5 | $s_{i} = f \cdot \ell_i$ | Permissible length of compartment *i* | Bulkhead positioning |
| 6 | $A = \sum p_i \cdot s_i$ | Subdivision Index (Attained) | SOLAS damage-stability |
| 7 | $R = A_{required} - A_{attained}$ | Residual subdivision index | Compliance check |
| 8 | $T_{mean} = \dfrac{T_F + T_A}{2}$ | Mean Draft | Freeboard check |
| 9 | $LCB = \dfrac{\int x \cdot A(x)\,dx}{\int A(x)\,dx}$ | Longitudinal Centre of Buoyancy | Trim & GA balance |
| 10 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric Height | Stability & GA topping-weight |
| 11 | $D_{margin} = 0.076\text{ m}$ (fixed) | Margin line below bulkhead deck | SOLAS statutory constant |
| 12 | $F_{FS} = \dfrac{f \cdot L_{BP}}{N_{compartments}}$ | Floodable-length share | Iterative bulkhead spacing |

### Critical SOLAS Floodable Length Factor (Regulation 5, Curve Form)

The factor **f** is read from the SOLAS curve as a function of the **subdivision load line (SL)**. For KTU board problems, a tabular approximation is acceptable:

| SL Ratio | $f$ | SL Ratio | $f$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.10 | 0.233 | 0.50 | 0.587 |
| 0.20 | 0.344 | 0.60 | 0.645 |
| 0.30 | 0.448 | 0.70 | 0.695 |
| 0.40 | 0.521 | 0.80 | 0.745 |

> **Why this matters in GA:** The value of **f** dictates *how far apart* you may place watertight bulkheads. A low SL (small freeboard) → small **f** → bulkheads must be closer → **more compartments, more doors, more cost.**

---

## 2.3 The "Why-How" Behind Each Design Step

| Step | Why it is done | How it is done |
|---|---|---|
| Place engine aft | Reduce shaft length, isolate noise/vibration from cabins, free centre for cargo | Locate ER at **0.65 L to 0.78 L aft** of AP for single-screw merchant ships |
| Place bridge forward | Maximise cargo hold length, give OOW clear forward view ≥ 2× ship's length | Bridge typically at **0.02 L to 0.05 L aft of FP** |
| Segregate fuel tanks from accommodation | SOLAS Reg. II-2 — fire safety | **Cofferdams** (empty spaces ≥ 760 mm) between tanks and A-class boundaries |
| Position bow thruster room | Maneuvering in port | Forward, below waterline, with watertight tunnel access |
| Add double bottom | Damage stability + tankage + structural strength | Height = **B/20** to **B/15**, span full LBP (where required) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

GA design is the **single most rework-intensive** step in shipbuilding. A poorly executed GA causes:
- Costly **re-piping** during outfitting (≈ 8–12 % of build cost)
- Class-rule **non-conformities** → dry-docking penalty
- Crew-ergonomic failures → operator complaints, insurance premiums rise
- Damage-stability failure → **vessel detention** at port-state inspection

Conversely, an optimal GA minimises steel weight, shortens build duration, and earns **class notation** upgrades.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Permissible Length of a Compartment (SOLAS Reg. 5)

**Given:**
- Length Between Perpendiculars, $L_{BP} = 120\text{ m}$
- Service: General Cargo, single-screw
- Subdivision load line (SL) coincides with summer load line
- Number of compartments flooded in damage case = **2**

**Find:** Permissible length of any one compartment.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the subdivision load line ratio

For a standard general-cargo ship with summer load line as SL, the **SL ratio** is read from the SOLAS diagram. For our case, summer load corresponds to a subdivision index, and the **factor f** is interpolated from the curve.

$$
\text{SL Ratio} = \frac{\text{Freeboard to SL}}{\text{Total freeboard}} = 0.45
$$

### Step 2 — Read the factor **f** from the SOLAS curve

From the curve table at SL Ratio = 0.45:

$$
f = 0.554 \quad \text{(interpolated between 0.40 → 0.521 and 0.50 → 0.587)}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Linear interpolation between (0.40, 0.521) and (0.50, 0.587):
> $$f = 0.521 + \frac{(0.45 - 0.40)}{(0.50 - 0.40)} \cdot (0.587 - 0.521)$$
> $$f = 0.521 + 0.5 \cdot 0.066 = 0.521 + 0.033 = 0.554$$

### Step 3 — Compute permissible compartment length

The permissible length for a single compartment when **2** compartments are flooded simultaneously is the product of **f** and a flooding-multiplier **k = 1.0** for symmetric cases:

$$
L_{perm} = f \cdot L_{BP} \cdot k
$$

$$
L_{perm} = 0.554 \cdot 120 \cdot 1.0
$$

$$
L_{perm} = 66.48\text{ m}
$$

### Step 4 — Check against typical engine-room length

For a single-screw cargo ship with B = 18 m, the engine room length is typically **8 to 12 m**, well within the permissible limit. Therefore:

$$
L_{ER,actual} \approx 10\text{ m} \quad \ll \quad 66.48\text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{COMPLIES}
$$

### Step 5 — Final bulkhead spacing

If we use **5 watertight bulkheads** along the cargo length (90 m), each compartment length is:

$$
L_{comp} = \frac{90}{4} = 22.5\text{ m}
$$

Since $22.5 \text{ m} \ll 66.48 \text{ m}$, the bulkhead spacing is **safe by SOLAS subdivision criterion**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Subdivision Index A (Attained)

**Given:**
- 4 watertight compartments in the floodable region
- Each compartment's permissible length: $s_1 = 50, s_2 = 60, s_3 = 55, s_4 = 45\text{ m}$
- Survival probability factors (SOLAS Reg. 7-2): $p_1 = 0.8, p_2 = 0.7, p_3 = 0.6, p_4 = 0.9$

**Find:** Attained subdivision index **A**.

---

### Step 1 — State the summation formula

$$
A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i
$$

### Step 2 — Compute each term explicitly

$$
p_1 \cdot s_1 = 0.8 \cdot 50 = 40.0
$$
$$
p_2 \cdot s_2 = 0.7 \cdot 60 = 42.0
$$
$$
p_3 \cdot s_3 = 0.6 \cdot 55 = 33.0
$$
$$
p_4 \cdot s_4 = 0.9 \cdot 45 = 40.5
$$

### Step 3 — Sum the contributions

$$
A = 40.0 + 42.0 + 33.0 + 40.5
$$

$$
A = 155.5
$$

### Step 4 — Compare with required subdivision index **R**

For this ship class, SOLAS requires $R = 0.5 \cdot L_{BP} \cdot N = 0.5 \cdot 120 \cdot 4 = 240$ (simplified form).

> *Conversion logic:* A is sufficient when **A ≥ R**.

Since $A = 155.5 < 240$, the design **DOES NOT comply** — the naval architect must:
- Reduce compartment lengths (more bulkheads), **or**
- Move bulkheads to higher-survival zones, **or**
- Re-route survival factor $p_i$ by adjusting machinery layout

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Draft and GA Trim Check

**Given:**
- Forward draft $T_F = 5.20\text{ m}$
- Aft draft $T_A = 5.80\text{ m}$
- LBP = 120 m

**Find:** Mean draft, trim, and freeboard compliance check.

---

### Step 1 — Mean draft

$$
T_{mean} = \frac{T_F + T_A}{2} = \frac{5.20 + 5.80}{2}
$$

$$
T_{mean} = \frac{11.00}{2} = 5.50\text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 — Trim

$$
\text{Trim} = T_A - T_F = 5.80 - 5.20 = +0.60\text{ m (trim by the stern)}
$$

### Step 3 — LCB (Longitudinal Centre of Buoyancy) sanity

For a ship of $C_B = 0.68$, LCB typically lies at $\approx 1\%$ to $2\%$ LBP aft of midship.

$$
\text{LCB}_{\text{typical}} \approx 0.015 \cdot 120 = 1.8\text{ m aft of midship}
$$

This **small stern LCB** explains why the GA places the heavier machinery (engines, generators, steering gear) **aft** — to align gravity (LCG) with buoyancy (LCB) and minimise trim.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Typical GA Component Placement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** "Where would you place X in the GA? Justify." Memorise this matrix.

| Component | Longitudinal Position (% LBP aft of AP) | Vertical Deck | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine Room | 65–78 | Lower deck / platform | Short shaft, noise isolation |
| Boiler Uptake | 60–65 | Through all decks to funnel | Exhaust routing |
| Bridge | 95–98 | Compass deck / navigation deck | Visibility |
| Funnel | 60–70 | Above nav deck | Draught clearance |
| Forepeak Tank | 0–3 | Below waterline | Slamming protection |
| Afterpeak Tank | 97–100 | Below waterline | Stern-tube bearing lubrication |
| Double Bottom Tanks | 10–90 | Lowest compartment | Ballast + structural |
| Cargo Holds | 15–60 | Tween decks + lower hold | Main revenue zone |
| Accommodation | 60–95 | Above main deck | Vibration-free, light, ventilated |
| Galley / Pantry | 70–80 | Main deck | Near crew, separate from cabins |
| Hospital | 75–85 | Above main deck | Quiet, well-lit |
| Lifeboats | 90–95 | Boat deck | Embarkation to deck |
| CO₂ Room | 65–75 | Outside accommodation block | Fire safety segregation |
| Emergency Generator | 80–90 | Above waterline, outside machinery block | Flood survivability |
| Paint / Lamp Room | 70–80 | Outside accommodation | Fire hazard segregation |

---

## 3.5 KTU Numerical — Tank Volume to GA Length Conversion

**Given:**
- Double-bottom ballast tank volume requirement: $V_{DB} = 1200\text{ m}^3$
- Tank breadth = ship breadth = 18 m
- Tank height (typical DB height) $h_{DB} = B / 15 = 1.2\text{ m}$
- Forepeak ballast tank: $V_{FP} = 350\text{ m}^3$, average cross-section $A_{FP} = 22\text{ m}^2$

**Find:** Required GA length for each tank zone.

---

### Step 1 — Double-bottom tank length

$$
L_{DB} = \frac{V_{DB}}{B \cdot h_{DB}} = \frac{1200}{18 \cdot 1.2}
$$

$$
L_{DB} = \frac{1200}{21.6} = 55.56\text{ m}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Treat the tank as a rectangular prism; $V = L \cdot B \cdot h$, so $L = V / (B \cdot h)$.

### Step 2 — Forepeak tank length

$$
L_{FP} = \frac{V_{FP}}{A_{FP}} = \frac{350}{22}
$$

$$
L_{FP} = 15.91\text{ m}
$$

### Step 3 — GA integration

These lengths are subtracted from the cargo-hold region when allocating watertight bulkheads. The cargo length remaining is:

$$
L_{cargo} = L_{BP} - L_{DB} - L_{FP} - L_{ER} - L_{ACCOM} - L_{bridge}
$$

$$
L_{cargo} = 120 - 55.56 - 15.91 - 10 - 18 - 3 = 17.53\text{ m (insufficient!)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** This signals a redesign — the GA **must** either increase tank height ($h_{DB}$), or accept side tanks, or shorten accommodation, or extend LBP. **This is the iterative nature of GA design that KTU examiners love to test.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Place Bulkhead Candidate] --> Q[Compute Permissible Length using SOLAS f factor]
    Q --> R{Actual length L actual is less than or equal to L perm}
    R -- Yes --> S[Accept Bulkhead Position]
    R -- No --> T[Shift Bulkhead or Add Additional Subdivision]
    T --> P
    S --> U[Record Permeability mu and Survival Factor p]
    U --> V[Compute Attained Index A]
    V --> W{A is greater than or equal to Required R}
    W -- Yes --> X[GA Subdivision Compliant]
    W -- No --> Y[Re-evaluate Zone Allocation]
    Y --> P
```

flowchart TB

```

> **Reading the diagram:** This represents the **vertical zoning** logic from the highest deck down to the keel. The same ship contains all four zones stacked along the **depth (D)** axis. **Cargo → Machinery → Stores → Hull** in the vertical sense (in addition to the longitudinal cargo-machinery-accommodation split).

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Access and Escape Route Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cabins[Crew Cabins A Class Boundary] --> Corr1[Main Corridor]
    Corr1 --> StairA[Main Stairway A Class Enclosed]
    StairA --> BoatDeck[Boat Deck Embarkation Station]
    Corr1 --> Escape2[Secondary Escape A 30 Boundary]
    Escape2 --> BoatDeck
    EngineRm[Engine Room] --> Tunnel1[Shaft Tunnel Escape Trunk]
    Tunnel1 --> StairA
    Cargo[Cargo Hold] --> Hatches[Weatherdeck Hatches]
    Hatches --> BoatDeck
```

> **Why this matters:** SOLAS Chapter II-2 mandates **two independent escape routes** from every main accommodation zone, separated by **A-class divisions**. The GA drawing must physically show these routes.
