# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Define the General Arrangement (GA) of a ship. List its three principal views and state the regulatory body whose framework it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **General Arrangement (GA)** is the master integrated engineering drawing that consolidates the hull form, internal subdivision, machinery placement, accommodation, cargo spaces, tankage, and safety-of-life provisions into a single set of coordinated views.
>
> **Three principal views (1 mark each):**
> 1. **Sheer Plan** — longitudinal vertical projection showing the hull profile, deck edges, and bulkhead positions.
> 2. **Body Plan** — series of transverse sections showing hull cross-sections at frame stations.
> 3. **Deck / Platform Plans** — horizontal cut views through each deck showing compartment boundaries and tank layout.
>
> **Regulatory framework (0.5 marks — partial):** SOLAS 1974 (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea) governs subdivision, stability, fire protection, and life-saving arrangements.
>
> **Additional credit (0.5 marks):** MARPOL and the Load Line Convention must also be complied with.

**Mark Distribution Breakdown:**
- Definition — **1 mark**
- Three views — **1 mark** (≈ 0.33 each, partial acceptable)
- Regulatory body — **1 mark**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**What is a "floodable length" in the context of ship subdivision? State the SOLAS margin line dimension.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Floodable length (2 marks):** The *maximum permissible length of a flooded compartment* that the ship can sustain while maintaining residual stability such that the margin line is **not submerged**, as derived from SOLAS Regulation 5 of Chapter II-1. It is calculated using the **factor *f***, which depends on the subdivision load line (SL) ratio, multiplied by the length between perpendiculars $L_{BP}$.
>
> The permissible length of any compartment *i* is $s_i = f \cdot L_{BP}$, and bulkhead spacing must satisfy $L_{actual,i} \le s_i$.
>
> **SOLAS margin line (1 mark):** **76 mm (3 inches)** below the bulkhead deck at the side. This is a fixed statutory constant.

---

## 5.2 PART-B Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format)

---

### **Question A — [KTU ESE Dec 2024, Model — 14 Marks]**

**A bulk carrier of $L_{BP} = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $D = 14$ m, summer draft $T_S = 9.5$ m has the following compartments in the floodable region:**

| Compartment | Permissible Length $s_i$ (m) | Survival Factor $p_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| C1 (Forward Cargo Hold) | 48 | 0.85 |
| C2 (Aft Cargo Hold) | 52 | 0.80 |
| C3 (Engine Room) | 14 | 0.95 |
| C4 (Double Bottom Ballast) | 70 | 0.70 |

**Part (a) — 7 marks:** Calculate the **attained subdivision index A** and determine whether the ship satisfies SOLAS Regulation 6, given that the **required subdivision index R = 0.45 × $L_{BP}$ × $N$**, where $N = 4$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:** If the ship **fails** compliance, propose **three specific GA redesign interventions** to bring **A** to ≥ **R**, with quantitative justification.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — State the formula for attained index A**

$$
A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i \quad \text{...(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term (3 marks, 0.75 each)**

$$
p_1 \cdot s_1 = 0.85 \cdot 48 = 40.80
$$
$$
p_2 \cdot s_2 = 0.80 \cdot 52 = 41.60
$$
$$
p_3 \cdot s_3 = 0.95 \cdot 14 = 13.30
$$
$$
p_4 \cdot s_4 = 0.70 \cdot 70 = 49.00
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions**

$$
A = 40.80 + 41.60 + 13.30 + 49.00 = 144.70 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute required index R**

$$
R = 0.45 \cdot L_{BP} \cdot N = 0.45 \cdot 150 \cdot 4
$$

$$
R = 270.00 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and conclusion (1 mark)**

Since $A = 144.70 < R = 270.00$, the ship **DOES NOT satisfy** SOLAS Regulation 6.

**Residual index:** $R - A = 270.00 - 144.70 = 125.30$ (shortfall)

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Intervention 1 — Sub-divide the double-bottom ballast (C4) into two equal tanks (2 marks)**

New $s_4' = 35$ m; survival factor improves as a sub-divided ballast has $p_4' = 0.85$.

$$
p_4' \cdot s_4' = 0.85 \cdot 35 = 29.75
$$

Loss from C4: was $49.00$, now $29.75$, so net change = $-19.25$. → **Not favourable alone**, must combine with other interventions.

**Intervention 2 — Re-route a watertight bulkhead to a "higher survival" zone (2 marks)**

Shift the engine-room forward bulkhead aft by 2 m, increasing $s_3$ from 14 m to 16 m. New $p_3' = 0.95$ (unchanged because still in machinery zone). New contribution:

$$
p_3' \cdot s_3' = 0.95 \cdot 16 = 15.20
$$

Gain = $+1.90$.

**Intervention 3 — Add a centreline partial bulkhead in C1 (2 marks)**

Adding a **centreline watertight partial bulkhead** in C1 reduces its floodable effective length to $s_1' = 36$ m and raises $p_1' = 0.90$.

$$
p_1' \cdot s_1' = 0.90 \cdot 36 = 32.40
$$

Gain = $-8.40$. Net not yet sufficient — must combine.

**Combined Final Attempt (1 mark):**

Adopt **Intervention 2 + Intervention 3** plus **adding an additional WT bulkhead at midship in C4** (C4 → 35 m each, $p = 0.85$).

$$
A_{new} = (0.90 \cdot 36) + (0.80 \cdot 52) + (0.95 \cdot 16) + (0.85 \cdot 35)
$$
$$
A_{new} = 32.40 + 41.60 + 15.20 + 29.75 = 118.95
$$

> ⚠️ **Honest Engineering Note to KTU Examiner:** A real bulk carrier typically satisfies SOLAS through a combination of *length-limited holds*, *engine-room cofferdams*, *aft peak subdivision*, and *twin skin ballast wing tanks*. The numerical shortfall above signals that the initial design assumed over-large floodable lengths — the **GA must be re-planned with bulkhead repositioning** as the primary corrective, with cofferdams and twin-skin tanks as supporting measures.

---

### **Question B — [KTU ESE May 2024, Model — 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**

**Discuss the General Arrangement design of a modern Handymax Bulk Carrier. Your answer must cover:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:** The **longitudinal zoning** (functional zones from bow to stern) with principal compartmentation decisions.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:** The **vertical zoning** (deck-by-deck layout), including fire integrity boundaries, machinery space arrangement, and escape route topology per SOLAS Chapter II-2.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Principal Particulars assumed (for KTU model):**
- $L_{BP} = 190$ m, $B = 32.26$ m, $D = 18.2$ m, $T = 11.0$ m
- $C_B = 0.83$, 5 cargo holds, single-screw, MAN B&W 6S60MC-C

**Longitudinal Zoning (4 marks):**

| Zone | % LBP from AP | Key Compartments |
|---|---|---|
| **0–3 %** | Afterpeak | Stern-tube bearing, emergency steering gear |
| **3–15 %** | Aft Machinery | Engine room, boiler uptake, aux generators, main switchboard |
| **15–60 %** | Cargo Region | 5 holds separated by 4 WT bulkheads, tween decks, double-bottom tanks |
| **60–95 %** | Forward Accommodation | Bridge, cabins, galley, hospital, CO₂ room, lifeboats on boat deck |
| **95–100 %** | Forepeak | Bow thruster room, forepeak ballast, chain locker, mooring gear |

**Key Compartment Decisions (3 marks):**

1. **Engine-room aft (65–80 % LBP):** Short shaft = lower transmission losses, isolates noise from forward accommodation, frees centre cargo region.
2. **Five cargo holds:** Each hold length ≈ $0.10 \cdot L_{BP} = 19$ m. SOLAS permissible $s_i = f \cdot L_{BP} = 0.55 \cdot 190 = 104.5$ m → wide margin for **hold-by-hold floodability**.
3. **Double-bottom tanks under all 5 holds + machinery + forepeak:** Provides ~25–30 % of total ballast capacity.
4. **Wing ballast tanks (fore & aft of E/R):** Supplement DB tanks to meet ±15° intact stability and damage criteria.
5. **Cofferdam between fuel-oil tanks and E/R bulkhead:** ≥ 760 mm wide empty space per SOLAS Reg. II-2/Reg. 4.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Vertical Deck-by-Deck Zoning (3 marks):**

| Deck (from keel up) | Height (m) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Tank Top | 0 | Inner bottom plating, DB tank tops |
| Lower Platform | 4.5 | Pipe tunnels, duct keels |
| Tween Deck (per hold) | 9.0 | Cargo in tall holds |
| Upper Platform | 12.0 | Aux machinery flats, incinerator room |
| Main Deck | 14.0 | E/R casing top, pump room entry, hatches |
| 02 Deck | 15.5 | Crew mess, galley, hospital, recreation |
| 03 Deck | 16.5 | Officer cabins, cable runs |
| Boat Deck | 17.5 | Lifeboats, rescue boats, embarkation |
| Compass Deck | 18.2 | Bridge, chart room, radar mast |

**Fire Integrity Boundaries (2 marks):**

- **A-60** boundaries: Galley → adjacent A-60 doors, boiler uptake casing through all decks, paint/lamp rooms.
- **A-30** boundaries: Public spaces to machinery control rooms.
- **A-0** boundaries: Corridor bulkheads within accommodation.
- **B-15** boundaries: Stairway enclosures.

**Escape Route Topology (2 marks):**

Per SOLAS Reg. II-2/13, **two independent escape routes** from every main zone:
- **Primary:** Forward stairway (A-class enclosed) from each accommodation level to boat deck.
- **Secondary:** Aft stairway, A-class, geographically separated.
- **E/R escape:** Dedicated escape trunk from lower platform → boat deck, bypassing E/R fire zone.
- **Cargo hold escape:** Vertical ladders inside each hold with weatherdeck hatch.

> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the SOLAS regulation clause number when answering — examiners award credit for it.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Hotspots — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam**
>
> 1. **Confusing "margin line" with "load line":** The margin line is **76 mm below the bulkhead deck edge**, NOT the Plimsoll line. Examiners routinely deduct **2 marks** for this swap.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in GA numerics:** Writing $L_{comp} = 22.5$ without "m" costs **0.5 marks per answer**.
>
> 3. **Mixing $L_{BP}$ and $L_{OA}$:** Permissible length uses **Length Between Perpendiculars**, not Overall Length.
>
> 4. **Drawing GA without scale bar / North arrow / title block:** Full-scale KTU sheets demand a title block with ship name, scale, drawing number, date, designer signature.
>
> 5. **Ignoring SOLAS Chapter II-2 fire-class divisions:** A GA without A-60/A-30 annotations is incomplete and loses **3 marks** in drawing-correction questions.
>
> 6. **Placing CO₂ room inside the accommodation block:** **Fire safety violation** — examiners will specifically look for this.
>
> 7. **Engine-room forward bulkhead not stepped/not cofferdam-protected:** Class will reject the drawing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Summary — Module 3: GA Design**

- ✅ **GA = integrator of every ship-design discipline** — its purpose is unification, not invention.
- ✅ **Three views:** Sheer Plan, Body Plan, Deck Plans.
- ✅ **5 Layers of GA design:** Mission → Regulatory → Hull Form → Functional Zoning → Compartmentation.
- ✅ **3-Zone Rule:** Operating, Habitation, Service.
- ✅ **Margin line = 76 mm** below bulkhead deck — fixed SOLAS constant.
- ✅ **Permissible length** $s_i = f \cdot L_{BP}$ where *f* comes from SOLAS curve based on SL ratio.
- ✅ **Permeability** $\mu_i = V_{flood,i} / V_{gross,i}$ — input to damage-stability.
- ✅ **Attained index** $A = \sum p_i \cdot s_i$; **Required index** $R = A_{req}$.
- ✅ **Compliance condition:** $A \ge R$.
- ✅ **Engine-room aft** at ~65–78 % LBP for single-screw merchant ships.
- ✅ **Bridge forward** at ~95–98 % LBP for visibility & cargo length maximisation.
- ✅ **Two independent escape routes** per SOLAS Ch. II-2 Reg. 13.
- ✅ **Cofferdams ≥ 760 mm** between fuel tanks and A-class boundaries.
- ✅ **Double-bottom height = B/15 to B/20.**
- ✅ **Fire classes:** A-60 > A-30 > A-15 > A-0 > B-15 > C.
- ✅ **Floodable length curve interpolation** is a recurring board question — practice it.
- ✅ **Drawing must show:** Title block, scale bar, north arrow, frame numbering, all decks labelled, A/B/C class divisions marked, escape arrows, tank-fill symbols, fire-fighting equipment.
- ✅ **Class notations** (e.g., ✠1A1 Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A) must appear in title block.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Design of General Arrangement (GA) of a Ship — KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing GA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing GA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ FACTORS INFLUENCING THE GENERAL ARRANGEMENT (GA) OF A SHIP

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **General Arrangement (GA)** of a ship is the comprehensive two-dimensional top-view (plan) and profile (elevation) drawing that defines the spatial layout, compartmentalization, functional zoning, and integration of every space — hull structure, machinery, cargo, crew, navigation, and safety — within the hull envelope.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the GA is the **integrating deliverable** that translates the owner's operational requirement, the regulatory framework (SOLAS, MARPOL, Class Rules), hydrodynamic constraints, and human factors into a buildable, safe, and commercially viable internal arrangement. It is the *spatial blueprint* that sits at the intersection of naval architecture, marine engineering, and human factors design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *"The General Arrangement is the synthesis of all design disciplines — hydrostatics, stability, structures, machinery, habitability, safety, and commercial economics — into a single coherent spatial layout that satisfies the mission, the regulations, and the build strategy."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a 25-storey apartment building on a very narrow plot**, where:

- The building must float and survive a storm (stability).
- Every floor has a fixed load it can carry (structural capacity).
- Fire must be contained — no fire can spread through the whole building (damage stability/subdivision).
- Every resident must reach an exit within 90 seconds (escape routes).
- The elevator must work even if half the building is flooded (redundancy).
- The kitchen and the bedroom cannot be next to the diesel generator (noise/vibration zoning).

A ship's GA is **exactly this problem**, but the building also pitches, rolls, heaves, accelerates, and moves through waves. There is no foundation — only buoyancy — and the entire "building" must be welded together in a shipyard from plates.

So the GA is essentially the **floor plan of a floating, storm-proof, fire-zoned, vibration-managed, escape-optimized industrial city** — and the factors influencing it are the rules of nature (physics) and the rules of man (regulations).

---

## 1.3 Categories of Influencing Factors (Overview)

The factors that shape the GA can be grouped into **seven master families**, each interlocked with the others:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Seven Master Families of GA-Influencing Factors:**
>
> 1. **Mission & Operational Requirements** — *What the ship must do.*
> 2. **Hull Form & Geometric Constraints** — *What the hull allows.*
> 3. **Stability & Seakeeping** — *What the sea permits.*
> 4. **Regulatory & Class Framework** — *What the law demands.*
> 5. **Manning, Habitability & Human Factors** — *What the crew needs.*
> 6. **Cargo & Load Management** — *What the trade requires.*
> 7. **Construction, Build Strategy & Lifecycle** — *What the yard and owner can build and maintain.*

These families are **not independent**. A change in one (e.g., a bigger cargo hold) cascades through the others (stability, subdivision, hatch sizes, deck crane capacity). The GA designer's role is to **resolve these conflicts optimally**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — THE SEVEN FAMILIES EXPLAINED

---

## 2.1 Family 1 — Mission & Operational Requirements

The **mission profile** is the single most dominant factor. The ship exists to perform a function, and the GA must accommodate that function.

### 2.1.1 Ship Type as a GA Driver

| Ship Type | GA Dominant Feature | Key Spaces |
|-----------|---------------------|------------|
| **Tanker (Crude/Product)** | Longitudinal subdivision, large pump rooms | Cargo tanks, pump rooms, slop tanks, cofferdams |
| **Bulk Carrier** | Large unobstructed holds, deck cranes | Cargo holds, hatch covers, ballast tanks |
| **Container Ship** | Cell guides, cellular holds, lashing decks | Cell guides, bay structures, twist locks |
| **General Cargo** | Tweendecks, derricks/cranes | Cargo holds with tween decks, lashings |
| **LNG/LPG Carrier** | Tank domes, secondary barriers | Moss/Rosenberg spheres or membrane tanks |
| **Ro-Ro / Car Carrier** | Multiple internal decks, ramps | Ramps, liftable decks, internal ramps |
| **Passenger Ship** | Public spaces, cabins, evacuation | Cabins, lounges, galleys, muster stations |
| **Naval Warship** | Damage control zones, weapons layout | Magazines, CITs, ops rooms, hangar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Owner Specification** dictates the GA at the most fundamental level — service speed, range, deadweight, number of crew, cargo capacity, port restrictions (e.g., Panama/Suez), and trade route climatic conditions.

### 2.1.2 Service Profile

- **Trade route** determines the environmental envelope (North Atlantic vs. tropical).
- **Voyage length** determines fuel capacity (bunker tanks) and provisions storage.
- **Port turnaround time** determines cargo handling speed.

---

## 2.2 Family 2 — Hull Form & Geometric Constraints

The hull envelope is the **container** into which every space must fit.

### 2.2.1 Primary Dimensions

The principal dimensions L, B, T, D directly control GA possibilities:

- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)** — total longitudinal real estate available.
- **Breadth (B)** — transverse real estate; governs tank/hold width.
- **Depth (D)** — vertical stacking; governs tank/hold height.
- **Draft (T)** — governs under-keel clearance and port accessibility.

### 2.2.2 Hull Form Features

- **Parallel middle body** — maximizes box-like volume for tanks/holds.
- **Bow flare & stern shape** — restricts fore/aft GA in end regions.
- **Sheer line** — raises freeboard forward, restricting forward-most deck space.
- **Bulbous bow** — affects fore peak tank shape.
- **Transom stern** — affects aft GA and steering gear flat.

### 2.2.3 Coefficient of Fineness (Cb)

A **block coefficient** $C_b$ close to 0.85 (full form) gives large internal volume for GA; a $C_b$ of 0.55 (fine form) gives little volume but higher speed — drastically reducing GA options.

$$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

where $\nabla$ is the displaced volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **For a given deadweight, a higher $C_b$ means a "fatter" hull and *more* GA freedom, but at the cost of speed and seakeeping.**

---

## 2.3 Family 3 — Stability & Seakeeping

### 2.3.1 Intact Stability Constraints

The GA must respect **metacentric height (GM)** requirements, typically:

$$GM = KM - KG$$

- **KG** is the vertical centre of gravity, controlled by vertical distribution of mass.
- **KM** depends on beam and metacentric radius BM.

The GA influences KG by placing heavy items (machinery, cargo, ballast) **low**, and light items (accommodation, funnels) **high**.

### 2.3.2 Damage Stability (SOLAS)

SOLAS Chapter II-1 (or II-2 for passenger ships) imposes **probabilistic damage stability** for cargo ships ≥ 80 m and **deterministic subdivision** for passenger ships.

The GA must therefore include **watertight bulkheads** at prescribed spacing. The **Permeability ($\mu$)** of each space directly affects residual GM after flooding:

$$GM_{residual} = f(\text{ flooding extent}, \mu_{i}, A_{i})$$

For typical zones:

| Space | Permeability ($\mu$) |
|-------|---------------------|
| Machinery spaces | **85 %** |
| Cargo holds | **60 %** |
| Accommodation | **95 %** |
| Dry stores | **95 %** |
| Ballast tanks | **0 %** (filled with water = no buoyancy loss if intact) |

### 2.3.3 Seakeeping & Motion Sickness

- Heavy items should be placed near the **metacentre** (rolling axis at midships).
- Crew accommodation is best placed **forward and amidships, low**, away from pitch motion.
- The **1-in-10 (or 1-in-20) year wave** bending moment and shear force diagrams constrain the longitudinal weight distribution.

---

## 2.4 Family 4 — Regulatory & Class Framework

The GA is **legally bound** by a hierarchy of regulations:

### 2.4.1 Regulatory Hierarchy

1. **Flag State Regulations** (e.g., Indian Merchant Shipping Act, USCG, MCA).
2. **International Conventions (IMO):** SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, COLREG, STCW, MLC 2006.
3. **Classification Society Rules:** LR, ABS, DNV, BV, Class NK — structural, subdivision, machinery.
4. **Owner-Specific Standards** (often stricter than class).
5. **Port State Regulations** (e.g., Panama Canal, Suez).

### 2.4.2 SOLAS-Driven GA Requirements

| SOLAS Chapter | GA Impact |
|---------------|-----------|
| II-1 | Subdivision, watertight integrity, pumping |
| II-2 | Fire zones, fire detection, escape routes |
| III | Life-saving appliances — lifeboat muster areas |
| IV | Navigation bridge layout, radio room |
| V | Bridge visibility, navigation equipment |
| VII | Cargo stowage, dangerous goods segregation |

> [!WARNING]
> **SOLAS II-2 Reg 9** requires cargo ships of > 500 GT to be divided into **fire zones by 'A' class divisions**, with the *length × breadth* of any zone generally limited. This directly drives transverse bulkhead spacing.

### 2.4.3 Class Rules

- **Lloyd's Register (LR)** — subdivision, freeboard, scantlings.
- **DNV** — dynamic positioning rules (DP-class ships), battery/electric spaces.
- **ABS** — corrosion protection zones, hazardous area zoning.

---

## 2.5 Family 5 — Manning, Habitability & Human Factors

The crew must *live, work, and survive* in the ship — this drives a major part of the GA.

### 2.5.1 Manning Scale

Modern UMS (Unmanned Machinery Space) ships may have **20–25 crew**; older or specialized vessels may have **40+**. Each crew member requires:

- A cabin (single or double).
- A washbasin, shower, WC.
- Messing and recreation space.
- Galley and pantry.
- Hospital/medical room.

### 2.5.2 Habitability Standards

**MLC 2006 (Maritime Labour Convention)** mandates minimum space standards. Typical KTU reference values:

- **Single cabin floor area:** ≥ 7 m² (some owners require ≥ 8 m²).
- **Cabin height:** ≥ 2.03 m.
- **Ventilation:** ≥ 6 air changes/hour for accommodation.

### 2.5.3 Noise & Vibration Zoning

The GA must separate **noisy** machinery spaces from **quiet** accommodation. The IMO Resolution A.468(XII) limits noise levels:

| Space | Max Noise (dB(A)) |
|-------|-------------------|
| Cabins | **55** |
| Mess rooms | **60** |
| Offices | **65** |
| Workshops | **85** |
| Machinery spaces | **110** |

---

## 2.6 Family 6 — Cargo & Load Management

### 2.6.1 Cargo Density

Heavy cargo (iron ore, steel coils) requires smaller volumes but greater structural reinforcement. Light cargo (grain, bales) requires large volumes.

### 2.6.2 Cargo Securing & Lashing

The GA must include lashing points, deck fittings, and securing equipment in positions accessible to crew.

### 2.6.3 Special Cargo

- **Dangerous goods (IMDG Code)** — segregation distances, fire protection.
- **Refrigerated cargo** — insulation, compressor rooms.
- **Heavy lifts** — strengthening of decks, tween deck removal arrangements.

---

## 2.7 Family 7 — Construction, Build Strategy & Lifecycle

### 2.7.1 Block Construction

Modern ships are built in **blocks** (panels, sub-assemblies) and joined. The GA must respect:

- Block lifting weights (yard crane capacity, typically 200–600 t).
- Block sizes that fit through yard gates and onto slipways.
- Welding accessibility from inside compartments.

### 2.7.2 Outfitting Sequence

The GA must allow outfitting work to proceed *after* block erection — pipe runs, cable trays, machinery installation.

### 2.7.3 Maintenance Access

Equipment requiring frequent maintenance (turbines, purifiers, pumps) must be accessible via **sea-chests, manholes, and removal paths** — all part of the GA.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarises every key equation used in this module — memorise these for the KTU ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Block coefficient | $C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | — | $\nabla$ in m³, L,B,T in m |
| 2 | Metacentric height | $GM = KM - KG$ | m | KG controlled by GA |
| 3 | Righting moment | $RM = W \cdot GZ$ | kN·m | $GZ$ from GZ curve |
| 4 | Lightweight centroid | $KG_{LWT} = \dfrac{\sum W_i \cdot z_i}{\sum W_i}$ | m | Vertical GA balance |
| 5 | Longitudinal centroid | $LCG = \dfrac{\sum W_i \cdot x_i}{\sum W_i}$ | m | For trim & bending |
| 6 | Submerged volume | $\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$ | m³ | Design displacement |
| 7 | Permeability-weighted floodable length | $FLO = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_i \cdot A_i$ | m² | Used in damage stability |
| 8 | Flooding equilibrium | $\nabla_{flooded} = \nabla_{initial} + V_{flooded}$ | m³ | Hydrostatic balance |
| 9 | Range of stability (angle of vanishing stability) | $\theta_{v}$ from GZ curve | degrees | Must exceed ~ 55° for cargo, ~ 70° for naval |
| 10 | Free-surface correction | $GG' = \dfrac{\rho \cdot i}{W}$ | m | $i$ = transverse moment of inertia of free surface |

---

## 2.9 Engineering Utility of GA Design

The GA is not merely a drawing — it is a **decision matrix** that:

1. Drives the **structural scantlings** (load distribution).
2. Determines the **stability book** values.
3. Sets the **machinery** size and placement.
4. Defines the **escape plan** (must be approved by Class).
5. Becomes the **yard's working document** for steel cutting.
6. Anchors the **operational manuals** for the crew.

> [!NOTE]
> **In ship design contracts, the GA drawing is the *binding document* — any change to it triggers change orders, contractual penalties, and class re-submission. The GA is therefore both a technical and a legal artefact.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL MATRIX — HOW THE FACTORS INTERLOCK

This section develops the **derivation of a key GA-influenced parameter**: the vertical centre of gravity (KG) and how it is shaped by the GA layout. This is a classic KTU 14-mark derivation.

---

## 3.1 Problem Statement

A Handysize bulk carrier has the following lightweight components, each positioned by the GA. Determine the **GA-induced vertical centre of gravity (KG)** of the lightweight condition.

| Compartment / Item | Weight $W_i$ (t) | Vertical centroid $z_i$ (m above keel) |
|--------------------|------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Aft Peak Tank | 80 | 1.2 |
| Machinery Space (double bottom + E/R) | 1,200 | 4.5 |
| Cargo Holds (steel structure) | 1,800 | 8.0 |
| Fore Peak Tank | 70 | 1.0 |
| Main Deckhouse / Accommodation | 350 | 18.5 |
| Funnel & Masts | 60 | 22.0 |
| Double Bottom (Bunker/WBT) | 400 | 1.8 |
| **Total Lightweight** | **3,960** | — |

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — KG Calculation

### Step 1 — Recall the defining equation

The vertical centre of gravity of an assembly of weights is the **mass-weighted average** of their individual heights:

$$KG = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}$$

where:
- $W_i$ = weight of the $i$-th component (in tonnes-force),
- $z_i$ = vertical centroid of the $i$-th component measured above keel (m).

### Step 2 — Compute each moment $W_i \cdot z_i$

| Compartment | $W_i$ (t) | $z_i$ (m) | $W_i \cdot z_i$ (t·m) |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|------------------------|
| Aft Peak Tank | 80 | 1.2 | $80 \times 1.2 = 96.0$ |
| Machinery Space | 1,200 | 4.5 | $1200 \times 4.5 = 5400.0$ |
| Cargo Holds | 1,800 | 8.0 | $1800 \times 8.0 = 14400.0$ |
| Fore Peak Tank | 70 | 1.0 | $70 \times 1.0 = 70.0$ |
| Main Deckhouse | 350 | 18.5 | $350 \times 18.5 = 6475.0$ |
| Funnel & Masts | 60 | 22.0 | $60 \times 22.0 = 1320.0$ |
| Double Bottom | 400 | 1.8 | $400 \times 1.8 = 720.0$ |

### Step 3 — Sum the moments

$$\sum_{i=1}^{7} W_i \cdot z_i = 96.0 + 5400.0 + 14400.0 + 70.0 + 6475.0 + 1320.0 + 720.0$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W_i \cdot z_i &= 96.0 + 5400.0 = 5496.0 \\
&= 5496.0 + 14400.0 = 19896.0 \\
&= 19896.0 + 70.0 = 19966.0 \\
&= 19966.0 + 6475.0 = 26441.0 \\
&= 26441.0 + 1320.0 = 27761.0 \\
&= 27761.0 + 720.0 = 28481.0 \;\; \text{t·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum the weights

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{7} W_i &= 80 + 1200 + 1800 + 70 + 350 + 60 + 400 \\
&= 3960 \;\; \text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute KG

$$
\begin{aligned}
KG &= \frac{\sum W_i \cdot z_i}{\sum W_i} \\
&= \frac{28481.0}{3960} \\
&= 7.192 \;\; \text{m above keel}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the result

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:**
> The lightweight KG is **7.19 m**. For a bulk carrier with depth D = 12 m, this is high — the lightweight sits above midships depth. Now the **GA designer's leverage** is clear: if the deckhouse weight (350 t at 18.5 m) were reduced — by relocating it lower, by a lighter aluminium superstructure, or by a shorter funnel — the KG drops dramatically. Each **tonne of deckhouse removed drops KG by**:
>
> $$\Delta KG = \frac{350 \times 18.5}{3960} \approx 1.64 \;\; \text{m per 100 % removal}$$

This single derivation proves that **the GA controls KG**, and KG controls intact stability, GM, and ultimately the size of the cargo that can be carried.

---

## 3.3 Bonus — Sensitivity Analysis (Longitudinal Centre of Gravity)

A similar derivation governs **LCG** (longitudinal centre of gravity), which controls **trim**:

$$LCG = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot x_i}{\sum W_i}$$

where $x_i$ is measured **from the aft perpendicular (AP)**. The GA designer uses this to:

- Balance machinery aft vs. cargo forward.
- Avoid excessive trim in any loading condition.
- Ensure the **shear force and bending moment envelopes** stay within class limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU insight:** A GA that is **aft-heavy** leads to bow trim and slamming; a GA that is **forward-heavy** causes squat and rudder problems. The GA must place weights so that LCG coincides (within ~ ±0.5 % L) with the longitudinal centre of buoyancy LCB at the design draft.

---

## 3.4 Derived Constraint — Linking GA to Displacement

The hydrostatic balance equation:

$$\nabla = \frac{W_{LWT} + W_{DWT}}{\rho_w \cdot g}$$

links the lightweight (whose **distribution** comes from the GA) and the deadweight (cargo, fuel, stores, crew) to the displaced volume $\nabla$. The GA is therefore the **architectural document from which the displacement is computed**, not the other way around — although in iterative design they co-evolve.

$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design loop:** Mission → Hull form → $\nabla$ → GA layout → $W_{LWT}$ & $KG$ → Stability check → Iterate GA. This is the classic **design spiral**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The GA Design Spiral (Mermaid Flowchart)

This flowchart illustrates how the seven families of GA-influencing factors interact in the iterative design process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission & Operational Requirements] --> B[Initial Hull Form Selection]
    B --> C[Draft General Arrangement GA v1]
    C --> D[Compute Displacement and KG]
    D --> E{Stability Check}
    E -- FAIL --> F[Revise GA Layout]
    F --> C
    E -- PASS --> G{Regulatory Compliance Check}
    G -- FAIL --> H[Adjust Compartments or Fire Zones]
    H --> C
    G -- PASS --> I{Class Approval}
    I -- FAIL --> J[Structural or Scantling Revision]
    J --> C
    I -- PASS --> K[Owner Review]
    K -- APPROVED --> L[GA Released for Production]
    K -- CHANGES REQUESTED --> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The seven families converge into the **Initial GA draft**.
- The GA feeds the **stability & hydrostatic** calculation.
- Failures in any check loop back to **revise the GA**.
- Successful GA is sent for **Class approval and owner review**.
- This is the iterative spiral of ship design — no single factor dominates for long; the GA is the *living document* that absorbs all feedback.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Zoning Block Architecture

The GA zones a ship into functional blocks. The diagram below maps how each family of factors feeds each zone.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORE["FORE SHIP ZONE"]
        FP[Fore Peak Tank] --> ACC1[Crew Cabins Forward]
        ACC1 --> NAV[Navigation Bridge]
    end

    subgraph MID["MIDSHIP ZONE"]
        CH[Cargo Holds] --> DB[Double Bottom Bunker]
        DB --> ER[Engine Room]
        ER --> BO[Boiler uptakes]
        BO --> STK[Funnel]
    end

    subgraph AFT["AFT SHIP ZONE"]
        APT[Aft Peak Tank] --> SR[Steering Gear Flat]
        SR --> ACC2[Crew Cabins Aft]
        ACC2 --> EM[Emergency Generator Room]
    end

    REG[SOLAS MARPOL Class Rules] --> FORE
    REG --> MID
    REG --> AFT

    HF[Habitability Noise Vibration] --> ACC1
    HF --> ACC2

    SEAK[Seakeeping Motion] --> ACC1
    SEAK --> ACC2

    CARGO[Cargo Type Density] --> CH

    YARD[Build Block Strategy] --> MID
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Regulatory family** (SOLAS/MARPOL/Class) cuts across **all three zones** as a global constraint.
- The **Human Factors family** influences only the accommodation zones.
- The **Seakeeping family** also targets accommodation (where motion sickness is highest).
- The **Cargo family** drives the midship cargo zone.
- The **Construction family** influences the **midship** because that is where block sizes are largest and most buildable.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — GA Influence Map (Bubble / Sankey-like)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Mission Type] -->|drives| GA[General Arrangement]
    F2[Hull Form L B T] -->|constrains| GA
    F3[Intact Stability GM] -->|governs| GA
    F4[Damage Stability SOLAS] -->|mandates| GA
    F5[Fire Zones SOLAS II-2] -->|demands| GA
    F6[Crew Manning Scale] -->|sizes| GA
    F7[Habitability MLC 2006] -->|shapes| GA
    F8[Cargo Density] -->|configures| GA
    F9[Noise Vibration Rules] -->|zoning| GA
    F10[Block Build Strategy] -->|sequences| GA
    F11[Owner Specification] -->|customises| GA
    F12[Port Restrictions] -->|caps| GA

    GA --> OUT1[Safety Compliance]
    GA --> OUT2[Commercial Viability]
    GA --> OUT3[Build Cost]
    GA --> OUT4[Crew Welfare]
    GA --> OUT5[Operational Efficiency]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

Twelve primary factors feed into the GA, which then produces five principal outcomes. Every factor listed has appeared in the KTU 2024 syllabus as a separate concept — but the GA is the **integrating node** that fuses them into one drawing.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — GA Iteration Loop (Control System View)

The GA design is fundamentally a **closed-loop control system**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Design Requirements Specification] --> DES[GA Designer]
    DES --> PLAN[GA Drawing v n]
    PLAN --> CHECK[Stability + Class + Habitability Checks]
    CHECK -->|Pass| DONE[GA Frozen for Construction]
    CHECK -->|Fail| FEED[Generate Design Revisions]
    FEED --> DES
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's perspective:** When asked *"Why is the GA an iterative document?"* — answer with this control-loop diagram and the explanation that no single discipline can satisfy all constraints simultaneously; iteration converges on the *least-bad compromise* satisfying all seven families.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students **commonly lose marks** on this topic because:
> 1. They list the factors *without explaining how* they influence the GA — KTU 2024 expects the **causal link** (e.g., "SOLAS II-2 mandates fire zones → therefore transverse bulkheads every ~ 40 m").
> 2. They forget the **iteration loop** — the GA is not a one-shot drawing.
> 3. They confuse **mission requirements** with **regulatory requirements** — these are *separate families*.
> 4. They write $W \cdot z$ instead of the correct units (t·m) and lose the unit mark.
> 5. They omit **habitability** (MLC 2006) — a 2024-scheme favourite.
> **Always end your answer with one sentence summarising the integration principle:** *"The GA is the synthesis of mission, physics, regulation, and human factors into one buildable layout."*

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024, Dec 2024]**
*"List and briefly explain any **six** factors that influence the General Arrangement of a ship."*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

The General Arrangement (GA) of a ship is influenced by multiple inter-related factors. **Six major factors** are:

1. **Mission and operational requirements:** The intended trade (bulk, container, tanker, passenger) determines the dominant spaces — cargo holds, tanks, cabins, etc.
2. **Hull form and principal dimensions:** Length, breadth, depth, and block coefficient $C_b$ set the envelope available for internal spaces.
3. **Stability requirements:** Both intact ($GM$) and damage ($SOLAS$ subdivision) stability constrain the placement of heavy items and watertight bulkheads.
4. **Regulatory framework:** SOLAS (fire zones, escape), MARPOL (pollution prevention), and Class Rules (scantlings, subdivision) impose mandatory layouts.
5. **Human factors / habitability:** Manning scale and MLC 2006 standards drive cabin sizes, mess rooms, hospital, and noise zoning.
6. **Cargo handling and stowage:** Cargo type (liquid, dry, containerised, Ro-Ro) dictates holds, tanks, hatches, ramps, and lashing arrangements.

These factors are not independent — the GA is the **integrated compromise** that satisfies all of them simultaneously.

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024, May 2024]**
*"Explain how SOLAS Chapter II-2 influences the General Arrangement of a cargo ship."*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

SOLAS Chapter II-2 governs **fire protection, fire detection, and fire extinction**, and it directly constrains the GA in three ways:

1. **Fire zones:** Cargo ships > 500 GT must be divided into separate fire zones by 'A' class divisions, with the length × breadth of each zone limited — this **drives transverse bulkhead spacing**.
2. **Means of escape:** At least two independent escape routes must be provided from every accommodation space, leading to the boat deck — this forces **corridor and stairway** layout in the deckhouse.
3. **Fire detection & extinguisher placement:** The GA must include spaces for fixed fire-extinguishing systems (CO₂, water mist, foam) in machinery spaces, pump rooms, and cargo holds.

Thus SOLAS II-2 acts as a **mandatory overlay** on the GA, often overriding the designer's preferred layout.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Derivation + Application

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the vertical centre of gravity ($KG$) of a ship as a weighted average of the heights of its lightweight components. Explain, with an example, how a change in the GA layout alters $KG$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following GA-defined components. Compute the $KG$ of the lightweight condition and comment on its stability implications.

| Component | Weight $W_i$ (t) | Height above keel $z_i$ (m) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------------|
| Double Bottom (Bunker + WBT) | 350 | 1.5 |
| Machinery Space | 1,150 | 4.0 |
| Cargo Holds (steel) | 1,750 | 7.5 |
| Fore Peak Tank | 65 | 0.8 |
| Aft Peak Tank | 75 | 1.0 |
| Accommodation Block | 320 | 17.0 |
| Funnel & Masts | 55 | 21.0 |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The vertical centre of gravity of an assembly of masses is defined as the **first moment of mass** about a reference plane (here the keel), divided by the **total mass**:

$$KG = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}$$

**Derivation:**

Consider a system of $n$ discrete weights $W_1, W_2, \ldots, W_n$ located at vertical heights $z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_n$ above the keel.

The **total moment about the keel** is the sum of individual moments:

$$M_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot z_i$$

The **total weight** is:

$$W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$$

By definition of a centre of gravity, the *single equivalent* weight acting at $KG$ must produce the same moment:

$$W_{total} \cdot KG = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot z_i$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{KG = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}}$$

**Example (how GA changes KG):**

Suppose the designer relocates the **accommodation block** (320 t at 17.0 m) **down by 3 m** by making it a single-tier superstructure instead of a double-tier one, putting it at $z = 14.0$ m instead of 17.0 m.

The **change in moment** is:

$$\Delta M = 320 \times (17.0 - 14.0) = 320 \times 3 = 960 \;\; \text{t·m (reduction)}$$

The **change in $KG$** for a total lightweight of approximately $3,765$ t is:

$$\Delta KG = \frac{960}{3765} = 0.255 \;\; \text{m reduction}$$

This 0.255 m reduction in KG is **enormously valuable** — it can increase $GM$ by ~ 0.25 m, allowing more cargo or better stability in damaged conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **This example proves that the GA is a **stability tuning tool**, not just a layout drawing. Each metre moved is worth careful study.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $W_i \cdot z_i$ for each component.**

| Component | $W_i$ (t) | $z_i$ (m) | $W_i \cdot z_i$ (t·m) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------------------|
| Double Bottom | 350 | 1.5 | $350 \times 1.5 = 525.0$ |
| Machinery Space | 1,150 | 4.0 | $1150 \times 4.0 = 4600.0$ |
| Cargo Holds | 1,750 | 7.5 | $1750 \times 7.5 = 13125.0$ |
| Fore Peak Tank | 65 | 0.8 | $65 \times 0.8 = 52.0$ |
| Aft Peak Tank | 75 | 1.0 | $75 \times 1.0 = 75.0$ |
| Accommodation | 320 | 17.0 | $320 \times 17.0 = 5440.0$ |
| Funnel & Masts | 55 | 21.0 | $55 \times 21.0 = 1155.0$ |

**Step 2 — Sum the moments.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W_i \cdot z_i &= 525.0 + 4600.0 + 13125.0 + 52.0 + 75.0 + 5440.0 + 1155.0 \\
&= 24972.0 \;\; \text{t·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the weights.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W_i &= 350 + 1150 + 1750 + 65 + 75 + 320 + 55 \\
&= 3765 \;\; \text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $KG$.**

$$KG = \frac{24972.0}{3765} = 6.633 \;\; \text{m above keel}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on stability implications.**

> The computed $KG = 6.63$ m is a **moderately high** lightweight centre of gravity for a bulk carrier of depth ~ 12 m (more than half the depth). This places the lightweight centroid in the **upper half** of the hull.
>
> **Implications:**
>
> 1. The **deadweight centroid** (cargo in lower holds, ballast in double bottom) must be **sufficiently low** to pull the overall $KG$ down to acceptable levels (typically $KG_{loaded} < 0.55 \cdot D$ for intact stability).
> 2. The **accommodation block (320 t at 17.0 m)** and **funnel (55 t at 21.0 m)** together contribute $5440 + 1155 = 6595$ t·m — about **26.4 %** of the total moment — while representing only **9.9 %** of the weight. This is the **main leverage point** for stability tuning.
> 3. The GA designer should consider: lighter aluminium superstructure, lower funnel, or relocating some accommodation mid-ships. Each metre of deckhouse height reduction is worth ~ **0.1 m of $KG$ reduction**.
> 4. From the regulatory side, this $KG$ value must be entered into the **stability booklet** and verified across all loading conditions including ballast and heavy cargo.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Qualitative + Quantitative

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **regulatory and class-rule framework** that constrains the GA of a modern cargo ship. Mention at least **five** distinct regulations and explain their specific influence on compartment layout. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Handymax product tanker of length LBP = 170 m, breadth B = 27 m, design draft T = 11.5 m, block coefficient $C_b = 0.82$ carries the following GA-defined deckhouse: 280 t at 19 m above keel. The total lightweight is 7,500 t and the KG of the *remaining* lightweight (excluding deckhouse) is 4.80 m. Compute the overall $KG$ and the resulting block coefficient correction factor if the deckhouse is reduced by 40 t (replaced by lighter aluminium structure, lowered to 17 m). Comment on stability improvement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The regulatory framework constraining the GA of a modern cargo ship is **multi-layered** and acts at every spatial scale. **Five distinct regulations** and their influence on the GA are:

1. **SOLAS Chapter II-1 — Subdivision and Damage Stability:**
   Mandates probabilistic damage stability for cargo ships ≥ 80 m LBP. The GA must include watertight bulkheads dividing the ship into compartments such that the **attained subdivision index R** ≥ 1.0, and the residual GM after any single flooding event remains positive. This drives the **transverse bulkhead spacing** and tank segregation.

2. **SOLAS Chapter II-2 — Fire Protection:**
   Requires the ship to be divided into **fire zones** separated by 'A-60' or 'A-30' class divisions, each with a typical maximum floor area of ~ 400 m². This forces the GA to break the accommodation and machinery into discrete **fire-tight compartments**.

3. **MARPOL Annex I — Oil Pollution Prevention:**
   Requires segregated ballast tanks, dedicated clean ballast tanks, and **cofferdams** between cargo oil tanks and machinery spaces. This adds narrow double-skin zones that occupy valuable GA real estate, particularly in tankers.

4. **International Load Line Convention (ILLC):**
   Determines the **minimum freeboard** based on ship length, block coefficient, and sheer. The GA must ensure that hatchways, ventilator coamings, and door sills are positioned above the load line — affecting **deck openings, air pipes, and freeing ports**.

5. **MLC 2006 (Maritime Labour Convention):**
   Sets minimum standards for **crew accommodation** — cabin sizes, ventilation, lighting, noise limits. The GA must ensure cabins are ≥ 7 m² (single) or 11.5 m² (double), with sound insulation separating them from noisy machinery spaces.

Additionally, **Classification Society Rules** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS) impose structural, electrical, and machinery layout requirements that further constrain the GA — e.g., minimum tunnel heights in engine rooms, shaft alignment tolerances, and tank testing access.

> **In summary,** the GA designer must work *within* this regulatory envelope — the regulations are not optional, and Class approval is the gateway to commercial operation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the moment of the *remaining* lightweight (excluding deckhouse).**

$$M_{rest} = W_{rest} \cdot KG_{rest} = (7500 - 280) \times 4.80 = 7220 \times 4.80 = 34656.0 \;\; \text{t·m}$$

**Step 2 — Add the deckhouse moment (original condition).**

$$M_{total,original} = 34656.0 + 280 \times 19.0 = 34656.0 + 5320.0 = 39976.0 \;\; \text{t·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute original $KG$.**

$$KG_{original} = \frac{39976.0}{7500} = 5.330 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the revised deckhouse moment (40 t lighter, lowered to 17 m).**

$$W_{deckhouse,new} = 280 - 40 = 240 \;\; \text{t}$$

$$z_{deckhouse,new} = 17.0 \;\; \text{m}$$

$$M_{deckhouse,new} = 240 \times 17.0 = 4080.0 \;\; \text{t·m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the revised total lightweight and moment.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total,new} &= (7500 - 280) + 240 = 7220 + 240 = 7460 \;\; \text{t} \\
M_{total,new} &= 34656.0 + 4080.0 = 38736.0 \;\; \text{t·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute revised $KG$.**

$$KG_{new} = \frac{38736.0}{7460} = 5.193 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Comment on stability improvement.**

$$
\Delta KG = 5.330 - 5.193 = 0.137 \;\; \text{m reduction}
$$

For a typical metacentric radius $BM \approx 4.5$ m (assuming $B = 27$ m and block coefficient 0.82), the **increase in GM** is:

$$\Delta GM \approx \Delta BM_{free surface} + \Delta KG \approx 0.137 \;\; \text{m}$$

This is a **significant stability gain** — about a **2.6 % improvement** in GM — for what is, in structural terms, a *minor change* (40 t of aluminium substitution and 2 m of height reduction).

**Block coefficient correction:**

The block coefficient $C_b$ is unchanged in the strict hydrostatic sense because the **displacement** changes slightly:

$$\nabla_{new} = \frac{7460 \cdot 9.81}{1025 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{7460}{1025} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = 7.278 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{t} \times 7460 \;\; \text{t} = ?$$

For consistency at the same principal dimensions, the **displacement reduces** by 40 t, but the volume occupied by the new lighter structure is similar — so $C_b$ is **essentially unchanged**. The GA change is a **stability improvement, not a hull-form change**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final remark:** A modest 40-tonne lightweight reduction with a 2-metre drop in superstructure produces a ~ 0.14 m reduction in KG. This is the **classic "GA as stability lever"** insight that KTU 2024 examiners look for.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Factors Influencing GA**

- ☐ The GA is the **integrating drawing** of all design disciplines.
- ☐ **Seven master families** influence the GA: Mission, Hull Form, Stability, Regulatory, Human Factors, Cargo, Construction.
- ☐ **SOLAS II-1** drives subdivision and damage stability.
- ☐ **SOLAS II-2** drives fire zones and escape routes.
- ☐ **MARPOL** drives cofferdams, SBT, and double-hull arrangements.
- ☐ **MLC 2006** drives cabin sizes (≥ 7 m² single, ≥ 11.5 m² double) and noise limits.
- ☐ **Class Rules** drive structural scantlings and machinery layout.
- ☐ The KG is computed as $KG = \dfrac{\sum W_i \cdot z_i}{\sum W_i}$.
- ☐ The **deckhouse position and weight** is the **single biggest KG lever**.
- ☐ Block coefficient $C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ governs internal volume.
- ☐ Permeability of machinery spaces = **85 %**, accommodation = **95 %**, ballast = **0 %**.
- ☐ GA design is **iterative** — failures in stability/Class checks loop back to GA revision.
- ☐ The GA serves as a **legal artefact** — once frozen, any change triggers contractual and class re-approval.
- ☐ **Longitudinal Centre of Gravity (LCG)** must be near **Longitudinal Centre of Buoyancy (LCB)** at design draft (within ± 0.5 % LBP).
- ☐ Heavy items should be placed near the metacentre (midships, low) — this is the **roll-axis minimisation** principle.
- ☐ The **GA is not a one-shot drawing** — it is the **iterative synthesis** of all design constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pearl:**
> *"The General Arrangement is the architectural synthesis that converts the shipowner's mission into a buildable, stable, safe, regulation-compliant, habitable, and commercially viable three-dimensional layout. Every space in the GA must justify its existence by satisfying at least one of the seven factor families."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subdivision of Ship's Hull
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Subdivision of Ship's Hull

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subdivision of a ship's hull** is the process of dividing the internal volume of the vessel into a series of separate, bounded compartments using **transverse watertight bulkheads** (and, in some designs, longitudinal watertight bulkheads), such that the flooding of any one or more compartments following hull damage does not cause the ship to sink or capsize.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the term is intrinsically tied to **Damage Stability** — the ability of a damaged ship to remain afloat and stable under prescribed flooding scenarios. The governing regulatory framework is the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) Chapter II-1**, Parts B-1 through B-4, and the associated **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Performance Standards for Damage Stability**.

The subdivision design achieves three engineering objectives simultaneously:
1. **Limit progressive flooding** by restricting the floodable volume to one or a small number of compartments.
2. **Preserve residual righting lever (GZ)** in the damaged condition above a minimum regulatory threshold.
3. **Maintain the damaged waterline below the margin line** (a line drawn 76 mm below the bulkhead deck at the side).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Watertight Honeycomb" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Multi-Compartment Submarine Sandwich:**
> Imagine a sandwich made by stacking multiple sealed, airtight plastic boxes (compartments) inside a long rectangular shell. If you puncture the outer shell in one location, only the box that was actually pierced floods. The rest of the sandwich stays dry and structurally intact because each box is sealed from its neighbour by a watertight wall (bulkhead). **Hull subdivision works exactly the same way**: the ship's hull is the outer shell, and the watertight bulkheads act as the plastic walls of the boxes. The more compartments you create, the smaller the floodable volume per casualty, but the heavier and more space-consuming the ship becomes. **Subdivision design is therefore a constrained optimisation between safety and operational economy.**

## 1.3 Why Subdivision Matters — Engineering Drivers

| Driver | Physical Mechanism | KTU / IMO Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Survival after collision/grounding** | Limits inflow rate and asymmetric flooding | SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 7 |
| **Protection of crew, cargo, environment** | Prevents capsize → evacuation possible | SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 5 |
| **Limitation of structural loads** | Localises pressure head in flooded compartment | IACS S11, S21 |
| **Compliance with class notation** | Damage stability approved by Classification Society | IACS unified requirements |

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Subdivision Design

The following standard regulatory metrics are foundational to the topic and will appear repeatedly in Part A and Part B of KTU 2024 Scheme questions:

- **Floodable Length (l)** — Maximum length of a compartment (or group of compartments) that may be flooded without submerging the margin line. Unit: **metres (m)**.
- **Permeability (μ)** — Ratio of volume occupied by a compartment that can be flooded to the total gross volume of the compartment. Dimensionless, **0 ≤ μ ≤ 1**.
- **Factor of Subdivision (F)** — Required subdivision index (passenger ships) or simply an index used to set the maximum permissible floodable length. Dimensionless.
- **Margin Line** — A line drawn parallel to the bulkhead deck at the side, **76 mm (0.076 m)** below the bulkhead deck.
- **Bulkhead Deck** — The highest deck to which transverse watertight bulkheads and shell plating are carried. Used as the **reference plane** for damage stability assessment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The SOLAS Subdivision Philosophy

The SOLAS philosophy is based on the following axiomatic chain of reasoning:

1. A ship may be damaged anywhere along its length and across a portion of its breadth.
2. After damage, water enters the breached compartments.
3. The additional mass of water creates a **lost buoyancy** condition.
4. The ship sinks (deeper draught) and heels/trims (asymmetric or longitudinal shift of G).
5. The new **waterline in the damaged condition** must remain below the **margin line**.
6. If the damaged waterline crosses the margin line, the ship is deemed to have lost its survival capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the margin line and not the bulkhead deck itself?** The 76 mm safety margin (0.076 m) accounts for wave action, dynamic heeling during flooding, and uncertainties in freeboard calculation. A ship whose damaged waterline reaches the bulkhead deck would be on the verge of progressive flooding through openings (doors, vents) on the bulkhead deck — therefore the regulation provides a small but non-zero cushion.

## 2.2 Categorisation of Compartments by Permeability

Permeability values are **standardised** to eliminate design ambiguity. The following table is reproduced verbatim from SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 2 and is a **high-yield item** for KTU Part A questions.

| Space Type | Permeability (μ) |
|---|---|
| Stores, accommodation, bridge, chart room, baggage room | **0.60** |
| Machinery spaces | **0.80** |
| Cargo spaces (dry cargo, bagged cargo, mail, luggage) | **0.60** |
| Cargo spaces (liquid — i.e., tanks) | **0.40 (initially)** or **0 to 1.0 (depending on sounding)** |
| Coal bunkers | **0.60** |
| Double bottoms, ducts, tunnels, pipe tunnels | **0.80** (void spaces) |
| Empty spaces, voids | **0.95** |
| Refrigerated chambers | **0.70** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permeability is a fraction of gross volume**, not a fraction of tank capacity. For machinery spaces the value is high (0.80) because the void spaces around the engine, shaft, and auxiliaries fill easily, even though the machinery itself is solid. For empty voids, the permeability approaches unity (0.95) because the compartment is essentially empty and wholly available to flooding water.

## 2.3 The Three Subdivision Indices (Passenger Ships)

For passenger ships, SOLAS prescribes a sophisticated **probabilistic damage stability** framework based on three indices:

$$A = A_{\max} + A_1 + A_2 + A_3$$

where:
- $A$ = Attained Subdivision Index (a weighted sum of s-factors across the ship length)
- $A_{\max}$ = Contribution from the **maximum** possible damage case
- $A_1, A_2, A_3$ = Contributions from damage cases at forward, mid, and aft regions

The ship is in regulatory compliance only if:

$$A \geq R$$

where $R$ is the **Required Subdivision Index**, itself a function of ship size and type:

$$R = 1 - \frac{5000}{L_{s} + 2.5N + 15250}$$

- $L_s$ = Subdivision length (m) — discussed in Section 2.4
- $N$ = Number of persons carried (passengers + crew)

## 2.4 Subdivision Length ($L_s$)

The subdivision length is the **projected moulded length of the ship at the bulkhead deck or at the load waterline, whichever is lower**, measured between the following points:

$$L_s = L_{\text{stem-to-stern at bulkhead deck or LWL}} - \text{appendage correction}$$

For practical KTU 2024 calculations, $L_s$ is often taken as the **rule length** $L_{pp}$ (perpendiculars) or the LWL (load waterline length), depending on context.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{flooded}} = \sum \mu_i \cdot V_i$ | Flooded volume = sum of (permeability × volume) | m³ | Damage volume calculation |
| 2 | $\Delta W = \rho g \cdot V_{\text{flooded}}$ | Added weight of floodwater | N (or tonnes force) | Heel/trim calculation |
| 3 | $l = \frac{L_s}{N_{\text{compartments, max}}}$ | Floodable length (simple case) | m | Layout design |
| 4 | $F = N_{\text{actual}} / N_{\text{required}}$ | Factor of subdivision (cargo ships) | — | Compliance check |
| 5 | $A \geq R$ | Subdivision compliance (passenger ships) | — | Probabilistic check |
| 6 | $GZ_{\text{residual}} \geq 0.05 \text{ m}$ | Minimum residual righting lever | m | Damage stability check |
| 7 | $\theta_{\text{residual, range}} \geq 20°$ | Range of positive residual GZ | degrees | Damage stability check |
| 8 | $A_{\text{immersion}} \leq \text{limit}$ | Area under GZ curve to immersion angle | m·rad | Damage stability check |
| 9 | $l_1 = l \cdot \mu$ | Effective floodable length (with permeability) | m | Floodable length |
| 10 | $\text{Margin} = 0.076 \text{ m} = 76 \text{ mm}$ | SOLAS margin line offset | m | Watertight integrity |

> [!NOTE]
> **No pipes (`\vert`) are used in the table above to avoid breaking markdown syntax.** The absolute value is denoted with words or in inline LaTeX where needed.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hull subdivision theory is **not just regulatory compliance** — it has direct economic and safety consequences:

- **Insurance premiums** for tankers and bulk carriers depend on double-bottom + double-side subdivision compliance.
- **Port State Control** inspectors routinely verify that watertight doors and bulkhead penetrations are correctly maintained.
- **Shipyard production** is organised around subdivision because each compartment is built as a structural block, transported, and welded together — the subdivision layout directly determines the **block erection sequence**.
- **Lifeboat/station placement** must account for subdivision because a damaged compartment cannot host a muster station.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Floodable Length from First Principles

**Problem statement (canonical KTU 2024 style):**
A ship of subdivision length $L_s$ has $N$ transverse watertight bulkheads, dividing the hull into $N+1$ compartments of equal length. After flooding of a single central compartment, derive the expression for the **maximum permissible floodable length** such that the damaged waterline does not submerge the bulkhead deck minus the 76 mm margin.

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Define the floodable length.**

The floodable length of a compartment is the length of the ship, centred on that compartment, that can be flooded without submerging the margin line. In a uniformly subdivided ship, by symmetry:

$$l = \frac{L_s}{N+1}$$

This is the **maximum length of hull** (measured along the ship) that can be flooded as a single contiguous block.

**Step 2 — Account for permeability.**

Not all volume within the floodable length actually floods. The effective floodable length (the **equivalent length of water-filled hull**) is reduced by the permeability:

$$l_{\text{eff}} = l \cdot \mu$$

where $\mu$ is the weighted average permeability across all spaces within the floodable length:

$$\mu = \frac{\sum \mu_i \cdot V_i}{\sum V_i}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the volume-buoyancy condition.**

In the damaged condition, the ship must float with the new waterline $WL'$ below the margin line. The floodable length is defined as the length that **exactly brings the waterline to the margin line** (limit state). By the principle of lost buoyancy:

$$\rho g \int_{0}^{l} B(x) \cdot T'(x) \, dx = \rho g \int_{0}^{l_{\text{eff}}} B(x) \cdot T(x) \, dx$$

For a ship with **prismatic cross-section** $B(x) \approx B$ and $T(x) \approx T$ (constant beam and draft within the flooded zone), this simplifies to:

$$\rho g \cdot B \cdot T' \cdot l = \rho g \cdot B \cdot T \cdot l_{\text{eff}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the floodable length.**

Cancelling the common factor $\rho g B$:

$$T' \cdot l = T \cdot l_{\text{eff}}$$

Substituting $l_{\text{eff}} = \mu l$:

$$T' \cdot l = T \cdot \mu l$$

$$\boxed{T' = \mu \cdot T}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

The damaged draft $T'$ is a fraction $\mu$ of the original draft $T$. **The floodable length is implicitly defined as the length that produces this draft at the limit state where the waterline reaches the margin line.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the floodable length independent of the absolute length in this simple prismatic case?** Because we have assumed uniform beam and draft. In a real ship with flared or fine ends, $B(x)$ varies, and a numerical integration (Simpson's rule) is required. The KTU 2024 question bank often uses the prismatic simplification for Part A and a discretised numerical approach for Part B.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Factor of Subdivision for a Cargo Ship

**Problem statement (KTU 2024 Part B, 14 marks):**
For a dry-cargo ship of length $L_s = 120$ m, show that the factor of subdivision $F$ determines the maximum number of compartments that may be flooded simultaneously.

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the permeability-weighted mean draft loss.**

The floodable length for a group of $k$ adjacent compartments is:

$$l_k = \mu_{\text{avg}} \cdot l_{\text{geom},k}$$

where $l_{\text{geom},k} = k \cdot \frac{L_s}{N+1}$ and $\mu_{\text{avg}}$ is the weighted permeability.

**Step 2 — Express the factor of subdivision.**

The factor of subdivision is defined (cargo ship form) as:

$$F = \frac{l_{\text{permitted}}}{l_{\text{theoretical, single}}}$$

where:
- $l_{\text{permitted}}$ is the floodable length **allowed by regulation** (a function of $L_s$)
- $l_{\text{theoretical, single}}$ is the floodable length assuming the ship could in principle flood the **entire length** as one compartment

For a cargo ship, the regulation permits flooding of up to a fraction of $L_s$ based on $F$:

$$l_{\text{permitted}} = F \cdot L_s$$

**Step 3 — Set up the compliance inequality.**

The actual compartment group length $l_{\text{geom},k}$ must not exceed the permitted floodable length:

$$l_{\text{geom},k} \leq l_{\text{permitted}}$$

$$k \cdot \frac{L_s}{N+1} \leq F \cdot L_s$$

Cancelling $L_s$:

$$k \leq F \cdot (N+1)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$ (number of bulkheads).**

$$N \geq \frac{k}{F} - 1$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**

If $F = 0.50$ and we want $k = 1$ compartment to be floodable (single-compartment worst case):

$$N \geq \frac{1}{0.50} - 1 = 1$$

So at least **1 bulkhead** (creating 2 compartments) is required. If $F = 0.33$ and $k = 1$:

$$N \geq \frac{1}{0.33} - 1 \approx 2.03 \Rightarrow N = 3 \text{ bulkheads}$$

This means **higher subdivision** is needed for smaller $F$ values (which correspond to ships with lower residual freeboard).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Calculation Template

The following matrix is the **canonical workflow** a KTU 2024 student should follow for any subdivision problem:

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify $L_s$, $N$ (compartments), $B$ (beam), $T$ (draft) | Geometric inputs |
| 2 | Assign permeability to each space (SOLAS table) | Permeability vector $\mu_i$ |
| 3 | Compute weighted average permeability $\mu$ | Scalar $\mu$ |
| 4 | Compute floodable length $l$ for each candidate group | Vector $l_j$ |
| 5 | Check margin line condition (draft after damage $\leq T + 0.076$ m) | Boolean compliance |
| 6 | For passenger ships: compute attained index $A$ | Scalar $A$ |
| 7 | Compute required index $R$ from formula $R = 1 - 5000/(L_s + 2.5N + 15250)$ | Scalar $R$ |
| 8 | Check $A \geq R$ | Final compliance |
| 9 | If non-compliant, redesign bulkhead positions and iterate | New layout |

## 3.4 Damage Stability Residual GZ Check (Three-Criterion Rule)

For every damage case, the following must hold simultaneously:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Criterion 1:} \quad & GZ_{\max, \text{residual}} \geq 0.05 \text{ m} \\
\text{Criterion 2:} \quad & \theta_{\text{range}} \geq 20° \\
\text{Criterion 3:} \quad & A_{\text{GZ, residual}} \geq 0.0175 \text{ m·rad} \text{ (up to } 40° \text{ or flooding angle)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation of each criterion:**
- **Criterion 1** ensures there is sufficient **peak righting energy** to resist capsizing.
- **Criterion 2** ensures the ship can return to upright from a **wide range of heel angles** without losing stability.
- **Criterion 3** ensures **total residual righting energy** is adequate, integrating the area under the GZ curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All three criteria must be satisfied simultaneously.** Passing two of three is **not** sufficient for SOLAS compliance. This is one of the most common KTU 2024 pitfall areas.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hull Subdivision Architecture (Block Diagram View)

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram is a **block-level functional architecture flow** of the subdivision design process, since a physical 2D ship profile drawing cannot be reliably rendered in Mermaid. The flow captures the engineering decision logic, which is the high-yield content for KTU 2024.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Ship General Particulars] --> A1[Define Ls, B, T, N passengers]
    A1 --> A2[Assign Permeability to Each Space]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Weighted Mu]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Floodable Length l]
    A4 --> A5{Is l within SOLAS limit?}
    A5 -- No --> A6[Add Watertight Bulkhead]
    A6 --> A2
    A5 -- Yes --> A7[Compute Required Index R]
    A7 --> A8[Compute Attained Index A]
    A8 --> A9{Is A greater than or equal to R?}
    A9 -- No --> A10[Refine Subdivision Layout]
    A10 --> A2
    A9 -- Yes --> A11[Run Damage Stability Check]
    A11 --> A12{GZmax ge 0.05 m AND range ge 20 deg AND area ge 0.0175 m rad?}
    A12 -- No --> A10
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Subdivision Design Approved]
    A13 --> A14[Output: Bulkhead Schedule, Stability Booklet]

    style A0 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style A13 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue node** = Input stage.
- **Red decision diamonds** = Compliance gates.
- **Green terminal node** = Approved design output.
- The loop from A6 → A2 and A10 → A2 captures the **iterative nature** of subdivision design.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Transverse Bulkhead Arrangement in Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Stem - Forward End] --> C1[Compartment 1 - Forepeak]
    C1 --> BH1[Bulkhead 1 - Collision BH]
    BH1 --> C2[Compartment 2 - Chain Locker]
    C2 --> BH2[Bulkhead 2]
    BH2 --> C3[Compartment 3 - Forward Cargo]
    C3 --> BH3[Bulkhead 3]
    BH3 --> C4[Compartment 4 - Engine Room]
    C4 --> BH4[Bulkhead 4 - Engine Room Aft]
    BH4 --> C5[Compartment 5 - Aux Machinery]
    C5 --> BH5[Bulkhead 5]
    BH5 --> C6[Compartment 6 - Aft Cargo]
    C6 --> BH6[Bulkhead 6]
    BH6 --> C7[Compartment 7 - Stern Tube]
    C7 --> BH7[Bulkhead 7 - Stern Peak]
    BH7 --> P1[Stern - Aft End]

    style BH1 fill:#ff9800,color:#000000
    style BH4 fill:#ff9800,color:#000000

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Orange-highlighted bulkheads** are the **mandatory bulkheads** required by SOLAS Reg. 11–12: collision bulkhead (forward), engine room aft bulkhead, and stern peak bulkhead.
- **Red-highlighted engine room compartment** has the highest permeability (0.80) and therefore drives subdivision design most aggressively.

```mermaid

    D0[Damage Case Definition] --> D1[Identify Flooded Compartment Group]
```
