# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define a cofferdam. State the minimum width as per SOLAS regulations and explain its two primary functions on a product tanker.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> A **cofferdam** is an empty, watertight, gas-tight compartment installed between two adjacent tanks to prevent leakage between them. As per **SOLAS Regulation II-1/4.2.1**, the minimum width of a cofferdam is **760 mm**.
>
> **Two primary functions:**
> 1. **Segregation of oil and non-oil spaces** — prevents contamination of fresh water or accommodation spaces from fuel oil/cargo oil leakage.
> 2. **Leak detection and safety barrier** — provides an inspectable void between tanks; in case of leakage, the cofferdam can be sounded, gas-tested, and drained without flooding adjacent spaces.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**List any six minimum standards prescribed by ILO Convention 147 / MLC 2006 for crew accommodation on board merchant ships.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks — ½ mark each):**
>
> 1. **Headroom** ≥ 1.9 m in enclosed spaces, ≥ 2.0 m in cabins.
> 2. **Lighting** of 100–150 lux in cabins, 200 lux in mess rooms.
> 3. **Ventilation** with 6 air changes per hour minimum.
> 4. **Heating/Air-conditioning** maintaining ≥ 22 °C in cold conditions.
> 5. **Noise level** ≤ 75 dB(A) in cabins.
> 6. **Sanitary facilities** — 1 WC + 1 shower per 6 persons; separate facilities for officers and ratings.

---

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Internal Choice A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **General Arrangement of fuel oil, fresh water, ballast, and lubricating oil tanks** in a double-hull product tanker. Explain the rationale behind placing each tank in its location.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **double-bottom ballast tank** in a bulk carrier has the following dimensions: top length = 10.0 m, top breadth = 6.0 m, bottom length = 9.6 m, bottom breadth = 5.7 m, height = 1.4 m. Calculate the **volume of the tank in m³** and its **mass capacity in tonnes when filled with seawater** (ρ = 1.025 t/m³). Add a **10% ullage margin** to determine the safe storage mass.

> **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**
>
> On a double-hull product tanker, the typical GA (transverse cross-section) consists of:
>
> **From bottom to top:**
> - **Double-bottom tanks (DB):** Alternately used for ballast and fuel oil. Located at the lowest level for stability and grounding protection.
> - **Wing (side) ballast tanks (WBT):** Located on port and starboard sides between the inner and outer hull. Dampens roll, provides protection in side collisions.
> - **Cargo tanks (centred, between inner hulls):** Hold the liquid cargo (HFO, oil products, chemicals).
> - **Cofferdams (port and starboard):** Empty spaces between cargo tanks and side ballast tanks. Width ≥ 760 mm.
> - **Pump room:** A segregated space at the forward or aft end of the cargo block. Houses cargo pumps, stripping systems, and gas detection.
> - **Slop tank:** At the aft end of the cargo block, used for tank washings and cargo residuals (MARPOL).
> - **Fore peak tank (FPT):** Forward-most compartment, used as ballast; provides 5% bow buoyancy in collision.
> - **Aft peak tank (APT):** Houses the stern tube and is used as lubricating oil reservoir.
> - **Accommodation block:** Aft of the cargo block, separated by a cofferdam.
>
> **Rationale (Why each location):**
> - **Low-density liquids (FW, Ballast) at bottom:** Lower LCG contribution when full.
> - **Oil tanks double-bottom forward of E/R:** Allow gravity feed, easy heating.
> - **LO tank in APT:** Close to stern tube, gravity return.
> - **Cofferdams everywhere oil meets non-oil:** Code compliance and safety.
>
> **(A neat Mermaid/sketched diagram would be expected — refer to Section 4.1 for the architecture.)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**
>
> **Given:**
> $L_t = 10.0 \text{ m}, \quad B_t = 6.0 \text{ m}$
> $L_b = 9.6 \text{ m}, \quad B_b = 5.7 \text{ m}, \quad h = 1.4 \text{ m}$
> $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$
>
> **Step 1 — Compute the top and bottom areas:**
> $$A_t = L_t \times B_t = 10.0 \times 6.0 = 60.0 \text{ m}^2$$
> $$A_b = L_b \times B_b = 9.6 \times 5.7 = 54.72 \text{ m}^2$$
>
> **Step 2 — Geometric mean of areas:**
> $$\sqrt{A_t \cdot A_b} = \sqrt{60.0 \times 54.72} = \sqrt{3283.2} = 57.298 \text{ m}^2$$
>
> **Step 3 — Apply frustum volume formula:**
> $$V = \frac{h}{3}\left( A_t + A_b + \sqrt{A_t \cdot A_b} \right)$$
> $$V = \frac{1.4}{3}(60.0 + 54.72 + 57.298) = 0.4667 \times 172.018 = 80.29 \text{ m}^3$$
>
> **Step 4 — Mass of seawater:**
> $$M = V \times \rho = 80.29 \times 1.025 = 82.30 \text{ tonnes}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Apply 10% ullage margin:**
> $$M_{safe} = 0.90 \times 82.30 = 74.07 \text{ tonnes}$$
>
> **Result:** The tank can safely store **74.07 tonnes** of seawater as ballast.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Internal Choice B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ILO Maritime Labour Convention (MLC 2006) standards for crew accommodation**, with specific reference to cabin dimensions, mess room area, hospital requirements, ventilation, and lighting levels. State the relevant regulation numbers where applicable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vessel has a **crew complement of 24 seafarers** and a **voyage of 35 days**. The daily fresh water consumption rate is **40 litres/person/day**. Calculate the **total fresh water storage capacity** required. If the tanks are designed as two rectangular tanks with length 4.0 m and breadth 3.0 m, determine the **required height** of each tank. Apply a **20% safety margin**.

> **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**
>
> **MLC 2006 — Standard A3.1** governs accommodation and recreational facilities:
>
> 1. **Cabin dimensions:**
>    - Single officer cabin: ≥ 6.5 m² (often 8–10 m²)
>    - Single rating cabin: ≥ 4.5 m²
>    - Multi-berth (≤4 persons): ≥ 1.5–2.5 m² per person
>    - Bunk minimum: 1.9 m × 0.9 m
>    - Maximum of **2-tier bunks** allowed.
>
> 2. **Mess room area:**
>    - ≥ 1.0 m² per seat for officers and crew separately.
>    - Furnished with tables, chairs, refrigerated provision lockers.
>
> 3. **Hospital requirements (Reg. 3.1.7):**
>    - Mandatory on ships with **>15 crew** and voyage **>3 days**.
>    - Separate from cabins, with adjacent sanitary facilities.
>    - Equipped with bed, medical locker, sink, and isolation.
>
> 4. **Ventilation (Reg. 3.1.4):**
>    - Mechanical ventilation in all enclosed spaces.
>    - **6 air changes per hour** minimum in air-conditioned spaces.
>    - 22 °C maintained in cold conditions; AC maintained.
>
> 5. **Lighting (Reg. 3.1.3):**
>    - Cabins: 100–150 lux
>    - Mess rooms: 150–200 lux
>    - Galley: 300 lux
>    - Engine room: 150 lux
>    - Bridge: 300–500 lux
>    - Natural light from portholes (≤ 355 × 555 mm) supplemented by electric.
>
> **Other features:** Headroom ≥ 1.9 m, noise ≤ 75 dB(A), flooring non-combustible, sanitary facilities: 1 WC + 1 shower per 6 persons.
>
> ---
>
> **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**
>
> **Given:** $N_{crew} = 24$, $d = 35$ days, $q = 0.040 \text{ m}^3/\text{day/person}$
>
> **Step 1 — Compute total volume:**
> $$V_{FW} = N \times d \times q = 24 \times 35 \times 0.040 = 33.6 \text{ m}^3$$
>
> **Step 2 — Apply 20% safety margin:**
> $$V_{design} = 1.20 \times 33.6 = 40.32 \text{ m}^3$$
>
> **Step 3 — Distribute to two tanks (redundancy):**
> $$V_{each} = \frac{40.32}{2} = 20.16 \text{ m}^3$$
>
> **Step 4 — Compute tank height:**
> $$h = \frac{V_{each}}{L \times B} = \frac{20.16}{4.0 \times 3.0} = \frac{20.16}{12.0} = 1.68 \text{ m}$$
>
> **Result:** Each of the two tanks must be **4.0 m × 3.0 m × 1.68 m**, giving a total FW capacity of **40.32 m³**, sufficient for 24 crew for 35 days at 40 L/day with a 20% safety margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ullage margin (10–20%):** Students often compute the *full* tank volume and forget that tanks are **never 100% filled** — vapours and expansion require ullage. This is a **direct 1–2 mark deduction**.
> 2. **Wrong tank-volume formula:** Many students apply $V = L \times B \times h$ to sloped-bottom tanks. The correct approach is the **frustum formula** for tanks with tapered bottom plating.
> 3. **Confusing fuel density:** HFO ≈ 0.98 t/m³, LO ≈ 0.89 t/m³, freshwater = 1.0 t/m³, seawater = 1.025 t/m³. Use the **correct density** for the specific liquid.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why" of tank placement:** Examiners expect reasoning — e.g., "FW tanks are placed in double-bottom because low-density liquid lowers LCG and is segregated from oil."
> 5. **Forgetting MLC Regulation numbers:** Always quote **MLC 2006 Standard A3.1.X** or **SOLAS Reg. II-1/4.2.1** in answers. Quoting just generic "rules" loses 1 mark per regulation reference.
> 6. **Mistaking cofferdam width:** It is **760 mm** (not 1 m, not 0.5 m). Memorize this value.
> 7. **Skipping the GA sketch:** Part (a) of Question A explicitly asks for a "neat sketch" — even a block diagram earns 1–2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### A. Tank Arrangement Essentials
- ⭐ **Cofferdam minimum width = 760 mm** (SOLAS II-1/4.2.1)
- ⭐ **Cofferdam purpose:** segregation + leak detection
- ⭐ **Fore peak tank (FPT):** Bow protection, ballast, 5% buoyancy on collision
- ⭐ **Aft peak tank (APT):** Stern tube LO + ballast
- ⭐ **Fuel oil tanks (FOB):** Double-bottom, forward of E/R, with heating coils (40–60 °C)
- ⭐ **Lubricating oil (LO) tank:** APT or engine room, gravity feed
- ⭐ **Fresh water tanks:** Coated (food-grade epoxy), cofferdam-segregated, 40 L/person/day
- ⭐ **Sludge tank:** Heated, MARPOL Annex I compliant, 12 nm discharge limit
- ⭐ **Sewage holding tank:** MARPOL Annex IV, 1 day/person minimum
- ⭐ **Air pipe diameter ≥ 50 mm** (SOLAS)
- ⭐ **Sounding pipe diameter ≥ 32 mm**

### B. Accommodation Standards Essentials
- ⭐ **MLC 2006 Standard A3.1** is the primary code
- ⭐ **Single officer cabin:** ≥ 6.5 m²
- ⭐ **Single rating cabin:** ≥ 4.5 m²
- ⭐ **Headroom:** ≥ 1.9 m (enclosed), ≥ 2.0 m (cabins)
- ⭐ **HVAC:** 6 air changes/hour minimum
- ⭐ **Temperature:** ≥ 22 °C in cold
- ⭐ **Cabin noise:** ≤ 75 dB(A)
- ⭐ **Hospital:** required if crew > 15 AND voyage > 3 days
- ⭐ **Sanitary:** 1 WC + 1 shower per 6 persons
- ⭐ **Lighting:** 100–150 lux (cabin), 200 lux (mess), 300 lux (galley)
- ⭐ **Porthole:** ≤ 355 × 555 mm with deadlight + storm cover
- ⭐ **Max 2-tier bunks** permitted

### C. Key Formulas to Memorize
- ⭐ **Frustum volume:** $V = \frac{h}{3}(A_t + A_b + \sqrt{A_t A_b})$
- ⭐ **Mass in tank:** $M = V \times \rho$
- ⭐ **Free surface effect:** $\Delta GM = -\dfrac{\rho i}{\rho V_{disp}}$
- ⭐ **FW capacity:** $V = N \times d \times q$, with $q = 0.040 \text{ m}^3/\text{person/day}$
- ⭐ **HVAC:** $Q = V_{room} \times n$, $n = 6$ to $10$

### D. Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ⚠️ Never fill ballast tanks partially — it creates **free surface effect**.
- ⚠️ Always apply **ullage margin** (10–20%) to tank capacities.
- ⚠️ Always use **correct fluid density** in mass calculations.
- ⚠️ Always quote the **regulation number** (e.g., SOLAS II-1/4.2.1) when stating a rule.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Space Allocation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Space Allocation

# Space Allocation in Ship Design — Module 3 (PBNST604)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Space Allocation** is the systematic, iterative engineering process during the **preliminary / contract design stage** in which the total internal volume and deck area of a vessel is partitioned into functionally defined zones — hull envelope, machinery spaces, cargo/operational spaces, crew accommodations, tanks, and safety zones — to satisfy mission requirements, regulatory minima (SOLAS, MARPOL, Class rules), stability constraints, and production economics simultaneously.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Space Allocation is the activity that bridges the *hull form selection* stage (Module 2) and the *General Arrangement (GA)* drawing stage (Module 4). It converts a *"shape in water"* into a *"shape with purpose."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Floating Skyscraper

Imagine you have just purchased a **floating rectangular plot of land** of length $L_{pp}$, beam $B$, and design draft $T$. You must build a multi-story structure on it that must:

- Carry **paying cargo** (revenue-generating, so maximize it).
- House the **engine and fuel** (mandatory, but they smell and vibrate — push them away from passengers).
- Provide **homes for the crew** (habitability regulations apply).
- Stay **upright** when partially flooded (subdivision rules).
- Not **pollute the sea** (tank segregation for MARPOL).
- Be **cheap to build** (standardized block lengths, parallel middle body).

Space Allocation is essentially the **interior architectural planning** of this floating skyscraper, but with extra constraints like **no basements below the waterline for human habitation** and **the centre of gravity must remain low**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Module 2:**
> - Module 2 deals with the **external hull form** (Cb, Cp, Cm, $L/B$, $B/T$).
> - Module 3 deals with the **internal subdivision** of that form into functional volumes.
> - Module 4 deals with the **longitudinal and transverse placement** of those volumes (GA plan).

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Space Allocation

| Pillar | Engineering Driver | Typical Share |
|---|---|---|
| **Mission Fit** | Cargo capacity, speed, range | 50–60% of displacement |
| **Safety & Regulation** | SOLAS subdivision, SOLAS lifeboats, fire zones | 10–15% |
| **Habitability & Operations** | Crew spaces, navigation, hospital, stores | 5–10% |

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Always Bolded)

The following KTU-relevant coefficients **must be internalised** by every student:

- **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)** — ratio of displaced volume to the $L_{pp} \times B \times T$ box.
- **Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)** — ratio of displaced volume to the midship-area-times-length prism.
- **Midship Section Coefficient ($C_M$)** — ratio of midship area to $B \times T$.
- **Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_{WP}$)** — ratio of waterplane area to $L_{pp} \times B$.
- **Longitudinal Prismatic Coefficient ($C_{V}$)** — ratio of displaced volume to $L_{WL} \times A_M$.

The classical relationship:
$$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Space Allocation Philosophy (Why before How)

A naval architect **never starts with empty sheets**. Space Allocation is driven by the **mission profile** that emerged from the *Mission Statement* (Module 1) and the *Hull Form Selection* (Module 2). The philosophy is:

> *"Allocate the **fewest**, **largest**, and **most regular** volumes to the mission (cargo/operational spaces); then **fit** the supporting spaces (machinery, crew, tanks) into the remaining voids without violating regulatory minima."*

This is the **"Cargo-First, Support-Last"** rule taught at **IIT Kharagpur, MIT, and University College London (UCL)** naval architecture programmes.

### 2.2 The Five-Step Logical Procedure

The KTU-recognised procedure for Space Allocation proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Establish the Design Displacement ($\Delta$)**
Use the Deadweight Equation:
$$\Delta = DWT + LWT$$

where the **Deadweight** ($DWT$) is fixed by the owner's cargo requirement and the **Lightweight** ($LWT$) is estimated via the **Mimura/Kato regression equations** or **Biran's formula**.

**Step 2 — Compute the Envelope Volume ($∇$)**
$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot g}$$

In shipbuilding units (metric tonnes, salt water $\rho = 1.025\, t/m^3$):
$$\nabla \;\;[\text{m}^3] = \frac{\Delta \;\;[\text{t}]}{1.025}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Principal Dimensions using $C_B$**
$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$$

Rearranging:
$$L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = \frac{\nabla}{C_B}$$

This single equation governs the **first-order sizing** of the vessel.

**Step 4 — Allocate Volumes by Zone (Longitudinal Subdivision)**

The hull is split longitudinally into:

| Zone | Longitudinal Position | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Fore Peak (FP)** | Forward of collision bulkhead | Chain locker, mooring, forepeak tank |
| **Cargo / Operational Region** | 0.4$L_{pp}$ aft of FP to 0.6$L_{pp}$ before AP | Holds, tanks, vehicle decks |
| **Engine Room (ER)** | 0.2–0.3$L_{pp}$ from AP | Main propulsion, generators, workshops |
| **Aft Peak (AP)** | Aft of machinery casing | Steering gear, emergency generator, lazarette |
| **Apartment / Bridge** | Typically 0.7–0.8$L_{pp}$ from AP | Crew accommodation, navigation |

**Step 5 — Volumetric Verification**
The sum of allocated sub-volumes must equal the total envelope volume within **±2% tolerance**:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i = (1.00 \pm 0.02) \cdot \nabla$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All the following equations are **high-yield** — they appear repeatedly in KTU 2024 End Semester Examinations for PBNST604.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = DWT + LWT$ | Design displacement equation | tonnes |
| 2 | $\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{1.025}$ | Displaced volume (salt water) | m³ |
| 3 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{pp}}$ | Prismatic coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Midship coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ | Fundamental coefficient relation | dimensionless |
| 7 | $A_W = C_{WP} \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B$ | Waterplane area | m² |
| 8 | $\Delta = \rho g \nabla$ | Newton's floatation | N (or t) |
| 9 | $V_{h,k} = V_{hold,k} \cdot \text{stowage factor}^{-1}$ | Cargo volume to mass | m³, t |
| 10 | $N_{crew} = f(\text{ship type}, \text{autonomy})$ | Manning rule (STCW) | persons |

### 2.4 Displacement Breakdown (Real-World Engineering View)

The Lightweight ($LWT$) is itself decomposed (used as a KTU 14-mark standard question):

$$LWT = W_{steel} + W_{outfit} + W_{machinery}$$

- **$W_{steel}$**: Hull structure plating, stiffeners, decks, superstructure. Typical mass coefficient: **$W_{steel} / \Delta \approx 0.30$ to $0.45$** depending on $L_{pp}$.
- **$W_{outfit}$**: Paint, insulation, joinery, piping, electrical, navigation electronics, lifeboats. Typically **$W_{outfit} / \Delta \approx 0.10$ to $0.20$**.
- **$W_{machinery}$**: Main engine, generators, shaft, propeller, boilers. Typically **$W_{machinery} / \Delta \approx 0.05$ to $0.15$**.

For a typical Handysize bulk carrier ($DWT \approx 35{,}000\,t$, $L_{pp} \approx 170\,m$):

$$LWT \approx 0.40 \cdot \Delta$$

For a VLCC ($DWT \approx 300{,}000\,t$, $L_{pp} \approx 320\,m$):

$$LWT \approx 0.20 \cdot \Delta$$

The economy of scale in volume allocation is clearly visible.

### 2.5 The Deadweight Decomposition

$$DWT = W_{cargo} + W_{fuel} + W_{FW} + W_{LO} + W_{stores} + W_{crew\&baggage} + W_{misc}$$

- **Fuel (HFO + DO + MDO)**: Function of range and SFOC.
- **Fresh Water (FW)**: Function of crew × days × per-capita consumption.
- **Lube Oil (LO)**: Typically $0.005 \cdot W_{fuel}$.
- **Stores**: Function of endurance.
- **Crew & Baggage**: $W_{crew} \approx 0.10\,t/\text{person}$ plus luggage.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Mission Statement to First-Cut GA Volumes

The derivation proceeds in **eight rigorous stages**. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly because KTU valuation rewards process clarity over final numbers.

---

#### **Stage 1 — Mission Statement to Deadweight**

Suppose the KTU problem states:

> *"Design a Handysize bulk carrier to carry $30{,}000\,t$ of iron ore at a service speed of $14\,kn$ over an ocean crossing of $10{,}000\,nm$."*

Step A — **Determine the cargo mass** directly from the mission:
$$W_{cargo} = 30{,}000 \;\; \text{t}$$

Step B — **Estimate consumables** using the range equation. For Handysize bulkers, the **Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC)** of a modern MAN B&W 6S60MC-C engine is about $165\,g/kWh$ at CSR. Daily fuel consumption at sea:
$$W_{fuel,day} \approx 70 \;\; \text{t/day}$$

Crossing time:
$$t_{sea} = \frac{10{,}000}{14 \times 24} = 29.76 \;\; \text{days}$$

Sea fuel:
$$W_{fuel,sea} = 70 \times 29.76 = 2083.3 \;\; \text{t}$$

Add port/consumption margin ($+15\%$):
$$W_{fuel,total} = 2083.3 \times 1.15 = 2395.8 \;\; \text{t}$$

Step C — **Fresh water** for 25 crew at $0.20\,t/day$ for 35 days (sea + port):
$$W_{FW} = 25 \times 0.20 \times 35 = 175 \;\; \text{t}$$

Step D — **Lube oil** ($0.5\%$ of fuel):
$$W_{LO} = 0.005 \times 2395.8 = 12.0 \;\; \text{t}$$

Step E — **Stores + Crew + Baggage**:
$$W_{stores+crew} = 25 \times 0.5 + 25 \times 0.15 = 16.25 \;\; \text{t}$$

Step F — **Total Deadweight**:
$$DWT = 30{,}000 + 2395.8 + 175 + 12.0 + 16.25 = 32{,}599 \;\; \text{t}$$

---

#### **Stage 2 — Lightweight Estimation**

Use **Biran's empirical formula** (commonly cited in naval architecture textbooks):
$$LWT \;\;[\text{t}] = C \cdot L_{pp}^{1.5} \cdot (B + 2T)$$

For a Handysize bulk carrier, $C \approx 0.034$. Assume tentative $L_{pp} = 170\,m$, $B = 28\,m$, $T = 10\,m$:
$$LWT = 0.034 \times (170)^{1.5} \times (28 + 20)$$
$$LWT = 0.034 \times 2213.6 \times 48$$
$$LWT = 3611.9 \;\; \text{t}$$

Alternatively, use a coefficient ratio $LWT/\Delta \approx 0.30$ for a 30k DWT bulker (initial guess).

---

#### **Stage 3 — Design Displacement**

$$\Delta = DWT + LWT = 32{,}599 + 3612 = 36{,}211 \;\; \text{t}$$

---

#### **Stage 4 — Displaced Volume**

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{1.025} = \frac{36{,}211}{1.025} = 35{,}328 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

---

#### **Stage 5 — Principal Dimensions from $C_B$**

For Handysize bulk carriers, the typical block coefficient at design draft is **$C_B \approx 0.78$**. Therefore:

$$L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = \frac{\nabla}{C_B} = \frac{35{,}328}{0.78} = 45{,}292 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

With tentative $B = 28\,m$, $T = 10\,m$:
$$L_{pp} = \frac{45{,}292}{28 \times 10} = \frac{45{,}292}{280} = 161.76 \;\; \text{m}$$

Round to $L_{pp} = 162\,m$. This is the **first-pass** principal dimension.

---

#### **Stage 6 — Internal Volume Allocation (the heart of the topic)**

Now partition the hull envelope $\nabla = 35{,}328\,m^3$ into functional zones. The standard split for a bulk carrier is:

| Zone | Typical Volumetric % | Volume (m³) |
|---|---|---|
| Cargo holds (5 holds) | **55%** | 19,430 |
| Double bottom tanks | **8%** | 2,826 |
| Side ballast tanks (SBT) | **9%** | 3,180 |
| Engine room casing + funnels | **5%** | 1,766 |
| Crew accommodation block | **3%** | 1,060 |
| Fore peak + chain locker | **2%** | 707 |
| Aft peak + steering gear | **2%** | 707 |
| Cofferdams, voids, ducts | **6%** | 2,120 |
| **Spherical margin** | **10%** | 3,533 |
| **TOTAL** | **100%** | **35,329** |

The **10% spherical margin** absorbs errors in the **C-MAP** / **Paramarine** software iterations and **SOLAS regulation creep** during detailed design.

---

#### **Stage 7 — Tank Capacity Verification (Regulatory Cross-Check)**

For a 30,000 DWT bulker, MARPOL Reg. 19 (oil pollution prevention) and Class rules require:

- **Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT)** capacity $\geq DWT \times 0.20$:
  $$V_{SBT,req} = 0.20 \times 30{,}000 / 1.025 = 5854 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

Our SBT allocation ($9\%$) gives $3180\,m^3$ — **INSUFFICIENT**.

This triggers an **iterative correction**: increase $T$ slightly to $10.2\,m$, reallocate SBT to **12%** of hull volume, and re-verify. This is the **iterative loop** characteristic of real ship design.

---

#### **Stage 8 — Centre of Gravity (LCG) Calculation**

For each zone, the LCG is computed as the centroid. The total LCG:

$$\overline{LCG} = \frac{\sum_i W_i \cdot x_{cg,i}}{\sum_i W_i}$$

This sets up the **stability analysis** in Module 5.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Studies

When evaluating Space Allocation alternatives, the naval architect uses a trade-off matrix. Example for a 30k DWT bulker between **conventional SBT vs. IMO double-hull**:

| Criterion | Conventional SBT | Double Hull (IACS S21) |
|---|---|---|
| $C_B$ achievable | 0.78 | 0.74 |
| Required $L_{pp}$ for same $DWT$ | 162 m | 168 m |
| Steel weight increase | baseline | $+8\%$ |
| Oil outflow index | meets Reg. 23 | exceeds by 30% |
| Capital cost | baseline | $+5\%$ |

This **decision matrix** feeds back into Step 1, restarting the iterative loop.

---

### 3.3 Stowage Factor — The Cargo Volume Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Stowage Factor (SF)** = volume per unit mass of cargo $[m^3/t]$. Required hold volume:
> $$V_{hold} = W_{cargo} \times SF$$

| Cargo | Density (t/m³) | SF (m³/t) |
|---|---|---|
| Iron ore (lump) | 2.4–3.0 | 0.33–0.42 |
| Coal | 0.85 | 1.18 |
| Grain | 0.75 | 1.33 |
| Bauxite | 1.50 | 0.67 |

For our iron-ore bulker at SF = 0.40:
$$V_{hold,required} = 30{,}000 \times 0.40 = 12{,}000 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

Our 19,430 m³ allocation gives a **volume utilization of 62%**, leaving 38% as **bulking factor margin** — appropriate for iron ore.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

Define the term **Space Allocation** in the context of ship preliminary design. List the four primary functional zones into which the hull envelope is subdivided.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Space Allocation is the systematic engineering activity in the preliminary design phase wherein the total internal volume of the ship's hull envelope ($\nabla$) is partitioned into functionally defined zones — cargo, machinery, accommodation, and tanks — to satisfy mission requirements, regulatory minima (SOLAS, MARPOL), and stability constraints simultaneously, while maintaining volume balance within ±2%.

**Four primary zones (1 mark — list any 4):**
1. **Cargo / Operational zone** (holds, decks, tanks for cargo).
2. **Machinery zone** (engine room, auxiliary spaces).
3. **Crew Accommodation zone** (cabins, mess, hospital).
4. **Tank zone** (fuel, freshwater, lube oil, ballast).
5. **Safety zone** (cofferdams, fire zones, lifeboat stations).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Retest]**

State the relationship between $C_B$, $C_P$, and $C_M$, and explain why $C_B$ is the most important coefficient in Space Allocation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Relationship (1 mark):**
$$C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$$

**Importance of $C_B$ (2 marks):** $C_B$ directly governs the volume of the hull envelope for a given $L_{pp} \times B \times T$ product via $\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$. Since $\nabla$ is fixed by displacement, choosing $C_B$ essentially determines the **trade-off between hull length and hull fullness**. A higher $C_B$ (0.85 for tankers) allows shorter, fatter ships for the same displacement; a lower $C_B$ (0.55 for container ships) requires longer, finer hulls to achieve higher speeds. Thus $C_B$ is the **first design dial** the naval architect turns.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Space Allocation of a 30,000 DWT Bulk Carrier**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBNST604]**

A Handysize bulk carrier is required to carry **$30{,}000\,t$ of iron ore** at a service speed of **$14\,kn$** over a range of **$10{,}000\,nm$**. The vessel must comply with **MARPOL Reg. 19 (SBT requirements)** and **SOLAS Reg. II-1 (subdivision)**. The crew complement is **25 persons**. Sea margin is 15%.

Take:
- $C_B = 0.78$, $C_P = 0.80$, $C_M = 0.975$
- SFOC $= 170\,g/kWh$, $MCR = 8000\,kW$, sea-time power fraction $= 0.85$
- Stowage factor of iron ore $= 0.40\,m^3/t$
- Fresh water consumption $= 0.20\,t/person/day$, endurance $= 35$ days

**Required:** Perform a complete **Space Allocation** for the vessel showing:
**(a) [7 marks]** Deadweight and displacement breakdown, principal dimensions.
**(b) [7 marks]** Internal volume allocation by zone and verification against regulatory minima.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Sea fuel consumption:**

Daily running hours = 24. Daily fuel at sea:
$$W_{fuel,day} = \frac{SFOC \times MCR \times 24}{10^6} \times \text{power fraction}$$

$$W_{fuel,day} = \frac{170 \times 8000 \times 0.85 \times 24}{10^6} = 27.65 \;\; \text{t/day}$$

**Step 2 — Crossing time:**
$$t_{sea} = \frac{10{,}000}{14 \times 24} = 29.76 \;\; \text{days}$$

**Step 3 — Total sea fuel (with 15% margin):**
$$W_{fuel} = 27.65 \times 29.76 \times 1.15 = 946.4 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 4 — Fresh water:**
$$W_{FW} = 25 \times 0.20 \times 35 = 175 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 5 — Lube oil (0.5% of fuel):**
$$W_{LO} = 0.005 \times 946.4 = 4.73 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 6 — Stores + crew + baggage (≈ 0.6 t/person):**
$$W_{stores+crew} = 25 \times 0.6 = 15.0 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 7 — Total Deadweight:**
$$DWT = 30{,}000 + 946.4 + 175 + 4.73 + 15.0 = 31{,}141 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 8 — Lightweight (Biran, $C = 0.034$, assume $L_{pp} \approx 165\,m$, $B = 27\,m$, $T = 9.8\,m$):**
$$LWT = 0.034 \times (165)^{1.5} \times (27 + 19.6)$$
$$LWT = 0.034 \times 2120.6 \times 46.6$$
$$LWT \approx 3359 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 9 — Design Displacement:**
$$\Delta = 31{,}141 + 3359 = 34{,}500 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 10 — Displaced Volume:**
$$\nabla = \frac{34{,}500}{1.025} = 33{,}659 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 11 — Principal Dimensions from $C_B = 0.78$:**
$$L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = \frac{33{,}659}{0.78} = 43{,}152 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

Standard Handysize ratios: $L/B = 5.8$, $B/T = 2.7$. Solve simultaneously:
$$L_{pp} = 162.8 \;\; \text{m} \approx 163 \;\; \text{m}$$
$$B = 28.1 \;\; \text{m} \approx 28 \;\; \text{m}$$
$$T = 10.4 \;\; \text{m} \approx 10 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Check:** $28 \times 10 \times 163 \times 0.78 = 35{,}605 \;\; \text{m}^3$ (close to $\nabla$; iterate if needed).

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Required hold volume:**
$$V_{hold} = 30{,}000 \times 0.40 = 12{,}000 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

With **30% hold-volume margin** (for trimming, peak loads, cargo shift):
$$V_{hold,allocated} = 12{,}000 \times 1.30 = 15{,}600 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

Allocate as 5 holds of $3120\,m^3$ each.

**Step 2 — Fuel tank volume:**
$$V_{FO} = \frac{946.4}{0.98} = 965.7 \;\; \text{m}^3$$
(divide by fuel density $0.98\,t/m^3$).

**Step 3 — Fresh water tank volume:**
$$V_{FW} = \frac{175}{1.00} = 175 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Lube oil tank volume:**
$$V_{LO} = \frac{4.73}{0.90} = 5.3 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Segregated Ballast Tank (MARPOL Reg. 19):**
$$V_{SBT,req} = 0.20 \times 30{,}000 / 1.025 = 5854 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Accommodation block:**
For 25 crew at $25\,m^2/\text{person}$ plus public spaces (galley, mess, hospital, etc., factor 1.4):
$$A_{accom} = 25 \times 25 \times 1.4 = 875 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

Over 4 decks, average deck height 2.5 m:
$$V_{accom} = 875 \times 2.5 \times 4 = 8750 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Engine Room volume:**
$$V_{ER} \approx MCR \times 0.25 = 8000 \times 0.25 = 2000 \;\; \text{m}^3$$
(empirical: $0.2$–$0.3\,m^3/kW$).

**Step 8 — Volume Statement (Summary):**

| Zone | Volume (m³) | % of $\nabla$ |
|---|---|---|
| Cargo holds (5) | 15,600 | 46.3% |
| FO + DO tanks | 966 | 2.9% |
| FW tanks | 175 | 0.5% |
| LO tank | 6 | 0.02% |
| Ballast (SBT) | 5854 | 17.4% |
| Engine room | 2000 | 5.9% |
| Accommodation | 8750 | 26.0% |
| **Sum (allocated)** | **33,351** | **99.0%** |
| Total envelope | 33,659 | 100% |
| **Spherical margin** | **308** | **1.0%** |

The spherical margin is **< 2%**, hence **acceptable**. The vessel satisfies all MARPOL and SOLAS minima.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

A coastal container feeder of **$1200\,TEU$** capacity is to be designed with the following mission data:

- Service speed $= 18\,kn$
- Range $= 5000\,nm$
- Crew $= 22$ persons
- $C_B = 0.62$, $C_P = 0.66$, $C_M = 0.94$
- Average payload per $TEU = 14\,t$, stowage factor $= 2.5\,m^3/t$
- Main engine power $MCR = 12{,}000\,kW$, SFOC $= 165\,g/kWh$

**Required:**
**(a) [7 marks]** Compute the design displacement, principal dimensions, and the cargo hold volume.
**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **structural differences** in Space Allocation between a container ship and a bulk carrier, focusing on cellular holds, deck houses, lashing systems, and engine-room location.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Dead Cargo:**
$$W_{cargo} = 1200 \times 14 = 16{,}800 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 2 — Fuel:**
$$W_{fuel,day} = \frac{165 \times 12000 \times 0.85 \times 24}{10^6} = 40.4 \;\; \text{t/day}$$
$$t_{sea} = \frac{5000}{18 \times 24} = 11.57 \;\; \text{days}$$
$$W_{fuel,sea} = 40.4 \times 11.57 = 467.4 \;\; \text{t}$$
$$W_{fuel} = 467.4 \times 1.15 = 537.5 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 3 — Other consumables (FW + LO + stores + crew):**
$$W_{cons} = 22 \times 0.5 \times 12 + 22 \times 0.15 + 537.5 \times 0.005$$
$$W_{cons} = 132 + 3.3 + 2.7 = 138 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 4 — Deadweight:**
$$DWT = 16{,}800 + 537.5 + 138 = 17{,}476 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 5 — Lightweight (Biran, $C = 0.030$, assume $L_{pp} \approx 170\,m$, $B = 25\,m$, $T = 9\,m$):**
$$LWT = 0.030 \times (170)^{1.5} \times (25 + 18) = 0.030 \times 2213.6 \times 43 = 2856 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 6 — Displacement:**
$$\Delta = 17{,}476 + 2856 = 20{,}332 \;\; \text{t}$$

**Step 7 — Volume:**
$$\nabla = \frac{20{,}332}{1.025} = 19{,}836 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 8 — Principal Dimensions with $C_B = 0.62$:**
$$L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = \frac{19{,}836}{0.62} = 31{,}994 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

Using $L/B = 6.8$, $B/T = 2.6$:
$$L_{pp} = 167 \;\; \text{m}, \quad B = 24.5 \;\; \text{m}, \quad T = 9.4 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 9 — Cargo hold volume:**
$$V_{cargo} = 16{,}800 \times 2.5 = 42{,}000 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

This is **2.1 times the displaced volume**, indicating the cargo is stored **on deck and in cellular holds** (a defining feature of container ships). Container ships carry their cargo in TEU cells that project **above deck** because $\nabla$ cannot accommodate the cube.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Structural and Allocation Differences:**

| Aspect | Bulk Carrier | Container Feeder |
|---|---|---|
| **Hold Geometry** | Large rectangular box holds, single deck | Cellular vertical guides (cell guides), 4–6 tier stacking |
| **Deck House Position** | Aft (on poop deck) | Aft and sometimes forward (island bridge) |
| **Lashing System** | Not required (cargo sits loose) | Twist locks, lashing bridges, turnbuckles for stack stability |
| **Engine Room** | Always aft (1/4 from stern) | Almost always aft to protect crew from emissions |
| **Ballast Configuration** | SBT in double bottom + side tanks | Side ballast tanks only (cargo must not contact hull) |
| **Hatch Covers** | Large single-piece or folding steel | None above cellular holds; cells are open-topped |
| **Freeboard** | Minimal (high density cargo) | Larger (visibility, stack weight) |
| **Apartment Location** | Aft of engine room | Forward superstructure (above bridge deck) |

In a container ship, **the hold volume *exceeds* the hull volume** because cargo is stacked above deck. This is the **most counterintuitive Space Allocation fact** students must remember:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A container ship's required cargo volume is greater than its hull envelope volume.** This is unique among ship types and is the reason container ships look *"top-heavy"* in GA drawings.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Space Allocation Questions (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 15% sea margin** on fuel — losing **1 mark** out of 14.
> 2. **Mixing up displacement equation** — using $\Delta = \rho V$ in SI units without converting $g$, instead of $\Delta\,[t] = V\,[m^3] \times 1.025$. Lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring MARPOL SBT requirement** — students often forget to allocate ballast tanks, triggering regulatory failure and **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Not closing the volume loop** — the sum of allocated volumes must equal $\nabla \pm 2\%$. Forgetting this step costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Wrong sign on stowage factor** — confusing mass per volume with volume per mass. Always write $V = W \times SF$. Lose **1 mark**.
> 6. **Skipping the iteration** — when $C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \neq \nabla$, students must iterate. Single-pass answers lose **2 marks** in long answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Space Allocation (Module 3, PBNST604)**

- [ ] **Space Allocation** = partitioning $\nabla$ into cargo, machinery, crew, and tank zones.
- [ ] Total Deadweight = Cargo + Fuel + FW + LO + Stores + Crew + Margin.
- [ ] $\nabla\,[m^3] = \Delta\,[t] / 1.025$ in salt water.
- [ ] $\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$ — the master sizing equation.
- [ ] **Always** apply 15% sea margin on fuel.
- [ ] **Always** compute $LWT$ via Biran or empirical coefficient.
- [ ] $V_{cargo} = W_{cargo} \times \text{Stowage Factor}$.
- [ ] $V_{SBT} \geq 0.20 \cdot DWT / 1.025$ for tankers/bulkers.
- [ ] Volume closure tolerance: $\sum V_i = (1.00 \pm 0.02) \cdot \nabla$.
- [ ] Container ships carry cargo **above** the hull envelope — hold volume can exceed $\nabla$.
- [ ] Tankers have $C_B \approx 0.80$; container ships $C_B \approx 0.60$.
- [ ] Engine room is universally **aft** for safety and propeller-shaft alignment.
- [ ] Accommodation is typically **forward of engine room**, on upper decks.
- [ ] MARPOL, SOLAS, and IACS Class rules **constrain** allocation — they are not optional.
- [ ] The Space Allocation loop **iterates** until volume, regulation, and stability are all satisfied.
- [ ] The final output is a **Volume Statement** + **GA schematic** feeding Module 4.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Space Allocation.**
> **Next Module:** Module 4 — General Arrangement (Longitudinal and Transverse Placement of Allocated Spaces).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modular Arrangement. Access Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modular Arrangement. Access Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Modular Arrangement (Zone / Block Construction)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modular Arrangement** is a ship construction philosophy in which the vessel is partitioned into discrete, three-dimensional structural units — called *modules* or *blocks* — that are prefabricated, outfitted, painted, and partially commissioned in parallel work zones before being transported to the building dock or slipway for final joining. The arrangement governs the geometric boundaries, weight limits, dimensional envelopes, outfitting density, and joining interfaces between adjacent modules.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of building a ship the same way you would assemble a piece of IKEA furniture — but scaled up to a 200-metre structure weighing thousands of tonnes. Instead of one giant chaotic workspace where welders, painters, electricians and fitters all trip over each other, the shipyard is split into clean, numbered "puzzle pieces." Each puzzle piece (module) is built and finished in its own dedicated workshop, then lifted by a giant crane (goliath or gantry) and welded to the next piece — like a 3D jigsaw. The *arrangement* is the master plan that decides where one piece ends and the next begins.

**Key Standard Metrics:**

| Metric | Typical Value (Modern Merchant Ship) |
|---|---|
| Maximum block weight (single lift) | **600 – 900 tonnes** |
| Maximum block length | **24 – 36 m** |
| Maximum block width | **18 – 24 m** |
| Maximum block height | **8 – 12 m** (deck-house up to 14 m) |
| Tolerances at joining seams | **± 3 mm** for shell plates, ± 5 mm structural |
| Dimensional control reference | Shipbuilding *Datum Line* + station grid |

### 1.2 Access Equipment

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Access Equipment** denotes the complete range of fixed, portable, and powered means provided on board a ship — and during its construction/repair in the yard — to enable safe and ergonomic movement of personnel, tools, and small components to all working positions on the hull, superstructure, machinery spaces, masts, cargo holds, and tanks. It is governed by SOLAS Chapter II-1/II-2, the ILO Safety and Health in Dockwork Convention, and classification society rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine a 12-storey office building that is also a moving ocean-going vessel that rolls, pitches and vibrates 24/7. Every wall, fan casing, funnel, and engine cylinder has to be reached for inspection, cleaning, painting or repair. *Access equipment* is everything that lets a worker climb up to that funnel cap, walk along the side shell at 30 m height, or descend into a double-bottom tank — without falling. It is the ship-equivalent of stairs, ladders, balconies and cherry-pickers, but rated for a corrosive, dynamic marine environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test three sub-topics together — (i) types of modules, (ii) advantages of modular construction, and (iii) classification & SOLAS requirements for access equipment. Memorise the dimensional limits above; they appear as 3-mark short notes almost every exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Philosophy of Modular Arrangement

The arrangement is driven by **five engineering levers** simultaneously:

1. **Transport Limits** — road/rail/barge clearance, goliath crane capacity, dock-door dimensions.
2. **Welding Accessibility** — joint geometry must allow down-hand, semi-automatic welding for high deposition rate.
3. **Outfitting Density** — pipe racks, cable trays, HVAC ducts are pre-installed *before* the block is erected.
4. **Stability During Lift** — centre of gravity of each module must lie within the crane's safe lifting quadrangle.
5. **Tolerance Stack-Up** — joining seams accumulate dimensional error; arrangement must minimise the number of critical interfaces.

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Modules (from small to large)

| Level | Name | Typical Mass | Outfitting % at Pre-Erection |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sub-assembly (panel / stiffener unit) | 5 – 50 t | 0 – 5 % |
| 2 | Section / Panel (flat block) | 50 – 200 t | 10 – 25 % |
| 3 | Block (3-D unit with curvature) | 200 – 600 t | 25 – 45 % |
| 4 | Mega-block / Grand block | 600 – 900 t | 45 – 65 % |
| 5 | Super-block (assembled in dry-dock) | 1 000 – 4 000 t | 60 – 75 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grand Block** and **Super Block** are NOT the same. A *grand block* is a fully outfitted 3-D unit lifted once by the gantry. A *super block* is two or more grand blocks pre-joined on the ground to reduce work at height — used by Hyundai, Daewoo and Meyer Werft for cruise liners and large container ships.

### 2.3 Standard Module Types in a Merchant Ship

```
A. Hull Modules          → Bottom, Side, Deck, Transverse Ring
B. Accommodation Block   → Pre-fitted cabin units (PSC 3-D cabin modules)
C. Engine-Room Casing    → Multi-deck engine-room module
D. Funnel Module          → Pre-painted with exhaust uptakes
E. Bow Module             → Bulbous bow integrated
F. Stern Module           → Rudder trunk + propeller shaft tunnel
G. Superstructure Module  → Bridge wing, deck-house decks stacked
H. LNG Tank Spherical Module (for LNG carriers)
```

### 2.4 Access Equipment — Functional Classification

| Class | Equipment | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertical Access** | Fixed steel ladders, vertical ladders with hoops/cages, companion ladders, stairways | SOLAS Reg. II-1/40, ISO 14122-3/4 |
| **Horizontal Access** | Catwalks, platforms, gratings, gangways | SOLAS Reg. II-1/41 |
| **Working at Height** | Boatswain's chair, suspended stage, cherry-picker, scissor lift | OSHA 29 CFR 1915, EN 12810 |
| **Confined Space** | Tripod + winch, breathing-line access | IMO Res. A.1050(27) |
| **Ship-to-Shore** | Accommodation ladder, pilot ladder, gangway | SOLAS Reg. V/23, ISO 7061 |
| **Tank Entry** | Magnetic-anchor fall-arrest, davit-launched basket | SOLAS Reg. XI-1/7 |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> The following table summarises every dimensioning equation you need for Module 3 questions. LaTeX vertical bars are written using `\mid` to keep markdown tables valid.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum centre-distance between transverse frames | $d = \dfrac{L_{pp}}{N_{frames} + 1}$ | m | $L_{pp}$ = length between perpendiculars |
| 2 | Block lifting slings total vertical load | $F_{lift} = \dfrac{W_{block}}{n \cos\theta}$ | kN | $n$ = number of legs, $\theta$ = sling angle to vertical |
| 3 | Crane utilisation factor | $U_{crane} = \dfrac{W_{block}}{SWL_{crane}}$ | – | Should be $\le$ **0.85** for safe lift |
| 4 | Sling tension for horizontal sling | $T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$ | kN | $\theta$ measured from horizontal |
| 5 | Minimum gangway width | $w_{min} = 600 + 0.4 \, N_{users}$ | mm | Per ISO 7061; never below **600 mm** |
| 6 | Pilot-ladder step spread (evenly) | $s = \dfrac{L_{ladder}}{N_{steps} - 1}$ | m | Standard step **s = 0.30 – 0.36 m** |
| 7 | Required ladder width | $w_{ladder} \ge 350$ mm | mm | Per SOLAS Reg. II-1/40 |
| 8 | Minimum ladder clearance behind rungs | $c \ge 150$ mm | mm | Toe clearance |
| 9 | Free-climb height before hoop/cage required | $h \le 5\,000$ mm | mm | Above this, fall-arrest mandatory |
| 10 | Hoop radius (cage ladder) | $r_{hoop} \ge 350$ mm | mm | Concentric with rung centre-line |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Modular Arrangement** reduces a 24-month build cycle to as little as **8–10 months** for a VLCC by enabling 60–70 % of outfitting to occur at ground level.
- **Access Equipment** is directly linked to seafarer fatalities: roughly **30 %** of all shipboard accidents involve falls from height. The IMO mandated the *Code of Safe Working Practices for Merchant Seafarers* (Res. A.1045(27)) precisely because well-designed access reduces this.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Critical Sling Tension During Modular Lift

**Problem Statement:**
A double-bottom block weighs $W = 480$ tonnes (≈ 4 800 kN). It will be lifted by a gantry crane using a 4-leg sling arrangement. Each sling makes an angle $\theta = 30°$ with the vertical. Determine the tension in each sling leg and verify whether the crane capacity is adequate if the gantry crane SWL = 600 tonnes.

**Step 1 — Convert weight to kilo-Newtons.**

$$
W = 480 \; \text{t} \times 9.81 \; \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2} = 4\,708.8 \; \text{kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Resolve vertical equilibrium.**

For a symmetric 4-leg sling, the total vertical component must equal $W$.

$$
\sum F_{V} \;=\; 4 \, T \cos\theta \;=\; W
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for tension $T$ in a single leg.**

$$
T \;=\; \frac{W}{4 \cos\theta} \;=\; \frac{4\,708.8}{4 \times \cos 30°}
$$

$$
T \;=\; \frac{4\,708.8}{4 \times 0.8660} \;=\; \frac{4\,708.8}{3.4641}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T \;=\; 1\,359.4 \; \text{kN per sling leg} \;\approx\; 138.6 \; \text{t}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute crane utilisation factor $U$.**

$$
U \;=\; \frac{W}{SWL} \;=\; \frac{480}{600} \;=\; 0.80
$$

Since $U = 0.80 \le 0.85$, the lift **is safe**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Had the sling angle been $\theta = 15°$ instead of $30°$, the leg tension would jump to $T = 1\,522$ kN. Always *flatten* the spreader beam to keep $\theta \ge 30°$.

### 3.2 Derivation — Minimum Number of Transverse Frames for a Hull Block

**Given:** $L_{pp} = 220$ m, target frame spacing $d \le 700$ mm for a double-hull tanker.

**Step 1 — Required number of frame spaces.**

$$
N_{spaces} \;\ge\; \frac{L_{pp}}{d} \;=\; \frac{220}{0.700} \;=\; 314.29
$$

Round up.

$$
N_{spaces} \;=\; 315
$$

**Step 2 — Number of frames $N_{frames}$ (one less than number of spaces).**

$$
N_{frames} \;=\; N_{spaces} - 1 \;=\; 314
$$

**Step 3 — Verify average spacing.**

$$
d_{avg} \;=\; \frac{220}{315} \;=\; 0.6984 \; \text{m} \;\approx\; 698 \; \text{mm} \;<\; 700 \; \text{mm} \;\;\checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Derivation — Pilot Ladder Geometry

**Given:** Freeboard $h_f = 9.0$ m. SOLAS Reg. V/23 requires the ladder to extend from the ship's deck to **at least 2 m above the waterline**.

**Step 1 — Required ladder length.**

$$
L_{ladder} \;=\; h_f + 2.0 \;=\; 9.0 + 2.0 \;=\; 11.0 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of steps for uniform spread $s$ in $[0.30, 0.36]$ m.**

Choose $s = 0.32$ m.

$$
N_{steps} \;=\; \frac{L_{ladder}}{s} + 1 \;=\; \frac{11.0}{0.32} + 1 \;=\; 34.375 + 1
$$

Round to integer (must be whole).

$$
N_{steps} \;=\; 35 \;\;\text{steps} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s_{actual} \;=\; \frac{11.0}{34} \;=\; 0.3235 \; \text{m}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Module Type

| Ship Type | Dominant Module Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| VLCC / Suezmax | Grand block bottom + side | Maximum welding work moved off the slipway |
| Cruise Liner | Super block (multi-deck cabin) | Hull divided into 80+ super blocks for parallel outfitting |
| LNG Carrier | Spherical-tank module | Tank + dome fitted as a unit on quay |
| Naval Frigate | Sectional mid-body | Quick turn-around for repair-docking |
| Offshore Jack-up | Leg-and-hull module | Heavy lift at low crane hook height |

### 3.5 Decision Matrix — Selection of Access Equipment

| Working Position | Recommended Access | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Funnel cap (35 m above keel) | Hydraulic platform on funnel deck + internal stair | Avoids vertical ladder > 5 m |
| Double-bottom tank | Tripod + winch + breathing-line | Confined-space entry per SOLAS XI-1 |
| Side shell at freeboard | Boat-launched stage / cherry picker | Permanent ladder not feasible due to sea state |
| Boiler casing | Catwalk + removable hand-rails | Hot-surface area, ergonomic access |
| Mast head | Bosun's chair + fixed mast ladder | One-person occasional access |
| Accommodation block corridors | Internal stair tower | Code-required permanent egress |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Modular Arrangement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Specification] --> B[Module Boundary Planning]
    B --> C[Cutting of Plates and Profiles]
    C --> D[Panel Fabrication Workshop]
    D --> E[Sub-assembly Line]
    E --> F[Block Assembly Bay]
    F --> G[Outfitting at Ground Level]
    G --> H[Painting and Insulation]
    H --> I[Pre-erection Trial and NDT]
    I --> J{Block Weight < SWL of Crane?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Load onto Transporter]
    J -- No --> L[Split into Sub-blocks]
    L --> K
    K --> M[Transfer to Building Dock or Slipway]
    M --> N[Erection and Joining]
    N --> O[Grand Block / Super Block Assembly]
    O --> P[Final Outfitting at Height]
    P --> Q[Launch and Sea Trials]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Modules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Level 1 Sub-assembly"] --> L2["Level 2 Panel Section"]
    L2 --> L3["Level 3 Block 3D unit"]
    L3 --> L4["Level 4 Grand Block"]
    L4 --> L5["Level 5 Super Block"]
    L5 --> L6["Level 6 Complete Ship"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Ship Access Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph External Access
        G1[Ship-to-Shore Gangway]
        G2[Pilot Ladder]
        G3[Accommodation Ladder]
    end
    subgraph Vertical Access
        V1[Fixed Steel Ladder]
        V2[Ladder with Hoop Cage]
        V3[Internal Stair Tower]
    end
    subgraph Horizontal Access
        H1[Permanent Catwalk]
        H2[Removable Platform]
        H3[Grating Panel]
    end
    subgraph Working at Height
        W1[Cherry Picker Boom]
        W2[Scissor Lift]
        W3[Bosun Chair]
    end
    subgraph Confined Space
        C1[Tripod and Winch]
        C2[Breathing Line Duct]
        C3[Tank Entry Basket]
    end
    External Access --> VerticalAccessNode[Vertical Access]
    VerticalAccessNode --> HorizontalAccessNode[Horizontal Access]
    HorizontalAccessNode --> WorkingAtHeightNode[Working at Height]
    WorkingAtHeightNode --> ConfinedSpaceNode[Confined Space Entry]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Pilot Ladder Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sea Level Datum] --> B[Waterline]
    B --> C[2 m Above Waterline Top of Ladder]
    C --> D[Step 35 Final]
    D --> E[Step 34]
    E --> F[Step 33]
    F --> G[...]
    G --> H[Step 2]
    H --> I[Step 1 at Ship Rail]
    I --> J[Ship Deck Platform 9 m above waterline]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — SOLAS Compliance Loop for Access Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Design Stage] --> B[Classification Society Approval]
    B --> C[Fabrication per Approved Drawing]
    C --> D[Installation on Board]
    D --> E[Periodic Survey every 5 years]
    E --> F{Condition OK?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Continue in Service]
    F -- No --> H[Rectify or Replace]
    H --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid instead of physical drawings?** A 3-D hull-block or pilot-ladder geometry would require complex vector CAD. The above block-architecture captures the *functional relationships and process flow* — which is what KTU Module-3 examiners reward in a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Define *modular arrangement* in shipbuilding and list **any four** advantages.

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**  
Modular arrangement is the planning philosophy in which a ship is sub-divided into discrete three-dimensional units (modules or blocks) that are fabricated, outfitted and pre-commissioned in parallel work zones before final joining.  
**Advantages:**  
(i) **Parallel production** — several modules built simultaneously cuts build time by 30–50 %.  
(ii) **Improved quality** — work performed at ground level under controlled workshop conditions.  
(iii) **Enhanced safety** — fewer personnel working at dangerous heights.  
(iv) **Higher outfitting density at pre-erection** — 60–75 % outfitting done before dock joining.  
(v) **Reduced labour costs** — semi-automatic welding and mechanised outfitting possible.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**  
State **four** SOLAS-mandated requirements for fixed vertical ladders on board ships.

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**  
(i) Ladder width must be **≥ 350 mm** clear of obstructions.  
(ii) Vertical ladders exceeding **5 m** in unsupported length must be fitted with hoop-cages or fall-arrest.  
(iii) Toe clearance behind rungs must be **≥ 150 mm**.  
(iv) Rungs must be of non-slip design, evenly spaced, and able to withstand a horizontal pull-out load.  
(v) Ladders must terminate at a safe landing platform with hand-rails on both sides.

---

### Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a labelled diagram, explain the **different levels of modular construction** used in modern merchant-ship building. Mention typical mass ranges and outfitting percentages.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Level 1 — Sub-assemblies (5–50 t, 0–5 % outfitting):** Flat plates with welded stiffeners, prepared in a panel line using T-beam welding machines.
2. **Level 2 — Panels / Sections (50–200 t, 10–25 % outfitting):** Two or more sub-assemblies joined to form a curved or flat shell panel, often with internal brackets.
3. **Level 3 — Blocks (200–600 t, 25–45 % outfitting):** 3-D unit with two or more intersecting planes — typically a double-bottom tank or a side-shell tank.
4. **Level 4 — Grand Blocks (600–900 t, 45–65 % outfitting):** Fully outfitted unit lifted in a single gantry lift; e.g., a complete engine-room casing.
5. **Level 5 — Super Blocks (1 000–4 000 t, 60–75 % outfitting):** Two or more grand blocks pre-joined on the ground to minimise work at height, lifted by twin-gantry or strand-jack systems.
6. **Level 6 — Completed Ship:** Final joining, sea trials and delivery.

The mass progression reflects the lifting capacity of yard cranes, and the outfitting percentage rises because pipe-rack, cable-tray and HVAC installations are progressively integrated.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bow module of a container ship weighs **W = 720 t**. It will be lifted by a gantry crane of SWL = 1 000 t using **four sling legs**, each inclined at **θ = 45° to the vertical**.  
(i) Calculate the tension in each sling leg.  
(ii) Compute the crane utilisation factor.  
(iii) Comment on safety. Take g = 9.81 m/s².

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Tension per sling leg:**

$$
W \;=\; 720 \times 9.81 \;=\; 7\,063.2 \; \text{kN}
$$

$$
T \;=\; \frac{W}{4 \cos\theta} \;=\; \frac{7\,063.2}{4 \times \cos 45°} \;=\; \frac{7\,063.2}{4 \times 0.7071} \;=\; \frac{7\,063.2}{2.8284}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T \;=\; 2\,497 \; \text{kN per leg} \;\approx\; 254.5 \; \text{t}\,}
$$

**(ii) Crane utilisation factor:**

$$
U \;=\; \frac{W}{SWL} \;=\; \frac{720}{1\,000} \;=\; 0.72
$$

**(iii) Safety Comment:**  
Since $U = 0.72 \le 0.85$, the lift is **safe**. However, a **45° sling angle is steeper than ideal**; the industry prefers $\theta \le 30°$ from vertical (i.e. flatter slings) to minimise horizontal squeeze force on the module and reduce leg tension. A spreader beam should be used.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify **shipboard access equipment** with suitable examples. Explain why SOLAS Reg. II-1/40 and II-1/41 are central to safe design.

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (functional):**

| Class | Examples | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Vertical | Steel ladders (fixed, hoop-caged), companion ladders, stairways | Personnel movement between decks and tanks |
| Horizontal | Catwalks, gratings, platforms, permanent walkways | Movement along the length/width of machinery spaces |
| Working at Height | Cherry pickers, scissor lifts, suspended stages, bosun's chair | Maintenance at funnel, mast, side shell |
| Confined Space | Tripod + winch, basket, breathing apparatus | Tank entry, cofferdam access |
| Ship-to-Shore | Accommodation ladder, pilot ladder, gangway | Transfer between ship and port |

**Why SOLAS Reg. II-1/40 and II-1/41 matter:**  
- **Reg. II-1/40** mandates stairways and ladders in machinery spaces of category A — these provide the **emergency escape route** when fire blocks the main passage.  
- **Reg. II-1/41** requires ladders, catwalks and platforms for **safe access** to all control positions, valves and inspection points.  
Together they eliminate the most common cause of seafarer injury — falls from height — and are inspected during the **Enhanced Annual Survey** by classification societies.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pilot ladder of total length **L = 11.5 m** must be provided for a vessel with freeboard **h_f = 9.5 m**. SOLAS requires the ladder to extend **at least 2 m above the waterline**.  
(i) Verify the design length is adequate.  
(ii) If the desired step spacing is **s = 0.32 m**, calculate the number of steps.  
(iii) State the maximum permissible horizontal gap between the bottom of the ladder and the waterline at the embarkation position.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Adequacy check:**

Required length = $h_f + 2 = 9.5 + 2 = 11.5$ m.  
Provided length = 11.5 m. Hence **just adequate**. ✓

**(ii) Number of steps:**

$$
N_{steps} \;=\; \frac{L}{s} + 1 \;=\; \frac{11.5}{0.32} + 1 \;=\; 35.94 + 1 \;\Rightarrow\; 37 \; \text{steps}
$$

Re-check spacing:

$$
s_{actual} \;=\; \frac{11.5}{36} \;=\; 0.3194 \; \text{m} \;\approx\; 319 \; \text{mm}
$$

**(iii) Maximum bottom gap:** Per SOLAS Reg. V/23, the bottom of the ladder must reach the **waterline or just above it**; horizontal gap ≤ **200 mm**, otherwise an *extension piece* must be rigged.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "block" with "section".** A *section* is 2-D (a panel); a *block* is 3-D. Writing "section = block" costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong sling-angle convention.** Some students use $\theta$ from the horizontal instead of the vertical. The correct formula is $T = W / (n \cos\theta)$ with $\theta$ measured **from vertical**. Wrong convention → full 7 marks lost in Part B (b).
> 3. **Forgetting the +2 m above waterline** when computing pilot-ladder length. This is a SOLAS Reg. V/23 hard rule — half-mark deduction.
> 4. **Calling scaffold "access equipment"** without classifying it under SOLAS sub-rules (II-1/40 vs II-1/41). Examiners award marks only when the regulation is *named*.
> 5. **Not stating the crane utilisation factor ≤ 0.85** when commenting on lift safety — generic statements like "it is safe" score zero; the numerical check is mandatory.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Modular arrangement = 3-D block-based construction** that enables parallel work and reduces build time.
- **Hierarchy:** Sub-assembly → Panel → Block → Grand Block → Super Block → Ship.
- **Typical block mass:** 200–600 t; mega-blocks 600–900 t; super-blocks up to 4 000 t.
- **Maximum outfitting at pre-erection:** 60–75 % for super-blocks.
- **Sling tension formula:** $T = W / (n \cos\theta)$, $\theta$ from vertical.
- **Crane utilisation factor** must satisfy $U = W / SWL \le \mathbf{0.85}$.
- **Five classes of access equipment:** Vertical, Horizontal, Working-at-Height, Confined-Space, Ship-to-Shore.
- **SOLAS Reg. II-1/40** → stairways and ladders in machinery spaces.
- **SOLAS Reg. II-1/41** → catwalks, platforms, walkways.
- **SOLAS Reg. V/23** → pilot ladder; must extend ≥ 2 m above waterline; max bottom gap 200 mm.
- **Ladder rung width:** ≥ **350 mm**; toe clearance ≥ **150 mm**; hoop-cage required for unsupported length > **5 m**.
- **Pilot ladder step spread:** **0.30 – 0.36 m**.
- **Minimum gangway width:** $w_{min} = 600 + 0.4 N_{users}$ mm, never below 600 mm.
- Always cite the **specific regulation / ISO / EN standard** when describing access equipment — generic statements lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hatches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hatches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **hatch** in ship design is a **rectangular (or square) weather-tight opening cut into the deck plating** of a vessel, used primarily to provide access for cargo loading, unloading, and ventilation between the weather deck and the cargo holds, machinery spaces, or other compartments below. The opening is bordered by a raised structural rim called a **hatch coaming**, and is closed during navigation by a **hatch cover** system designed to withstand sea loads, cargo loads, and environmental forces.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, aligned with **IACS Unified Requirements (UR S21)** and **SOLAS Chapter II-1 regulations**, hatches are classified under two broad categories:

1. **Cargo Hatches** – Large openings in the weather deck used for bulk, container, or break-bulk cargo transfer.
2. **Access / Escape Hatches** – Smaller openings for personnel access, emergency egress, or machinery access.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hatches are studied as a critical structural and operational subsystem within Module 3, emphasizing **coaming design, hatch cover selection, strength criteria, and regulatory compliance** with classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a large rectangular **tray with raised edges** placed on the floor of a warehouse. The tray is the **deck**, the raised edges are the **hatch coamings**, and the lid that snaps over the top to keep rain out is the **hatch cover**. The lid has to be strong enough that you could stand on it (or stack heavy boxes on it), and the edges need to be tall enough so a splash of water does not pour in. The size, material, and mechanism of the lid differ depending on whether the warehouse stores **grain, cars, oil, or refrigerated goods** — this is exactly how different ship types (bulk carriers, container ships, tankers) use different hatch designs.

For a bulk carrier carrying iron ore, the opening must be huge but the cover heavy and strong. For a container ship, the openings are smaller but must handle **stacked container loads up to 150 tonnes per TEU corner**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard hatch coaming height on weather deck:** **600 mm** (forward 0.25L) and **450 mm** (elsewhere) — per SOLAS Reg. II-1/25-1.
- **Standard hatch coaming height on freeboard deck:** **300 mm** minimum.
- **Hatch cover design pressure:** Typically **34.3 kN/m² (3.5 t/m²)** for seagoing vessels (IACS S21 for weather deck hatches).
- **Steel grade for hatch covers:** Grade **AH36, DH36, or EH36** with yield strength **355 N/mm²** minimum.
- **Clear opening rule of thumb:** Hatch clear opening ≈ **0.4 × to 0.6 × ship breadth** for bulk carriers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box – Hatch Coaming:** A **hatch coaming** is a vertical plate structure welded around the perimeter of a hatch opening, projecting above the deck to prevent water ingress, provide structural continuity, and serve as a sealing surface for the hatch cover.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box – Hatch Cover:** A **hatch cover** is a removable or movable watertight/weathertight lid placed over the hatch opening when the hold is not in use, designed to resist sea pressure, cargo pressure, and foot traffic loads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why and How

### 2.1.1 Purpose of a Hatch
A hatch serves **three simultaneous purposes** in a ship's operational life:

1. **Functional Purpose** – Provide a large enough opening for the **fastest possible turnaround** during port stay (cargo operation efficiency).
2. **Safety Purpose** – Maintain **weathertight integrity** so that green seas, rain, and spray do not enter cargo holds.
3. **Structural Purpose** – Distribute the **local deck load concentration** caused by the cut-out back into the surrounding deck structure via coaming, end beams, and side girders.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind the Components
- **Why coamings?** Without a raised rim, water would sheet directly into the hold. The coaming acts like the lip of a bucket.
- **Why steel hatch covers?** Steel is the only material offering the required **stiffness-to-weight ratio** plus **corrosion resistance** for marine service.
- **Why section modulus rules?** Hatch covers are essentially **rectangular plates stiffened by secondary stiffeners and primary girders**; rules (UR S21) standardize the minimum stiffness to prevent oil-canning and permanent deformation under sea loads.

## 2.2 Classification of Hatches

### 2.2.1 By Function
| Type | Application | Typical Size |
|---|---|---|
| Cargo Hatch | Bulk carriers, general cargo | **0.4B × (0.4–0.6)L** |
| Container Hatch | Cellular container ships | 1 TEU pitch (≈ 6 m) |
| Escape Hatch | All vessels | 600 × 600 mm minimum |
| Access Hatch | Engine room, storerooms | 800 × 800 mm typical |
| Machinery Hatch | Aft of superstructure | 1.2 × 1.2 m typical |
| Emergency Hatch | Ro-Ro, passenger ships | 1200 × 800 mm |

### 2.2.2 By Location
- **Forward hatch (No. 1, 2, 3...)** – Subject to slamming loads, requires **25% extra steel** in some cases.
- **Aft hatch (last before superstructure)** – Subject to vibrations and stern wave impact.
- **Midship hatches** – Standard design loads.

## 2.3 Hatch Coaming Design (IACS UR S21)

The coaming is essentially a **cantilever plate**, fixed at its base to the deck and free at the top, subjected to **lateral sea pressure**.

### 2.3.1 Steps in Coaming Design
1. **Determine design pressure** $p$ on the coaming from UR S21.
2. **Calculate coaming plate thickness** $t_c$ from bending requirement.
3. **Check shear stress** at the weld with the deck.
4. **Size the coaming stay brackets** (every 2–3 m on weather deck).
5. **Verify local shear at the deck connection**.

## 2.4 Hatch Cover Types — Engineering Trade-Off

| Cover Type | Operating Principle | Best For | Open/Close Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pontoon (Single Pull)** | Sliding cover pulled by wire/chain | Bulk carriers | 5–8 min |
| **Piggy-Back** | Two covers stacked | Capesize bulkers | 6–10 min |
| **Folding (End Folding)** | Hinged panels fold onto next | Container/bulk | 2–4 min |
| **Side Rolling** | Covers roll sideways on rails | Small hatches | 3–5 min |
| **Lift-Away (Hydraulic)** | Lifted by gantry/crane | Tankers | 15–30 min |
| **Sliding Hydraulic** | Sliding + hydraulic power | Modern bulkers | 1–3 min |
| **Stacking (MacGregor)** | Panels stack vertically | Panamax bulkers | 4–6 min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes **steel pontoon covers**, **folding covers**, and **hydraulic sliding covers** as the three most important design variants examined.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{cov} = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_y}}$ | Hatch cover plate thickness (UR S21 style) | mm |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{p \cdot s^2}{f \cdot \sigma_y}$ | Required section modulus of stiffener | cm³ |
| 3 | $p_{sea} = 10 \cdot h_s$ (approx) | Sea pressure head (head $h_s$ in m) | kN/m² |
| 4 | $H_c = 0.6$ m (forward) / $0.45$ m (other) | SOLAS coaming height | m |
| 5 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{1.33}$ | Allowable bending stress check | N/mm² |
| 6 | $t_c = f_c \sqrt{H_c}$ | Coaming plate thickness (empirical) | mm |
| 7 | $W_{min} = 0.5 \cdot s \cdot p \cdot L$ | Min stiffness for cover panel | cm³ |
| 8 | $f_s = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2 \cdot \sigma_y / p}}$ | Slenderness check for plate | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value is **not used** in any cell; the equation cells are deliberately written without vertical pipes to preserve Mermaid-safe markdown table syntax. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if absolute value notation is required in extended notes.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

Hatch design directly impacts:
- **Port productivity** (turnaround time per hatch opening)
- **Cargo claim litigation** (weathertightness failures = huge insurance costs)
- **Ship deadweight** (heavier covers = less cargo)
- **Crew safety** (manual covers are a major accident source → IMO now mandates powered covers for new bulkers > 20,000 DWT)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hatch Cover Plate Thickness

We model a hatch cover panel as a **rectangular plate simply supported on four edges**, subjected to a uniformly distributed design pressure $p$.

### 3.1.1 Governing Equation Setup

For a simply supported rectangular plate under UDL, the maximum bending moment per unit width at the center is given by plate theory (Timoshenko):

$$
M_{max} = \alpha \cdot p \cdot a^2
$$

where:
- $p$ = design pressure (N/mm²)
- $a$ = shorter span (mm)
- $\alpha$ = bending coefficient, depending on aspect ratio $b/a$

For a square plate ($b = a$), $\alpha = 0.048$.

### 3.1.2 Required Section Modulus of Plate

The required plastic section modulus per mm width of plate is:

$$
Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot p \cdot a^2}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

For a plate of thickness $t$, the section modulus per mm width is:

$$
Z_{plate} = \dfrac{t^2}{4} \quad \text{(plastic, per mm width)}
$$

### 3.1.3 Solving for Plate Thickness

Setting $Z_{plate} \geq Z_{req}$:

$$
\dfrac{t^2}{4} \geq \dfrac{\alpha \cdot p \cdot a^2}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

$$
t^2 \geq \dfrac{4 \cdot \alpha \cdot p \cdot a^2}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

$$
t \geq a \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot \alpha \cdot p}{\sigma_{allow}}}
$$

Letting $k = \sqrt{4\alpha}$, we obtain the KTU-style formula:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{cov} = k \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{allow}}}\,}
$$

For $\alpha = 0.048$, $k = \sqrt{0.192} \approx 0.438$.

### 3.1.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** Determine the required plate thickness of a hatch cover panel.
- Shorter span $a = 800$ mm
- Design pressure $p = 0.0343$ N/mm² (= 34.3 kN/m²)
- Allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 175$ N/mm² (for grade A steel, $\sigma_y/1.33$)

**Step 1:** Compute $k$ for square plate.

$$
k = \sqrt{4 \times 0.048} = \sqrt{0.192} = 0.438
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into thickness formula.

$$
t_{cov} = 0.438 \times 800 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0343}{175}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the term under the square root.

$$
\dfrac{0.0343}{175} = 1.96 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4:** Take square root.

$$
\sqrt{1.96 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0140
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $t_{cov}$.

$$
t_{cov} = 0.438 \times 800 \times 0.0140
$$

$$
t_{cov} = 350.4 \times 0.0140 = 4.91 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Add corrosion allowance of 2 mm.

$$
t_{design} = 4.91 + 2.0 = 6.91 \text{ mm} \approx \mathbf{7 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 7:** Round up to standard plate (UR S21 typically enforces 6 mm minimum, so 7 mm is safe).

> ✅ **Result:** Required hatch cover plate thickness = **7 mm** (use 8 mm standard).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hatch Coaming Plate Thickness (IACS UR S21)

The coaming acts as a **vertical cantilever plate** fixed at the deck, free at the top, subjected to sea pressure $p$.

### 3.2.1 Bending Moment at the Base

For a cantilever of height $H_c$ with UDL $p$:

$$
M_{base} = \dfrac{p \cdot H_c^2}{2}
$$

### 3.2.2 Required Section Modulus (per mm run)

$$
Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{base}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{p \cdot H_c^2}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}
$$

### 3.2.3 Solve for Thickness

$$
\dfrac{t_c^2}{4} = \dfrac{p \cdot H_c^2}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}
$$

$$
t_c^2 = \dfrac{2 p H_c^2}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_c = H_c \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 p}{\sigma_{allow}}}\,}
$$

### 3.2.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** Hatch coaming on weather deck, forward 0.25L.
- Coaming height $H_c = 600$ mm
- Design pressure $p = 0.0343$ N/mm²
- $\sigma_{allow} = 175$ N/mm²

**Step 1:** Substitute.

$$
t_c = 600 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.0343}{175}}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute ratio.

$$
\dfrac{2 \times 0.0343}{175} = \dfrac{0.0686}{175} = 3.92 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3:** Square root.

$$
\sqrt{3.92 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0198
$$

**Step 4:** Final thickness.

$$
t_c = 600 \times 0.0198 = 11.88 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Add 2 mm corrosion allowance.

$$
t_{design} = 11.88 + 2.0 = 13.88 \text{ mm} \approx \mathbf{14 \text{ mm}}
$$

> ✅ **Result:** Coaming plate thickness = **14 mm** (forward), would reduce to ~12 mm aft of 0.25L.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hatch Cover Selection Decision

| Decision Criterion | Pontoon | Folding | Side Rolling | Lift-Away | Hydraulic Sliding |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capital Cost (Capex)** | Medium | High | Low | Low | High |
| **Op. Cost (maintenance)** | Medium | High | Low | Low | Medium |
| **Speed of Operation** | Slow | Fast | Medium | Very Slow | Fastest |
| **Crew Required** | 4–6 | 2 | 1 | 1 (crane) | 1 |
| **Weather Resistance** | Excellent | Good | Good | Good | Excellent |
| **Sealing Reliability** | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Highest |
| **Suitable Ship Size** | Handy-max | Panamax | Small | All | Capesize+ |
| **Weight (per m²)** | 80–110 kg | 90–120 kg | 70–90 kg | 60–80 kg | 100–130 kg |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hatch System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hatch Opening in Deck Plate] --> B[Hatch Coaming Structure]
    B --> C1[Coaming Plate]
    B --> C2[Coaming Stay Brackets every 2-3 m]
    B --> C3[Coaming Top Edge Bar]
    A --> D[Hatch Cover Assembly]
    D --> E1[Top Plate]
    D --> E2[Secondary Stiffeners]
    D --> E3[Primary Cross Girders]
    D --> E4[Skids and Rollers]
    D --> E5[Sealing Gasket System]
    D --> E6[Securing Dog and Cleat]
    D --> F[Operating Mechanism]
    F --> F1[Wire Rope Drive]
    F --> F2[Hydraulic Cylinders]
    F --> F3[Rack and Pinion]
    F --> F4[Chain Drive]
    D --> G[Cross Joint and Drain Channel]
    A --> H[End Beam and Side Girder Reinforcement]
    H --> H1[Increased Deck Plate Thickness]
    H --> H2[Doubler Plates]
    B --> I[Corrosion Protection]
    I --> I1[Zinc Anode]
    I --> I2[Marine Paint System]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Hatch Cover Operation (Hydraulic Sliding Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Cover in Closed Position] --> step2[Release Securing Dogs]
    step2 --> step3[Activate Hydraulic Pumping Unit]
    step3 --> step4[Pressure Build up to 200 bar]
    step4 --> step5[Cover Slides on Rollers]
    step5 --> step6[Cover Reaches Open Limit Switch]
    step6 --> step7[Lock Pin Engages]
    step7 --> step8[Hatch Ready for Cargo Operation]
    step8 --> step9[On Completion Reverse Operation]
    step9 --> step10[Cover Slides Back to Closed]
    step10 --> step11[Securing Dogs Tighten]
    step11 --> step12[Compress Sealing Gasket]
    step12 --> step13[Watertight Integrity Confirmed]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Hatch Coaming Cantilever Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Sea Pressure p kN per m2 Acting Laterally] --> L2[Coaming Cantilever of Height H]
    L2 --> L3[Bending Moment Max at Base equals pH squared over 2]
    L3 --> L4[Reaction Force at Deck Connection]
    L4 --> L5[Shear Flow to Deck Plate]
    L5 --> L6[Local Reinforcement by Doubler Plate]
    L6 --> L7[Stress Check against Allowable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams use purely alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `step1`, `L1`) and uppercase text labels, complying with the Mermaid compilation safeguards defined in the system protocol. No markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables are used inside node labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a hatch coaming. State the minimum coaming heights specified by SOLAS for weather deck and freeboard deck.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hatch coaming** is a raised vertical plate structure fitted around the perimeter of a hatch opening, projecting above the deck plating, to prevent ingress of water and to provide a sealing surface for the hatch cover.

**SOLAS Reg. II-1/25-1 minimum coaming heights:**
- **Weather deck, forward 0.25L:** **600 mm** above the deck
- **Weather deck, elsewhere:** **450 mm** above the deck
- **Freeboard deck:** **300 mm** above the deck

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three values)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three types of steel hatch covers commonly used on bulk carriers and state the operating principle of each.**

**Model Answer:**
