```mermaid
    style A fill:#e1e1e1
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#e1ffe1
    style F fill:#e1ffe1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a "guard rail" as per SOLAS. State the minimum height of the top course and the maximum allowable spacing between stanchions.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A **guard rail** is a safety barrier consisting of horizontal courses supported by vertical stanchions, fitted along the periphery of all exposed decks to prevent personnel from falling overboard.

**As per SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 43:**
- The **top course** of the guard rail shall be at a height of **not less than 1000 mm** above the deck.
- The **bottom course** shall be fitted at a height not exceeding **230 mm** above the deck.
- The **maximum spacing** between stanchions shall be **1.5 m**, except when a bulwark is fitted in lieu of the lower course, in which case the spacing may be increased to **2.5 m**.

*Mark Distribution:*
- Definition: 1 mark
- Height 1000 mm: 1 mark
- Stanchion spacing 1.5 m (with bulwark alternative): 1 mark

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between a "bulwark" and a "guard rail" on a ship. State the SOLAS regulation governing each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | Bulwark | Guard Rail |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Solid plating (watertight) | Open horizontal bars |
| **Primary function** | Prevent personnel fall + prevent water ingress | Prevent personnel fall only |
| **Height** | Typically 1000–1400 mm | Minimum 1000 mm |
| **Openings** | Scuppers for drainage | Natural gaps between courses |
| **SOLAS Regulation** | II-1, Reg. 43.2 | II-1, Reg. 43.2 |

Both are technically covered under the **same SOLAS regulation (II-1/43.2)** but are differentiated by their **physical form**. A bulwark provides additional protection against **green seas washing overboard** and is mandatory on the **forward exposed decks** of most vessels.

*Mark Distribution:*
- Two correct distinguishing points: 2 marks
- SOLAS citation: 1 mark

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the **constructional details of an accommodation ladder**. State the SOLAS requirements for its width, step rise, handrail height, and inclination angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **SOLAS requirements for a pilot ladder** with respect to step dimensions, side ropes, spreaders, length, and manropes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Accommodation Ladder (7 marks):

An **accommodation ladder** is a stepped ladder installed on the **ship's side** to provide safe access between the vessel and a tender/boat or a low quay. It is hinged at the upper end to the ship's bulwark gate and rests on the lower end via a trunnion-and-saddle arrangement that connects to a gangway.

**Key Constructional Features (with dimensions):**

1. **Upper Platform** – A small deck-level platform hinged to the bulwark port, of size approximately **760 mm × 760 mm**, fitted with removable handrails.

2. **Flight of Steps** – Constructed of aluminium alloy (preferred) or mild steel, with:
   - Clear **width between handrails ≥ 600 mm**.
   - **Step rise** between **150 mm and 230 mm** (uniform).
   - Step tread **effective depth ≥ 80 mm**, fitted with anti-slip nosings.
   - **Light-line** painted on each step edge (50 mm wide) for night visibility.

3. **Lower Platform** – Trunnion-mounted platform at the bottom of the ladder flight, used to join the ladder to the gangway. Equipped with:
   - Handrails height **900–1000 mm** above platform level.
   - Safety chains at the gangway interface.

4. **Handrails** – Both sides, **height 900–1000 mm** above step nosings, with intermediate rail.

5. **Saddle/Boom** – A davit or king-post with wire rope suspension to support the weight of the ladder and gangway.

6. **Maximum Inclination** – **45° from horizontal** (some flags allow up to 55° for naval vessels).

7. **Material** – Aluminium alloy to **AA 5083-H116** for weight reduction and corrosion resistance.

**SOLAS Reference:** SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 42.

*Sketch:*

```
SHIP'S DECK ─┐ ┌── Upper Platform (hinged)
             │ │ 
   ──── Top  ┤ ├──── Handrails 900-1000 mm
             │ │
             │ │   Steps (rise 150-230 mm)
             │ │  ◢◣◢◣◢◣◢◣
             │ │ ◢◣◢◣◢◣◢◣◢◣
             │ │◢◣◢◣◢◣◢◣◢◣◢
             │ │
             │ └── Lower Platform (trunnion)
             │    │
             └────┘
```

*Mark Distribution for (a):*
- Sketch + general arrangement: 3 marks
- Five dimensions correctly listed: 3 marks
- SOLAS reference: 1 mark

---

#### Solution to (b) — Pilot Ladder SOLAS Requirements (7 marks):

A **pilot ladder** is a flexible ladder designed exclusively for the safe embarkation and disembarkation of marine pilots. It is regulated by **SOLAS Chapter V, Regulation 23** and **IMO Resolution A.1045(27)**.

**SOLAS Requirements:**

1. **Length** – Shall not exceed **9 metres** in a single length. Beyond 9 m, intermediate platforms must be provided.

2. **Number of Steps** – Maximum **10 steps** per flight. Beyond 10 steps, an intermediate platform is mandatory.

3. **Step Dimensions:**
   - Width: **115 mm** minimum.
   - Thickness: **25 mm** minimum.
   - Length: **480 mm** (measured along the side ropes).
   - Spacing: **310 mm to 350 mm** between consecutive steps (uniform).

4. **Step Material** – **Hardwood** (oak, elm, teak). **No paint, varnish, or knots** are permitted on the upper surface. Each step must withstand a **single point load of 1.5 kN** at the centre without failure.

5. **Side Ropes** – Two ropes, **uncovered manila, sisal, or synthetic fibre equivalent**, of diameter **18 mm to 22 mm**. Each rope must have a **breaking load of at least 24 kN**.

6. **Spacers/Spreaders** – At least **three wooden spreaders**, each of dimensions **50 mm × 40 mm × 300 mm**, fitted at intervals of **not more than 2.1 m** to hold the ladder away from the ship's side.

7. **Manropes** – **Two separate manropes** (not part of the ladder) of diameter **28 mm to 32 mm**, rigged at the top of the ladder for the pilot to hold while climbing.

8. **Securing** – The ladder must be rigged so that it hangs **clear of the hull** and the **lowest step is at or below the waterline** (or quay level, whichever is higher) but **not more than 300 mm above** it.

9. **Lighting** – Adequate illumination must be provided at the ladder position during night transfers.

*Mark Distribution for (b):*
- Five correct specifications with values: 5 marks
- SOLAS Reg V/23 reference: 1 mark
- Clear arrangement description: 1 mark

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **arrangement of guard rails and stanchions** on the exposed weather deck of a ship. State the SOLAS height, spacing, and load requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A guard rail spans between two stanchions **1.5 m apart**. The design horizontal load per SOLAS is **750 N/m**. Using standard beam-deflection theory, determine the **minimum second moment of area (I)** of the rail tube if the maximum deflection is limited to **10 mm**. Take $E = 200$ GPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Guard Rail Arrangement (7 marks):

**Definition:** Guard rails are **horizontal safety barriers** consisting of **three courses** (top, intermediate, and bottom) supported by **vertical stanchions**, fitted on all **exposed decks** of a ship to prevent personnel from falling overboard.

**Arrangement Description:**

1. **Top Course (Top Rail):**
   - Located at a height of **1000 mm** above the deck sheer line.
   - Continuous across all stanchions.
   - Designed to withstand a **horizontal distributed load of 750 N/m**.

2. **Intermediate Course:**
   - Located at approximately **500 mm** above the deck.
   - The vertical opening between the top and intermediate rails must not exceed **380 mm**.

3. **Bottom Course (Toe-rail):**
   - Located **not more than 230 mm** above the deck.
   - Prevents the heel of a shoe from slipping under.

4. **Stanchions:**
   - Vertical posts, typically of **40 mm diameter solid bar** or **40 NB steel tube**.
   - Spaced at **not more than 1.5 m apart** for two-course systems.
   - Welded to the deck plating with **doubler plates** if necessary.

5. **Removable Sections:**
   - Guard rails may be **removable** at door locations (bulwark gates, ladder openings), but the gap must be **as small as practicable**.

**SOLAS Reference:** SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 43.2.

**Sketch:**

```
═══════════════ Top Rail (1000 mm) ═══════════════
                  │    │    │    │    │
                  │    │    │    │    │   Stanchions
                  │    │    │    │    │   (≤ 1.5 m)
═══════════════ Intermediate (500 mm) ═════════════
                  │    │    │    │    │
                  │    │    │    │    │
                  │    │    │    │    │
─────────────── Toe-rail (≤ 230 mm) ───────────────
▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ DECK PLATING ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓
```

*Mark Distribution for (a):*
- Sketch with three labelled courses: 3 marks
- Three SOLAS dimensions (1000 mm, 380 mm, 230 mm, 1.5 m): 3 marks
- Reference: 1 mark

---

#### Solution to (b) — Beam Deflection Calculation (7 marks):

**Given:**
- Span between stanchions: $L = 1.5$ m
- Design horizontal load: $q = 750$ N/m
- Maximum allowable deflection: $\delta_{max} = 10$ mm $= 0.010$ m
- Young's modulus: $E = 200$ GPa $= 200 \times 10^{9}$ N/m²

**Step 1 — Identify the loading condition.**

The rail acts as a **simply-supported beam** under **uniformly distributed load**.

**Step 2 — Apply the standard deflection formula.**

For a simply-supported beam with UDL:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 q L^{4}}{384 E I}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for I.**

$$I = \frac{5 q L^{4}}{384 E \delta_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$I = \frac{5 \times 750 \times (1.5)^{4}}{384 \times (200 \times 10^{9}) \times (0.010)}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$5 \times 750 \times (1.5)^{4} = 3750 \times 5.0625 = 18{,}984.375 \; \text{N·m}^{3}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$384 \times (200 \times 10^{9}) \times 0.010 = 7.68 \times 10^{11} \; \text{N·m}^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Compute I.**

$$I = \frac{18{,}984.375}{7.68 \times 10^{11}} = 2.472 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^{4}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to cm⁴.**

$$I = 2.472 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{8} = 2.472 \; \text{cm}^{4}$$

**Step 9 — Selection of standard tube.**

A standard **25 mm NB × 3 mm wall mild steel tube** provides $I = 2.618 \; \text{cm}^{4}$, which exceeds the required minimum by **5.9%** and is therefore **acceptable**.

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{I_{min} = 2.472 \; \text{cm}^{4}}$$

*Mark Distribution for (b):*
- Formula statement: 2 marks
- Numerical substitution: 2 marks
- Correct final value: 2 marks
- Practical tube selection: 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing units:** $E$ must be in **Pa**, lengths in **m**, deflection in **m** — NOT mixing cm and m. A 30% mark deduction is common for unit errors.
> 2. **Missing the "removable section" clause:** Students often forget that guard rails must have **small removable sections** at doors/port — this is a 2-mark trap question.
> 3. **Forgetting the bulwark relaxation:** Stanchion spacing can be **2.5 m** if a bulwark + 1.2 m top rail is provided — not just the standard 1.5 m.
> 4. **Pilot ladder dimensions:** Students often confuse **step length (480 mm)** with **step spacing (310–350 mm)**. The 480 mm is measured *along the side rope*, not the deck.
> 5. **Wrong SOLAS chapter:** Pilot ladders are **Chapter V**, NOT Chapter III (which is for lifeboats). Many students lose marks here.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Memorise Before Exam:**

### **A. Guard Rails & Stanchions — Must-Know Numbers**
- ✦ Top rail height: **≥ 1000 mm**
- ✦ Intermediate opening (gap below top): **≤ 380 mm**
- ✦ Bottom gap (above deck): **≤ 230 mm**
- ✦ Stanchion spacing (two-course): **≤ 1.5 m**
- ✦ Stanchion spacing (with bulwark): **≤ 2.5 m**
- ✦ Design load: **750 N/m horizontal**
- ✦ Deflection limit: **≤ 10 mm**
- ✦ Stanchion diameter: **≥ 40 mm solid / 40 NB tube**
- ✦ Code: **SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 43**

### **B. Accommodation Ladder — Must-Know Numbers**
- ✦ Width (clear): **≥ 600 mm**
- ✦ Step rise: **150–230 mm**
- ✦ Step tread: **≥ 80 mm effective**
- ✦ Handrail height: **900–1000 mm**
- ✦ Maximum slope: **45° from horizontal**
- ✦ Material: **Aluminium alloy AA 5083-H116**
- ✦ Code: **SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 42**

### **C. Pilot Ladder — Must-Know Numbers**
- ✦ Length: **≤ 9 m** (per flight)
- ✦ Steps per flight: **≤ 10**
- ✦ Step width: **115 mm**
- ✦ Step thickness: **25 mm**
- ✦ Step length: **480 mm** (along side ropes)
- ✦ Step spacing: **310–350 mm**
- ✦ Step material: **Hardwood, no paint/varnish**
- ✦ Side rope diameter: **18–22 mm**
- ✦ Side rope breaking load: **≥ 24 kN**
- ✦ Spreaders: **3 wooden, every 2.1 m**
- ✦ Spreader size: **50 × 40 × 300 mm**
- ✦ Manropes: **28–32 mm diameter, separate**
- ✦ Step test load: **1.5 kN at centre**
- ✦ Code: **SOLAS Ch. V, Reg. 23 + IMO A.1045(27)**

### **D. Common Comparison Traps for KTU Questions**
- **Bulwark vs Guard Rail:** Bulwark = solid; Guard Rail = open bars.
- **Accommodation Ladder vs Pilot Ladder:** Accommodation = rigid, stepped; Pilot = flexible, rungs.
- **Chapter II-1 vs Chapter V:** Life-safety equipment is Ch. II-1; Navigation/transfer is Ch. V.
- **Manrope vs Side Rope:** Manrope is **separate**, at the top, for the pilot to hold; side ropes are **part of the ladder**.

### **E. Formula Recap (Always Revise Before Exam)**
- ✦ Simply-supported beam UDL deflection: $\delta = \dfrac{5qL^{4}}{384EI}$
- ✦ Cantilever stanchion moment: $M = F \cdot h$
- ✦ Bending section modulus: $Z \geq \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{allow}}$
- ✦ Circular tube $I$: $I = \dfrac{\pi(D^{4} - d^{4})}{64}$
- ✦ Circular solid $I$: $I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** In the exam, if a question asks *"State the SOLAS requirements for X"*, **always lead with the regulation number** (e.g., *SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 43.2*), then list the dimensions. Examiners award **automatic partial credit** for correct code citation even if dimensions are partially wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lights and Sound Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lights and Sound Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (COLREGs Framework)

**Lights and Sound Signals** in ship design refer to the standardized visual (lights and shapes) and audible (whistle, bell, gong) signaling systems mandated by the **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs)**, adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**. These regulations prescribe the exact configuration, color, arc of visibility, range, position, and operational use of lights and sound signal appliances fitted to every seagoing vessel, so that any ship can be recognized instantly — both at night (by its lights) and during restricted visibility (by its sound signals) — irrespective of the seafarer's nationality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic is assessed primarily under **CO3 (Apply statutory and regulatory design requirements to ship arrangements)** and maps to **Bloom's Level L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Road Traffic Analogy

Imagine driving a car on a busy highway in a foreign country. Even if you cannot read the local road signs, the moment you see **red brake lights**, a **flashing yellow indicator**, or hear a **car horn**, you instantly understand the other driver's intent. Lights and sound signals on a ship work **exactly** the same way:

- **Navigation lights** = Car headlights, brake lights, and indicators (showing position, course, and intent).
- **Day shapes** = Hand signals used by traffic police (showing status during daytime).
- **Sound signals** = Car horns (communicating intent in fog, congestion, or close-quarters situations).
- **Light arcs (e.g., 225°, 112.5°)** = The directional coverage of the headlight beam.

A ship officer encountering a vessel at night can, in **one glance**, tell whether it is a **power-driven vessel, a sailing vessel, a tug, a trawler, or a vessel at anchor**, and judge its **heading and aspect** purely from the lights it shows. This is the genius of COLREGs — universal "ship language" by light and sound.

### 1.3 Standardized Physical Metrics

The key engineering metrics embedded in the regulations are:

- **Nautical mile (nm)** = **1852 m** (range of visibility of lights).
- **Degree of arc** for sector lights (e.g., masthead **225°**, sidelights **112.5°** each, stern **135°**).
- **Standard ship lengths** used to define light range: **≥ 50 m**, **20–50 m**, **12–20 m**, **< 12 m**.
- **Sound blast durations**: **Short blast ≈ 1 s**, **Long blast = 4–6 s**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why COLREGs exist:** Before 1972 (when COLREGs were adopted), every maritime nation had its own rules, leading to confusion and disasters at sea. COLREGs unified the world under a single visual and audible language.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Lights (Rule 20–Rule 31, COLREGs)

Lights are categorized by **function**, **arc of visibility**, and **color**. Each light has a precisely defined role.

#### 2.1.1 By Function
- **Masthead Light** — White, 225° arc, placed on the fore-and-aft centerline.
- **Side Lights (Port & Starboard)** — Red (port) and Green (starboard), 112.5° each.
- **Stern Light** — White, 135° arc, placed near the stern.
- **All-Round Light** — 360° visibility (e.g., for towing, not under command).
- **Towing Light** — Yellow, 135° arc, mounted above the stern light.
- **Special Lights** — Blue flashing (pilot vessel), Yellow flashing (aircraft carrier), Red (RAM).

#### 2.1.2 By Arc of Visibility (Logic Behind the Geometry)

The total horizontal arc around a ship sums up as follows:

$$\text{Masthead} + \text{Port Side} + \text{Starboard Side} + \text{Stern} = 360°$$

$$225° + 112.5° + 112.5° + 135° = 585°$$

> [!NOTE]
> This exceeds 360° because the **masthead (22.5°)** and **side lights (each 22.5°)** **overlap forward**, and the **sidelights (each 22.5°)** **overlap aft with the stern light (22.5°)**. This overlap is intentional — it **eliminates blind spots** when the vessel is being observed from any bearing.

#### 2.1.3 Vertical Positioning Hierarchy (Rule 23)

Lights must be vertically stacked in a specific hierarchy, with the **lowest practical height**, so as not to be obscured:

- **Order from top to bottom on a power-driven vessel underway:**
  1. Second masthead light (if length ≥ 50 m)
  2. Masthead light (1st)
  3. Side lights
  4. Stern light

### 2.2 Day Shapes (Rule 20–27)

For vessels at anchor or aground, and for restricted maneuverability, COLREGs mandate **day shapes** (black geometric balls, diamonds, cylinders) hoisted where they are most visible. These are **only used from sunrise to sunset**.

### 2.3 Sound Signal Appliances (Rule 32–Rule 37)

- **Whistle** — fitted on vessels **≥ 12 m**, used for maneuvering and signaling.
- **Bell** — fitted on vessels **≥ 20 m**, used for anchoring and fog signals.
- **Gong** — fitted on vessels **≥ 100 m**, used at the stern in addition to the bell.
- **Typhon/horn** — supplementary whistle for large vessels.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Lights & Sound Signals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every numerical value** a KTU student must memorize for the ESE (End Semester Examination).

| Parameter | Symbol / Spec | Value / Formula | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range of visibility — Masthead | $R_{mh}$ | **6 nm** | Vessel $L \geq 50$ m |
| Range of visibility — Masthead | $R_{mh}$ | **5 nm** | Vessel $L \geq 20$ m, $< 50$ m |
| Range of visibility — Masthead | $R_{mh}$ | **3 nm** | Vessel $L \geq 12$ m, $< 20$ m |
| Range of visibility — Masthead | $R_{mh}$ | **2 nm** | Vessel $L < 12$ m |
| Range of visibility — Sidelight/Stern | $R_{s}$ | **3 nm** | $L \geq 50$ m |
| Range of visibility — Sidelight/Stern | $R_{s}$ | **2 nm** | $L \geq 12$ m |
| Arc — Masthead light | $\theta_{mh}$ | **225°** | All power-driven vessels |
| Arc — Each sidelight | $\theta_{sl}$ | **112.5°** | Each side |
| Arc — Stern light | $\theta_{st}$ | **135°** | All-round aft |
| Arc — All-round light | $\theta_{ar}$ | **360°** | Special roles |
| Arc — Towing light | $\theta_{tw}$ | **135°** | Yellow, above stern |
| Short blast duration | $t_{sb}$ | **≈ 1 second** | Maneuvering signal |
| Long blast duration | $t_{lb}$ | **4 to 6 seconds** | Maneuvering signal |
| Interval between blasts | $t_{int}$ | **≈ 1 second** | — |
| Prolonged blast (danger) | $t_{pb}$ | **4 to 6 seconds** | Fog — vessel at anchor |
| Bell/Gong ring duration | $t_{bell}$ | **≈ 5 seconds** | Anchor signal |
| Sound signal — vessel $\geq 100$ m bell spacing | — | **Forward + Aft** | Bell forward, Gong aft |
| 1 nautical mile | $1$ nm | **1852 m** | Range unit |
| Overlap angle (forward) | $\alpha_{fwd}$ | $\theta_{mh} - 2\theta_{sl}$ → $225° - 2(112.5°) = 0°$ overlap... corrected: $225° - 2(90°+22.5°) = 225° - 225° = 0°$... true overlap = **45°** | Each sidelight covers $22.5°$ beyond the beam |
| Overlap angle (aft) | $\alpha_{aft}$ | **22.5°** | Each sidelight overlaps stern light by $22.5°$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write sidelight arc as **90°**. The correct value is **112.5°** per side, because the side light covers **from dead ahead to 22.5° abaft the beam** (i.e., $90° + 22.5° = 112.5°$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **naval architecture** and **ship design**, the position and mounting of navigation lights directly affect:
- **Hull form design** — light brackets and foundations must be built into the hull plating.
- **Mast/kingpost structural design** — masthead lights impose loadings on masts.
- **Electrical design** — load analysis for the navigation light circuit (typically backed by the **emergency generator**).
- **Stability calculations** — additional top weight from masthead lights and brackets.
- **EEDI/EEXI compliance** — every additional navigational lamp adds to the ship's electrical load.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Arc of Visibility and Blind-Spot Coverage

**Problem Statement:** Derive and verify that the combination of masthead light (225°), port sidelight (112.5°), starboard sidelight (112.5°), and stern light (135°) covers the entire 360° horizon **without any blind spots**.

#### Step 1 — Define the Reference Frame

Let the ship's heading (forward direction) define the **0° reference**. Bearings increase **clockwise** (i.e., **starboard side = +ve, port side = -ve**). The full horizon to be covered spans:

$$\theta_{total} = 360°$$

#### Step 2 — Masthead Light Coverage

The masthead light is centered on **0°** (dead ahead) and extends **112.5°** to either side:

$$\theta_{mh} = 2 \times 112.5° = 225°$$

Thus, the masthead covers bearings from **$-112.5°$** (port beam) to **$+112.5°$** (starboard beam).

#### Step 3 — Side Light Coverage

Each sidelight extends from **0° (dead ahead)** to **$-22.5°$ abaft the beam** for port, and **$+22.5°$ abaft the beam** for starboard:

$$\theta_{sl,port} = 112.5° = 90° (\text{dead ahead to beam}) + 22.5° (\text{abaft the beam})$$

$$\theta_{sl,stbd} = 112.5° = 90° + 22.5°$$

#### Step 4 — Stern Light Coverage

The stern light covers **$67.5°$ on each side of dead astern (180°)**:

$$\theta_{st} = 2 \times 67.5° = 135°$$

Thus, stern light spans bearings **$112.5°$ to $247.5°$** (i.e., $180° \pm 67.5°$).

#### Step 5 — Combine Coverage and Check for Blind Spots

Let us tabulate the coverage ranges:

| Light | From (°) | To (°) | Arc (°) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masthead | $-112.5$ | $+112.5$ | 225 |
| Port Sidelight | $-180$ | $-22.5$ | 112.5 |
| Starboard Sidelight | $0$ | $+202.5$* | 112.5 |
| Stern Light | $+112.5$ | $+247.5$ | 135 |

\* Using $360° - 157.5° = 202.5°$ for continuity.

#### Step 6 — Verify Continuity

The masthead extends to **$+112.5°$**. The stern light begins at **$+112.5°$**. They **meet exactly** with no gap.

The port sidelight ends at **$-22.5°$**. The stern light begins (going clockwise past 180°) at... Let us redo this cleanly.

$$\theta_{total,covered} = \theta_{mh} + 2 \cdot \theta_{sl} + \theta_{st}$$

$$\theta_{total,covered} = 225° + 2(112.5°) + 135°$$

$$\theta_{total,covered} = 225° + 225° + 135° = 585°$$

#### Step 7 — Interpret the Overlap

$$\theta_{overlap} = \theta_{total,covered} - 360° = 585° - 360° = 225°$$

This **225° overlap** consists of:
- **Forward overlap** (masthead + both sidelights ahead of the beam): $90° + 90° + 45° = 225°$... let us calculate:

$$\text{Forward overlap} = \theta_{mh} - (360° - 2 \cdot \theta_{sl,abaft}) - \theta_{st,forward}$$

A cleaner approach:
- Masthead covers from $-112.5°$ to $+112.5°$.
- Port sidelight covers from $-180°$ (which is same as $+180°$ going the other way) to $-22.5°$.
- Starboard sidelight covers from $0°$ to $+202.5°$ (equivalently from $0°$ to $-157.5°$).
- Stern light covers from $+112.5°$ to $+247.5°$.

**Continuity check:**

At $+112.5°$: masthead **ends**, stern light **begins** → no gap ✓
At $-112.5°$: masthead **ends**, port sidelight covers it ✓
At $0°$: port and starboard sidelights both **begin** ✓
At $-22.5°$: port sidelight **ends**; masthead covers it ✓

Hence, the entire **360° horizon** is covered by **at least one light at every bearing**, with overlaps ensuring redundancy. **Q.E.D.**

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Range of Visibility for a 35-meter Vessel

**Problem Statement:** A vessel of length $L = 35$ m is fitted with navigation lights. Determine the minimum range of visibility for its masthead, side, and stern lights.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Length Bracket

Since $20 \text{ m} \leq L = 35 \text{ m} < 50 \text{ m}$, this vessel falls in the **$20 \leq L < 50$ m bracket**.

#### Step 2 — Apply the Standard Ranges

For this bracket:

$$\boxed{R_{masthead} = 5 \text{ nm}, \quad R_{side} = R_{stern} = 2 \text{ nm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For vessels $L \geq 50$ m, side/stern range is 3 nm. Below 50 m, it is 2 nm (with side lights possibly 3 nm depending on vessel type).

### 3.3 Derivation: Sound Signal for "Operating Astern Propulsion"

**Problem Statement:** A vessel wishes to signal that it is **operating astern propulsion** using its whistle. Determine the sequence and timing of the sound signal.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Rule

COLREGs Rule 34(a)(iii): **Three short blasts** means *"I am operating astern propulsion."*

#### Step 2 — Determine the Timing

$$\boxed{t_{blast} \approx 1 \text{ s}, \quad t_{gap} \approx 1 \text{ s}}$$

$$\text{Total sequence duration} = 3 \times 1 + 2 \times 1 = 5 \text{ s}$$

#### Step 3 — Sequence Diagram

$$W(t) = \begin{cases} \text{ON} & t \in [0,1] \cup [2,3] \cup [4,5] \\ \text{OFF} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where $W(t)$ is the whistle output state.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Light Configuration by Vessel Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a **favourite KTU exam table**. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Vessel Type | Day Shape | Lights (Top to Bottom, Bow to Stern) |
|---|---|---|
| Power-driven vessel underway | None | 2nd masthead (if $\geq 50$ m), Masthead, Side, Stern |
| Vessel at anchor | 1 black ball (forward) | All-round white, less bright |
| Vessel aground | 3 black balls (vertically) | 2 all-round red (vertically) |
| Vessel not under command (NUC) | 2 black balls (vertically) | 2 all-round red (vertically) |
| Vessel restricted in ability to maneuver (RAM) | Ball-Diamond-Ball | 3 all-round (red-white-red, vertically) |
| Pilot vessel on duty | None | All-round white + all-round red (lower) |
| Fishing vessel (trawling) | 2 cones apex together | All-round green (above) + all-round white (masthead) |
| Sailing vessel | None | Side lights + stern light |
| Vessel towing | Diamond shape (where length $\leq 200$ m) | 2 mastheads (vert.), side, stern, towing yellow |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Navigation Light Placement on a Power-Driven Vessel

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ship Hull - Top View] --> B[Bow Forward Direction]
    A --> C[Port Side - Red Light]
    A --> D[Starboard Side - Green Light]
    A --> E[Stern - White Light]
    A --> F[Masthead Light 1 - White]
    A --> G[Masthead Light 2 - White]
    F --> F1[Arc 225 degrees]
    F1 --> F2[112.5 deg starboard]
    F1 --> F3[112.5 deg port]
    C --> C1[Arc 112.5 deg red]
    D --> D1[Arc 112.5 deg green]
    E --> E1[Arc 135 deg white]
    G --> G1[Only if ship length greater than 50 m]
    H[Vessel Length greater or equal 50 m] --> G1
    I[Vessel Length 20 to 50 m] --> J[Single Masthead Light Only]
    K[Vessel Length 12 to 20 m] --> L[Masthead Range 3 nm]
    M[Vessel Length less than 12 m] --> N[Masthead Range 2 nm]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture: Sound Signal System

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Sound Signal Source] --> W[Whistle]
    S --> B[Bell]
    S --> G[Gong]
    W --> W1[One Short Blast - Altering Starboard]
    W --> W2[Two Short Blasts - Altering Port]
    W --> W3[Three Short Blasts - Astern Propulsion]
    W --> W4[Five Short Blasts - Doubt or Danger]
    W --> W5[One Long Blast - Course Confirmation in Narrow Channel]
    W --> W6[Prolonged Blast - Warning Signal in Fog]
    B --> B1[Five Seconds Rapid Ringing - Anchor Signal]
    B --> B2[Five Seconds Rapid Ringing - Vessel Aground Signal]
    G --> G1[Used at Stern for Vessels greater or equal 100 m]
    G1 --> G2[Supplement to Forward Bell]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Light Arc Coverage (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Port Beam 270 deg] --> P2[Port Sidelight covers 22.5 deg abaft]
    P2 --> P3[Masthead covers 112.5 deg each side]
    P3 --> P4[Starboard Sidelight covers 22.5 deg abaft]
    P4 --> P5[Starboard Beam 90 deg]
    P5 --> P6[Stern Light covers 135 deg centered at 180 deg]
    P6 --> P1
    Q[Total Coverage 360 deg] --> P1
    Q --> P3
    Q --> P6
    R[Overlaps remove blind spots] --> P2
    R --> P4
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fog Signal Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[Visibility Reduces below 2 nm] --> F2[Activate Sound Signal]
    F2 --> F3[Power-driven Vessel Making Way]
    F3 --> F4[One Prolonged Blast every 2 minutes]
    F2 --> F5[Power-driven Vessel Stopped]
    F5 --> F6[Two Prolonged Blasts every 2 minutes]
    F2 --> F7[Vessel at Anchor]
    F7 --> F8[Bell Rapid Ringing 5 seconds every minute]
    F7 --> F9[Gong Aft if Ship greater or equal 100 m]
    F8 --> F10[Gong Different Tone Implies Bow Section]
    F2 --> F11[Vessel Aground]
    F11 --> F12[Bell Plus 3 Distinct Bell Strikes before and after rapid ringing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the arc of visibility (in degrees) for: (i) Masthead light, (ii) Each sidelight, and (iii) Stern light of a power-driven vessel underway.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(i) Masthead light:** **225°** — fixed across the forward arc from $22.5°$ abaft the beam on one side to $22.5°$ abaft the beam on the other side. **(1 mark)**
- **(ii) Each sidelight:** **112.5°** — from dead ahead to $22.5°$ abaft the beam on that side. **(1 mark)**
- **(iii) Stern light:** **135°** — fixed across the aft arc from $67.5°$ on one side to $67.5°$ on the other side. **(1 mark)**

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What does a sound signal of "five short blasts" on the whistle indicate under COLREGs? Mention the duration of a short blast and a long blast.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Meaning:** **Five or more short and rapid blasts on the whistle indicate "Doubt" or "Danger"** — used when the vessel operator is uncertain of the other vessel's intentions or wishes to warn of a hazardous situation. **(2 marks)**
- **Short blast duration:** approximately **1 second**. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Long blast duration:** **4 to 6 seconds**. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Discuss in detail the navigation lights required to be exhibited by a **power-driven vessel of length 80 m, underway**, as per COLREGs. Explain the color, position, arc of visibility, and minimum range of each light. Also explain the day shape rules that would apply if the vessel were to anchor or run aground.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lights for an 80 m Power-Driven Vessel Underway [7 Marks]**

Since $L = 80$ m $\geq 50$ m, the vessel falls in the **largest size bracket**.

1. **Masthead Light No. 1 (Fore-Masthead):** **(1 mark)**
   - Color: White
   - Position: Forward, on fore-and-aft centerline, at least 6 m above the hull.
   - Arc: 225°.
   - Range: 6 nm.

2. **Masthead Light No. 2 (Main-Masthead):** **(1 mark)**
   - Color: White.
   - Position: Aft of and higher than the fore-masthead, on centerline; vertical separation $\geq 4.5$ m.
   - Arc: 225°.
   - Range: 6 nm.
   - *(Required only for vessels $\geq 50$ m.)*

3. **Port Side Light:** **(1 mark)**
   - Color: Red.
   - Position: Left side of the vessel.
   - Arc: 112.5° (from dead ahead to 22.5° abaft the beam).
   - Range: 3 nm.

4. **Starboard Side Light:** **(1 mark)**
   - Color: Green.
   - Position: Right side.
   - Arc: 112.5°.
   - Range: 3 nm.

5. **Stern Light:** **(1 mark)**
   - Color: White.
   - Position: Near the stern, on centerline.
   - Arc: 135° (67.5° on each side of dead astern).
   - Range: 3 nm.

**Geometric verification:**
$$225° + 2(112.5°) + 135° = 585°$$
$$585° - 360° = 225° \text{ of overlap (no blind spots).}$$

**(b) Day Shapes for Anchorage and Aground [7 Marks]**

**Scenario 1: Vessel at anchor [3 Marks]**
- **Day shape:** **One black ball**, not less than **0.6 m in diameter**, hoisted in the **foremost part of the vessel**, where it is best seen. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Lights at night:** **One all-round white light** in the forward part, lower than the masthead light. A second all-round white light may be shown at the stern for vessels $\geq 50$ m. **(1.5 marks)**

**Scenario 2: Vessel aground [4 Marks]**
- **Day shapes:** **Three black balls** in a **vertical line**, the highest being replaced by a **red flag** or any other equivalent device. **(2 marks)**
- **Lights at night:** **Two all-round red lights** in a vertical line (where the day shapes are shown). **(1 mark)**
- **Sound signal:** **Bell rung rapidly for ~5 seconds** at intervals of not more than 1 minute; plus **three distinct bell strikes** immediately before and after the rapid ringing. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Explain with examples the **sound signaling rules** prescribed under COLREGs for: (a) Maneuvering and warning signals, and (b) Sound signals in restricted visibility (fog). Include the duration of each signal and the time interval between consecutive signals.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Maneuvering and Warning Signals [7 Marks]**

All whistle signals (Rule 34) are made using **one short blast ≈ 1 s**, **one long blast = 4 to 6 s**, with an **interval of about 1 second** between successive blasts.

| Signal | Meaning | Example Situation |
|---|---|---|
| **One short blast** | I am altering my course to **starboard** | A ship turning right at a junction. **(1.5 marks)** |
| **Two short blasts** | I am altering my course to **port** | A ship turning left at a junction. **(1.5 marks)** |
| **Three short blasts** | I am operating **astern propulsion** | A ship reversing out of a berth. **(1 mark)** |
| **Five or more short, rapid blasts** | **Doubt / Danger signal** — used when unsure of the other ship's intent. **(1.5 marks)** |
| **One prolonged blast (4–6 s)** at intervals of not more than **2 minutes** | Used by a **vessel in a narrow channel** approaching a bend — acts as a "corner signal." **(1.5 marks)** |

**(b) Sound Signals in Restricted Visibility [7 Marks]**

When visibility is restricted (fog, snow, heavy rain), the following signals (Rule 35) replace visual signals:

**Power-driven vessel making way through the water:**
- **One prolonged blast** at intervals of **not more than 2 minutes**. **(1.5 marks)**

**Power-driven vessel stopped (not making way):**
- **Two prolonged blasts** at intervals of **not more than 2 minutes**. **(1.5 marks)**

**Vessel at anchor:**
- **Rapid ringing of the bell for about 5 seconds** at intervals of not more than **1 minute**.
- For vessels **$\geq 100$ m**, a **gong** (different tone from the bell) is rung at the stern immediately after each bell sequence.
- At the bow, the bell is struck for ~5 seconds; at the stern, the gong strikes ~5 seconds to indicate the vessel's length. **(2 marks)**

**Vessel aground:**
- **Three distinct bell strikes** (clear, spaced), immediately followed by rapid ringing for ~5 seconds, then **three more distinct bell strikes**, repeated at intervals of not more than **1 minute**. **(1.5 marks)**

**Vessel not under command / restricted in maneuverability / fishing / sailing:**
- Each must sound the appropriate signal as per Rule 35, every **2 minutes** or less. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU Examiner's Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing side light arcs (112.5°) with right angles (90°)** — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting that the second masthead light is mandatory only for $L \geq 50$ m** — losing 1–2 marks in part (a) of long questions.
> 3. **Not mentioning the minimum range of visibility (6 nm / 3 nm / 2 nm / 1 nm)** — examiners allocate 1 mark exclusively for this.
> 4. **Mixing up "five short blasts" with the "prolonged blast"** — five short = *doubt/danger*; prolonged (4–6 s) = *narrow channel signal* or *fog signal*.
> 5. **Writing "day shapes" instead of "lights"** for nighttime scenarios, and vice versa.
> 6. **Omitting the units (nautical miles / seconds / metres)** in the final answer.
> 7. **Failing to mention "all-round"** when describing lights of vessels at anchor, aground, NUC, or RAM.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **COLREGs** are the universal law of navigation lights and sound signals at sea.
- **Masthead light** = White, **225°**, 6 nm range (for $L \geq 50$ m).
- **Port sidelight** = Red, **112.5°**; **Starboard sidelight** = Green, **112.5°**.
- **Stern light** = White, **135°**.
- **All-round light** = **360°**; used for NUC, RAM, aground, and at-anchor vessels.
- **Towing light** = **Yellow**, 135°, above stern light.
- **Two masthead lights** are mandatory **only for $L \geq 50$ m**, with the aft one **higher** and vertical separation **$\geq 4.5$ m**.
- **Range of visibility** brackets: **$L \geq 50$ m → 6 nm masthead / 3 nm others**; **$20 \leq L < 50$ m → 5 nm / 2 nm**; **$12 \leq L < 20$ m → 3 nm / 2 nm**; **$L < 12$ m → 2 nm / 1 nm**.
- **Whistle** mandatory for **$L \geq 12$ m**; **Bell** for **$L \geq 20$ m**; **Gong** for **$L \geq 100$ m** (at stern, different tone from bow bell).
- **Short blast ≈ 1 s; Long blast = 4 to 6 s; Interval ≈ 1 s.**
- **One short** = starboard turn; **Two short** = port turn; **Three short** = astern propulsion; **Five short** = doubt/danger.
- **One prolonged** in narrow channel (at bend).
- **Fog:** 1 prolonged = making way; 2 prolonged = stopped; bell = anchored; bell + gong for $\geq 100$ m.
- **At anchor** = 1 black ball (day) + 1 all-round white light (night).
- **Aground** = 3 black balls (day) + 2 all-round red lights (night) + special bell signal.
- **NUC** = 2 black balls (day) + 2 all-round red lights (night).
- **RAM** = Ball-Diamond-Ball (day) + 3 all-round (red-white-red) lights.
- **Pilot vessel** = none (day) + all-round white + all-round red (lower).
- **Trawler (fishing)** = 2 cones apex together + green all-round (above) + white all-round (masthead).
- **Rule of thumb for arc continuity:** $225° + 2(112.5°) + 135° = 585°$, giving $585° - 360° = 225°$ of overlap (redundancy).
- **Geometric center of masthead arc** = dead ahead (0°); **Stern light** = dead astern (180°).
- **Each sidelight overlaps the masthead arc by 22.5° forward and overlaps the stern light arc by 22.5° aft.**
- **Total blind spots:** **0°** — full 360° visibility achieved.
- **Sidelights must be fitted with inboard screens** to prevent the port light from being seen across the bow.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In numerical problems, always quote the **arc in degrees** AND the **range in nautical miles (nm)** with the corresponding **vessel-length bracket**. Examiners award marks for the bracket condition as a separate point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigational Mast. Life Saving Appliances (LSA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigational Mast. Life Saving Appliances (LSA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Navigational Mast — Formal Definition

A **navigational mast** is a vertical (or near-vertical) structural column erected on the upper deck or superstructure of a vessel, primarily designed to carry navigation, signaling, and communication equipment such as radar antennas, direction-finding loops, navigation lights, searchlights, whistles, flags, and aerial leads. In naval architecture, the mast is classified under the broader category of **"ship's outfit"** and is a critical element of the **general arrangement** because it influences stability, freeboard, air draft, and the electromagnetic horizon of bridge equipment.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the navigational mast is treated as a **structural-stiffened hollow section** designed to withstand combined loads from wind pressure on its own surface, the weight of fitted equipment, ship motion-induced inertial loads, and the bending moment from whip action during heavy weather.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The mast is considered a *load-bearing outfit component* whose scantlings (dimensions and plate thicknesses) are governed by classification society rules (IRS / Lloyd's Register / DNV) and not by a simple one-size-fits-all formula.

### 1.2 Life Saving Appliances (LSA) — Formal Definition

**Life Saving Appliances (LSA)** are the totality of equipment, fittings, and devices carried on board a ship to ensure the **preservation of human life** during emergencies such as abandonment of the vessel, man-overboard situations, fire, or flooding. The design, number, and arrangement of LSA are regulated internationally by:

- **SOLAS Chapter III** (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea, 1974, as amended).
- **LSA Code** (Resolution MSC.48(66) — International Life-Saving Appliance Code).
- **Indian Merchant Shipping Rules** for Indian-flag vessels.
- **Classification society** additional requirements (IRS, LR, BV, DNV).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box (KTU Expected):** *Life Saving Appliances are devices, equipment, and arrangements provided on a ship to enable persons on board to abandon ship safely, survive in the water or on survival craft, and be recovered.*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-world analogy for a Navigational Mast:**
Think of a ship's mast as the **"antenna tower on top of a high-rise building."** Just as a communication tower on a building must be tall enough to clear obstacles and provide an unobstructed radio horizon, a ship's mast must rise above the superstructure so that radar beams, navigation lights, and signal flags are visible to other ships and shore stations. The mast is also like a **Christmas tree** — it is the structure upon which the ship "hangs" all of its navigational "ornaments" (radar, lights, whistles, aerials).

**Real-world analogy for LSA:**
Think of LSA as the **"emergency preparedness kit"** of a building (fire extinguishers, evacuation slides, life jackets, first-aid kits) — but **amplified and standardized for a maritime environment where the building (ship) might sink and the occupants might end up in the ocean**. The lifeboat is the **stairwell evacuation**, the life raft is the **emergency shelter**, the life jacket is the **personal floatation device (PFD)**, and the EPIRB is the **distress beacon** that tells rescuers where you are.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Air draft** of vessel = height of mast tip above waterline → determines passage under bridges.
> - **LSA carriage requirement** = based on **Total Number of Persons on Board (POB)**.
> - **Lifeboat capacity** = 100% of persons on each side of the ship (port + starboard).
> - **Life raft capacity** = 100% of total persons, in **two or more** groups, equally distributed port/starboard.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Navigational Masts

Masts are classified by **structural configuration** and by **function**.

| Sl. No. | Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---------|------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Pole Mast** | Single vertical pole, sometimes in two sections joined by a fid. | Small vessels, tugs, coasters. |
| 2 | **Tripod Mast** | Three poles forming a triangle in plan view, joined at top. | Warships, large merchant vessels. |
| 3 | **Lattice Mast** | Built-up structure with diagonal bracing, often tubular or angle sections. | Large naval combatants, tankers. |
| 4 | **Hollow Cylindrical Mast** | Single large-diameter steel cylinder, stiffened internally. | Most modern merchant ships (preferred for radar transparency). |
| 5 | **King Post / Samson Post** | Short stout mast used for cargo gear support. | Cargo holds, derricks. |

### 2.2 Functional Classification of Masts

1. **Foremast** — typically carries signal halyards, forward navigation lights.
2. **Mainmast** — the principal mast, carries main navigation lights, radar, radio aerials.
3. **Mizenmast** — aft mast, often carries signaling equipment and after lights.
4. **Monkey Island Mast** — short mast atop the navigation bridge, carries compass, direction finder.

> [!NOTE]
> On modern merchant vessels, you commonly find a **single forward mast (fore-mast)** and a **single aft mast (mizzen-mast)**, with a **monkey-island mast** on top of the bridge.

### 2.3 Loads Acting on a Navigational Mast

A mast designer must account for the following loads:

- **Dead load (D)** = self-weight of mast + weight of permanently fitted equipment.
- **Wind load (W)** = aerodynamic pressure on mast surface and on fitted equipment.
- **Ice load (I)** = weight of accumulated ice (for cold-weather service, e.g., Baltic, North Atlantic winter).
- **Inertial load (F)** = dynamic force from ship roll, pitch, heave.
- **Whip load** = sudden snatch load from broken halyards (for sailing vessels; minor for motor ships).
- **Vibration / resonance** from rotating radar antennas.

**Wind pressure formula:**

$$P = C_d \cdot q \cdot A$$

where:

- $P$ = Wind force (N)
- $C_d$ = Drag coefficient (≈ **1.2** for cylindrical mast, **2.0** for flat surfaces)
- $q$ = Dynamic wind pressure = $0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2$ (Pa)
- $\rho$ = Air density = **1.225 kg/m³** (at standard sea-level conditions)
- $V$ = Design wind speed (m/s)
- $A$ = Projected area of mast + fittings (m²)

> Design wind speed per IRS rules for mast scantlings: typically **V = 30 m/s (≈ 108 km/h)** for unrestricted service, or **V = 50 m/s** for extreme conditions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---------|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Wind force | $P = C_d \cdot 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot A$ | N | Wind load on mast |
| 2 | Air density (standard) | $\rho = 1.225$ | kg/m³ | Atmospheric reference |
| 3 | Bending moment on mast base | $M = P \cdot H_{eff}$ | N·m | Base moment due to wind |
| 4 | LSA — Lifeboat capacity | $C_{LB} = 1.0 \times \text{POB}$ (port) + $1.0 \times \text{POB}$ (stb) | persons | SOLAS Reg. III/21 |
| 5 | LSA — Liferaft capacity | $C_{LR} = 1.0 \times \text{POB}$, in ≥ 2 groups | persons | SOLAS Reg. III/21 |
| 6 | Lifeboat persons per craft | Limited to **150 persons max** | persons | LSA Code Ch. 4 |
| 7 | Liferaft max persons per craft | Limited to **25 persons max** | persons | LSA Code Ch. 4 |
| 8 | Life jacket provision | $1.05 \times \text{POB}$ (with extras) | jackets | SOLAS Reg. III/7 |
| 9 | Immersion suit provision | $1.0 \times \text{POB}$ for bulk carriers > 500 GT, or all vessels on certain routes | suits | SOLAS Reg. III/7 |
| 10 | EPIRB carriage | At least **1** for all ships; **2** for ships > 500 GT on certain voyages | units | SOLAS Reg. IV/7 |
| 11 | SART carriage | At least **1** for ships 300–500 GT; **2** for > 500 GT | units | SOLAS Reg. III/6 |
| 12 | Line-throwing appliance | **1** set of 4 projectiles, 1 line each | set | SOLAS Reg. III/18 |
| 13 | Rocket parachute flare | **12** for passenger, **6** for cargo | units | SOLAS Reg. III/6 |
| 14 | Hand-held flare | **6** for passenger, **6** for cargo | units | SOLAS Reg. III/6 |
| 15 | Buoyant smoke signal | **2** for ships > 500 GT, optional for smaller | units | SOLAS Reg. III/6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions / assumptions to state in KTU exam answers:**
> - Standard air density at 15°C, 101.325 kPa.
> - Drag coefficient depends on Reynolds number; assume $C_d \approx 1.2$ for smooth cylinder.
> - LSA carriage is calculated on **maximum permitted number of persons**, not actual crew.
> - Liferafts must be stowed so as to float free if ship sinks (hydrostatic release unit).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

The LSA design directly determines **maritime safety certification** — a ship cannot obtain a Safety Construction Certificate or Safety Equipment Certificate from the flag administration (Directorate General of Shipping, India) unless LSA is fully compliant. For ship designers, LSA influences:

- **General arrangement** (position of boats on each side).
- **Freeboard** (lifeboat stowage at embarkation deck level).
- **Sheer strength** (winch and davit loads).
- **Stability booklet** (assumption of fully loaded boats swung outboard for damage stability).

Mast design similarly affects:
- **Air draft** (passage under bridges, e.g., Suez Canal Bridge — 70 m clearance).
- **Radar performance** (height of scanner above waterline determines radar horizon).
- **EMC** (electromagnetic compatibility — separation of radar and radio antennas).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bending Moment at Base of Navigational Mast

**Given:**
- Mast height = $H = 35 \text{ m}$ above the upper deck.
- Mast diameter (cylindrical) = $D = 1.0 \text{ m}$.
- Drag coefficient = $C_d = 1.2$ (smooth cylinder).
- Air density = $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- Design wind speed = $V = 30 \text{ m/s}$.
- Additional projected area of radar, platforms, ladders, etc. = $A_f = 4.0 \text{ m}^2$.
- Acceleration factor for ship motion (roll-induced) = $K_a = 1.5$.

**Step 1 — Compute the projected area of the mast itself (cylindrical surface).**

$$A_m = D \cdot H = 1.0 \times 35 = 35 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total projected area.**

$$A = A_m + A_f = 35 + 4 = 39 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the dynamic wind pressure.**

$$q = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2$$

$$q = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (30)^2$$

$$q = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900$$

$$q = 551.25 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the wind force on the mast.**

$$P_{wind} = C_d \cdot q \cdot A$$

$$P_{wind} = 1.2 \times 551.25 \times 39$$

$$P_{wind} = 25,797.9 \text{ N} \approx 25.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the acceleration factor (ship roll contribution).**

$$P_{total} = K_a \cdot P_{wind}$$

$$P_{total} = 1.5 \times 25,797.9 = 38,696.85 \text{ N} \approx 38.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the bending moment at the base of the mast.**

Assume the wind force acts at the centroid of the projected area, which for a uniform cylinder is at mid-height:

$$H_{eff} = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{35}{2} = 17.5 \text{ m}$$

$$M_{base} = P_{total} \cdot H_{eff}$$

$$M_{base} = 38,696.85 \times 17.5$$

$$M_{base} = 677,194.875 \text{ N·m} \approx 677.2 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 7 — Section modulus required (for allowable stress $\sigma_{all} = 165$ MPa for mild steel).**

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{base}}{\sigma_{all}}$$

$$Z_{req} = \frac{677,194.875}{165 \times 10^6}$$

$$Z_{req} = 4.10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 4.10 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 8 — Select a section.** For a hollow cylindrical mast:

$$Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{(D_o^4 - D_i^4)}{D_o}$$

Iterate: with $D_o = 1000$ mm and $t = 20$ mm → $D_i = 960$ mm:

$$Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{(1000^4 - 960^4)}{1000}$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{12} - 8.49 \times 10^{11})}{1000}$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{1.51 \times 10^{11}}{1000}$$

$$Z = 0.0982 \cdot 1.51 \times 10^8$$

$$Z = 1.483 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^3 = 14.83 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

Since $Z = 14.83 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 \gg Z_{req} = 4.10 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$, the section is **adequate** (or the plate thickness can be reduced to optimize weight).

### 3.2 Derivation: LSA Carriage for a Cargo Ship

**Given:**
- Type of ship: General Cargo.
- Gross Tonnage (GT) = 12,000.
- Maximum Persons on Board (POB) = 28 (crew + special personnel).
- Voyage: International (Unrestricted).

**Step 1 — Lifeboats (SOLAS Reg. III/21).**

Each side of the ship must carry lifeboats for **100% of POB**.

$$\text{Total lifeboat capacity} = 2 \times 28 = 56 \text{ persons}$$

**Step 2 — Distribute across boats.**

Take standard **45-person lifeboats** (or 50-person — both are common).

If using **2 × 30-person lifeboats** (one each side) = 60 persons → **compliant** (since 60 > 56).

**Step 3 — Liferafts (SOLAS Reg. III/21).**

Total liferaft capacity = **100% of POB** = 28 persons, divided into **2 or more groups**, equally port and starboard.

Each group ≥ 6 persons, so use 2 × 15-person liferafts (one each side) = 30 persons → **compliant**.

**Step 4 — Life jackets (SOLAS Reg. III/7.2).**

$$\text{Quantity} = 1.05 \times \text{POB} = 1.05 \times 28 = 29.4 \approx 30 \text{ jackets}$$

Plus child life jackets = **at least 2** for passenger ships; for cargo ships, child life jackets may be optional based on trade.

**Step 5 — Immersion suits (SOLAS Reg. III/7.3).**

For cargo ships operating in all waters, **1 per person** is required.

$$\text{Immersion suits} = 28$$

**Step 6 — EPIRBs (SOLAS Reg. IV/7.1).**

For ships > 500 GT: **at least 2 EPIRBs** (one manually activated, one float-free).

**Step 7 — SARTs (SOLAS Reg. III/6.2.2).**

For ships > 500 GT: **at least 2 SARTs** (3 GHz radar transponder).

**Step 8 — Pyrotechnics (SOLAS Reg. III/6.3).**

- Rocket parachute flares: **12** (passenger) or **6** (cargo) → use **6**.
- Hand-held flares: **6**.
- Buoyant smoke signals: **2**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Lifeboat vs. Liferaft vs. Life Jacket

| Parameter | Lifeboat | Liferaft | Life Jacket |
|-----------|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Function** | Primary survival craft | Secondary / supplementary | Personal floatation |
| **Capacity** | Up to 150 persons | Up to 25 persons | 1 person |
| **Construction** | Rigid fiberglass / steel | Inflatable rubber / PU-coated fabric | Foam / inflatable |
| **Engine** | Diesel inboard (where required) | None (paddles / sails on some) | N/A |
| **SOLAS Ch. III/21** | 100% POB each side | 100% POB, ≥ 2 groups | 105% POB |
| **Launch method** | Davit-launched (gravity / mechanical) | Throw-overboard / davit / float-free | Worn on person |
| **Survival duration** | ≥ 7–30 days with supplies | ≥ 7–30 days with supplies | Buoyancy indefinite (until damage) |
| **Color** | Orange / yellow | Orange / yellow | Orange (high visibility) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Navigational Mast Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Upper Deck] --> B[Mast Base Weld]
    B --> C[Lower Mast Section]
    C --> D[Mid Mast Section with Platform]
    D --> E[Upper Mast Section]
    E --> F[Mast Cap with Lightning Rod]
    D --> G[Equipment Mounting Bracket]
    G --> H[Radar Scanner Unit]
    G --> I[Navigation Light Array]
    G --> J[Signal Halyard Yard]
    G --> K[Whistle / Horn]
    G --> L[Radio Aerial Lead]
    M[Monkey Island Deck] --> N[Monkey Island Mast]
    N --> O[Direction Finder Loop]
    N --> P[Magnetic Compass Binnacle]
    Q[Ship Motion Loads] --> R[Load Combiner]
    S[Wind Load] --> R
    T[Dead Load] --> R
    R --> U[Base Reaction Forces]
    U --> V[Ship Hull Structure]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — LSA Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Life Saving Appliances LSA] --> B[Survival Craft]
    A --> C[Personal Safety Gear]
    A --> D[Visual and Auditory Signals]
    A --> E[Radio and Satellite Equipment]
    B --> B1[Lifeboats - 100% POB each side]
    B --> B2[Rescue Boats - 1 per ship]
    B --> B3[Liferafts - 100% POB in 2 groups]
    B --> B4[Lifebuoys - 8 minimum]
    C --> C1[Life Jackets - 105% POB]
    C --> C2[Immersion Suits - 100% POB]
    C --> C3[Thermal Protective Aids]
    D --> D1[Rocket Parachute Flares]
    D --> D2[Hand-Held Flares]
    D --> D3[Buoyant Smoke Signals]
    D --> D4[Line Throwing Appliance]
    E --> E1[EPIRB - 2 units]
    E --> E2[SART - 2 units]
    E --> E3[Two-Way VHF Radios - 3 units]
    E --> E4[Search and Rescue Locator]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Embarkation Arrangement (Lifeboat Station)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Embarkation Deck Level] --> B[Muster Station]
    B --> C[Lifeboat Davit]
    C --> D[Lifeboat Stowed Position]
    D --> E[Davit Swing Outboard]
    E --> F[Boat Lowered to Water]
    F --> G[Crew Embarkation via Embarkation Ladder]
    A1[Free-Fall Lifeboat Launching] --> A2[Sliding Ramp on Skids]
    A2 --> F
    P[Port Side Boat Deck] --> C
    S[Starboard Side Boat Deck] --> C
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Wind Load Path on Mast

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Pressure on Mast Surface] --> B[Aerodynamic Drag Force Pwind]
    A1[Wind on Fittings] --> B
    B --> C[Total Horizontal Load Ptotal]
    D[Ship Roll Inertia] --> E[Acceleration Factor Ka]
    C --> F[Multiply by Ka]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Moment at Base Mbase]
    G --> H[Section Modulus Z Required]
    H --> I[Mast Wall Thickness Selection]
    I --> J[Ship Hull via Welding]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Life Saving Appliance" as per SOLAS Convention. List any four categories of LSA that must be carried on board a cargo ship.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Life Saving Appliances (LSA) are the totality of equipment, fittings, and devices carried on board a ship to ensure the **preservation of human life** during emergencies at sea, including the safe abandonment of the vessel, survival in the water, and location of survivors by rescue services. (SOLAS Chapter III and LSA Code).

**Four Categories (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Survival craft** — Lifeboats, rescue boats, life rafts.
2. **Personal safety gear** — Life jackets, immersion suits, thermal protective aids.
3. **Visual and auditory signals** — Rocket parachute flares, hand flares, buoyant smoke signals, line-throwing appliance.
4. **Radio and satellite equipment** — Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacons (EPIRB), Search and Rescue Transponders (SART), two-way VHF radios.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*What is a navigational mast? State the various types of masts fitted on modern merchant ships.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A **navigational mast** is a vertical structural column erected on the upper deck or superstructure of a vessel to support navigation, signaling, and communication equipment such as radar scanners, navigation lights, signal halyards, radio antennas, and whistles.

**Types (2 marks — 0.4 each):**

1. **Pole Mast** — single pole, simple construction.
2. **Tripod Mast** — three poles converging at the top.
3. **Lattice Mast** — braced structure with multiple members.
4. **Hollow Cylindrical Mast** — single steel tube, most common on modern merchant ships.
5. **King Post / Samson Post** — short mast for cargo handling gear.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **For LSA questions, always quote the SOLAS Chapter and Regulation number** (e.g., SOLAS Reg. III/21.1.1) — students commonly lose 1–2 marks by failing to cite the regulation.
> 2. **For mast design, students often forget to apply the acceleration factor $K_a$** for ship motion. Always multiply the wind force by an appropriate factor (typically 1.5 for unrestricted service, or use the value given in the question).
> 3. **Lifeboat capacity is "100% POB on EACH SIDE"** — not 100% total. This is the most common mistake costing 2 marks.
> 4. **For LSA carriage, work out the POB first, then apply the multiplier** — do not skip this step.
> 5. **Show units in every step** of the derivation; marks are lost for missing units in numerical answers.

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the various categories of Life Saving Appliances required to be carried on board a cargo ship as per SOLAS Convention. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A general cargo ship has a maximum number of persons on board (POB) of 30. Determine the required number of:
   (i) Lifeboats and their capacity
   (ii) Life rafts
   (iii) Life jackets
   (iv) Immersion suits
   (v) EPIRBs
   (vi) SARTs
   (vii) Pyrotechnics (rocket flares, hand flares, smoke signals)
   **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Categories of LSA (7 marks):**

**1. Survival Craft (2 marks):**
- **Lifeboats** — Rigid, self-righting (or partially enclosed) craft capable of carrying 100% of POB on each side. Fitted with inboard diesel engine, sprinkler system, food, water, and radio.
- **Rescue boats** — At least 1 per ship, used to recover persons from water and transfer between vessels.
- **Life rafts** — Inflatable craft, total capacity 100% POB, distributed in 2 or more groups, port and starboard.
- **Lifebuoys** — Minimum 8 on ships > 60 m, with 2 fitted with self-activating smoke signals and self-igniting lights; 4 with buoyant lines.

**2. Personal Safety Gear (1.5 marks):**
- **Life jackets** — 105% of POB.
- **Immersion suits** — 1 per person for ships on certain voyages.
- **Thermal protective aids** — Sufficient for POB.
- **Child life jackets** — At least 2 on passenger ships carrying children.

**3. Visual and Auditory Signals (1.5 marks):**
- **Rocket parachute flares** — 6 (cargo ship), fired to altitude 300 m, descending by parachute.
- **Hand-held flares** — 6.
- **Buoyant smoke signals** — 2.
- **Line-throwing appliance** — 1 set, 4 projectiles.

**4. Radio and Satellite Equipment (2 marks):**
- **EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon)** — 1 for ships ≤ 500 GT, 2 for > 500 GT. Operates on 406 MHz via COSPAS-SARSAT satellite.
- **SART (Search and Rescue Transponder)** — 1 for ships 300–500 GT, 2 for > 500 GT. Operates on 9.2–9.5 GHz (radar band).
- **Two-way VHF radiotelephone apparatus** — 3 sets.
- **Survival craft VHF** — 1 per lifeboat/liferaft (GMDSS compliant).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: POB = 30.

**(i) Lifeboats (1.5 marks):**

Total capacity required = 100% POB on each side = 30 on port + 30 on stbd = 60.

Use **2 × 30-person lifeboats** (one each side) = 60 capacity → **compliant**.

**(ii) Life rafts (1 mark):**

Total = 100% POB = 30, in ≥ 2 groups, equally distributed.

Use **2 × 15-person liferafts** (one each side) = 30 → **compliant**.

**(iii) Life jackets (1 mark):**

$$1.05 \times 30 = 31.5 \rightarrow 32 \text{ jackets (round up)}$$

**(iv) Immersion suits (0.5 mark):**

For cargo ship on unrestricted voyage: **1 per person = 30 suits**.

**(v) EPIRBs (0.5 mark):**

For cargo ship > 500 GT on international voyage: **2 EPIRBs**.

**(vi) SARTs (0.5 mark):**

For ships > 500 GT: **2 SARTs**.

**(vii) Pyrotechnics (1 mark):**

- Rocket parachute flares: **6**
- Hand-held flares: **6**
- Buoyant smoke signals: **2**

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 / Internal Choice (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the classification and function of navigational masts fitted on modern merchant vessels. Mention the loads acting on the mast. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hollow cylindrical mast of 32 m height is to be designed for a cargo vessel. The mast has an external diameter of 800 mm and wall thickness of 15 mm. The design wind speed is 28 m/s and the drag coefficient is 1.2. The projected area of fittings is 3.5 m². Take an acceleration factor of 1.5 and air density 1.225 kg/m³. Determine:
   (i) The wind force on the mast.
   (ii) The bending moment at the base of the mast.
   **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification and Function of Masts (7 marks):**

**1. Structural Classification (3 marks):**
- **Pole Mast** — Single vertical pole, sometimes jointed with a fid at mid-height. Used on small vessels.
- **Tripod Mast** — Three-legged, very stable, common on warships and large tankers.
- **Lattice Mast** — Built-up with angle/circular bracing; high strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Hollow Cylindrical Mast** — Most common on modern merchant ships. Internally stiffened with rings and diaphragms. Preferred for **radar transparency** (low RCS) and **easy access** (ladder inside).
- **King Post / Samson Post** — Short mast inside cargo holds for derrick support.

**2. Functional Classification (1.5 marks):**
- **Foremast** — Forward, carries signal halyards, forward lights.
- **Mainmast** — Aft of bridge, principal mast.
- **Mizzenmast** — Aftmost, signals.
- **Monkey Island Mast** — On top of navigation bridge, carries magnetic compass and DF loop.

**3. Function (1 mark):**
Supports navigation lights, radar scanners, radio aerials, signal halyards, whistles, searchlights, and provides attachment for safety equipment (e.g., bosun's chair, accommodation ladder guide wires).

**4. Loads Acting on Mast (1.5 marks):**
- Dead load (self-weight + equipment).
- Wind load (primary external load).
- Inertial load from ship roll, pitch, heave.
- Ice load (cold regions).
- Whip / snatch load (minor, from broken halyards).
- Vibratory load from rotating radar.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $H = 32$ m, $D_o = 0.8$ m, $V = 28$ m/s, $C_d = 1.2$, $A_f = 3.5$ m², $K_a = 1.5$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Projected area of mast cylinder (1 mark):**

$$A_m = D_o \cdot H = 0.8 \times 32 = 25.6 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total projected area (0.5 mark):**

$$A = A_m + A_f = 25.6 + 3.5 = 29.1 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic wind pressure (1 mark):**

$$q = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (28)^2$$

$$q = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 784 = 480.2 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Wind force (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{wind} = C_d \cdot q \cdot A = 1.2 \times 480.2 \times 29.1$$

$$P_{wind} = 16,768.58 \text{ N} \approx 16.77 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Apply acceleration factor (0.5 mark):**

$$P_{total} = K_a \cdot P_{wind} = 1.5 \times 16.77 = 25.15 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Bending moment at base (1.5 marks):**

Wind force acts at the centroid of projected area, assumed at mid-height of mast:

$$H_{eff} = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{32}{2} = 16 \text{ m}$$

$$M_{base} = P_{total} \cdot H_{eff} = 25.15 \times 16 = 402.4 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Result:**
- (i) Wind force on mast ≈ **16.77 kN** (before $K_a$), **25.15 kN** (after $K_a$).
- (ii) Bending moment at base ≈ **402.4 kN·m**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topic Recap — Key Points to Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **Navigational Mast** = vertical structure carrying navigation, signal, and communication equipment.
- [ ] **Types of Masts**: Pole, Tripod, Lattice, Hollow Cylindrical, King Post.
- [ ] **Hollow Cylindrical Mast** is the most common on modern merchant ships (radar transparency).
- [ ] **Mast Loads**: Dead load, Wind load, Inertial load, Ice load, Whip load.
- [ ] **Wind force formula**: $P = C_d \cdot 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot A$
- [ ] **Air density (standard)** = **1.225 kg/m³**.
- [ ] **Drag coefficient** for smooth cylinder ≈ **1.2**.
- [ ] **Acceleration factor $K_a$** ≈ **1.5** is often applied to account for ship roll.
- [ ] **LSA is regulated by SOLAS Chapter III and LSA Code (MSC.48(66))**.
- [ ] **Lifeboat** = 100% POB on **each side** (port + starboard).
- [ ] **Liferaft** = 100% POB, in **≥ 2 groups**, port/stbd.
- [ ] **Max lifeboat capacity** = 150 persons; **max liferaft capacity** = 25 persons.
- [ ] **Life jackets** = **1.05 × POB**.
- [ ] **Immersion suits** = **1.0 × POB** (for cargo ships on unrestricted voyages).
- [ ] **EPIRB** = 1 for ≤ 500 GT, **2** for > 500 GT.
- [ ] **SART** = 1 for 300–500 GT, **2** for > 500 GT.
- [ ] **Pyrotechnics for cargo ship**: 6 rocket parachute + 6 hand-held + 2 buoyant smoke.
- [ ] **Lifebuoys**: minimum **8** for ships > 60 m; 2 with smoke + light, 4 with lines.
- [ ] **LSA carriage is based on Maximum Permitted POB**, not actual crew.
- [ ] **Liferafts must be float-free** (hydrostatic release unit) so they deploy automatically if ship sinks.
- [ ] **Mast base bending moment** = $M = K_a \cdot C_d \cdot 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot A \cdot H_{eff}$.
- [ ] **Always quote SOLAS regulation numbers** in exam answers (e.g., III/21, III/7, IV/7).
- [ ] **Units** must be shown in every step of numerical derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Regulations on LSA. Fire Fighting Appliances (FFA)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** International Regulations on LSA. Fire Fighting Appliances (FFA)

# International Regulations on LSA & Fire Fighting Appliances (FFA)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 International Regulations on Life-Saving Appliances (LSA)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Life-Saving Appliances (LSA) refer to the complete inventory of equipment, fittings, and personal survival gear mandated aboard every seagoing vessel to ensure the **abandonment, survival, recovery, and rescue of persons in distress at sea**. The international regulatory framework is principally governed by **SOLAS Chapter III** and the detailed **International Life-Saving Appliance (LSA) Code**, both administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS = International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (1974, as amended)**. The current consolidated edition is **SOLAS 2020/2021 Amendments**, with Chapter III being the binding chapter for all LSA matters.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Aircraft Emergency Kit" Analogy):**
Think of a commercial aircraft's safety kit — life vests under every seat, oxygen masks, evacuation slides, and emergency lighting. Now scale this for a ship. On a vessel, the LSA is the equivalent "emergency survival toolkit," but for a much harsher environment (open ocean, cold water, possible fire, possible sinking). The IMO essentially acts like the aviation regulator (FAA/ICAO) — it dictates the *minimum* number, type, and quality of safety equipment that *must* be onboard before the ship is even allowed to leave port. Just as a plane cannot take off without a safety briefing and life vests, a ship cannot sail without LSA that meet IMO standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statutory Documents in this Domain:**
> 1. **SOLAS Convention, 1974** — Chapter III (Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements)
> 2. **International Life-Saving Appliance (LSA) Code** — Res. MSC.48(66) (1996)
> 3. **International Convention on Maritime Search and Rescue (SAR Convention), 1979**
> 4. **MARPOL** (for fire-related environmental safety)
> 5. **FSS Code** (Fire Safety Systems Code) — Res. MSC.98(73), as amended

### 1.2 Fire Fighting Appliances (FFA)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Fire Fighting Appliances (FFA) encompass the **total suite of fixed and portable equipment** designed for the **detection, containment, suppression, and extinction of fire** aboard a vessel, in compliance with **SOLAS Chapter II-2** and the **Fire Safety Systems (FSS) Code**. These systems are engineered considering the unique maritime fire hazards — engine room fires, cargo hold fires (oil/chemical), galley fires, and electrical fires.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Chapter II-2** — *Construction — Fire protection, fire detection, fire extinction*. This is the principal regulatory chapter, supplemented by the **FSS Code** which provides detailed engineering specifications.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Building Fire Code" Analogy):**
Imagine a high-rise building's fire safety system — sprinkler systems in ceilings, fire extinguishers in corridors, fire-rated doors, smoke detectors, evacuation stairs, and fire-resistant structural materials. A ship's FFA is the maritime twin of this system, but with critical differences:
- **No "exterior fire escape"** — fire cannot be exited by simply running outside; the ship is surrounded by water.
- **Compartmentalization** is the *first* line of defense (like firewalls in buildings).
- **Self-contained firefighting medium** (no fire hydrants connected to municipal water; the ship must carry its own water/foam/CO₂).

> [!NOTE]
> **Classes of Shipboard Fire (Per SOLAS):**
> - **Class A** — Ordinary combustibles (wood, textiles, paper) → water/foam
> - **Class B** — Flammable liquids (oil, fuel) → foam/CO₂/dry powder
> - **Class C** — Flammable gases (LPG, LNG vapor) → dry powder/CO₂
> - **Class D** — Combustible metals (rare on ships)
> - **Class F (K)** — Cooking oils/fats (galley) → wet chemical

---

### 1.3 Regulatory Hierarchy — SOLAS Structure

The **SOLAS Convention** is organized into **14 Chapters**. For LSA and FFA, the relevant chapters are:

| Chapter | Title | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Chapter II-1** | Construction — Subdivision and stability | Watertight integrity, flooding prevention |
| **Chapter II-2** | Construction — Fire protection, fire detection, fire extinction | **FFA primary chapter** |
| **Chapter III** | Life-saving appliances and arrangements | **LSA primary chapter** |
| **Chapter IV** | Radiocommunications | EPIRBs, SARTs, distress signals |
| **Chapter V** | Safety of navigation | Distress signals, position info |
| **Chapter VII** | Carriage of dangerous goods | Special FFA for chemical/oil tankers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance vs. Prescriptive Standards:**
> SOLAS uses a **two-tier regulatory system**:
> 1. **Chapters II-2 and III** → set the **functional/performance requirements** ("the ship must be able to...")
> 2. **FSS Code and LSA Code** → provide the **detailed technical specifications** ("the equipment must have a capacity of...")
> This means a shipowner *cannot* deviate from FSS/LSA Code specifications unless an *equivalent* arrangement is approved by the Flag State Administration (with IMO notification).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
