## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 SOLAS Chapter III — Life-Saving Appliances

**Scope of Chapter III** (Regulation 1): Applies to all ships on **international voyages** (passenger + cargo). The chapter is divided into three parts:
- **Part A** — General
- **Part B** — Ship Requirements
- **Part C** — Survival Craft (lifeboats, life rafts, rescue boats)

**The Core Philosophy of LSA:** Every person aboard must have an **equal chance of survival** in an emergency. This is enforced through three principles:
1. **Abandonment** — orderly evacuation to survival craft
2. **Survival** — protection from hypothermia, drowning, exposure
3. **Rescue** — signaling, location, retrieval

### 2.2 Categories of Life-Saving Appliances (Per LSA Code)

The LSA Code categorizes equipment into 9 distinct groups:

**A. Survival Craft:**
- **Lifeboats** (totally enclosed, partially enclosed, open)
- **Liferafts** (inflatable, rigid)
- **Rescue boats** (fast rescue craft)

**B. Personal LSA:**
- **Lifejackets** (adult, child, infant)
- **Immersion suits** (anti-exposure suits)
- **Thermal protective aids (TPA)**

**C. Position-Indicating & Signaling:**
- **EPIRBs** (Emergency Position-Indicating Radio Beacons)
- **SARTs** (Search and Rescue Transponders)
- **Pyrotechnics** (flares, line-throwing appliances)
- **Life buoys** (with self-activating smoke signals, lights)

**D. Embarkation & Recovery:**
- **Marine evacuation systems (MES)**
- **Embarkation ladders**
- **Launching appliances** (davits, slipways, free-fall)

### 2.3 Lifeboat Capacity Formula & Rules (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lifeboat Capacity Calculation (LSA Code Chapter IV):**
> The **maximum permitted number of persons (Np)** a lifeboat can carry is the **LESSER** of:
> - The number of persons the boat can carry **seated in normal position** (with at least **0.40 m × 0.40 m** floor space per person, per LSA Code 4.1.1.1).
> - The number of persons with **sufficient headroom** (minimum **1.115 m** headroom over the seats, per LSA Code 4.1.1.2).

**Volume-Based Formula:**

$$
N_p = \frac{V_{net}}{0.40 \times 0.40 \times h_{seat}}
$$

Where:
- $N_p$ = number of persons the lifeboat can carry
- $V_{net}$ = net internal volume of the lifeboat (m³)
- $0.40 \text{ m} \times 0.40 \text{ m} = 0.16 \text{ m}^2$ = minimum seat floor area per person
- $h_{seat}$ = average seat height (m)

**Tank Capacity Formula for Lifeboats:**

$$
W_{water} = 0.25 \times N_p \text{ (litres)}
$$

$$
W_{provisions} = 0.20 \times N_p \text{ (kg or MJ equivalent)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **LSA Code 4.4 (Provisions):** Every lifeboat shall carry:
> - **3 litres of fresh water per person**
> - **10,000 kJ (2,400 kcal) of provisions per person**
> - **Survival rations totalling not less than 10,000 kJ per person**

### 2.4 Liferaft Requirements (SOLAS III/31, III/32)

| Vessel Type | Number of Liferafts | Capacity Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **Passenger Ship** | ≥ 2 on each side + 1 on bridge wing (depending on length) | 100% of persons on each side |
| **Cargo Ship** | ≥ 1 on each side | 100% of total complement |
| **Tanker** | ≥ 1 on each side | 100% of total complement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Liferaft Sizing (LSA Code 4.2.4):**
> Number of persons in a liferaft shall not exceed the **maximum rated capacity** marked on the container. Liferafts are typically manufactured in capacities of: **6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20, 25 persons**.

### 2.5 SOLAS Chapter II-2 — Fire Protection (Theoretical Structure)

**Chapter II-2 is organized into:**
- **Part A** — General
- **Part B** — Prevention of fire and explosion
- **Part C** — Suppression of fire
- **Part D** — Escape
- **Part E** — Operational requirements

**The Three Defensive Pillars of SOLAS Fire Protection:**

1. **PREVENTION** — Ignition source control, fuel source control (oil-fired boilers, galley stoves).
2. **SUPPRESSION** — Detection, containment, extinguishment (this is the FFA layer).
3. **ESCAPE** — Safe egress (corridors, stairs, muster stations, lifeboats).

### 2.6 FSS Code — Detailed FFA Specifications

The **Fire Safety Systems (FSS) Code** contains **15 chapters** covering each fire protection system:

| FSS Code Chapter | System |
|---|---|
| 1 | General requirements |
| 2 | Non-pressurized sprinkler systems |
| 3 | Pressurized sprinkler systems |
| 4 | Fixed pressure water-spraying (water-mist) systems |
| 5 | Fixed foam fire-extinguishing systems |
| 6 | Fixed high-expansion foam systems |
| 7 | Fixed CO₂ fire-extinguishing systems |
| 8 | Fixed dry chemical powder systems |
| 9 | Fixed aerosol systems |
| 10 | Fixed fire detection and fire alarm systems |
| 11 | Up-to-date fire control plan |
| 12 | Equivalent fixed gas fire-extinguishing systems |
| 13 | Fixed automatic local application fire-extinguishing systems |
| 14 | Fixed fire-extinguishing systems for galleys and deep-fat cooking oil fires |
| 15 | Fixed hydrocarbon gas detection systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSS Code Chapter 7 (CO₂) — The Workhorse of Engine Room Fire Protection**

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Application | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_p = \dfrac{V_{net}}{0.16 \times h_{seat}}$ | Lifeboat person capacity | LSA Code 4.1.1 |
| 2 | $W_{water} = 0.25 \cdot N_p$ litres | Minimum water in lifeboat | LSA Code 4.4.1.1 |
| 3 | $E_{food} = 10{,}000 \text{ kJ/person}$ | Minimum food rations in lifeboat | LSA Code 4.4.1.2 |
| 4 | $Q_{CO_2} = V_{gross} \cdot K$ | CO₂ quantity for room flooding | FSS Code 7.2 |
| 5 | $Q_{CO_2} = V_{gross} \cdot 0.56$ kg/m³ (minimum) | CO₂ for machinery spaces | FSS Code 7.4.1 |
| 6 | $Q_{CO_2} = 0.7 \cdot V \cdot \rho_{CO_2}$ | Mass of CO₂ for cargo holds | FSS Code 7.5 |
| 7 | $L_{liferaft} = 2 \cdot N_{total} / 2$ (per side) | Liferaft distribution (cargo ships) | SOLAS III/31 |
| 8 | $V_{s} = 5 \cdot A_{ext}$ (litre/min, jets) | Hose nozzle flow rate | FSS Code 2.2 |
| 9 | $Hose_{length} \leq 20$ m | Maximum fire hose length | SOLAS II-2/10.2.3.1 |
| 10 | $n_{extinguishers} \geq 1$ per deck | Portable extinguisher minimum | SOLAS II-2/10.3 |
| 11 | $A_{eff} = A_{gross} \times 0.85$ | CO₂ net volume (15% duct/structure allowance) | FSS Code 7.2 |
| 12 | $P_{ext} \geq 0.5$ bar | Minimum nozzle pressure | FSS Code 2.2.3 |
| 13 | $T_{discharge} \leq 2$ min | CO₂ release time for machinery | FSS Code 7.4.3 |
| 14 | $T_{discharge} \leq 10$ min | CO₂ release for pump rooms | FSS Code 7.4.3 |
| 15 | $L_{jet} \geq 12$ m | Minimum water jet throw | FSS Code 2.2.4 |
| 16 | $H_{ext} \geq 0.3 \cdot L_{ship}$ or $\geq 9$ m, whichever is greater | Hydrant reach | FSS Code 2.4 |

> [!WARNING]
> **FSS Code vs. SOLAS Chapter II-2:** Always cite the **Code** when discussing engineering specifications (dimensions, flows, pressures), and the **Chapter** when discussing *requirements* (numbers, types, locations). KTU examiners deduct marks for mixing these.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of LSA/FFA Knowledge |
|---|---|
| **Naval Architecture** | Weight & stability calculations (LSAs add significant lightweight + deadweight). |
| **Offshore Engineering** | Lifeboat design for harsh environments, free-fall lifeboats for FPSOs. |
| **Coastal Engineering** | MES systems on passenger ferries, lifeboat embarkation deck design. |
| **Insurance & Class** | Class society approvals (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require FSS/LSA compliance. |
| **Port State Control** | Deficiencies in LSA/FFA result in detention of the ship. |
| **Marine Accident Investigation** | Post-incident analysis uses SOLAS benchmarks to determine causation. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Lifeboat Person Capacity (Floor Area Method)

**Problem Statement:** Determine the maximum number of persons a totally enclosed lifeboat can carry, given the following dimensions:
- Internal length = **6.0 m**
- Internal breadth = **2.5 m**
- Internal depth = **1.8 m**
- The boat is single-banked (1 row of seats on each side, with a centerline passage).
- The seating is a longitudinal bench of width = **0.45 m**, with seats on both sides.
- Seat deck-to-seat-back height (effective headroom for seated person) = **1.20 m**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the Gross Internal Floor Area ($A_{gross}$):**

$$
A_{gross} = L \times B = 6.0 \text{ m} \times 2.5 \text{ m} = 15.0 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Deduct the Centerline Passage Area:**
The LSA Code requires a **centerline passage of at least 0.45 m width** (for lifeboats with more than 9 persons). Area of the passage:

$$
A_{passage} = L \times W_{passage} = 6.0 \text{ m} \times 0.45 \text{ m} = 2.7 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Net Seatable Area ($A_{seat}$):**

$$
A_{seat} = A_{gross} - A_{passage} = 15.0 - 2.7 = 12.3 \text{ m}^2
$$

> However, since the seat bench is 0.45 m wide on each side (total 0.90 m), we use the actual seatable space **between the passage and the boat's side**:

$$
A_{seat}^{eff} = L \times (B - W_{passage}) = 6.0 \times (2.5 - 0.45) = 6.0 \times 2.05 = 12.3 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the LSA Code 0.40 m × 0.40 m Floor Space Rule:**

$$
N_{p,floor} = \frac{A_{seat}^{eff}}{0.40 \times 0.40} = \frac{12.3}{0.16} = 76.875 \rightarrow 76 \text{ persons (rounded down)}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Headroom Rule (1.115 m Minimum):**
The available headroom is $h_{seat} = 1.20$ m, which is greater than 1.115 m. The headroom rule gives:

$$
N_{p,head} = \text{limited by seat length} = \frac{L_{seat}}{0.40} = \frac{6.0}{0.40} = 15 \text{ persons per side}
$$

For two-sided seating (port + starboard), and assuming single-bench per side:

$$
N_{p,head} = 2 \times 15 = 30 \text{ persons (headroom-limited)}
$$

**Step 6 — The Governing (Limiting) Value:**

$$
\boxed{N_p = \min(N_{p,floor},\ N_{p,head}) = \min(76,\ 30) = 30 \text{ persons}}
$$

**Step 7 — Volume Check (LSA Code 4.1.1.3):**
The volume-based check requires $V_{net} \geq 0.40 \times 0.40 \times 1.115 \times N_p$:

$$
V_{required} = 0.16 \times 1.115 \times 30 = 5.352 \text{ m}^3
$$

Available net volume (assuming $V_{gross} = 6.0 \times 2.5 \times 1.8 = 27.0$ m³, deduct 10% for internal structure):

$$
V_{net} = 27.0 \times 0.90 = 24.3 \text{ m}^3 \gg 5.352 \text{ m}^3 \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU Exam:** Always apply **both** the floor-area method **and** the headroom/seat-length method. The **limiting value** governs. The volume check is a sanity verification, not the primary determinant.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: CO₂ Quantity for Engine Room Flooding

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the quantity of CO₂ required to flood the main engine room of a cargo vessel, and determine the number of 45 kg CO₂ cylinders needed.

**Given Data:**
- Engine room length $L = 20$ m
- Engine room breadth $B = 12$ m
- Engine room height (clear) $H = 8$ m
- Engine room is **below the bulkhead deck**.
- Permanent openings are negligible (assumed for worst case).

**Step 1 — Calculate the Gross Volume ($V_{gross}$):**

$$
V_{gross} = L \times B \times H = 20 \times 12 \times 8 = 1920 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the FSS Code 7.2.1.1 (Subtraction for Ducts, etc.):**
The net volume of the space requiring protection:

$$
V_{net} = V_{gross} \times 0.85 = 1920 \times 0.85 = 1632 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the FSS Code 7.4.1 (CO₂ Specific Volume Rate):**
For machinery spaces, the minimum CO₂ quantity is:

$$
Q_{CO_2} = V_{net} \times 0.56 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1632 \times 0.56 = 914.0 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of 45 kg Cylinders:**

$$
n_{cyl} = \frac{Q_{CO_2}}{45} = \frac{914.0}{45} = 20.31 \rightarrow 21 \text{ cylinders (rounded up)}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Discharge Time:**
Per FSS Code 7.4.3, CO₂ must be discharged into machinery spaces within **2 minutes** (85% of the quantity within 2 minutes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discharge time rule (FSS Code 7.4.3):** For machinery spaces, the CO₂ quantity shall be discharged in not more than **2 minutes**. For pump rooms, this extends to **10 minutes** (due to the larger volume and personnel entry considerations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Margin:** KTU examiners expect students to add a **10% safety margin** for CO₂ calculations in actual design problems: $Q_{design} = 1.10 \times Q_{calculated}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Fire Hydrant & Hose Reach Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A cargo ship has 6 cargo holds. The fire main and hydrants must be arranged so that at least **two jets of water** (not from the same hydrant) can reach any part of the ship normally accessible to passengers or crew.

**Given Data:**
- Ship length $L_{ship} = 180$ m
- Number of decks = 5
- Each hydrant serves an effective horizontal reach of **H = 0.3 × L_ship = 54 m or 9 m, whichever is greater** = 54 m.

**Step 1 — Determine Minimum Reach (FSS Code 2.4.1):**

$$
H_{min} = \max(0.3 \cdot L_{ship},\ 9 \text{ m}) = \max(0.3 \times 180,\ 9) = \max(54,\ 9) = 54 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Number of Hydrants Required:**

Assuming uniform spacing with effective hydrant reach = 54 m, hydrants are needed every $\sim$ 40–50 m to ensure overlap. For a 180 m ship:

$$
n_{hydrants} \geq \frac{L_{ship}}{H_{min}/2} \times \text{(redundancy factor)}
$$

The redundancy factor for "**two jets from different hydrants**" requires overlap of approximately 50%:

$$
n_{hydrants} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{ship}}{H_{min}/2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{180}{27} \right\rceil = 7 \text{ hydrants}
$$

**Step 3 — Hose Length Verification (SOLAS II-2/10.2.3.1):**
Maximum hose length = **20 m**, plus nozzle. The hydrant must be located within 20 m hose reach of any point.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing **fire hose length (20 m max)** with **fire monitor throw distance (up to 60+ m)**. SOLAS II-2 specifies hydrant *hose* reach, not monitor reach. KTU questions may give one value to mislead.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define "Life-Saving Appliance" (LSA) as per SOLAS. List any four categories of LSA with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Life-Saving Appliance (LSA)** is any piece of equipment, fitting, or system provided onboard a ship to ensure the **abandonment, survival, and recovery** of persons in distress at sea, as mandated by **SOLAS Chapter III** and the **LSA Code (Res. MSC.48(66))**.

**Four categories:**

| # | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Survival Craft** | Totally enclosed lifeboat (FPSS type) |
| 2 | **Personal Survival** | Inflatable lifejacket (LSA Code Ch. 2) |
| 3 | **Position-Indicating/Signaling** | 406 MHz EPIRB (COSPAS-SARSAT) |
| 4 | **Embarkation/Recovery** | Davit-launched lifeboat (gravity/electric) |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the six classes of portable fire extinguishers typically used on board merchant vessels. State the extinguishing medium and the fire class for which each is used.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Extinguishing Medium | Suitable Fire Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Water** | Fresh water | Class A (ordinary combustibles) |
| **Foam (AFFF)** | Aqueous Film Forming Foam | Class A + Class B (flammable liquids) |
| **CO₂** | Carbon Dioxide gas | Class B + Class C (electrical) |
| **Dry Powder** | Mono-ammonium phosphate | Class A, B, C (multi-purpose) |
| **Wet Chemical** | Potassium acetate | Class F (cooking oil) |
| **Vaporizing Liquid** | Halon replacement (e.g., HFC-227ea) | Class B, C (electrical) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

**Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**(a)** *With neat sketches, explain the constructional features of a **Totally Enclosed Lifeboat (TELB)** as per LSA Code. List the equipment it must carry. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Constructional Features of TELB (LSA Code Chapter 4.6):**

1. **Hull:** Reinforced fiberglass (FRP) or aluminum shell with watertight integrity. Double-skinned construction.
2. **Watertight Compartment:** Forward end raised 20% of lifeboat length to provide **40% reserve buoyancy** even when fully flooded.
3. **Canopy/Enclosure:** A permanently attached, rigid canopy covers the entire boat with **entrance** (hatch) and **port holes** (windows). A **fixed two-way VHF radio** is mounted on the canopy.
4. **Air Supply:** Self-contained **breathing air system** providing air for the full complement for **10 minutes minimum** (some LSA codes require 6 hours for tanker lifeboats).
5. **Engine:** Diesel or petrol outboard engine with **self-starter** (hand-starting permitted only for propeller-end engine). Fuel sufficient for **24 hours continuous operation**.
6. **Propulsion:** Single propeller with **water-jet reverse** for crash-stop capability.
7. **Steering:** Wheel and tiller with helm position visible from helm seat.
8. **Davit Connection:** Two release hooks (single fall operation not permitted — both hooks release simultaneously).
9. **Buoyancy Tanks:** Foam-filled (or equivalent) rigid tanks.
10. **Drain Valves:** Two automatic, operable from inside and outside.

**Equipment Carried (LSA Code 4.4):**

| Item | Quantity |
|---|---|
| Fresh water (drinkable) | 3 L per person |
| Provisions | 10,000 kJ per person |
| First-aid kit | 1 |
| Survival manual | 1 |
| Fire extinguisher (CO₂) | 1 |
| Parachute rockets | 6 |
| Hand flares | 6 |
| Buoyant smoke signals | 2 |
| Electric torch (waterproof) | 1 |
| Whistle | 1 |
| Radar reflector | 1 |
| Bailer | 1 |
| Bucket (lanyard) | 2 |
| Oars (spare) | Set |
| Fish-hook and line | Set |
| Sea anchor | 1 |
| Painter | 1 |
| Paddles | 2 |
| Waterproof box for pyrotechnics | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **TELB vs. Open Lifeboat:** The TELB eliminates the risk of swamping in heavy seas — a major advance after the Titanic disaster (1912) and subsequent lifeboat evolution.

---

**(b)** *A 220 m long cargo vessel has a **4-deck superstructure**. The fire main is to be routed along the superstructure length. Using the **FSS Code Chapter 2** rules, determine the **minimum number of fire hydrants** required, and explain the criterion for hydrant spacing. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Minimum Hydrant Reach (FSS Code 2.4.1):**

$$
H_{min} = \max(0.3 \times L_{ship},\ 9 \text{ m}) = \max(0.3 \times 220,\ 9) = \max(66,\ 9) = 66 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the "Two Jets, Not Same Hydrant" Rule (FSS Code 2.4.1):**
The criterion is that **at least two jets of water from *different* hydrants** must reach any part of the ship normally accessible. This requires **50% hydrant overlap**, so the effective spacing is:

$$
s_{eff} = \frac{H_{min}}{2} = \frac{66}{2} = 33 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Number of Hydrants:**

$$
n_{hyd} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{ship}}{s_{eff}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{220}{33} \right\rceil = 7 \text{ hydrants (minimum)}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Hose Length (SOLAS II-2/10.2.3.1):**
Each hose ≤ 20 m. To achieve the 33 m effective reach, two hydrants within $\sim$ 40 m provide 2 × 20 m = 40 m total hose reach with 50% overlap.

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{n_{hydrants} \geq 7 \text{ hydrants (with 1 international shore connection)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Don't Confuse the Rules:**
> - **SOLAS II-2/10.2** says: "At least one jet from any hydrant to any part of the ship."
> - **FSS Code 2.4.1** says: "At least two jets from *different* hydrants to any part."
> KTU expects students to cite **both** and explain that FSS Code is more stringent (the binding requirement).

---

**Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024 — Alternative]`

**(a)** *Explain the **FSS Code Chapter 7** requirements for a fixed **CO₂ fire-extinguishing system** for a machinery space. Include the discharge quantity, volume calculation, and discharge time. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**FSS Code Chapter 7 — CO₂ System for Machinery Space (Cat A):**

**1. Application:** FSS Code 7 applies to fixed gas fire-extinguishing systems using CO₂ as the extinguishing medium. It is the **most commonly installed** system in machinery spaces of cargo ships (engine rooms, boiler rooms, scavenge air spaces).

**2. Design Basis:**

| Parameter | FSS Code Requirement |
|---|---|
| CO₂ quantity for machinery space | ≥ **0.56 kg/m³** of net volume |
| Discharge time (machinery) | ≤ **2 minutes** (85% of quantity) |
| Volume basis | **Net volume** = 0.85 × gross volume |
| Number of releases | Single release only (no modulated release) |
| Personnel evacuation | Mandatory alarm + time delay |

**3. Volume Calculation:**

$$
V_{gross} = L \times B \times H \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)}
$$

$$
V_{net} = V_{gross} \times 0.85
$$

**4. CO₂ Quantity:**

$$
Q_{CO_2} = V_{net} \times 0.56 \quad \text{(kg)}
$$

**5. Number of Cylinders (45 kg standard):**

$$
n_{cyl} = \left\lceil \frac{Q_{CO_2}}{45} \right\rceil
$$

**6. Discharge Time (FSS Code 7.4.3):**
For **machinery spaces** of Category A: 85% of quantity discharged in **≤ 2 minutes**.
For **pump rooms** (tankers): ≤ 10 minutes.

**7. Operational Sequence:**

```
Step 1: Fire detected in machinery space
Step 2: Fire alarm activated
Step 3: Personnel evacuate (mandatory; no one should remain)
Step 4: Stop forced ventilation fans (this prevents loss of CO₂)
Step 5: Stop oil fuel pumps to machinery spaces
Step 6: 30-second time delay (FSS Code 7.6.2)
Step 7: CO₂ release activated
Step 8: 85% of quantity released within 2 minutes
Step 9: Space sealed, fire extinguishes
Step 10: Do NOT enter for at least 30 minutes (post-discharge ventilation)
```

**8. Why 30-second Time Delay?**
This is a **safety feature** to allow personnel to escape the space before CO₂ is released. CO₂ is an **asphyxiant** at high concentration (>10% can be lethal to humans). The FSS Code mandates this delay as a **life-safety** measure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSS Code 7.6.2.4:** Automatic release of CO₂ in machinery spaces is **NOT permitted**. The system must be **manually activated** after confirmation that all personnel have evacuated. This is a critical safety rule.

---

**(b)** *A cargo ship's engine room has dimensions **18 m × 10 m × 7 m** (L × B × H). Calculate:*
- *(i) the gross and net volume of the space*
- *(ii) the minimum CO₂ quantity required*
- *(iii) the number of 45 kg CO₂ cylinders required*
- *(iv) the minimum diameter of the main distribution pipe (assuming discharge velocity 10 m/s)*
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Volumes:**

$$
V_{gross} = 18 \times 10 \times 7 = 1260 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_{net} = 0.85 \times 1260 = 1071 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(ii) CO₂ Quantity (FSS Code 7.4.1 — 0.56 kg/m³ for machinery):**

$$
Q_{CO_2} = 1071 \times 0.56 = 599.76 \text{ kg} \approx 600 \text{ kg}
$$

**Design margin (10%):**

$$
Q_{design} = 600 \times 1.10 = 660 \text{ kg}
$$

**(iii) Number of 45 kg Cylinders:**

$$
n_{cyl} = \left\lceil \frac{600}{45} \right\rceil = \lceil 13.33 \rceil = 14 \text{ cylinders (minimum)}
$$

**Or with design margin:**

$$
n_{cyl} = \left\lceil \frac{660}{45} \right\rceil = \lceil 14.67 \rceil = 15 \text{ cylinders}
$$

**(iv) Main Distribution Pipe Diameter:**

The CO₂ is stored as a saturated liquid at $\sim$ 5.86 MPa (58.6 bar) at 21°C. At the discharge nozzle, CO₂ expands. Assume a discharge mass flow rate over 2 minutes:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{600 \text{ kg}}{120 \text{ s}} = 5.0 \text{ kg/s}
$$

At storage conditions, CO₂ density $\rho_{liq} \approx 770 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Volumetric flow rate:

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{5.0}{770} = 0.00649 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \frac{\dot{V}}{v} = \frac{0.00649}{10} = 6.49 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

Pipe diameter:

$$
D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 6.49 \times 10^{-4}}{3.1416}} = 0.0287 \text{ m} = 28.7 \text{ mm}
$$

**Standard pipe size:** next standard size = **32 mm (1¼″) NB**.

> [!NOTE]
> **FSS Code 7.4.3 — Discharge Time Verification:** The 600 kg CO₂ must be released within 2 minutes. With 15 cylinders, the manifold delivers CO₂ rapidly enough to satisfy this (typical cylinder discharge is 3–5 kg/s per cylinder, with multi-cylinder manifold).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.85 volume reduction factor** → overestimate CO₂. KTU deducts 2 marks.
> 2. **Using 0.70 kg/m³ instead of 0.56 kg/m³** → confusion between machinery and pump room densities. KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Rounding down the number of cylinders** → must always round *up* in CO₂ design (deficit is dangerous).
> 4. **Forgetting to cite the FSS Code chapter number** → marks lost for procedural non-compliance.

---

### 5.3 Additional Conceptual Questions (for self-assessment)

**Q3.** `[KTU Model Question Bank — Module 4]`
**Compare totally enclosed lifeboats (TELBs) and free-fall lifeboats. In which vessel types are free-fall lifeboats mandatory, and why?**

**Model Answer Hints:**
- TELBs: Davit-launched, traditional, used on most cargo ships
- Free-fall: Drop-launched from aft, mandatory on **tankers carrying oil/bulk chemicals with LBP > 85 m** per SOLAS III/43 (1998 amendments, post-ERIKA/CARLA casualties)
- Free-fall advantages: speed of evacuation (4–6 sec launch), no personnel exposure at embarkation deck during fire
- Disadvantages: Need clear drop zone aft, structural reinforcement

**Q4.** `[KTU Model Question Bank]`
**What is the difference between FSS Code Chapter 2 and Chapter 3? When is each used?**

**Model Answer Hints:**
- Ch. 2: **Non-pressurized sprinkler** (water tank, no pump pressurization) — older systems
- Ch. 3: **Pressurized sprinkler** (continuously pressurized water supply) — modern systems, used in passenger ship accommodation

**Q5.** `[KTU Model Question Bank]`
**What is the role of the FSS Code in SOLAS? Why is it called a "Code" rather than a "Chapter"?**

**Model Answer Hints:**
- FSS Code is a **mandatory** code (adopted under SOLAS II-2)
- It provides **detailed engineering specifications** for fire protection systems
- It is updated more frequently than SOLAS chapters (modular amendment process via MSC resolutions)
- Codes are **technical standards**, Chapters are **legal obligations**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: LSA & FFA**

**LSA — Key Points:**

- ☐ **SOLAS Chapter III** + **LSA Code (MSC.48(66))** govern LSA.
- ☐ Lifeboat capacity = $\min\left(\dfrac{A_{seat}}{0.16},\ \dfrac{L_{seat}}{0.40}\right)$.
- ☐ Headroom minimum = **1.115 m**.
- ☐ Liferafts: 100% complement on each side for cargo ships.
- ☐ Lifejackets: 100% + 5% adult spares + appropriate children/infant %.
- ☐ ≥ 8 life buoys per cargo ship; ≥ 2 with self-igniting lights.
- ☐ EPIRB: 1 (cargo) / 1 (passenger) — 406 MHz, COSPAS-SARSAT.
- ☐ SART: 1 (cargo) / 2 (passenger), 9 GHz X-band radar transponder.
- ☐ Pyrotechnics: 12 rocket parachute flares, 6 hand flares, 4 line thrower charges.
- ☐ **TPA** (Thermal Protective Aid) for 100% liferaft complement.

**FFA — Key Points:**

- ☐ **SOLAS Chapter II-2** + **FSS Code (MSC.98(73))** govern FFA.
- ☐ FSS Code has **15 chapters**, one per system type.
- ☐ **CO₂** for machinery: **0.56 kg/m³**, **2-min discharge**.
- ☐ **CO₂** for pump room: **0.70 kg/m³**, **10-min discharge**.
- ☐ **Net volume = 0.85 × Gross volume** (15% deduction for ducts, structure).
- ☐ **Free-fall lifeboats** mandatory for tankers (LBP > 85 m, post-2002 amendments).
- ☐ **30-second time delay** mandatory before CO₂ release.
- ☐ **No automatic CO₂ release** in machinery spaces.
- ☐ Fire main must reach all parts; **two jets from different hydrants** to any point.
- ☐ **Fire hose length** ≤ 20 m; nozzle pressure ≥ 0.5 bar.
- ☐ **Main + Emergency fire pump** required; emergency pump must be **independently powered**.
- ☐ **International shore connection** required for all cargo ships.
- ☐ **Hydrostatic release unit (HRU)** for liferafts — automatic inflation if ship sinks.
- ☐ **Davit-launched** liferafts on cargo ships (or approved throw-overboard type).

**Critical Numerical Values to Memorize:**

| Constant | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Seat area/person | **0.16 m²** | LSA Code 4.1.1 |
| Minimum headroom | **1.115 m** | LSA Code 4.1.1 |
| Liferaft capacity range | **6 to 25 persons** | LSA Code 4.2 |
| CO₂ rate (machinery) | **0.56 kg/m³** | FSS Code 7.4.1 |
| CO₂ rate (pump room) | **0.70 kg/m³** | FSS Code 7.5 |
| CO₂ rate (paint locker) | **1.20 kg/m³** | FSS Code 7.7 |
| CO₂ discharge time (machinery) | **≤ 2 min** | FSS Code 7.4.3 |
| CO₂ discharge time (pump room) | **≤ 10 min** | FSS Code 7.4.3 |
| CO₂ time delay | **≥ 30 s** | FSS Code 7.6.2 |
| Volume reduction factor | **0.85** | FSS Code 7.2 |
| Hose length max | **20 m** | SOLAS II-2/10.2.3.1 |
| Nozzle pressure min | **0.5 bar** | FSS Code 2.2.3 |
| Water jet throw min | **12 m** | FSS Code 2.2.4 |
| Hydrant reach | **max(0.3L, 9 m)** | FSS Code 2.4.1 |
| Water/lifeboat person | **3 L** | LSA Code 4.4.1 |
| Food/lifeboat person | **10,000 kJ** | LSA Code 4.4.1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** SOLAS Chapter II-2 questions often test the **fire main** system and the **two-jet rule**. SOLAS Chapter III questions test the **lifeboat capacity formula** and the **embarkation/muster sequence**. FSS Code numerical problems dominate the 14-mark Part B question. Always cite the **Code chapter** alongside the SOLAS regulation for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Regulations on FFA. Structural Fire Protection. Ventilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** International Regulations on FFA. Structural Fire Protection. Ventilation ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Fire-Fighting Appliances (FFA) — Formal KTU/IMO Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fire-Fighting Appliances (FFA)** are the complete set of equipment, systems, and materials carried on board a vessel for the purpose of detecting, controlling, suppressing, and extinguishing fires, as mandated by **SOLAS Chapter II-2**, the **FSS Code (International Code for Fire Safety Systems)**, and the **FTP Code (International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures)**. **Sub-divisions of FFA (per FSS Code):** | Category | Description | Examples | |----------|-------------|----------| | **Portable FFA** | Hand-held/movable units | Extinguishers (foam, CO₂, dry powder, water) | | **Fixed FFA** | Permanently installed | CO₂ flooding, water mist, foam systems, sprinkler | | **Fire Detection** | Early warning systems | Smoke/heat/flame detectors, alarms | | **Life-Saving FFA** | Escape aids | Fireman's outfits, breathing apparatus, EEBD | --- ### 1.2 Structural Fire Protection (SFP) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Structural Fire Protection (SFP)** is the passive fire protection provided by the ship's structure (bulkheads, decks, linings, ceilings, insulation, and penetrations) that prevents the spread of fire and smoke, maintains the integrity of escape routes, and preserves the buoyancy/stability of the vessel for a specified time under standard fire test conditions (**ISO 834 / FTP Code Part 3**). **Hierarchical Classification of Divisions (SOLAS II-2/Reg. 3):** - **A-Class Divisions** — Steel or equivalent non-combustible divisions that prevent passage of smoke/flame for **60 minutes** max; tested to FTP Code Part 3. Ratings: **A-60, A-30, A-15, A-0**. - **B-Class Divisions** — Non-combustible divisions that prevent passage of flame for **30 minutes** max; ratings: **B-15, B-0**. - **C-Class Divisions** — Constructed of non-combustible materials; no fire resistance time requirement. --- ### 1.3 Ventilation — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ventilation** in SOLAS context refers to the system of ducts, fans, dampers, and controls that supply fresh air and exhaust contaminated/smoke-laden air from compartments. Under SOLAS II-2, ventilation is a *dual-hazard* system — it provides habitability AND must prevent smoke/flame spread between fire zones via **fire dampers** and **closing appliances**. --- ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Building as a "Sandwich Box":** > Imagine a passenger ship as a multi-story apartment building. Each cabin (compartment) is separated by firewalls (bulkheads) — like the cardboard dividers in a lunch box. **FFA** is like the fire extinguisher you keep on each floor (active protection). **SFP** is the cardboard itself — it must not burn through for a given time so fire cannot jump from one "section" to another. **Ventilation** is the air-conditioning duct — but if fire starts in one flat, the duct must automatically seal itself (fire dampers) so smoke doesn't travel through the ductwork into neighbouring flats. **Real-world fire analogy:** When a high-rise building catches fire, firefighters trust the **fire-rated concrete floors** (SFP) more than anything else. Similarly, a ship with A-60 divisions can withstand **60 minutes of hydrocarbon furnace exposure** at **1100 °C** — long enough to evacuate and contain the casualty. --- ### 1.5 Core Physical Constants & Standards > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Fire Engineering Constants (ISO 834 / Hydrocarbon Curve):** > - **Standard fire test temperature:** $T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ °C, where $T_0 = 20\,°C$, $t$ = time in minutes. > - **Hydrocarbon curve temperature:** $T(t) = T_0 + 1080(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5t})$ °C. > - **Flashover threshold:** $\approx$ **600 °C**. > - **Human survivability limit:** $\approx$ **120 °C** (breathing air), **65 °C** (skin contact, 1 min). > - **Reference IMO convention:** **SOLAS 1974 (as amended)** — Chapter II-2. > - **Reference codes:** **FSS Code**, **FTP Code**, **MSC/Circ.1120**, **MSC.1/Circ.1503**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 International Regulatory Framework — Hierarchical Logic > The IMO regulatory pyramid for fire safety is structured into 4 layers: 1. **SOLAS Convention (Chapter II-2)** — *Highest legal authority*. Sets the **Goals** and **Functional Requirements** (e.g., "the ship shall be designed such that fire does not compromise life safety"). 2. **FSS Code (Res. MSC.98(73))** — *Design-specific*. Lays down prescriptive requirements for each system: extinguisher sizing, sprinkler flow rates, foam concentrates, etc. 3. **FTP Code (Res. MSC.307(88))** — *Test methodology*. Defines how fire resistance of materials/divisions is **tested** in furnaces. 4. **Classification Society Rules** (LR, DNV, BV, ABS) — *Interpretation & detailed application*. Provides engineering tables, scantling rules, and recognized solutions. --- ### 2.2 SOLAS Chapter II-2 — Core Regulatory Triggers **Regulation II-2/4 — Probability of Ignition (Goals-based):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "4 Pillars" of SOLAS II-2 (Reg. 2 — Fire Safety Goals):** > 1. **Pillar I** — Prevent ignition of flammable substances (oil, gas). > 2. **Pillar II** — Reduce fire hazard through **structural fire protection**, **passive systems**, **active systems**. > 3. **Pillar III** — Detect and extinguish fire in **compartment of origin**. > 4. **Pillar IV** — Provide **safe escape** for passengers and crew. **Fire Detection Systems (FSS Code Ch. 9):** - **Fixed fire detection & fire alarm systems** are required in: - All passenger ships (every compartment). - Cargo ships — *accommodation, control stations, service spaces*. - Heat detectors must be set at **≤ 78 °C** (fixed temperature) OR **≤ 30 °C/min rate-of-rise**. --- ### 2.3 Structural Fire Protection — Tabulated Decision Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Selection of Fire Integrity between Adjacent Spaces (SOLAS II-2/Table 9.1, 9.2, 9.3):** | Space "A" ↓ | Space "B" → | Required Div. | Rating | |-------------|-------------|---------------|--------| | Control Station | Accommodation | A | **A-30** (cargo), **A-60** (passenger) | | Machinery (Cat. A) | Accommodation | A | **A-60** | | Cargo Pump Room | Accommodation | A | **A-60** | | Corridor (accomm.) | Cabin | B | **B-15** (some exempt) | | Galley | Accommodation | A or B | **B-15** or **A-0** | | Open Deck | Open Deck | None | — | | Sanitary/Similar | Any | C | **C** | **Bulkhead/Deck Construction Logic (per SOLAS II-2/Reg. 9.2.2):** $$ \text{Fire Integrity Requirement} = f(\text{Space type}, \text{Risk class}, \text{Ship type}) $$ **Insulation Type Selection:** - **Non-combustible** (per FTP Code Part 1) — required for **A & B class** divisions. - **Low flame spread** (per FTP Code Part 5) — for surface finishes. - **Examples:** Mineral wool (Rockwool), Calcium silicate, Promasil, Ceramic fibre blankets. --- ### 2.4 Ventilation — Fire Safety Requirements > [!IMPORTANT] > **SOLAS Reg. II-2/9.7 — Ventilation Duct Crossings:** > - Ducts passing through **A-class divisions** must be fitted with **fire dampers** at the penetration. > - Ducts passing through **B-class divisions** must be of non-combustible material. > - Ducts in **accommodation/service spaces** must be insulated to A-30 standard within machinery spaces. **Closing Appliances (FSS Code Ch. 15):** - **Power ventilation** must have **master controls** outside the ventilated space. - **Fire dampers** must be **automatic** (fusible link at **70 °C**) AND **manually operable** from outside the space. - **Louvres & cowls** for natural ventilation. **Means of Escape Ventilation (Reg. II-2/13):** - Escape routes (corridors, stairways) must be fitted with **independent mechanical ventilation** (or natural draft) capable of keeping routes **smoke-free for 30 minutes minimum**. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Examination:** | Formula | Description | Units | |---------|-------------|-------| | $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | ISO 834 standard fire curve | °C, t in min | | $T(t) = 1080(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5t})$ | Hydrocarbon fire curve | °C, t in min | | $q = \frac{k}{\delta} \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat flux through insulation (Fourier) | kW/m² | | $t_{\text{crit}} = c \cdot \rho \cdot \delta \cdot \Delta T_{\text{allow}} / q$ | Time for back-side temp to reach limit | minutes | | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Ventilation air flow rate | m³/s | | $N_{CO_2} = 0.3 \cdot V_{\text{net}}$ | CO₂ quantity for machinery space (FSS) | kg | | $Q_{\text{water mist}} \geq 10 \cdot L_{\text{max}}$ | Water mist nozzle discharge | L/min | | $A_{\text{corr}} \geq 0.025\,\text{m}^2$ | Min free cross-section of stairway smoke extraction | m² | **Important Boundary Conditions:** - Use `\vert` or `\mid` notation in derivations (avoid pipe symbol in tables). - $\Delta T_{\text{allow}} = 140\,°C$ average / **180 °C single point** for A-class divisions. - Fire damper fusible link: **70 °C** (standard), **141 °C** (galleys/saunas). --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > These systems directly influence: **ship concept design** (zoning philosophy), **scantling** (extra plate thickness for fire integrity), **escape route placement**, **engine room layout**, **PCTC/Passenger ship safety case**, **offshore FPSO blast resistance**, and **submarine compartmentalization**. Naval architects spend ~3–5% of design effort on SFP/FFA compliance — failure to meet SOLAS II-2 results in **detention by Port State Control** and **loss of class**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Insulation Thickness for A-60 Division > **Problem:** A steel bulkhead of thickness **$t_s = 6$ mm** is required to achieve A-60 rating (60 minutes fire integrity). Determine the **required thickness of mineral wool insulation** on the unexposed side. **Given:** - Steel thickness: $t_s = 6$ mm = **0.006 m** - Steel thermal conductivity: $k_s = 45$ W/(m·K) - Mineral wool conductivity: $k_i = 0.04$ W/(m·K) - Allowable average temperature rise on unexposed side: $\Delta T_{\text{allow}} = 140$ °C - Standard fire curve (ISO 834): 60 min exposure → $T(60) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(481) \approx 945\,°C$ **Step 1: Determine fire-side temperature at 60 min.** $$ T(60) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 60 + 1) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(481) $$ $$ \log_{10}(481) = 2.682 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(60) = 20 + 345 \times 2.682 \approx 945\,°C $$ **Step 2: Heat transfer through composite (steel + insulation).** For a steady-state 1-D Fourier heat conduction (simplified approximation): $$ q = \frac{T_{\text{fire}} - T_{\text{ambient}}}{\dfrac{t_s}{k_s} + \dfrac{t_i}{k_i}} $$ Substituting: $$ q = \frac{945 - 20}{\dfrac{0.006}{45} + \dfrac{t_i}{0.04}} = \frac{925}{1.333 \times 10^{-4} + 25\,t_i} $$ **Step 3: Set allowable heat flux such that back-side temperature rise is ≤ 140 °C.** Using the criterion: $q \leq 5$ kW/m² (single-side average for A-class, conservative): $$ 5 \times 10^{3} = \frac{925}{1.333 \times 10^{-4} + 25\,t_i} $$ $$ 1.333 \times 10^{-4} + 25\,t_i = \frac{925}{5000} = 0.185 $$ $$ 25\,t_i = 0.185 - 0.0001333 = 0.1849 $$ $$ t_i = \frac{0.1849}{25} = 7.39 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \approx 7.4\,\text{mm} $$ **Step 4: Round up to standard commercial thickness — adopt $t_i = 10$ mm**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** A **10 mm mineral wool** insulation on the unexposed face of the bulkhead yields A-60 integrity. In practice, designers use **30–50 mm** to account for penetrations, supports, and to satisfy the **"single-point" 180 °C criterion** at localized positions. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Required CO₂ Quantity for Engine Room Flooding > **Problem:** A cargo ship main engine room has net volume **$V = 800$ m³**. Determine the CO₂ quantity required per FSS Code Ch. 5. **FSS Code formula:** $$ Q = 0.3 \cdot V_{\text{net}} \quad \text{(kg)} $$ **Calculation:** $$ Q = 0.3 \times 800 = 240\,\text{kg} $$ > **Reserve units:** $Q_{\text{reserve}} = 240\,\text{kg}$ (must be stored outside the protected space). **Bottles calculation:** Standard CO₂ bottle = **45 kg (filled)** → **44 kg usable**. - Bottles for flooding: $\lceil 240/44 \rceil = 6$ bottles. - Reserve bottles: **6 additional**. - **Total = 12 bottles**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Ventilation Flow Rate for Smoke-Free Escape > **Problem:** A stairway serving 3 accommodation decks, cross-section **$A = 0.8$ m × 1.0 m**. Determine required mechanical extract rate to maintain ≤ 5% smoke obscuration for 30 min. **SOLAS Reg. II-2/13.3.1.4:** Minimum 6 air changes/hour (ACH). $$ Q_{\text{extract}} = \frac{\text{ACH} \times V_{\text{zone}}}{3600} $$ Assuming stairway effective height $h = 8$ m: $$ Q = \frac{6 \times (0.8 \times 1.0 \times 8)}{3600} = \frac{38.4}{3600} = 0.0107\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ Q \approx 10.7\,\text{L/s} \approx 38.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{hr} $$ > Adopt **2 × 50% fans** for redundancy (one fan failure → 50% flow still operational). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Compliance Verification Flow | Step | Action | SOLAS Reference | |------|--------|-----------------| | 1 | Identify space category | Reg. II-2/3.2 | | 2 | Determine required fire rating (A/B/C) | Tables 9.1–9.3 | | 3 | Compute fire exposure curve | FTP Code Part 3 | | 4 | Verify bulkhead/deck construction | FTP Code Part 1 | | 5 | Verify insulation thickness | ISO 834 calc. or approved test | | 6 | Verify duct penetrations + dampers | Reg. II-2/9.7 | | 7 | Verify fire detection coverage | FSS Code Ch. 9 | | 8 | Verify extinguisher sizing | FSS Code Ch. 4, 5 | | 9 | Verify escape routes smoke-free | Reg. II-2/13 | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — SOLAS II-2 Compliance Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SOLAS Chapter II-2
Goals and Functional Reqs] --> B[Identify Space Type
Passenger or Cargo Ship] B --> C[Apply Tables 9.1 to 9.3
Required Fire Integrity] C --> D{Class of Division} D -- A-Class --> E[Steel Bulkhead
plus Non-combustible Insulation] D -- B-Class --> F[Non-combustible
B-15 or B-0] D -- C-Class --> G[Non-combustible
C - No Time Rating] E --> H[FTP Code Test
ISO 834 Furnace] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Verify Penetrations
Ducts and Cables] I --> J[Fire Dampers
Fusible Link 70C] J --> K[Ventilation
Master Control Outside] K --> L[Detection and Alarm
FSS Code Ch. 9] L --> M[FFA Deployment
Portable plus Fixed] M --> N[Escape Routes
Smoke-free 30 min] N --> O[Final Compliance
Certificate Issued] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Ventilation Fire Safety Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR FW[Fire Source
Machinery Room] --> D1[Duct Penetration] D1 --> FD[Fire Damper
Fusible 70C] FD --> ACC[Accommodation Space] ACC --> MC[Master Control
Outside Space] MC --> ES[Escape Corridor] ES --> EX[Extract Fan
6 ACH minimum] EX --> OUT[Safe Discharge
Outside] FW -.Heat Detection 78C.-> HD[Heat Detector] HD --> CO2[CO2 Flooding
0.3 kg per m3] CO2 --> FW ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fire Integrity Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD SP[Adjacent Space Pair A and B] --> Q1{Is B a Control Station} Q1 -- Yes --> R1[A-30 or A-60] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is B Machinery Category A} Q2 -- Yes --> R2[A-60] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is B Accommodation} Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is A Galley} Q4 -- Yes --> R3[B-15] Q4 -- No --> Q5{Is A Cargo Hold} Q5 -- Yes --> R4[A-60] Q5 -- No --> R5[B-15 or A-0] Q3 -- No --> Q6{Is B Open Deck} Q6 -- Yes --> R6[No Division Needed] Q6 -- No --> R7[Refer to SOLAS Table 9.3] ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Schematic — A-60 Bulkhead Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fire Side
T = 945C at 60 min] --> B[Steel Plate
t = 6 mm] B --> C[Mineral Wool
t = 30 mm] C --> D[Steel Plate
t = 4 mm] D --> E[Unexposed Side
T less than 140C avg] A -.Cable Pen. Fire Stop.-> X[Intumescent Seal] A -.Duct Pen. Fire Damper.-> Y[Auto Damper] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Q1. [3 Marks]** *Define the FSS Code and the FTP Code as applicable to ship fire protection. State one key difference between them.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer:** > **FSS Code (International Code for Fire Safety Systems)** — adopted by SOLAS Res. MSC.98(73) — provides *design specifications* for fixed and portable fire-extinguishing systems (e.g., sprinkler, CO₂, foam). > **FTP Code (International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures)** — adopted by Res. MSC.307(88) — provides *test procedures* to evaluate fire resistance of materials, divisions, and surface finishes. > **Key difference:** FSS Code specifies **what to install**; FTP Code specifies **how to test** the fire performance of installed systems/materials. *(3 marks)* --- **Q2. [3 Marks]** *What is the difference between an A-60 and a B-15 division? Give one example where each is typically applied.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer:** > - **A-60 division** — Steel or equivalent non-combustible construction with insulation; tested to **ISO 834** standard fire for **60 minutes**; prevents passage of smoke and flame and limits temperature rise on unexposed side to ≤ 140 °C average / 180 °C single-point. Used between **machinery (Cat. A)** and **accommodation**. > - **B-15 division** — Non-combustible construction with insulation; tested for **15 minutes**; used between **corridors and cabins** in accommodation. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the international regulatory framework governing fire safety on board ships. Discuss the role of SOLAS, FSS Code, FTP Code, and classification society rules with their hierarchical relationship.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer:** > **1. SOLAS Convention (Chapter II-2) — Highest Authority (2 marks):** > Adopted in 1974, amended continuously. Chapter II-2 (last major revision 2000, MSC.99(73)) sets the **fire safety goals** and **functional requirements** in a "**goal-based**" approach. It defines 4 goals: > - Prevent ignition (Pillar I). > - Reduce fire hazard (Pillar II — SFP + active FFA). > - Confine fire to compartment of origin (Pillar II). > - Provide safe escape (Pillar IV). > > **2. FSS Code — Design Specifications (2 marks):** > Res. MSC.98(73) provides prescriptive **engineering specifications** for each system — sprinkler flow rates (e.g., 5 L/min·m² for Category A machinery), CO₂ concentration (30% for machinery), foam concentrate (3% AFFF for machinery), fire detection sensitivity (smoke obscuration 0.5–1%/m). > > **3. FTP Code — Test Methodology (1.5 marks):** > Res. MSC.307(88), 13 parts — Part 1 (non-combustibility test), Part 3 (A/B class division test), Part 5 (surface flammability). Defines the **ISO 834 furnace**, the **time-temperature curve**, and acceptance criteria (140 °C avg / 180 °C single-point for A-class). > > **4. Classification Society Rules (1.5 marks):** > LR, DNV, BV, ABS, KR translate SOLAS requirements into **engineering rules** with scantlings, recognized materials lists, and approved test certificates. The class society issues the **Class Certificate**; the **Flag State** issues the **SOLAS Certificate** (often delegated to Recognized Organization = RO). > > **Hierarchical relationship:** SOLAS → FSS Code + FTP Code → Class Rules → Manufacturer's Approved Certificates → Ship Documentation. **(b) [7 Marks]** *A bulkhead between a machinery space (Category A) and an accommodation corridor in a cargo ship is required to provide A-60 integrity. Describe in detail the construction of such a bulkhead including materials, insulation, and penetrations.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer:** > **1. Structural Steel Plate (2 marks):** > Bulkhead fabricated from **grade A steel** plate (minimum yield 235 MPa) with thickness **≥ 6 mm** for watertightness; stiffened by flanged brackets at **600 mm centres**. Tested to **FTP Code Part 3 — ISO 834 fire exposure** for 60 minutes; furnace temperature rises to **945 °C**. > > **2. Insulation Material (2 marks):** > Non-combustible mineral wool (e.g., Rockwool or Paroc) conforming to **FTP Code Part 1** (non-combustibility test) with thermal conductivity $\leq$ **0.04 W/(m·K)** at 20 °C. Applied on the **unexposed (corridor) face** as 30–50 mm thick panels. Also approved: calcium silicate, vermiculite, ceramic fibre. Outer surface may be covered with 0.5 mm galvanised steel sheet for mechanical protection. > > **3. Penetrations (1.5 marks):** > - **Cable transits** — Intumescent fire-stop blocks (e.g., Roxtec, Multilock); tested to FTP Part 3 Annex 1. > - **Duct penetrations** — Fitted with **automatic fire dampers** with fusible link at **70 °C** (SOLAS II-2/9.7); manually operable from outside. > - **Pipe penetrations** — Sealed with approved non-combustible collars or intumescent wraps. > > **4. Acceptance Criteria (1.5 marks):** > - Integrity (no flame/smoke passage) for ≥ 60 min. > - Insulation: average temperature rise on unexposed side ≤ **140 °C**; single-point ≤ **180 °C**. > - Load-bearing capacity retained for 60 min. > - **Approved test certificate** from a recognized laboratory (e.g., BRE, Lloyd's, DNV) required. > - All work supervised and **witnessed by class surveyors**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain SOLAS requirements for structural fire protection in passenger ships. Discuss the concept of "main vertical zones" and their importance.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer:** > **1. Main Vertical Zones (MVZ) — Definition (2 marks):** > Per SOLAS Reg. II-2/3.23, MVZs are sections into which the **hull, superstructure, and deckhouses** are divided by **A-60 class divisions**, with average length per zone not exceeding **40 m**. On passenger ships, this limits the spread of fire and smoke from one zone to another. > > **2. Importance of MVZs (2 marks):** > - **Compartmentalization** — Fire is confined to the zone of origin. > - **Evacuation** — Passengers can be evacuated from non-affected zones to muster stations. > - **Stability** — Progressive flooding limited by transverse watertight bulkheads aligned with MVZ boundaries. > - **SOLAS Reg. II-2/9.2.2** — Atriums and long corridors that connect multiple MVZs are limited in length unless fitted with **smoke extraction systems** and **fixed fire-extinguishing systems**. > > **3. Structural Requirements (1.5 marks):** > - Decks and bulkheads bounding **MVZs must be A-60**. > - **Stairways and elevators** serving multiple MVZs must be enclosed in **A-class smoke-tight shafts**. > - **Openings** in MVZ bulkheads limited to **1 door + 1 control valve** minimum; doors of **self-closing fire-tested** type. > > **4. Special Cases (1.5 marks):** > - **Atriums** — open spans across multiple decks must have active smoke control (≥ 10 air changes/hr) and sprinkler protection. > - **External boundaries** — windows in MVZ external bulkheads limited to "fire-rated" type (FTP Part 3) where adjacent to other zones within 3 m. **(b) [7 Marks]** *Describe the ventilation requirements for fire safety on board ships with reference to SOLAS Chapter II-2. Include the requirements for fire dampers, duct penetrations, and escape route ventilation.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer:** > **1. Duct Materials (1.5 marks):** > Per SOLAS Reg. II-2/9.7.1, ventilation ducts must be constructed of **non-combustible material** (per FTP Part 1). Ducts in **accommodation spaces** insulated to **A-30** standard where passing through machinery spaces. > > **2. Fire Dampers (2 marks):** > - All ducts passing through **A-class divisions** must be fitted with **automatic fire dampers** (Reg. II-2/9.7.2). > - Dampers must have **fusible link rated 70 °C** (or 141 °C in galleys/saunas). > - Dampers must be **manually operable** from outside the protected space via a **fail-safe** mechanism (spring-loaded). > - **Indicator** must be provided at the master control station showing **OPEN/CLOSED** status. > > **3. Master Control of Power Ventilation (1.5 marks):** > Per FSS Code Ch. 15, all power ventilation fans serving **accommodation, service, and cargo spaces** must have **master controls** at a location **outside the ventilated spaces**. Fans can be **stopped** from a single location during fire. > > **4. Escape Route Ventilation (2 marks):** > Per SOLAS Reg. II-2/13.3: > - All **corridors, stairways, and ladders** serving as escape routes must be fitted with either **independent natural draft** or **mechanical ventilation**. > - Minimum **6 air changes per hour** (for passenger ships). > - System must remain operational for **≥ 30 minutes** after fire detection. > - Must be supplied from an **emergency source of power**. > - **Smoke extraction** via independent duct ≥ **0.025 m²** free cross-section at the top of stairway. > > **5. Penetrations of B-class and Decks (Bonus — up to 1 mark):** > - Ducts through B-class divisions need **non-combustible** construction; fire damper not mandatory. > - Ducts through **horizontal fire-rated decks** — fire dampers mandatory at the deck plane. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE MARKS in KTU valuation:** > 1. **Confusing A-class with B-class ratings** — remember: **A-class prevents smoke AND flame**; B-class only flame. > 2. **Forgetting the FUSIBLE LINK temperature** — **70 °C** (general) or **141 °C** (galley/sauna). > 3. **Mixing ISO 834 curve (cellulosic) with Hydrocarbon curve** — SOLAS uses **ISO 834**; offshore platforms use **hydrocarbon curve**. > 4. **Omitting "manually operable"** for fire dampers — automatic alone is **NOT enough** for SOLAS. > 5. **Wrong reference to FSS Code chapter numbers** — CO₂ = Ch. 5, Sprinkler = Ch. 8, Foam = Ch. 6, Detection = Ch. 9, Ventilation = Ch. 15. > 6. **Incorrect use of 30% CO₂ concentration** for cargo pump rooms — it is **45%**; machinery spaces use **30%**. > 7. **Diagram drawing in Part B** — if a sketch of bulkhead cross-section is asked, students often forget the **stiffener spacing** or **penetration seal**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST:** > - **SOLAS II-2** is the **mother regulation**; Chapter II-2 covers **all fire safety**. > - **4 Pillars** — **P**revent ignition, **R**educe hazard, **C**onfine fire, **E**scape. > - **FSS Code = design**; **FTP Code = test method**. > - **A-class = 60 min**; **B-class = 30 min**; **C-class = no time**. > - **A-ratings: 0, 15, 30, 60** (sub-divisions). > - **B-ratings: 0, 15**. > - **ISO 834 furnace** → 945 °C at 60 min. > - **Allowable back-side: 140 °C avg / 180 °C single point**. > - **Insulation must be NON-COMBUSTIBLE** (per FTP Code Part 1). > - **Mineral wool**, calcium silicate, ceramic fibre are common A-class insulants. > - **Fire damper fusible link: 70 °C** standard; **141 °C** for galley. > - **Fire damper must be automatic + manually operable** from outside. > - **Power ventilation has master control** outside spaces. > - **Escape route ventilation ≥ 6 ACH** for passenger ships. > - **CO₂ for machinery = 0.3 kg/m³ net volume** + 100% reserve. > - **Foam concentrate (AFFF) 3%** for machinery, **6%** for helicopter decks. > - **Detection: Heat at ≤ 78 °C** OR **rate-of-rise ≤ 30 °C/min**. > - **MVZ length ≤ 40 m** on passenger ships; **A-60 division**. > - **Penetrations** = cable transits (intumescent) + ducts (fire dampers) + pipes (collars). > - **Approval certificates** required from class for every component. > - **Flag State vs Class**: Flag = SOLAS cert; Class = Class cert (often delegated to RO). > - **Port State Control** can detain the ship if FFA/SFP non-compliance detected. > - **Cross-reference SOLAS Reg. II-2/4, 5, 7, 9, 10, 13** for full coverage.

topics.title: Insulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Insulation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Ship Design II (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, **Insulation** refers to the systematic application of specialized materials and structural configurations aboard a vessel to **control the transfer of heat, sound, vibration, and fire** between adjacent compartments or between the ship's interior and the external marine environment. Insulation engineering in shipbuilding is governed by three primary functional classifications: 1. **Thermal Insulation** – The reduction of conductive, convective, and radiative heat flow across structural boundaries (bulkheads, decks, hull plating, refrigerated chambers). 2. **Acoustic Insulation** – The mitigation of airborne and structure-borne noise transmission, especially in machinery spaces, accommodation areas, and superstructures. 3. **Fire Insulation** – The provision of passive fire protection through non-combustible barriers (Class A, B, C divisions) that meet SOLAS and IMO FTP Code requirements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 4 emphasizes **thermal insulation of refrigerated cargo spaces (reefer ships, LNG carriers), machinery space lagging, domestic hot/cold water systems, and the structural integration of insulation with double-bottom and bulkhead scantlings.** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine drinking hot tea in a steel cup versus a ceramic cup with an air gap. - The **steel cup** conducts heat rapidly to your hand — uncomfortable. - The **ceramic cup with trapped air** keeps the heat inside the beverage — comfortable. A ship in the ocean faces the *exact same problem*, only at an industrial scale. The ocean is a vast, cold heat sink (around **4°C to 28°C** depending on latitude and depth). Without insulation: - A reefer cargo of frozen fish would thaw within hours. - Crew quarters would become unbearably hot in tropical crossings and freezing cold in polar routes. - Engine-room heat (often **45°C–60°C ambient**) would bleed into the bridge and cabins. - A single fire outbreak in the engine room could engulf the entire vessel. > [!NOTE] > **Insulation = A passive, material-based "thermal blanket" sandwiched between the structural steel and the inner lining**, working silently 24/7 without consuming power. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics The following bolded constants and metrics form the foundation of insulation design per KTU and IMO conventions: - **Thermal Conductivity (k or λ):** Measured in **W/(m·K)** — quantifies a material's ability to conduct heat. - **Thermal Resistance (R):** Measured in **(m²·K)/W** — the reciprocal of thermal conductance per unit area. - **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-value):** Measured in **W/(m²·K)** — the rate of heat transfer through a composite assembly. - **Sound Transmission Class (STC):** A dimensionless rating describing airborne sound insulation. - **Fire Resistance Rating:** Expressed in **minutes** (e.g., A-60 = 60 minutes of structural integrity under standard fire exposure). - **Surface Emissivity (ε):** Dimensionless, range 0 to 1. - **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ): 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** — for radiative heat transfer calculations. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Modes of Heat Transfer Relevant to Ship Insulation The design of marine insulation requires mastery of three fundamental heat transfer modes. Each behaves differently and demands specific material strategies. ### 2.1.1 Conduction - Heat transfer through a solid or stationary fluid by molecular vibration and electron drift. - Governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**. - In ships, conduction is the **dominant mode** through steel plating, bulkheads, and decks. - Insulation reduces conduction by introducing **low-k materials** (k typically 0.02–0.05 W/(m·K) for foams, versus 45 W/(m·K) for mild steel). ### 2.1.2 Convection - Heat transfer by bulk fluid motion (air or seawater). - Two subtypes: - **Natural convection** – buoyancy-driven (e.g., warm air rising in an engine room). - **Forced convection** – fan or pump-driven (e.g., ventilation ducting). - Insulation design must account for **convective heat gains/losses** at exposed surfaces, often through surface films characterized by convective coefficients $h_c$. ### 2.1.3 Radiation - Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves, no medium required. - Dominant at high temperatures (engine exhausts, boiler casings). - Quantified by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**. - Mitigated by **low-emissivity (low-ε) reflective foils** (e.g., aluminum foil facings with ε ≈ 0.03–0.05). ## 2.2 Fourier's Law — The Bedrock of Conductive Insulation Design The one-dimensional steady-state conductive heat flow through a plane wall is given by: $$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$$ Where: - $Q$ = heat flow rate (W) - $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material (W/(m·K)) - $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²) - $\Delta T$ = temperature difference across the material (K or °C) - $L$ = thickness of the material (m) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $Q \propto 1/L$ and $Q \propto k$. This dual dependency is why insulation engineers prefer **low-k materials applied in thick layers** rather than thin layers of high-k materials. ## 2.3 Composite Wall (Multi-Layer Insulation) Analysis Ship insulation is rarely a single material. A typical refrigerated space wall comprises (from outside to inside): 1. Outer steel plating 2. Vapor barrier 3. Primary insulation (PU foam / mineral wool) 4. Secondary insulation 5. Inner lining (stainless steel / plywood / glass cloth) 6. Air film (inner surface) For an **n-layer composite wall** in series, the total thermal resistance is the sum of individual layer resistances: $$R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i} + R_{si} + R_{se}$$ Where: - $L_i$ = thickness of the $i^{th}$ layer (m) - $k_i$ = thermal conductivity of the $i^{th}$ layer (W/(m·K)) - $R_{si}$ = internal surface film resistance ((m²·K)/W) - $R_{se}$ = external surface film resistance ((m²·K)/W) The **U-value (overall heat transfer coefficient)** is then: $$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}}$$ The **heat flux** (per unit area) is: $$q = U \cdot \Delta T$$ ## 2.4 Refrigeration Heat Load Calculation For a refrigerated cargo chamber, the **total heat ingress** must be calculated to size the refrigeration plant. It comprises: $$Q_{total} = Q_{conduction} + Q_{convection} + Q_{radiation} + Q_{respiration} + Q_{service}$$ Where: - $Q_{conduction} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ (through walls, deck, overhead) - $Q_{convection}$ = from air infiltration during door openings - $Q_{radiation}$ = solar load through exposed deck - $Q_{respiration}$ = heat generated by living cargo (fruits, vegetables) - $Q_{service}$ = heat from lights, fans, crew entry ## 2.5 Surface Temperature and Condensation Control A critical design check is to ensure the **inner surface temperature** of the insulation (on the warm side) is **above the dew point** of the adjacent air, otherwise **condensation** will form, degrading insulation and causing corrosion. For a composite wall, the temperature at the interface between layer $i$ and layer $i+1$ is: $$T_i = T_{inside} - q \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{i} R_j$$ If $T_i < T_{dewpoint}$, **vapor barrier placement** must be revised to the **warm side** of the insulation. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application in Ship Design** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fourier's Law (Conduction) | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | W | Heat flow through plating, bulkheads | | 2 | Heat Flux | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{k \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | W/m² | Heat flux per unit area | | 3 | Thermal Resistance (single layer) | $R = \dfrac{L}{k}$ | (m²·K)/W | Resistance of one insulation layer | | 4 | Composite Wall Resistance | $R_{total} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i} + R_{si} + R_{se}$ | (m²·K)/W | Multi-layer refrigerated chamber | | 5 | U-value (Overall) | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}}$ | W/(m²·K) | Total heat transfer coefficient | | 6 | Heat Load (Composite Wall) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Refrigeration plant sizing | | 7 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Radiation) | $q_{rad} = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | W/m² | Hot pipe lagging, exhaust insulation | | 8 | Convective Heat Transfer | $q_{conv} = h_c \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$ | W/m² | Surface film losses | | 9 | Interface Temperature | $T_i = T_{in} - q \cdot \sum R_j$ | K or °C | Condensation control check | | 10 | Cylindrical Wall (Pipe Lagging) | $Q = \dfrac{2 \pi L \cdot k \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}$ | W | Lagging of exhaust pipes, steam lines | | 11 | Pipe Lagging Heat Loss per meter | $\dfrac{Q}{L} = \dfrac{2\pi(T_1 - T_2)}{\dfrac{1}{r_1 h_i} + \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2 h_o}}$ | W/m | Insulated steam/exhaust piping | | 12 | Log Mean Radius (cylindrical) | $\bar{r} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | m | Cylindrical insulation design | | 13 | Critical Radius of Insulation | $r_{cr} = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$ | m | Determines if insulation helps or hurts | | 14 | SOLAS A-60 Standard | 60 min fire integrity | min | Bulkhead between engine room and accommodation | | 15 | Dew Point Depression Check | $T_{interface} \geq T_{dew}$ | °C | Prevent moisture ingress | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pipe Insulation Note:** For small diameter pipes, adding insulation can *increase* heat loss if the pipe radius is below the **critical radius of insulation** $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$. Always verify this in machinery lagging design. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Application Area** | **Primary Insulation Function** | **Common Materials** | |---|---|---| | Reefer cargo chambers | Thermal (–30°C cargo preservation) | PU foam, PIR panels, corkboard | | LNG cargo tanks | Thermal (–163°C cargo) | Perlite, vacuum insulation | | Engine room casing | Acoustic + Thermal | Mineral wool, glass wool | | Accommodation bulkheads | Acoustic + Thermal | Rock wool + mass-loaded vinyl | | A-60 fire divisions | Fire integrity | Mineral wool, vermiculite, calcium silicate | | Steam pipe lagging | Thermal (heat retention) | Calcium silicate, mineral wool | | Domestic hot water lines | Thermal (heat retention) | Closed-cell elastomeric foam | | Air-conditioning ducts | Thermal (anti-condensation) | Armaflex-class elastomeric foam | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: U-Value of a Composite Ship Bulkhead **Problem Setup:** A refrigerated cargo chamber bulkhead consists of the following layers (from warm side, accommodation, to cold side, reefer chamber): | Layer | Material | Thickness L (mm) | Thermal Conductivity k (W/(m·K)) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Internal surface film | — | $h_{si}$ = 8.0 W/(m²·K) | | 2 | Stainless steel lining | 1.5 | 16.0 | | 3 | Corkboard (primary) | 75 | 0.040 | | 4 | Polyurethane foam (secondary) | 50 | 0.022 | | 5 | Vapor barrier (negligible R) | 0 | — | | 6 | Mild steel bulkhead | 10 | 45.0 | | 7 | External surface film | — | $h_{se}$ = 23.0 W/(m²·K) | **Given Conditions:** - Accommodation side air temperature: $T_{warm} = +20°C$ - Reefer chamber temperature: $T_{cold} = -25°C$ - Bulkhead area: $A = 20$ m² **Objective:** Calculate the total heat ingress $Q$ through this bulkhead. ### Step 1: Express Each Layer's Thermal Resistance Recall the general resistance formula for plane layers: $$R = \frac{L}{k} \quad \text{(for solid layers, in m}^2\cdot\text{K/W)}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{h} \quad \text{(for surface films, in m}^2\cdot\text{K/W)}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Individual Resistances **Layer 1 — Internal surface film resistance:** $$R_{si} = \frac{1}{h_{si}} = \frac{1}{8.0} = 0.1250 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ **Layer 2 — Stainless steel lining:** $$R_{2} = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.0015}{16.0} = 0.00009375 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ This is negligibly small, as expected for a metallic layer. **Layer 3 — Corkboard (primary insulation):** $$R_{3} = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.075}{0.040} = 1.8750 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ **Layer 4 — Polyurethane foam (secondary insulation):** $$R_{4} = \frac{L_4}{k_4} = \frac{0.050}{0.022} = 2.2727 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ **Layer 5 — Vapor barrier:** Negligible thermal resistance. $R_5 \approx 0$. **Layer 6 — Mild steel bulkhead:** $$R_{6} = \frac{L_6}{k_6} = \frac{0.010}{45.0} = 0.0002222 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ Again, negligible — steel is an excellent conductor. **Layer 7 — External surface film resistance:** $$R_{se} = \frac{1}{h_{se}} = \frac{1}{23.0} = 0.04348 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ ### Step 3: Sum the Series Resistances $$R_{total} = R_{si} + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_5 + R_6 + R_{se}$$ Substituting each value: $$R_{total} = 0.1250 + 0.00009375 + 1.8750 + 2.2727 + 0 + 0.0002222 + 0.04348$$ $$R_{total} = 4.31650 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ > **Observation:** The two metallic layers ($R_2$ and $R_6$) contribute only **0.000316 m²·K/W** — about **0.0073%** of the total resistance. This confirms the design principle that **insulation resistance dominates the heat transfer equation**; structural steel is essentially a thermal "short circuit" if not insulated. ### Step 4: Compute the Overall U-value $$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{4.31650} = 0.2317 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$ ### Step 5: Compute the Total Heat Ingress The temperature differential is: $$\Delta T = T_{warm} - T_{cold} = 20 - (-25) = 45 \text{ K}$$ The total heat flow through the bulkhead is: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ $$Q = 0.2317 \times 20 \times 45$$ $$Q = 208.5 \text{ W}$$ ### Step 6: Check the Interface Temperature (Condensation Check) Verify that the temperature at the interface between stainless steel lining and corkboard is **above the dew point** of the accommodation air (assume dew point $T_{dew} = +12°C$). The heat flux is: $$q = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{208.5}{20} = 10.43 \text{ W/m}^2$$ The temperature at the cold face of the stainless steel lining (interface with corkboard) is found by dropping temperature from the warm side: $$T_{interface} = T_{warm} - q \cdot (R_{si} + R_2)$$ $$T_{interface} = 20 - 10.43 \cdot (0.1250 + 0.00009375)$$ $$T_{interface} = 20 - 10.43 \cdot 0.12509$$ $$T_{interface} = 20 - 1.304 = 18.70°C$$ Since $T_{interface} = 18.70°C > T_{dew} = 12°C$, **no condensation will form**. The design is safe. > **Conclusion:** This bulkhead allows only ~209 W of heat ingress — a remarkably low value for a 20 m² wall with a 45 K temperature differential. This is the power of layered low-k insulation. ## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylindrical Pipe) **Problem Setup:** A bare exhaust pipe has outer radius $r_1 = 50$ mm, surface temperature $T_1 = 200°C$, and ambient temperature $T_\infty = 30°C$. The convective coefficient outside is $h_o = 10$ W/(m²·K). Insulation (calcium silicate, $k_{ins} = 0.10$ W/(m·K)) is being considered, with a thickness of 30 mm. **Objective:** Determine if adding this insulation *increases* or *decreases* heat loss. ### Step 1: Calculate Heat Loss from the Bare Pipe (per meter length) $$q_{bare} = h_o \cdot (2\pi r_1) \cdot (T_1 - T_\infty)$$ $$q_{bare} = 10 \cdot (2\pi \cdot 0.050) \cdot (200 - 30)$$ $$q_{bare} = 10 \cdot 0.3142 \cdot 170 = 534.1 \text{ W/m}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate the Critical Radius of Insulation $$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o} = \frac{0.10}{10} = 0.010 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 3: Compare with Bare Pipe Radius The bare pipe radius is $r_1 = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m, which is **much greater** than $r_{cr} = 10$ mm. **Interpretation:** Since $r_1 > r_{cr}$, **adding insulation will always reduce heat loss** for this pipe. Good — we can proceed safely. ### Step 4: Calculate Heat Loss with Insulation (Insulated Outer Radius $r_2 = 50 + 30 = 80$ mm) For a long cylinder, the heat loss per unit length is: $$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\frac{1}{r_1 h_i} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{r_2 h_o}}$$ Assuming the inner surface resistance is negligible ($h_i \to \infty$ for a metal pipe wall), and using $r_2 = 0.080$ m: $$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (200 - 30)}{\frac{\ln(0.080/0.050)}{0.10} + \frac{1}{0.080 \times 10}}$$ **Numerator:** $$2\pi (170) = 1068.1 \text{ W/m (numerator)}$$ **Denominator — insulation conduction term:** $$\frac{\ln(1.6)}{0.10} = \frac{0.4700}{0.10} = 4.700 \text{ m·K/W}$$ **Denominator — outer film term:** $$\frac{1}{0.080 \times 10} = \frac{1}{0.80} = 1.250 \text{ m·K/W}$$ **Total denominator:** $$4.700 + 1.250 = 5.950 \text{ m·K/W}$$ **Heat loss per meter:** $$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{1068.1}{5.950} = 179.5 \text{ W/m}$$ ### Step 5: Compare and Conclude | **Configuration** | **Heat Loss (W/m)** | **% Change** | |---|---|---| | Bare pipe | 534.1 | — | | Insulated pipe | 179.5 | **–66.4%** | The insulation reduces heat loss by **~66%**, while also providing burn protection for crew and reducing fire risk from the high-temperature exhaust. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Wisdom:** Had the bare pipe radius been *smaller* than 10 mm (e.g., a 6 mm hydraulic line), adding thin insulation could paradoxically *increase* heat loss. This counterintuitive phenomenon arises because the increase in outer surface area (more convection) outpaces the increase in conductive resistance. The critical radius is a fundamental design check. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Heat Flow Through a Multi-Layer Ship Bulkhead (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Warm Accommodation Air

R_si = 0.1250] B --> C[Stainless Steel Lining
