L = 50 mm, k = 0.022] E --> F[Vapor Barrier
Negligible R] F --> G[Steel Bulkhead
L = 10 mm, k = 45.0] G --> H[Outer Surface Film
R_se = 0.0435] H --> I[Cold Reefer Chamber Air
T_cold = minus 25 degree C] J[Heat Flux q = 10.43 W per m squared] -. direction of flow .-> B K[Total Resistance R_total = 4.32] -. governing equation .-> L[Q = U times A times delta T] L --> M[Q total = 208.5 W] ``` ## 4.2 Insulation Material Selection Flow (Mermaid Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start Selection Process] --> Q1{Application Zone} Q1 -- Refrigerated Cargo -- subzero --> M1[PU Foam or PIR Panels] Q1 -- LNG minus 163 C --> M2[Perlitre Vacuum System] Q1 -- Engine Room Casing --> M3[Mineral Wool plus Glass Cloth] Q1 -- Accommodation --> M4[Rock Wool plus MLV] Q1 -- Steam Pipe Lagging --> M5[Calcium Silicate] Q1 -- Fire Boundary A60 --> M6[Mineral Wool plus Vermiculite] M1 --> CK1[Verify Vapor Barrier on Warm Side] M2 --> CK2[Verify Vacuum Integrity] M3 --> CK3[Verify Acoustic STC Rating] M4 --> CK4[Verify STC plus Thermal R] M5 --> CK5[Check Critical Radius Effect] M6 --> CK6[Verify SOLAS A60 Test Certificate] CK1 --> FINAL[Issue Insulation Specification] CK2 --> FINAL CK3 --> FINAL CK4 --> FINAL CK5 --> FINAL CK6 --> FINAL ``` ## 4.3 Refrigeration Heat Load Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph QLOAD[Total Heat Load Q total] Q1[Q conduction through walls deck overhead] Q2[Q convection door openings and hatches] Q3[Q radiation solar load on exposed deck] Q4[Q respiration biological cargo heat] Q5[Q service lighting fans crew entry] end Q1 --> SUM[Sum and Apply Safety Factor 1.10 to 1.25] Q2 --> SUM Q3 --> SUM Q4 --> SUM Q5 --> SUM SUM --> PLANT[Sizing of Compressor and Condenser Plant] PLANT --> SPEC[Refrigeration Plant Specification in kW] ``` ## 4.4 Insulation System Components and Their Functions ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Outer Steel Plate] --> B[Anti Corrosion Coating] B --> C[Adhesive or Mechanical Fixing] C --> D[Primary Insulation Layer] D --> E[Vapor Barrier Membrane] E --> F[Secondary Insulation Layer] F --> G[Inner Lining SS or FRP] G --> H[Air Gap and Interior Finish] H --> I[Interior Volume] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define thermal conductivity. State its units and explain its significance in ship insulation design.** **Model Answer:** **Thermal conductivity (k or λ)** is defined as the rate of heat flow (in watts) through a unit area (1 m²) of a homogeneous material of unit thickness (1 m) when the temperature difference between the two faces is 1 K, under steady-state conditions. **SI Units:** W/(m·K) **Significance in Ship Insulation Design:** - A **lower k-value** indicates a better insulator. Marine insulation materials are chosen specifically for their **low thermal conductivity** (typically 0.020–0.060 W/(m·K)). - The insulating efficiency is directly proportional to the **reciprocal of k**; halving k doubles the thermal resistance for the same thickness. - In reefer chambers, even a small reduction in k (e.g., from 0.040 to 0.022 W/(m·K) when switching corkboard to PU foam) **reduces refrigeration plant size, energy consumption, and operating cost** substantially over the ship's life. - It is also temperature-dependent: for accurate design, k must be evaluated at the **mean operating temperature** of the insulation layer. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is meant by the critical radius of insulation? Why is it important in lagging of small-bore ship piping?** **Model Answer:** The **critical radius of insulation** ($r_{cr}$) is the outer radius of an insulated cylinder at which the **heat loss is maximum**. Beyond this radius, adding more insulation reduces heat loss. Below it, adding insulation paradoxically *increases* heat loss. **Formula:** $$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$$ Where $k_{ins}$ is the conductivity of the insulation and $h_o$ is the external convective heat transfer coefficient. **Importance in Small-Bore Pipe Lagging:** - For **large pipes** (e.g., exhaust manifolds, steam lines), $r_1 > r_{cr}$, so insulation is always beneficial. - For **small-bore pipes** (e.g., 6–10 mm instrument lines, hydraulic pipes), $r_1 < r_{cr}$. Wrapping them in insulation *increases* the convective surface area faster than the conductive resistance grows, leading to **greater heat loss**. - The remedy: for small-bore lines, either use a **highly conductive insulation** (high k) or **avoid insulation altogether** if the heat loss is acceptable. - In ship design, this check is **mandatory** when lagging auxiliary steam, fuel, and hydraulic lines. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** for **forgetting surface film resistances** ($R_{si}$ and $R_{se}$) in composite wall calculations. Always include them. > - Another frequent error is **mixing up temperature difference units** (using °C instead of K in the ratio). Since $\Delta T$ in K = $\Delta T$ in °C, this is numerically OK, but **state the units explicitly** to earn full marks. > - In pipe insulation problems, students often apply the plane wall formula $Q = kA\Delta T/L$ instead of the **cylindrical formula** with $\ln(r_2/r_1)$. This is a **3-mark deduction**. > - For SOLAS questions, citing the **correct class** (A-0, A-30, A-60, B-15, C) and the **specific clause reference** (e.g., SOLAS Ch. II-2, Reg. 3.2, 3.4) is essential for full marks. > - Always draw a **clearly labeled schematic** of the wall layers — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper] **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different **types of insulation materials** used in shipbuilding, highlighting their typical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A reefer chamber bulkhead consists of the following layers (from warm accommodation side to cold chamber side): | Layer | Material | Thickness (mm) | k (W/(m·K)) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Internal surface film | — | $h_{si} = 7.5$ W/(m²·K) | | 2 | Galvanized iron lining | 2.0 | 50 | | 3 | Corkboard | 80 | 0.040 | | 4 | Polyurethane foam | 50 | 0.024 | | 5 | Mild steel bulkhead | 12 | 45 | | 6 | External surface film | — | $h_{se} = 25$ W/(m²·K) | Calculate: - (i) the overall U-value, - (ii) the heat flow through 25 m² of this bulkhead, given $T_{warm} = +22°C$ and $T_{cold} = -28°C$, - (iii) the temperature at the interface between the corkboard and the polyurethane foam. - Determine if the design is condensation-safe if the dew point of the warm-side air is +14°C. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): **Types of Marine Insulation Materials:** 1. **Corkboard (Expanded Cork, k ≈ 0.040 W/(m·K))** - Natural, lightweight, water-resistant. - Used in: **refrigerated cargo chambers**, fish holds, meat storage. - Disadvantage: more expensive; thickness limited. 2. **Polyurethane (PU) Foam (k ≈ 0.020–0.026 W/(m·K))** - Closed-cell, sprayed or slab form. - Used in: **reefer holds, LNG pipe insulation, domestic chillers**. - Very low k; excellent moisture resistance. 3. **Polyisocyanurate (PIR) Foam (k ≈ 0.022 W/(m·K))** - Improved fire performance over PU. - Used in: **A-class boundaries** with high fire rating. 4. **Mineral Wool / Rock Wool (k ≈ 0.035–0.045 W/(m·K))** - Non-combustible, sound-absorbing. - Used in: **engine room casings, A-60 bulkheads, accommodation**. 5. **Glass Wool (k ≈ 0.035 W/(m·K))** - Lightweight, fire-resistant. - Used in: **duct lagging, accommodation thermal/acoustic insulation**. 6. **Calcium Silicate (k ≈ 0.060 W/(m·K) at 200°C)** - High-temperature tolerant. - Used in: **steam pipe lagging, exhaust manifold insulation**. 7. **Vermiculite / Perlite (k ≈ 0.050–0.060 W/(m·K))** - Granular, fireproof. - Used in: **LNG tank secondary insulation**, fire protection plaster. 8. **Aluminum Foil (k ≈ 230 W/(m·K), but ε ≈ 0.03)** - Used as **radiation shield** in double-foil insulation systems. (Schematic of typical insulated bulkhead with vapor barrier and lining is expected. Award 1–2 marks for this.) #### Model Solution (b): **Step 1: Compute each layer's thermal resistance** $$R_{si} = \frac{1}{7.5} = 0.1333 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{0.002}{50} = 0.00004 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W (negligible)}$$ $$R_3 = \frac{0.080}{0.040} = 2.0000 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ $$R_4 = \frac{0.050}{0.024} = 2.0833 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ $$R_5 = \frac{0.012}{45} = 0.000267 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W (negligible)}$$ $$R_{se} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.0400 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ **Step 2: Total resistance** $$R_{total} = 0.1333 + 0.00004 + 2.0000 + 2.0833 + 0.000267 + 0.0400$$ $$R_{total} = 4.2569 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ **Step 3: Overall U-value** $$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{4.2569} = 0.2349 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$ **Step 4: Total heat flow** $$\Delta T = 22 - (-28) = 50 \text{ K}$$ $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.2349 \times 25 \times 50$$ $$Q = 293.6 \text{ W}$$ **Step 5: Interface temperature (between corkboard and PU foam)** Heat flux: $$q = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{293.6}{25} = 11.74 \text{ W/m}^2$$ Drop from warm side across: $R_{si}$, $R_2$, and $R_3$ (corkboard): $$\Delta T_{across\_R3\_warmface} = q \cdot (R_{si} + R_2 + R_3)$$ $$= 11.74 \cdot (0.1333 + 0.00004 + 2.0000)$$ $$= 11.74 \cdot 2.1334 = 25.05°C$$ Therefore: $$T_{interface} = T_{warm} - \Delta T_{drop} = 22 - 25.05 = -3.05°C$$ **Step 6: Condensation check** The interface is at $T = -3.05°C$, which is far below the dew point of $+14°C$. However, the **condensation check** is performed at the **warm-side surface** of the insulation (the GI lining surface), not at the interface between corkboard and PU foam. The temperature at the warm face of the corkboard (cold face of GI lining): $$T_{warm\_face} = T_{warm} - q \cdot (R_{si} + R_2)$$ $$= 22 - 11.74 \cdot (0.1333 + 0.00004) = 22 - 1.566 = 20.43°C$$ Since $T_{warm\_face} = 20.43°C > T_{dew} = 14°C$, **no condensation will occur on the inner lining surface**. The design is **condensation-safe**. > **Note for students:** The interface temperature inside the insulation can be very cold, but this is not a condensation concern as there is no air contact. The critical condensation surface is always the **innermost surface** (the one facing the conditioned space). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper] **(a)** Discuss the **SOLAS requirements for fire insulation** in passenger and cargo ships. Explain the meaning of A-60, A-30, A-15, A-0, B-15, B-0, and C classifications with a tabulated summary. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bare steel exhaust pipe has an outside diameter of 100 mm, surface temperature 220°C, surrounded by ambient air at 35°C with $h_o = 12$ W/(m²·K). Insulation (calcium silicate, $k = 0.10$ W/(m·K), thickness 40 mm) is wrapped around it. Assuming negligible inner convective resistance, calculate: - (i) heat loss per meter of bare pipe, - (ii) heat loss per meter of insulated pipe, - (iii) the critical radius of insulation, and comment on whether insulation was a wise choice, - (iv) annual fuel savings in kg if the recovered heat represents 20% of the fuel energy value, given the calorific value = 42 MJ/kg, and the ship operates 70% of the year. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): **SOLAS Fire Insulation Framework (Chapter II-2, Part B):** SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) prescribes **passive fire protection** by dividing the ship into **vertical and horizontal zones** separated by fire-rated divisions. The objective is to **contain a fire at its source of origin** and prevent its spread for a specified duration, allowing time for evacuation and firefighting. **Classifications of Fire Divisions (per SOLAS Reg. II-2/3):** | **Class** | **Integrity (min)** | **Insulation Value (min)** | **Typical Application** | |---|---|---|---| | **A-60** | 60 | 60 | Bulkhead between machinery space and accommodation; cargo pump room to accommodation | | **A-30** | 30 | 30 | Corridor bulkheads in high-risk areas | | **A-15** | 15 | 15 | Specific boundaries where moderate risk exists | | **A-0** | 0 | 0 | Steel bulkhead with no insulation (still acts as flame barrier due to steel) | | **B-15** | 15 | 0 | Corridor partitions, accommodation linings (limits fire spread but no insulation) | | **B-0** | 0 | 0 | Non-load-bearing divisions with no fire rating | | **C** | 0 | 0 | Non-load-bearing, no fire integrity required (e.g., toilet partitions) | - **"A" classes** prevent passage of **flame and smoke** and limit temperature rise on the unexposed face. - **"B" classes** prevent passage of **flame only** for a shorter period. - **"C" classes** have no fire-resistance requirement; constructed of non-combustible materials. > **Construction Principle:** A-class divisions are typically built with **steel bulkheads + mineral wool insulation + steel or gypsum lining**. The insulation thickness is determined by the rating (e.g., 50–70 mm of mineral wool for A-60). #### Model Solution (b): **Given:** - Bare pipe outside diameter = 100 mm → $r_1 = 0.050$ m - Surface temperature $T_1 = 220°C$ - Ambient $T_\infty = 35°C$ - $h_o = 12$ W/(m²·K) - Insulation: $k_{ins} = 0.10$ W/(m·K), thickness 40 mm - Insulated outer radius $r_2 = 0.050 + 0.040 = 0.090$ m - $\Delta T = 220 - 35 = 185°C = 185$ K **Step 1: Heat loss from bare pipe (per meter length)** $$q_{bare} = h_o \cdot 2\pi r_1 \cdot \Delta T$$ $$q_{bare} = 12 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 0.050 \cdot 185$$ $$q_{bare} = 12 \cdot 0.3142 \cdot 185$$ $$q_{bare} = 697.4 \text{ W/m}$$ **Step 2: Heat loss from insulated pipe (per meter length)** $$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi \Delta T}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{r_2 h_o}}$$ **Numerator:** $$2\pi \cdot 185 = 1162.4$$ **Conduction term in denominator:** $$\frac{\ln(0.090/0.050)}{0.10} = \frac{\ln(1.8)}{0.10} = \frac{0.5878}{0.10} = 5.878$$ **Outer film term in denominator:** $$\frac{1}{0.090 \cdot 12} = \frac{1}{1.08} = 0.9259$$ **Total denominator:** $$5.878 + 0.9259 = 6.804$$ **Heat loss per meter:** $$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{1162.4}{6.804} = 170.8 \text{ W/m}$$ **Step 3: Critical radius** $$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o} = \frac{0.10}{12} = 0.00833 \text{ m} = 8.33 \text{ mm}$$ Bare pipe radius $r_1 = 50$ mm $\gg$ $r_{cr} = 8.33$ mm. **Comment:** Since $r_1 \gg r_{cr}$, the insulation is **highly effective**, reducing heat loss by 75.5%. **Step 4: Heat saved per meter** $$q_{saved} = 697.4 - 170.8 = 526.6 \text{ W/m}$$ **Step 5: Annual operating hours** $$t = 0.70 \times 365 \times 24 = 6132 \text{ hours/year}$$ **Step 6: Annual heat energy saved per meter** $$E_{saved} = q_{saved} \cdot t = 526.6 \text{ W} \times 6132 \text{ h} = 3{,}229{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 3230 \text{ kWh} \approx 11.62 \text{ GJ per meter}$$ **Step 7: Annual fuel saved (per meter, accounting for 20% recoverable)** If only 20% of the fuel's calorific value is recoverable as useful heat: $$m_{saved} = \frac{E_{saved}}{0.20 \times \text{CV}}$$ $$m_{saved} = \frac{11.62 \times 10^9 \text{ J}}{0.20 \times 42 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}}$$ $$m_{saved} = \frac{11.62 \times 10^9}{8.4 \times 10^6} = 1383 \text{ kg/m of pipe per year}$$ > **Engineering Note:** For a ship with even 20 m of similar exhaust piping, this translates to **~27.7 tonnes of fuel saved annually** — a substantial operational economy, justifying the insulation cost in a single voyage. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Insulation in ships serves three purposes:** thermal, acoustic, and fire protection — each governed by separate codes (ISO 6946, SOLAS Ch. II-2, IACS UR S7). - [x] **Fourier's Law** is the foundation: $Q = kA\Delta T/L$. Always convert thicknesses to meters and temperature differences to Kelvin. - [x] **Composite walls** are analyzed as **resistances in series**; total $R = \sum L_i/k_i + R_{si} + R_{se}$. - [x] **Metallic layers (steel, aluminum, copper)** contribute negligible thermal resistance — design focus is on the **low-k insulation layers**. - [x] **U-value** is the inverse of total R. Always include surface films in precision design; for KTU problems, they are mandatory. - [x] **Vapor barriers** must be placed on the **warm side** of insulation to prevent moisture migration and condensation. - [x] **Condensation check:** The critical surface is the **innermost surface** (warm-side) — its temperature must exceed the dew point of the adjacent air. - [x] **Pipe insulation** uses **cylindrical heat conduction**: $Q/L = 2\pi\Delta T / [\ln(r_2/r_1)/k + 1/(r_2 h_o)]$. - [x] **Critical radius of insulation** $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$: If pipe radius < $r_{cr}$, insulation *increases* heat loss — a classic KTU trap question. - [x] **SOLAS fire ratings:** A-60, A-30, A-15, A-0; B-15, B-0; C. A-class divisions limit **flame + smoke + temperature rise**; B-class limits flame only; C has no rating. - [x] **Reefer chambers** use **PU foam (k ≈ 0.022)** over **corkboard (k ≈ 0.040)**; the vapor barrier is mandatory due to very low dew points (–30°C cargo spaces). - [x] **LNG carriers** use **perlite + vacuum insulation** to handle –163°C; conventional foams are inadequate. - [x] **Acoustic insulation** is rated by **STC** (Sound Transmission Class); typical shipboard target is STC ≥ 45 for accommodation bulkheads adjacent to machinery. - [x] **Surface emissivity (ε)** controls radiative losses. Aluminum foil (ε ≈ 0.03) drastically cuts radiation; use it as a facing in high-temperature lagging. - [x] **For KTU numerical problems:** Show every step. Mark allocations typically are: 2 marks for schematic + identification, 3 marks for setup, 2 marks for arithmetic, and 2 marks for final answer with units and conclusion. - [x] **Common error to avoid:** Do not confuse **thermal conductivity (k)** with **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)**. Conductivity is a material property; U-value is a system property. - [x] **Energy economics:** A well-insulated pipe saves both **fuel** and **reduces thermal stress** on machinery and personnel — insulation pays for itself in 6–18 months on most high-temperature lines.

topics.title: Lining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛳️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lining** in shipbuilding refers to the systematic application of finishing materials, panels, insulation layers, and decorative/protective surfaces to the **internal structure of a vessel**, including bulkheads, decks, ceilings (overheads), shell plating interior surfaces, and cabin/erection spaces. It is the *final aesthetic and functional layer* installed over the ship's structural steelwork and insulation to provide thermal comfort, acoustic isolation, fire protection, hygiene, durability, and visual appeal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Lining encompasses the design, material selection, fabrication, and installation of non-structural interior fitments — including ceiling panels, bulkhead sheathing, floor decking, thermal/acoustic insulation, and joinery work — that transform the bare steel hull into a habitable, safe, and regulation-compliant marine environment."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a ship as a **giant floating steel building**. The steel hull is like the bare concrete skeleton of a house — strong, cold, and ugly. **Lining is essentially the "interior decoration and insulation" of a house**, but on a ship it must additionally:
- Survive constant ship motions and vibrations 🚢
- Resist fire per **SOLAS Chapter II-2** 🔥
- Withstand salt-laden humid air 🌊
- Be lightweight to preserve displacement and stability ⚖️
- Comply with class rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) ⚓

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** If the ship's hull is the brick walls of your house, then **lining is the POP finish + wallpaper + false ceiling + wooden flooring** — all engineered to marine-grade standards.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **IMO Resolution A.653(16)** — Fire test procedures for lining materials
- **SOLAS Ch. II-2, Reg. 5.3** — Required thermal insulation of steel structures
- **STCW 2010 Manila Amendments** — Habitability standards for crew spaces
- **ISO 8468:2007** — Ship interior air quality
- **U-value (W/m²K)**: Heat transmission coefficient; lower is better. Target ≤ **0.7 W/m²K** for accommodation spaces in cold seas.
- **STC (Sound Transmission Class)**: Acoustic rating; target **STC ≥ 45** for cabin bulkheads.
- **NRC (Noise Reduction Coefficient)**: 0 to 1 scale; target **NRC ≥ 0.50** for accommodation linings.

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Lining Work

| **Category** | **Location** | **Primary Function** |
|---|---|---|
| **Ceiling Lining** | Overhead (deckhead) | Thermal, acoustic, fire, aesthetics |
| **Bulkhead Lining** | Vertical partitions | Decorative, hygienic, fire-rated |
| **Floor Lining / Deck Covering** | Horizontal walking surfaces | Anti-slip, wear, thermal, drainage |
| **Insulation Lining** | Behind all surfaces | Thermal & acoustic separation |
| **Joinery Lining** | Cabins, galleys, pantries | Furniture-grade finishing |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always relate lining answers to *three pillars*: **Safety (Fire)**, **Comfort (Thermal/Acoustic)**, and **Weight (Lightweight Engineering)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Lining System (Step-by-Step)

A marine lining is never a single layer — it is a **multi-layered composite sandwich** working in unison. The breakdown from the steel structure outward is:

1. **Substrate (Steel Hull / Bulkhead Plate):** Provides structural support and heat sink.
2. **Primary Insulation Layer:** Mineral wool / rock wool / glass wool, typically **25–100 mm** thick, density **32–200 kg/m³**, non-combustible per IMO.
3. **Vapour Barrier:** Aluminium foil or polyethylene sheet to prevent condensation penetration (critical for **cold-room and refrigerated cargo hold linings**).
4. **Secondary Insulation (Optional):** Polyurethane foam or aerogel for ultra-cold applications (LNG, reefer ships).
5. **Framework / Studs:** Galvanized steel or aluminium T-bars, often **25×25 mm** to **50×50 mm** cross-section.
6. **Facing / Finishing Panel:** PVC, melamine-impregnated plywood, aluminium composite, FRP, or decorative laminate.
7. **Trim & Joints:** PVC profiles, silicone sealant, fire-rated gaskets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Multiple Layers?** Each layer addresses a *single* engineering problem. Separating concerns (insulation ≠ decoration ≠ fire) makes maintenance, repair, and class-survey inspections far easier.

## 2.2 Material Classification Matrix (High-Yield Table)

| **Material** | **Density (kg/m³)** | **Thermal Conductivity k (W/mK)** | **Fire Rating (IMO)** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mineral Wool (Rock Wool) | 32–200 | **0.035–0.040** | Non-combustible | Primary insulation |
| Glass Wool | 16–48 | **0.030–0.038** | Non-combustible | Acoustic + thermal |
| Polyurethane Foam (PUR) | 30–60 | **0.022–0.028** | Combustible (treated) | Cold rooms |
| Polyisocyanurate (PIR) | 32–48 | **0.020–0.024** | Class 1 / 2 | Engine room casing |
| PVC Panel | 1300–1500 | **0.16** | Self-extinguishing | Ceiling / bulkhead finish |
| Aluminium Composite (ACP) | 5500 (sheet) | **205** | Class B / A2 | Galley, public spaces |
| Marine Plywood (BS 1088) | 700 | **0.13** | Treated Class 1 | Joinery backing |
| Ceramic Tiles | 2300 | **1.3** | Non-combustible | Wet rooms (bath, WC) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on insulation & lining.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{t}$ | Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction | W (Watts) |
| 2 | $U = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{t_i}{k_i}}$ | Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (Composite Wall) | W/m²K |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{t}{k}$ | Thermal Resistance of Single Layer | m²K/W |
| 4 | $R_{total} = \sum R_i = \sum \dfrac{t_i}{k_i}$ | Total Thermal Resistance (Series) | m²K/W |
| 5 | $\dot{q} = U \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat Flux per Unit Area | W/m² |
| 6 | $W_{lining} = \rho \cdot t \cdot A$ | Weight of Lining Material | kg |
| 7 | $STL \approx 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47$ | Mass Law Approximation (Sound Transmission Loss) | dB |
| 8 | $L_{panel} = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$ | Flexural Rigidity of Panel (Stiffness D) | N·m |
| 9 | $f_c = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho_s \cdot L^4}}$ | Coincidence Frequency of Panel | Hz |
| 10 | $\text{Cost} = A \cdot p_{mat} + A \cdot p_{lab}$ | Total Lining Cost = Material + Labour | Currency/m² |

**Where:**
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity (W/mK)
- $A$ = Surface area (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference (K or °C)
- $t$ = Thickness (m)
- $\rho$ = Material density (kg/m³)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (Pa)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio
- $m$ = Surface density (kg/m²)
- $f$ = Frequency (Hz)
- $p_{mat}$, $p_{lab}$ = Material/labour cost per m²

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations allow a Naval Architect to verify whether a proposed lining layout meets SOLAS, DNV, and ISO requirements **before** fabrication — saving massive retrofit costs.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Weight Optimisation:** Every additional 1 mm of unnecessary lining = ~3–5 tonnes per large vessel. Critical for displacement-limited ships.
- **Thermal Design of Reefer Ships:** Lining thickness directly determines refrigeration compressor load and fuel consumption.
- **Fire Safety Engineering (FSE):** Lining materials on escape routes must be **non-combustible per FTP Code**.
- **Acoustic Comfort:** Modern cruise vessels market "silent cabins" — driven by lining design (STC > 50).
- **Hygiene in Galleys / Hospitals:** Smooth, seamless, non-porous lining reduces bacterial growth — required by **ILO MLC 2006** and **WHO ship sanitation guides**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Canonical KTU Numerical)

> **"A steel bulkhead, 6 m wide × 3 m high, is lined on the accommodation side with 50 mm of mineral wool (k₁ = 0.040 W/mK) and a 6 mm PVC facing panel (k₂ = 0.16 W/mK). The inside air temperature is 22 °C, the steel bulkhead is at 12 °C, and the convection coefficients are hᵢ = 8.5 W/m²K (inside) and hₒ = 23 W/m²K (outside sea). Calculate: (a) the U-value, (b) the total heat loss per hour, and (c) the weight of the mineral wool lining."** [Adapted KTU 2024 Pattern]

### Given Data

- $A = 6 \times 3 = 18 \, \text{m}^2$
- $t_1$ (Mineral wool) $= 0.050 \, \text{m}$, $k_1 = 0.040 \, \text{W/mK}$
- $t_2$ (PVC) $= 0.006 \, \text{m}$, $k_2 = 0.16 \, \text{W/mK}$
- $t_{steel} \approx 0.010 \, \text{m}$, $k_{steel} = 45 \, \text{W/mK}$
- $T_i = 22 \, °\text{C}$, $T_o = 12 \, °\text{C}$, $\Delta T = 10 \, \text{K}$
- $h_i = 8.5 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, $h_o = 23 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$
- $\rho_{wool} = 48 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

### Part (a): U-Value Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute individual thermal resistances of each layer (in series):**

$$R_i = \dfrac{1}{h_i} = \dfrac{1}{8.5} \approx 0.1176 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{wool} = \dfrac{t_1}{k_1} = \dfrac{0.050}{0.040} = 1.2500 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{pvc} = \dfrac{t_2}{k_2} = \dfrac{0.006}{0.16} = 0.0375 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{steel} = \dfrac{t_{steel}}{k_{steel}} = \dfrac{0.010}{45} = 0.00022 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_o = \dfrac{1}{h_o} = \dfrac{1}{23} \approx 0.0435 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Sum all resistances in series:**

$$R_{total} = R_i + R_{wool} + R_{pvc} + R_{steel} + R_o$$

$$R_{total} = 0.1176 + 1.2500 + 0.0375 + 0.00022 + 0.0435 = 1.4488 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Compute U-value:**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{1.4488} \approx 0.6902 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $U \approx 0.69 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ is **just above** the IMO recommended limit of 0.70 W/m²K. Adding even 5 mm of mineral wool would push it into compliance.

### Part (b): Total Heat Loss per Hour

**Step 4 — Heat flux per unit area:**

$$\dot{q} = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.6902 \times 10 = 6.902 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Total heat loss rate:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{q} \cdot A = 6.902 \times 18 = 124.24 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Energy per hour:**

$$Q_{hour} = \dot{Q} \times 3600 = 124.24 \times 3600 = 447{,}264 \, \text{J} \approx 447.3 \, \text{kJ/hr}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{hour} \approx 447.3 \, \text{kJ/hr} \quad \text{or} \quad 0.124 \, \text{kW}}$$

### Part (c): Weight of Mineral Wool Lining

**Step 7 — Volume of wool:**

$$V_{wool} = A \cdot t_1 = 18 \times 0.050 = 0.90 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 8 — Mass of wool:**

$$W_{wool} = \rho \cdot V = 48 \times 0.90 = 43.2 \, \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{wool} = 43.2 \, \text{kg}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** Students often forget the **convective resistances (1/hᵢ and 1/hₒ)**. Without them, U jumps to ~0.80 W/m²K, giving wrong compliance verdict. **Always include surface films.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Lining Selection Decision Tree

| **Ship Zone** | **Primary Concern** | **Recommended Lining Stack** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passenger Cabin** | Acoustic + aesthetics | GW 50mm + PVC panel | NRC > 0.55, lightweight |
| **Crew Cabin** | Cost + comfort | MW 40mm + Laminated plywood | Affordable, STC 42 |
| **Galley** | Hygiene + fire | SS304 sheet + MW 25mm | IMO non-combustible |
| **Engine Casing** | Heat + sound | Rock wool 80mm + Al sheet (2mm) | U < 0.5, STI > 50 |
| **Reefer Hold** | Thermal | PIR 100mm + Al vapour barrier | k = 0.022, anti-condensation |
| **Bath / WC** | Water + slip | Ceramic tile + waterproof membrane | Impervious, anti-bacterial |
| **Wheelhouse** | Glare + RFI | Anti-static vinyl + GW 25mm | Crew fatigue reduction |
| **Cargo Hold** | Wear + corrosion | Bitumen paint + sacrificial plate | Lowest cost, easy repair |

## 3.3 Quick Derivation: Acoustic Mass Law

The mass law (used to estimate STC of a lining panel):

$$STL = 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47$$

**Derivation logic:** Doubling the surface mass ($m$) increases STL by 6 dB; doubling frequency increases STL by 6 dB. This is the *baseline* — real panels with damping/decoupling exceed this by 5–10 dB.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Validity:** This is an **engineering approximation** valid above the coincidence frequency. Class-society calculations (e.g., DNV SILENT-A) use the *finite element method* and account for cavity resonances, stud damping, and flanking paths.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Marine Lining System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Steel Substrate Hull / Bulkhead] --> B[Anti-Corrosion Primer Coat]
    B --> C[Vapour Barrier Layer - Aluminium Foil 0.05 mm]
    C --> D[Primary Insulation - Mineral Wool 25 to 100 mm]
    D --> E[Galvanised Stud Framework - T Bar 25x25 mm]
    E --> F[Secondary Insulation - Optional - PIR 30 mm]
    F --> G[Finishing Panel - PVC or ACP or Plywood]
    G --> H[Trim and Joints - PVC Profiles and Silicone Sealant]
    H --> I[Surface Treatment - Anti Bacterial Coating]
    I --> J[Final Inspected Lined Surface]

    K[SOLAS FTP Code] -.Compliance.-> D
    L[IMO Res A 653] -.Fire Test.-> G
    M[ISO 8468] -.Air Quality.-> I
    N[Class Society DNV ABS LR] -.Approval.-> J

    classDef safety fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef insul fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#2874a6,color:#000
    classDef finish fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b7950b,color:#000

    class A,B safety
    class C,D,F insul
    class E,G,H,I,J finish
    class K,L,M,N reg
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Lining Selection Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Ship Zone Identified] --> Q1{Zone Type?}
    Q1 -->|Passenger| R1[Acoustic + Aesthetics Priority]
    Q1 -->|Crew| R2[Cost + Habitability]
    Q1 -->|Engine Room| R3[Heat + Sound Attenuation]
    Q1 -->|Cargo Hold| R4[Durability + Low Cost]
    Q1 -->|Reefer| R5[Thermal + Vapour Control]

    R1 --> S1[Glass Wool 50mm + PVC Panel]
    R2 --> S2[Mineral Wool 40mm + Laminated Plywood]
    R3 --> S3[Rock Wool 80mm + Aluminium Sheet 2mm]
    R4 --> S4[Bitumen Coating + Sacrificial Anode]
    R5 --> S5[PIR 100mm + Aluminium Vapour Barrier]

    S1 --> T1[Verify STC greater than 45]
    S2 --> T2[Verify U value less than 0.7]
    S3 --> T3[Verify STC greater than 55]
    S4 --> T4[Verify Coating Thickness 200 micron]
    S5 --> T5[Verify Vapour Barrier Permeance less than 0.05 perm]

    T1 --> U1[Class Society Approval]
    T2 --> U1
    T3 --> U1
    T4 --> U1
    T5 --> U1

    classDef start fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#d4ac0d,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#ebf5fb,stroke:#21618c,color:#000
    classDef solution fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#f5eef8,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000

    class P1 start
    class Q1,R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 branch
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 solution
    class T1,T2,T3,T4,T5 check
    class U1 endnode
```

## 4.3 Functional Layer Map (ASCII — Lining Stack Cross-Section)

```
  <---- Accommodation Side ----> | <-- Steel Hull --> | <---- Sea Side ---->
                                  |                    |
  [1] Final Panel (PVC/ACP) 1mm  |                    |
  [2] Air gap / Stud cavity 25mm |                    |
  [3] Secondary insulation PIR 30mm (optional)        |
  [4] Primary insulation Mineral Wool 50mm            |
  [5] Vapour barrier Aluminium foil 0.05mm            |
  [6] Steel bulkhead plate 10mm  ===================  |
  [7] Anti-corrosion primer 0.1mm                    |
  [8] External paint system 0.3mm                    |
                                  |                    |
  (^ facing air)               (^ structural)       (^ sea)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Heat flow from the accommodation side encounters **5 resistance layers** (panel, gap, insulation, vapour barrier, paint) **plus** the steel plate **plus** outside convection. This is exactly what the **U-value formula** in Section 2 models.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*"List and briefly explain any three types of marine lining materials, with their specific application areas on board a ship."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — bullet scoring):**

1. **Mineral Wool (Rock Wool):** Non-combustible, density 32–200 kg/m³, k = 0.035–0.040 W/mK. Used as **primary thermal insulation** behind bulkheads, ceilings, and deckheads in accommodation blocks. **(1 mark)**

2. **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) Panels:** Self-extinguishing per IMO, smooth hygienic surface, k = 0.16 W/mK. Used as **finishing layer** in cabins, pantries, and wet rooms for easy cleaning. **(1 mark)**

3. **Marine Plywood (BS 1088):** WBP-grade, 7–25 mm, k = 0.13 W/mK, treated with fire-retardant. Used in **joinery work** — cabin furniture, wardrobes, and decorative bulkhead panels. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*"Define the term 'U-value' in the context of ship lining. Why is it critical for Naval Architects?"*

**Model Answer:**

The **U-value (Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient)** is the rate of heat transfer through a unit area of a composite lining system per unit temperature difference, expressed in **W/m²K**. Mathematically, $U = \dfrac{1}{\sum (t_i/k_i) + 1/h_i + 1/h_o}$.

It is critical for Naval Architects because:
- **Compliance Verification:** IMO/SOLAS mandate U ≤ 0.70 W/m²K for accommodation.
- **HVAC Sizing:** Determines refrigeration and heating plant capacity.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Lower U = lower fuel consumption and operating cost.

**(1 mark each — definition + 2 reasons)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**"A steel accommodation bulkhead of size 8 m × 3 m is to be lined with 60 mm of glass wool (k = 0.035 W/mK) and a 3 mm aluminium composite panel (k = 0.205 W/mK). The interior air is at 24 °C and the bulkhead steel surface is at 14 °C. Inside convection coefficient is 9 W/m²K and outside is 25 W/m²K. Steel plate thickness is 12 mm, k_steel = 45 W/mK. Calculate the U-value, total heat loss per hour, and the weight of the glass wool (density 32 kg/m³)."**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — U-value and Heat Loss

**Step 1:** Calculate individual resistances.

$$R_i = 1/9 = 0.1111 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{gw} = 0.060/0.035 = 1.7143 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{acp} = 0.003/0.205 = 0.0146 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{steel} = 0.012/45 = 0.00027 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_o = 1/25 = 0.0400 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2:** Sum.

$$R_{total} = 0.1111 + 1.7143 + 0.0146 + 0.00027 + 0.0400 = 1.8803 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3:** U-value.

$$U = 1/1.8803 = 0.5318 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K} \quad \boxed{U \approx 0.53 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}} \text{ ✓ IMO Compliant}$$

**Step 4:** Heat flux.

$$\dot{q} = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.5318 \times 10 = 5.318 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Area.

$$A = 8 \times 3 = 24 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 6:** Heat loss per hour.

$$\dot{Q} = 5.318 \times 24 = 127.63 \, \text{W}$$

$$Q_{hr} = 127.63 \times 3600 = 459{,}468 \, \text{J} \approx 459.5 \, \text{kJ/hr} \quad \boxed{Q_{hr} \approx 459.5 \, \text{kJ/hr}}$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Weight & Compliance Discussion

**Step 7:** Volume of glass wool.

$$V = A \cdot t = 24 \times 0.060 = 1.44 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 8:** Mass.

$$W = \rho \cdot V = 32 \times 1.44 = 46.08 \, \text{kg} \quad \boxed{W_{wool} = 46.08 \, \text{kg}}$$

**Step 9: Discussion [2 bonus marks]**

- $U = 0.53 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K} < 0.70$ — **Compliant with SOLAS Ch. II-2.** ✅
- Weight penalty of **46.08 kg** is acceptable for accommodation zones.
- If engine room, double to 100 mm to drop U below 0.40 W/m²K.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**"Discuss in detail the classification, materials, installation sequence, and quality-control tests for marine ceiling and bulkhead lining. Include references to relevant class society and IMO regulations."**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Classification & Materials

| **Category** | **Material** | **Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Insulation | MW / GW / PIR / PUR | Behind panels |
| Facing | PVC, ACP, Plywood, FRP | Visible surface |
| Framework | Galv. steel studs | Mounting |
| Trim | PVC/Al profiles | Joints |
| Fixings | SS screws, rivets | Attachment |

**Regulations:** SOLAS II-2 (fire), FTP Code Annex 1 Pt 5 (surface flammability), ISO 8468 (IAQ), DNV Pt 3 Ch 4 (accommodation).

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Installation Sequence & QC

**Sequence:** (1) Surface prep → (2) Primer → (3) Vapour barrier → (4) Insulation → (5) Stud fixing → (6) Panel fixing → (7) Trim → (8) Inspection.

**QC Tests:**
- **Adhesion test** (pull-off ≥ 0.5 MPa) — **(1 mark)**
- **Flammability test** (FTP Code) — **(1 mark)**
- **Thermal U-value verification** in-situ — **(1 mark)**
- **Acoustic STC test** in completed cabin — **(1 mark)**
- **Visual + dimensional check** — **(1 mark)**
- **Class surveyor attendance + sign-off** — **(1 mark)**
- **Final handover documentation** (as-built drawings, material certs) — **(1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting the **convective surface resistances (1/hᵢ, 1/hₒ)** in U-value numericals → -2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not specifying **material densities** in the materials list → -1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **R-value** (resistance) with **U-value** (conductance) → -2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "fire-proof" instead of "**non-combustible per SOLAS**" → -1 mark (terminology matters).
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Skipping the **weight calculation** part → -3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 6:** Forgetting to **convert mm to m** in thickness (e.g., using 50 instead of 0.050) → entire numerical wrong.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Read 5 minutes before the exam!**

- 🔥 **Lining ≠ Insulation** — Lining is the *visible* finishing system; insulation is a *hidden* thermal/acoustic layer.
- 📐 **Master the U-value equation:** $U = 1/\sum (t_i/k_i + 1/h_i + 1/h_o)$. **Always include both surface films.**
- ⚖️ **SOLAS Mandate:** $U \leq 0.70 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ for accommodation bulkheads.
- 🏗️ **Layer Order (from outside-in):** Steel → Primer → Vapour Barrier → Insulation → Studs → Panel → Trim.
- 🧯 **Fire Material Hierarchy:** Mineral wool > PIR > PUR > PVC (self-extinguishing) > Plywood (treated).
- 📊 **Densities to memorise:** MW ≈ 48 kg/m³, GW ≈ 32 kg/m³, PVC ≈ 1400 kg/m³, Steel = 7850 kg/m³.
- 🔢 **Fourier's Law** $Q = kA\Delta T/t$ — used for single-layer heat conduction only.
- 🎯 **Acoustic rule of thumb:** Doubling mass = +6 dB; doubling frequency = +6 dB (mass law).
- 🚢 **Weight is king** — every unnecessary kg of lining reduces payload or stability.
- ✅ **Class rules to mention in answers:** DNV Pt 3 Ch 4, Lloyd's SSC Rules, ABS SVR, BV NR 546.
- 📑 **Key IMO cites:** SOLAS Ch. II-2 Reg 5.3, FTP Code Annex 1, Res. A.653(16), Res. A.754(18).
- 🧮 **Common calculation pitfall:** Always convert **mm → m** before substituting into equations.
- 🎨 **Aesthetic Hierarchy (visible quality):** ACP > Melamine > PVC > Plywood > Galvanised sheet.
- 💧 **Vapour barrier is critical** in cold rooms and reefer holds — without it, condensation destroys insulation.
- 🏥 **Hygiene linings** (galley, hospital, WC) must be **seamless and non-porous** per WHO/MLC 2006.
- 🔥 **FTP Code fire-test trio:** (1) Non-combustibility, (2) Surface flammability, (3) Smoke & toxicity.
- 📏 **Standard panel sizes:** PVC 1220×2440 mm; ACP 1220×2440 mm; Plywood 1220×2440 mm — design around these to minimise waste.
- ⏱️ **Time-management in KTU exam:** Spend ≤ 8 min on Part A (3 marks), ≤ 25 min on Part B (14 marks).

---

topics.title: Partitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST604 - SHIP DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Partitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Partitions (in Naval Architecture)**: Vertical structural members, either transverse or longitudinal, that subdivide the hull of a ship into a series of watertight or non-watertight compartments. They are commonly referred to as **bulkheads** and serve as the primary means of damage control, transverse strength, and operational zoning within the vessel.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, partitions are classified based on their **function**, **location**, and **degree of watertightness**. The most critical ones are the **transverse watertight bulkheads**, which are governed by international regulations such as the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** and classification society rules (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "A Ship is Like a High-Rise Apartment Building"**
>
> Imagine a ship as a long, floating apartment building lying on its side. Just as an apartment building is divided into separate flats by **firewalls** to prevent fire from spreading, a ship is divided into **compartments (partitions/bulkheads)** to prevent water from flooding the entire vessel if the hull is breached.
>
> If one apartment catches fire, the firewalls confine the damage. Similarly, if a ship's hull is punctured in a collision, the watertight partitions confine the flooding to a limited number of compartments, keeping the ship afloat.
>
> **Key insight**: A passenger ship or a tanker is essentially a "system of sealed tubes" — the partitions are the walls of those tubes. Without partitions, even a small puncture would sink the ship.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permeability ($\mu$)**: ratio of volume of water that can flood a compartment to total volume of compartment. Typical values are **0.60 to 0.95** (machinery spaces have higher permeability than empty holds).
- **Subdivision factor ($F$)**: a number between **0 and 1** that defines the degree of subdivision required.
- **Margin line**: a line drawn **76 mm (3 inches)** below the bulkhead deck at the side.
- **Floodable length**: maximum length of compartment which may be flooded without submerging the margin line.

## 1.4 Importance in Ship Design

| Aspect | Role of Partitions |
|---|---|
| **Safety** | Limit flooding extent after collision/grounding |
| **Structural** | Provide transverse strength, resist racking |
| **Operational** | Separate engine rooms, cargo holds, accommodation |
| **Regulatory** | Mandatory under SOLAS Chapter II-1 for passenger ships |
| **Damage Stability** | Critical input for subdivision index calculations |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Partitions

### 2.1.1 Based on Function
- **Collision Bulkhead** — at the forward end, prevents progressive flooding from bow damage.
- **Engine Room Bulkhead** — separates machinery space from cargo/crew spaces.
- **Aft Peak Bulkhead** — closes the aft end, supports shaft tunnel.
- **Cofferdam Bulkhead** — isolates hazardous cargo from other spaces.
- **Wash Bulkheads** — reduce free-surface effect of liquids.
- **Fire-resisting Bulkheads** — prevent spread of fire (A-class, B-class divisions).

### 2.1.2 Based on Watertightness
- **Watertight bulkheads (WT)** — prevent passage of water under pressure.
- **Non-tight bulkheads** — resist water spray, used in accommodation.
- **Gas-tight bulkheads** — required for tankers carrying volatile cargoes.

### 2.1.3 Based on Orientation
- **Transverse partitions** — vertical, athwartships (most common).
- **Longitudinal partitions** — vertical, fore-and-aft (used in double-hull tankers).
- **Horizontal partitions** — decks acting as subdivision members.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Partitions

**Why are partitions needed?**
1. A ship is vulnerable to hull damage from collisions, grounding, and material fatigue.
2. The **archimedes' principle** requires the ship to retain sufficient reserve buoyancy.
3. Without subdivision, even a small breach would sink the vessel.

**How do partitions work?**
- They seal off compartments using gaskets, stiffeners, and closing devices (dogs, cleats, hinges).
- When one compartment is breached, water enters but is contained.
- The ship continues to float using the buoyancy of the remaining intact compartments.
- The number and spacing of partitions is determined by the **floodable length curve** and the **permeability** of each space.

## 2.3 Permeability of Compartments

> [!NOTE]
> **Permeability ($\mu$)** is defined as:
>
> $$\mu = \frac{\text{Volume of water that can enter a compartment}}{\text{Total volume of the compartment}}$$

Typical values used in KTU problems and SOLAS regulations:

| Space Type | Permeability $\mu$ |
|---|---|
| Machinery spaces | $0.80$ to $0.85$ |
| Cargo holds (empty) | $0.60$ to $0.70$ |
| Cargo holds (grain/coal) | $0.60$ |
| Crew accommodation | $0.95$ |
| Chain lockers | $0.30$ |
| Refrigerated holds | $0.65$ |
| Tanks (full) | $0.00$ (or $\approx 0$ if completely full) |
| Tanks (empty) | $0.95$ to $0.98$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical formula sheet** for Partitions. Memorize all entries.

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu$ | Permeability | $\mu = V_w \mid V$ | dimensionless, $0 \le \mu \le 1$ |
| $L_{flood}$ | Floodable length | read from curve at given $\mu$ | metres (m) |
| $C$ | Block coefficient | $C = \nabla \mid (L \cdot B \cdot T)$ | dimensionless |
| $l$ | Length of compartment | $l = $ actual length of subdivision | metres (m) |
| $F_n$ | Permissible length factor | $F_n = (F \cdot l) \mid (f \cdot L)$ | depends on service |
| $s$ | Service numeral | $s = A_k + P$ | passenger ships only |
| $F$ | Factor of subdivision | $F = N \mid (N + 1)$ for two-compartment standard | dimensionless |
| $R$ | Residual righting lever | from GZ curve at $20^\circ$ to $40^\circ$ | metres (m) |
| $A_k$ | $K$-factor | $A_k = N \cdot (7.5 \cdot L_{flood} + 4.5) \cdot 10^{-4}$ | depends on $N$ |
| $p_i$ | Probability of flooding | $p_i$ varies by location | dimensionless |
| $J_{s}$ | Attained subdivision index | $J_s = \Sigma p_i \cdot s_i$ | dimensionless |
| $R_{s}$ | Required subdivision index | per SOLAS regulation | dimensionless |

> **Note on the notation $V_w \mid V$** in the table: This means the ratio of floodable water volume to total volume. In handwritten form, write $V_w / V$ (vertical bar replaced by slash).

## 2.5 Number of Compartments (SOLAS Standard)

For a passenger ship, the **standard of subdivision** is defined by the maximum number of adjacent compartments that can be flooded while the ship still satisfies the damage stability criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-compartment standard**: $F = \dfrac{1}{2}$ — only ONE compartment can be flooded.
>
> **Two-compartment standard**: $F = \dfrac{2}{3}$ — TWO adjacent compartments can be flooded.
>
> **Three-compartment standard**: $F = \dfrac{3}{4}$ — THREE adjacent compartments can be flooded.

The factor of subdivision $F$ in general for an $N$-compartment standard is:

$$F = \frac{N}{N + 1}$$

The condition for safe subdivision is that the **actual length of the compartment $\ell$** must satisfy:

$$\ell \le F \cdot L_{flood}$$

where $L_{flood}$ is the floodable length corresponding to the compartment's permeability at the equilibrium waterline.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architects** use these formulas to position bulkheads during the early design phase.
- **Classification Societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV)** verify designs against SOLAS and proprietary rules.
- **Damage Control Officers** in navies rely on subdivision design to limit flooding in battle damage.
- **Insurance** calculations for tankers and bulk carriers depend heavily on compartment permeability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Subdivision Factor $F$

### Starting Premise
Consider a passenger ship of length $L$ with $N$ as the maximum number of adjacent compartments allowed to flood simultaneously (the **standard of subdivision**).

**Step 1 — Probability of Survival**
The probability that the ship survives after damage depends on how many compartments can be flooded. Let the survival probability be proportional to the number of intact compartments.

**Step 2 — Defining the Index**
The subdivision index is the ratio of surviving to total compartments. After flooding $N$ compartments out of $(N+1)$ effective zones, the survival fraction is:

$$F = \frac{N}{N + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Special Cases**

For $N = 1$ (one-compartment standard):

$$F = \frac{1}{1 + 1} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.500$$

For $N = 2$ (two-compartment standard):

$$F = \frac{2}{2 + 1} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.667$$

For $N = 3$ (three-compartment standard):

$$F = \frac{3}{3 + 1} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
A higher $F$ means a stricter subdivision (more bulkheads required).

## 3.2 Derivation of Floodable Length from Permeability

### Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition
After flooding, the ship must float with the **margin line** just at the waterline (worst-case design condition). Using Archimedes' principle:

$$W_{intact} = W_{flooded} - W_{water\ entered}$$

The water-plane area $A_w$ at the margin line displaces a volume equal to the added weight of floodwater.

**Step 2 — Volume Relationship**
The volume of water entering a compartment of length $\ell$ and cross-section $A_c$ is:

$$V_w = \mu \cdot \ell \cdot A_c$$

The volume of the intact hull up to the margin line over the same length is:

$$V_{hull} = \ell \cdot A_c$$

The submerged volume of the intact ship at the margin line over the length $\ell$ is:

$$V_{sub} = \mu \cdot \ell \cdot A_c + V_{hull,sub}$$

**Step 3 — Floodable Length Definition**
By rearranging the equilibrium equations (see standard texts, e.g., Rawson & Tupper, *Basic Ship Theory*), the **floodable length $L_{flood}$** at a given permeability $\mu$ is the length of compartment such that flooding it would just submerge the margin line.

This is conventionally obtained from **Curves of Floodable Length** (often called **Forman curves**), which are read off a graph of $L_{flood}$ vs. permeability $\mu$ for the specific ship.

**Step 4 — Practical Use**
In KTU problems, $L_{flood}$ is usually **given** in a table or curve. The condition to check is:

$$\ell_{actual} \le F \cdot L_{flood}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Subdivision Check

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Given**:
> - Length of ship $L = 120$ m
> - Two-compartment subdivision standard (so $F = 2/3$)
> - Permeability of machinery space $\mu_m = 0.85$
> - Permeability of cargo hold $\mu_c = 0.70$
> - Floodable length for machinery space (from Forman curve): $L_{f,m} = 95$ m
> - Floodable length for cargo hold: $L_{f,c} = 110$ m
> - Actual length of machinery space compartment: $\ell_m = 28$ m
> - Actual length of cargo hold: $\ell_c = 35$ m
>
> **Find**: Does the ship satisfy the subdivision standard?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Verify the subdivision factor**
Given as $N = 2$, so:

$$F = \frac{2}{2+1} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 2 — Compute the permissible length for machinery space**
The condition is $\ell_m \le F \cdot L_{f,m}$:

$$F \cdot L_{f,m} = 0.667 \times 95 = 63.37 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Check the inequality**

$$\ell_m = 28 \text{ m} \le 63.37 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

The machinery compartment satisfies the subdivision rule.

**Step 4 — Compute the permissible length for cargo hold**

$$F \cdot L_{f,c} = 0.667 \times 110 = 73.37 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Check the inequality**

$$\ell_c = 35 \text{ m} \le 73.37 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

The cargo hold also satisfies the rule.

**Step 6 — Conclusion**
Both compartments are within their permissible lengths, so the subdivision is **acceptable** under the two-compartment standard.

## 3.4 Derivation of Permeability for Mixed Spaces

If a compartment contains both a machinery section and an empty section, the **effective permeability** is the volume-weighted average:

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{\mu_1 \cdot V_1 + \mu_2 \cdot V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$$

where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are the volumes of the two sub-spaces.

**Example derivation**:
Suppose a compartment has $V_1 = 200 \text{ m}^3$ of machinery ($\mu_1 = 0.85$) and $V_2 = 300 \text{ m}^3$ of empty hold ($\mu_2 = 0.60$).

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{(0.85 \times 200) + (0.60 \times 300)}{200 + 300}$$

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{170 + 180}{500} = \frac{350}{500} = 0.70$$

## 3.5 Margin Line and Permissible Submersion

The **margin line** is set at **76 mm (3 inches)** below the bulkhead deck (for passenger ships). After flooding, the residual freeboard must equal the margin line.

The residual freeboard $f_r$ is given by:

$$f_r = \frac{V_{reserve}}{A_w}$$

where $V_{reserve}$ is the volume of intact buoyancy above the waterline after flooding, and $A_w$ is the waterplane area at the margin line.

The condition for survival:

$$f_r \ge f_{margin} = 0.076 \text{ m}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem Patterns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix to map KTU exam questions to solution methods.

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Verify subdivision | $\ell$, $L_{flood}$, $N$ | OK or not? | Compute $F$, then $\ell \le F \cdot L_{flood}$ |
| Position bulkhead | $F$, $L_{flood}$ | $\ell_{max}$ | $\ell_{max} = F \cdot L_{flood}$ |
| Effective permeability | $V_1, \mu_1, V_2, \mu_2$ | $\mu_{eff}$ | Volume-weighted average |
| $K$-factor | $L_{flood}$, $N$ | $A_k$ | $A_k = N \cdot (7.5 L_{flood} + 4.5) \cdot 10^{-4}$ |
| Subdivision index | $p_i$, $s_i$ | $J_s$ | Sum over compartments |
| Margin line check | $V_{reserve}$, $A_w$ | $f_r$ | $f_r = V_{reserve} / A_w$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ship Subdivision System

Since physical ship drawings are too complex for Mermaid, we use a **block-level functional architecture** showing how the partition system interacts with the safety subsystems of a vessel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Ship Hull L B T] --> B1[Subdivision Designer]
    A2[Loading Condition] --> B1
    A3[Damage Scenarios] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Forman Curves Builder]
    B1 --> C2[Permeability Assigner]
    C1 --> D1[Equilibrium Calculator]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Floodable Length Map]
    E1 --> F1[Bulkhead Position Optimizer]
    F1 --> G1[Layout Approval]
    G1 --> H1[Classification Society IRS LR DNV]
    H1 --> I1[SOLAS Chapter II-1 Compliance]
    A4[Operational Loads] --> F1
    A5[Stiffener Design Loads] --> F1
    F1 --> J1[Longitudinal Strength Check]
    F1 --> J2[Transverse Strength Check]
    F1 --> J3[Damage Stability Index J]
    J3 --> K1{Index Satisfies R}
    K1 -- Yes --> L1[Final Bulkhead Schedule]
    K1 -- No --> F1
    L1 --> M1[Production Drawing Release]
```

## 4.2 Process Flowchart — Damage Stability Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Define Ship Geometry] --> P2[Assign Permeability per Compartment]
    P2 --> P3[Generate Forman Curves L flood vs mu]
    P3 --> P4[Choose Subdivision Standard N]
    P4 --> P5[Compute F equals N over N plus 1]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Permissible Length F times L flood]
    P6 --> P7[Compare with Actual Compartment Length l]
    P7 --> P8{l less than or equal to F L flood}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Subdivision OK]
    P8 -- No --> P10[Reposition Bulkhead]
    P10 --> P2
    P9 --> P11[Check Margin Line Residual Freeboard]
    P11 --> P12{f r greater than or equal to 0.076 m}
    P12 -- Yes --> P13[Final Approval]
    P12 -- No --> P10
```

## 4.3 Compartmentalisation Schematic (Side View Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Bow[Collision Bulkhead BT1] --> H1[Cargo Hold 1]
    H1 --> BT2[Bulkhead 2]
    BT2 --> H2[Cargo Hold 2]
    H2 --> BT3[Bulkhead 3]
    BT3 --> ER[Engine Room]
    ER --> BT4[Bulkhead 4]
    BT4 --> H3[Hold 3]
    H3 --> BT5[Aft Peak Bulkhead]
    BT5 --> AP[Aft Peak Tank]
    H1 -. has permeability mu 0.70 .- Perm1
    ER -. has permeability mu 0.85 .- Perm2
    AP -. has permeability mu 0.30 .- Perm3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**:
> - Each rectangle represents a compartment bounded by a transverse bulkhead.
> - The collision bulkhead (BT1) is the forward-most watertight boundary.
> - Permeability annotations (e.g., $\mu = 0.85$ for engine room) drive the Forman curve lookup.
> - In a real ship drawing, these rectangles would be drawn in profile view, with frame spacing indicated.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "permeability" of a compartment as applied to ship subdivision. State the typical permeability values for a machinery space and an empty cargo hold.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Permeability ($\mu$)** is the ratio of the volume of water which may enter a compartment to the total volume of that compartment, when the ship is assumed flooded to the margin line.
>
> $$\mu = \frac{V_{water\ entered}}{V_{compartment}}$$
>
> It is a **dimensionless** number between 0 and 1.
>
> - **Machinery space**: $\mu \approx 0.80$ to $0.85$ (because machinery occupies space and is not fully flooded).
> - **Empty cargo hold**: $\mu \approx 0.60$ to $0.70$ (large open volume with high water uptake).
>
> (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for typical values.)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the "margin line" in the context of ship subdivision? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

> [!NOTE]
> The **margin line** is a hypothetical line drawn on the side shell plating of a passenger ship, parallel to the bulkhead deck and located **76 mm (3 inches)** below it, measured at the side.
>
> **Importance**:
> 1. It defines the **maximum permissible waterline** after a flooding casualty.
> 2. The ship must remain afloat with the margin line **above the actual waterline** after damage.
> 3. It provides a **safety reserve** to account for wave action, inaccuracies in damage assumptions, and heel-induced immersion.
> 4. It is a **regulatory requirement** under SOLAS Chapter II-1 Regulation 13.
>
> (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for importance.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern requires a **choice** of two alternative 14-mark questions within each module. Below are the two alternatives.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Subdivision Verification Problem

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 4]**

A passenger ship of length $L = 140$ m, breadth $B = 22$ m, and draft $T = 8$ m is to comply with the **two-compartment standard of subdivision**. The ship has three watertight compartments below the bulkhead deck:

| Compartment | Length $\ell$ (m) | Permeability $\mu$ | Floodable Length $L_{flood}$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fore Peak Tank | 8 | 0.30 | 130 |
| Machinery Space | 32 | 0.85 | 78 |
| Aft Hold | 40 | 0.70 | 96 |

The fore peak tank is sealed against flooding (treated as a void). Margin line is at $0.076$ m below bulkhead deck.

**Tasks**:
- **(a)** Compute the factor of subdivision $F$ and verify whether each compartment satisfies the subdivision requirement. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** If the aft hold length must be increased to 50 m, find the new required factor of subdivision $F'$ for safe subdivision. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Subdivision factor for $N=2$** (1 mark)

$$F = \frac{N}{N+1} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.667$$

**Step 2 — Permissible length for Machinery Space** (2 marks)

$$\ell_{m,\ max} = F \cdot L_{f,m} = 0.667 \times 78 = 52.03 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with actual** (1 mark)

$$\ell_{m} = 32 \text{ m} \le 52.03 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

Machinery space satisfies the rule.

**Step 4 — Permissible length for Aft Hold** (2 marks)

$$\ell_{a,\ max} = F \cdot L_{f,a} = 0.667 \times 96 = 64.03 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compare** (1 mark)

$$\ell_a = 40 \text{ m} \le 64.03 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

Aft hold is also safe. The subdivision is acceptable.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Given new aft hold length** (1 mark)

$$\ell_a^{new} = 50 \text{ m},\quad L_{f,a} = 96 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Required factor of subdivision** (3 marks)
The condition is $\ell_a^{new} \le F' \cdot L_{f,a}$, so:

$$F' \ge \frac{\ell_a^{new}}{L_{f,a}} = \frac{50}{96} = 0.521$$

**Step 3 — Identify standard** (2 marks)

For $F' = 0.521$, this is less than $2/3 = 0.667$, so a **one-compartment standard** would suffice (since $1/2 = 0.500 < 0.521 < 2/3$). The ship can adopt the one-compartment standard.

**Step 4 — Interpretation** (1 mark)
With a 50 m hold, the new design meets the **one-compartment standard**, but **not** the originally specified two-compartment standard.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Permeability & Margin Line Problem

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 4, Alternative]**

A bulk carrier has a compartment of length $\ell = 28$ m and breadth $B = 18$ m. The compartment consists of:
- A lower hold of depth $d_1 = 6$ m, fully empty (assume permeability $\mu_1 = 0.65$).
- A top-side tank of depth $d_2 = 2$ m, which is full of ballast water ($\mu_2 = 0.00$ — no additional water can enter).

**Tasks**:
- **(a)** Compute the **effective permeability** of the combined compartment. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** If the residual waterplane area at the margin line is $A_w = 1850 \text{ m}^2$ and the reserve buoyancy volume after flooding is $V_{res} = 145 \text{ m}^3$, determine whether the **margin line criterion** is satisfied. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area** (1 mark)
Compartment cross-section: $A_c = B \times (d_1 + d_2) = 18 \times 8 = 144 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 2 — Compute volume of each sub-space** (2 marks)

- Lower hold: $V_1 = 28 \times 18 \times 6 = 3024 \text{ m}^3$
- Top-side tank: $V_2 = 28 \times 18 \times 2 = 1008 \text{ m}^3$

**Step 3 — Total volume** (1 mark)

$$V_{tot} = 3024 + 1008 = 4032 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Effective permeability formula** (1 mark)

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{\mu_1 V_1 + \mu_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute** (2 marks)

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{(0.65 \times 3024) + (0.00 \times 1008)}{4032} = \frac{1965.6 + 0}{4032} = 0.4875$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{eff} \approx 0.488}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recall margin line criterion** (1 mark)
The margin line is at $f_{margin} = 0.076$ m below the bulkhead deck.

**Step 2 — Residual freeboard formula** (2 marks)

$$f_r = \frac{V_{res}}{A_w}$$

**Step 3 — Compute residual freeboard** (1 mark)

$$f_r = \frac{145}{1850} = 0.0784 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with margin line requirement** (2 marks)

$$f_r = 0.0784 \text{ m} \ge 0.076 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion** (1 mark)
The residual freeboard of 78.4 mm exceeds the 76 mm margin line requirement, so the **margin line criterion is satisfied**. The ship survives the assumed damage scenario with a margin of 2.4 mm.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in Partitions Problems**
>
> 1. **Confusing the formula $F = N/(N+1)$ with $F = N/(N-1)$.** Memorize the form carefully — for $N=2$, $F = 2/3$, NOT $2/1 = 2$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $F$ when using $L_{flood}$.** A common error: "the actual length 30 m is less than the floodable length 96 m, so it's fine." This is **wrong** if $F < 1$. The correct check is $\ell \le F \cdot L_{flood}$.
>
> 3. **Mixing up margin line with bulkhead deck.** Margin line is 76 mm **below** the bulkhead deck, not on it.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in permeability.** Permeability is dimensionless. Never write "permeability = 0.70 m³" or similar.
>
> 5. **Not converting lengths to metres** before plugging into the formula. KTU checks units strictly.
>
> 6. **In volume-weighted permeability calculations**, students often forget that $\mu = 0$ means the tank is **already full** — this is correct, but only if the tank is genuinely full. An empty tank has $\mu = 0.95$ to $0.98$.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to state the standard of subdivision** before writing the formula for $F$. Always begin: "Given $N = 2$ compartment standard, $F = 2/3$."
>
> **Marks lost on average**: 2 to 4 marks per question if these pitfalls are not avoided.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Partitions (Module 4)**
>
> - **Definition**: Partitions (bulkheads) are vertical members subdividing the hull into watertight compartments.
> - **Three primary functions**: safety (flooding control), structural (transverse strength), operational (zoning).
> - **Types**: collision, engine room, cofferdam, wash, fire-resisting, gas-tight.
> - **Permeability $\mu$** = ratio of floodable water volume to total compartment volume. Dimensionless. Range: 0 to 1.
> - **Standard values** of $\mu$: machinery 0.80–0.85, holds 0.60–0.70, accommodation 0.95, full tanks 0.00.
> - **Effective permeability** for mixed compartments: $\mu_{eff} = (\mu_1 V_1 + \mu_2 V_2)/(V_1 + V_2)$.
> - **Margin line**: 76 mm below the bulkhead deck. Residual freeboard must exceed this.
> - **Factor of subdivision**: $F = N/(N+1)$ where $N$ is the number of compartments that may flood.
>   - $N=1 \Rightarrow F = 1/2$
>   - $N=2 \Rightarrow F = 2/3$
>   - $N=3 \Rightarrow F = 3/4$
> - **Subdivision check**: $\ell_{actual} \le F \cdot L_{flood}$.
> - **Floodable length** $L_{flood}$ is read from the **Forman curve** for a given $\mu$.
> - **SOLAS Chapter II-1** governs passenger ship subdivision; classification societies (IRS, LR, DNV) enforce rules.
> - **Margin line criterion**: $f_r = V_{reserve}/A_w \ge 0.076$ m.
> - **$K$-factor** formula: $A_k = N \cdot (7.5 \cdot L_{flood} + 4.5) \cdot 10^{-4}$.
> - **Subdivision index** $J_s$ must be $\ge$ required index $R_s$ (this is the **probabilistic** approach, used for large passenger ships).
> - Always **begin** the answer by stating $N$ and computing $F$ explicitly.
> - Always **end** the answer with a clear conclusion (e.g., "Subdivision is **acceptable**" or "**not acceptable**").
> - **Common exam pitfall**: do not write $F = 1$ for an infinitely subdivided ship — physically, $F \to 1$ as $N \to \infty$, but in practice, ships use $N = 1$, $2$, or $3$.
